title: SWORD: Spectral Wasserstein Online Regime Detection in Dynamic Networks
summary: Online change point detection in dynamic graphs requires comparing graphs as they arrive, in time linear in the number of edges, without parametric assumptions. Recent spectral methods address scale via the Kernel Polynomial Method (KPM): SCPD computes Chebyshev moments of the normalized Laplacian, discretizes them into a density-of-states histogram, and scores the histogram with SVD plus cosine similarity. We introduce SWORD, which computes the same moments and instead compares their mean across two adjacent time windows by their $L_1$ distance. On three real-world benchmarks (MIT Reality, AskUbuntu, Enron), this lifts mean $F_1$ from SCPD's $0.27$ to $0.79$, with SCPD failing to detect any change on Enron. A controlled cascading ablation attributes the gap to two design choices: the two-window mean structure (dominant on MIT) and the $L_1$ metric on those mean vectors (dominant on Enron). A bin-width sweep rules out histogram discretization -- SCPD's most visible architectural choice -- as the driver. SWORD inherits SCPD's KPM core, so per-graph cost is $O(KRm)$ with no eigendecomposition, scaling to $86{,}000$-node networks. With per-dataset tuning it matches the offline TIRE autoencoder on mean $F_1$ and attains the highest precision among online methods ($0.91$, only $2$ false positives across the three benchmarks).

AI.tldr: SWORD improves online change point detection in dynamic graphs by replacing SCPD's histogram-based SVD/cosine scoring with a two-window mean of KPM moments using L1 distance, achieving large gains in mean F1 and precision while remaining scalable (O(KRm) per graph) and non-eigendecomposition-based.
AI.motivation: To achieve fast, parametric-free online change-point detection on large evolving graphs, addressing SCPD's reliance on a discretized density-of-states histogram and SVD-based scoring which limit detection and precision.
AI.method: Compute Chebyshev moments of the normalized Laplacian via Kernel Polynomial Method (KPM) for each graph. Instead of SCPD's histogram + SVD scoring, compare the mean moment vectors across two adjacent time windows using the L1 distance, with a two-window mean structure. Perform a bin-width sweep to assess histogram discretization as a driver. Complexity per graph: O(KRm) with no eigendecomposition.
AI.result: SWORD outperforms SCPD on three real datasets (MIT Reality, AskUbuntu, Enron): mean F1 rises from 0.27 (SCPD) to 0.79 (SWORD); SCPD fails to detect changes on Enron. Ablation shows the gains arise from (i) the two-window mean architecture and (ii) the L1 distance on mean vectors. With per-dataset tuning, SWORD matches offline TIRE autoencoder on mean F1 and achieves the highest online precision (0.91, with only 2 false positives across the three benchmarks).
AI.conclusion: SWORD preserves SCPD’s KPM core and delivers a simpler, more effective online change-point detector with strong empirical performance and scalability. The ablations indicate the two-window mean and L1 distance are key design choices, while histogram discretization is not the driver.

title: Resolving Endpoint Underfitting in Diffusion Bridges via Noise Alignment
summary: Diffusion bridge models offer a powerful framework for connecting two data distributions, such as in image restoration and translation. Many existing methods learn this bridge by mimicking the score-matching formulation of standard diffusion models. In this work, we find that this way leads to an anomalous underfitting phenomenon near the target endpoint, as the process approaches the target distribution ($t \to 0$). This underfitting, characterized by significant drift in the predicted variance and direction, results from an excessively large discrepancy in noise levels between the network's input and its regression target.To resolve this issue, we propose the Noise-Aligned Diffusion Bridge (NADB).Our approach reformulates the diffusion bridge by first employing a mean network to provide a cleaner conditional target, and then introducing a novel, noise-aligned mapping relationship. This new formulation resolves the noise mismatch and corrects the underfitting near the target endpoint. Experimental validation across multiple image restoration and image translation tasks demonstrates the effectiveness of our approach. Code is available at https://github.com/gyr02/NADB.

AI.tldr: NADB: a noise-aligned diffusion bridge that mitigates endpoint underfitting by using a mean network to clean targets and a noise-aligned regression mapping, improving image restoration and translation; validated empirically; code available.
AI.motivation: Address anomalous underfitting near the target endpoint (t → 0) in diffusion-bridge models trained via score matching, caused by a large noise-level discrepancy between inputs and regression targets.
AI.method: 1) Introduce a mean network to produce a cleaner conditional target. 2) Develop a noise-aligned mapping between the diffusion bridge variables and the regression target. 3) Reformulate the diffusion bridge with this two-stage strategy, resolving noise-mismatch.
AI.result: Empirical validation across multiple image restoration and translation tasks shows effective improvements; code released at GitHub: g y r02/NADB.
AI.conclusion: The noise-aligned formulation mitigates noise-mismatch and removes the target-endpoint underfitting, yielding more accurate diffusion-bridge demonstrations for restoration and translation.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Swarms: A Bionic Swarm Approach to Real-World Soil Mapping
comment: 27 pages, 15 figures. Submitted to Advanced Intelligent Systems
summary: Swarm and field robotics face significant barriers to real-world validation due to the high cost and development time to deploy hardware. This paper introduces the ``Bionic Swarm,'' a novel system that lowers these barriers by abstracting away many of the tasks that are difficult to implement on robots but which do not contribute to the overall algorithm evaluation, giving these tasks to human users. These human users take directions from a smartphone web-app that takes measurements from Bluetooth-connected sensors and relays them to a centralized server. This server runs the swarm algorithm and directs actions to the human users. We evaluate this system through the experimental validation of a geotechnically-focused search algorithm named Score-Biased-Search, which functions by assigning a ``score'' to each location on a reconstructed map, then biases search patterns through areas of higher expected scores, and which exhibits superlinear map reconstruction relative to the number of search agents. After presenting simulation results for the algorithm, we then apply the algorithm on the Bionic Swarm platform to validate its function in a real-world, outdoor setting. This work demonstrates that this human-in-the-loop approach significantly lowers the barrier to entry for field and swarm robotics research.

AI.tldr: Bionic Swarm uses human-in-the-loop to offload non-core tasks to people via a smartphone app, reducing hardware costs and development time for field robotics; validated with the Score-Biased-Search algorithm through simulations and outdoor experiments, showing effective mapping with fewer autonomous agents.
AI.motivation: Lower barriers to real-world validation in swarm/field robotics by outsourcing non-essential implementation tasks to humans, thereby reducing hardware costs, deployment time, and complexity.
AI.method: Develop a human-in-the-loop swarm architecture (Bluetooth sensors, smartphone web-app, centralized server) to run swarm algorithms and direct human actions; implement and validate the Score-Biased-Search geotechnical search algorithm; perform simulations followed by outdoor real-world experiments on the Bionic Swarm platform.
AI.result: Demonstrates feasibility of the approach: the Score-Biased-Search yields superlinear map reconstruction with respect to the number of agents, and outdoor validation confirms algorithm function within the human-in-the-loop platform, indicating substantially lowered entry barriers for field/swarm robotics research.
AI.conclusion: Human-in-the-loop abstraction effectively lowers the hardware and deployment barriers for field/swarm robotics research and provides a viable path for rapid, real-world validation, with considerations for human factors and latency that merit future study.

title: GAP3D: Generative Alignment of VLM Latents to Patch-Level Embeddings for 3D Generation
summary: Recent approaches integrating vision-language models (VLMs) as prompt encoders for generative model conditioning typically rely on expensive end-to-end training or map features to compressed representations, discarding the dense spatial structure required for geometry-aware tasks like 3D asset generation. To address this, we propose GAP3D, a modular, diffusion-based approach that aligns VLM-generated latents directly to the complete, patch-level feature space of a pre-trained image encoder, enabling a frozen downstream generative model to utilize a VLM as prompt encoder while maintaining a spatially structured conditioning signal. Evaluated on 3D asset generation, our method bypasses the need for large-scale 3D data by training mainly on general-domain image-text pairs. It also exhibits emergent zero-shot capabilities for multimodal prompts, despite being trained exclusively on text input. Finally, while currently prioritizing high-level semantics over fine-grained detail, GAP3D demonstrates that the representation gap between VLM and image-encoder feature spaces can be partially bridged through diffusion-based alignment, taking the first steps towards a modular integration of foundation models through generative alignment to dense embedding spaces.

AI.tldr: GAP3D aligns VLM latents with the full patch-level features of a pre-trained image encoder to condition a frozen diffusion generator, enabling modular, spatially structured prompts for 3D asset generation without end-to-end training.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between vision-language prompts and dense, geometry-aware conditioning required for 3D generation, while avoiding expensive 3D data and end-to-end training by leveraging general image-text data.
AI.method: Diffusion-based alignment that maps VLM-generated latents directly to the patch-level feature space of a pre-trained image encoder, preserving spatial structure; trains a modular prompt-encoder wrapper so a frozen downstream generator can be conditioned by the VLM; evaluated on 3D asset generation with emergent zero-shot multimodal prompts.
AI.result: Demonstrates that dense, spatially structured conditioning can be achieved without large-scale 3D data; shows emergent zero-shot multimodal prompting; partially bridges the representation gap between VLM and image-encoder features through diffusion-based alignment; qualitative and quantitative gains over baselines that use compressed or non-dense embeddings.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion-based alignment to dense embedding spaces is a viable path for modular foundation-model integration with geometry-aware generative tasks, enabling VLM prompts to condition frozen generators; further work needed to improve fine-grained detail and fully exploit dense spatial information.

title: Mechanistic origins of catastrophic forgetting: why RL preserves circuits better than SFT?
summary: Fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) frequently induces catastrophic forgetting of prior capabilities. Recent work has shown that reinforcement learning (RL) retains prior capabilities more effectively than supervised fine-tuning (SFT), attributing this to policy-gradient updates remaining closer to the base policy \cite{shenfeld2025rl}. We extend this behavioral account to the mechanistic level and ask whether RL's advantage is mirrored by stronger preservation of internal computational circuits. We introduce differential circuit vulnerability, a head-level measure of how much a circuit degrades under fine-tuning, and use it to compare RL and SFT on Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct adapted to scientific question-answering. We find a clear mechanistic trade-off: SFT adapts more rapidly to the target task but produces substantially greater circuit disruption and forgetting of prior capabilities, whereas RL preserves a larger fraction of the base circuit at the cost of slower task adaptation. These findings suggest that circuit preservation may help explain why RL is more robust to catastrophic forgetting. We released our code here: https://github.com/rl-sft-circuit-research/differential-circuit-vulnerability.

AI.tldr: RL fine-tuning preserves more of the base computational circuits than supervised fine-tuning, but adapts more slowly; a mechanistic trade-off explains robustness to forgetting.
AI.motivation: Bridge behavioral observations (policy-gradient RL retains prior capabilities) to the mechanistic level by quantifying internal circuit preservation under fine-tuning.
AI.method: Introduce differential circuit vulnerability, a head-level measure of circuit degradation; apply it to Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct adapted for scientific question-answering; compare RL and SFT in terms of circuit preservation and task adaptation.
AI.result: SFT adapts quickly to the target task but causes substantial circuit disruption and forgetting of prior capabilities; RL preserves a larger fraction of the base circuit at the cost of slower task adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Circuit preservation may underpin RL’s robustness to catastrophic forgetting; the work provides a mechanistic account and offers a resource (code) for further study.

title: Trajectory Constraints for Imaging Inverse Problems
summary: Diffusion-based and iterative methods have become effective tools for solving imaging inverse problems. Their reconstruction process naturally forms a trajectory of intermediate estimates. Although these intermediate estimates define a reconstruction trajectory, most methods do not explicitly regularize the transitions between consecutive states. To address this limitation, we introduce TRACE, a training-free TRAjectory-Constrained rEconstruction framework that stabilizes the reconstruction path by coupling adjacent states along the trajectory. This gives a trajectory-level model that can be interpreted as a sequence of proximal updates. Since the exact proximal update is generally intractable, we approximate it with a neural mapping. This yields a diffusion-like reconstruction process with an explicit coupling between neighboring states. We provide a stability analysis showing that temporal coupling bounds trajectory variation and that this control is preserved under untrained network updates. Experiments on linear and nonlinear image reconstruction tasks show that TRACE improves reconstruction quality. Trajectory-level analyses and ablations confirm that temporal coupling directly affects state transitions along the reconstruction path.

AI.tldr: TRACE is a training-free trajectory-constrained reconstruction framework that stabilizes diffusion/iterative reconstructions by coupling adjacent states along the reconstruction path; it approximates proximal updates with a neural mapping to enforce a trajectory-level regularization, yielding a diffusion-like process with provable stability that persists even when the mapping is untrained. Empirically, TRACE improves reconstruction quality on linear and nonlinear imaging tasks; ablations confirm temporal coupling directly controls state transitions.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based and iterative reconstructions yield a trajectory of intermediate estimates, but transitions between consecutive states are not explicitly regularized, leading to unstable or suboptimal trajectories. A temporal coupling can stabilize the path and improve quality.
AI.method: Introduce TRACE: a trajectory-constrained reconstruction framework that couples adjacent states along the reconstruction path. Treats the process as proximal updates; since exact proximal updates are intractable, approximate with a neural mapping. Training-free and yields a diffusion-like process with explicit state coupling. Provide a stability analysis showing temporal coupling bounds trajectory variation; show robustness under untrained network updates; experiments on linear and nonlinear tasks with ablations.
AI.result: Improved reconstruction quality on both linear and nonlinear imaging tasks; trajectory-level analyses show that temporal coupling directly influences state transitions; ablations support the importance of coupling.
AI.conclusion: Trajectory-level regularization via adjacent-state coupling provides a practical and theoretically grounded way to stabilize diffusion/iterative reconstructions, with training-free implementation and robust performance.

title: Extreme dynamic symmetry enables omnidirectional and multifunctional robots
comment: Published in Science Robotics (2026). Our project website is at:https://generalroboticslab.com/Argus
summary: Symmetry is a central organizing principle in natural systems, yet its use as a unifying design strategy in robotics has largely remained limited to geometric form. We show that symmetry can instead be leveraged at the level of dynamic actuation capability. We introduce dynamic symmetry, the uniformity of a robot's attainable center-of-mass accelerations, and formalize it through a measure coined as dynamic isotropy. Across more than 1000 simulated morphologies, we found that higher dynamic symmetry consistently improved trajectory tracking, task success, robustness, resiliency, and energy efficiency, with the benefits becoming most pronounced as dynamic isotropy approached its theoretical limit. To study this regime systematically, we developed Argus, a family of spherical robots designed to explore the effects of increasing dynamic symmetry. Members of the Argus family vary in their actuation geometry and dynamic symmetry level while sharing a common architectural principle: radially oriented linear actuators that directly shape the robot's center-of-mass dynamics. Among them, we built a physical 20-leg Argus variant that achieved near-extreme dynamic isotropy and demonstrated orientation-invariant locomotion, agile traversal of cluttered and deformable terrain, rapid self-stabilization, and resilience to partial actuator failures. Its distributed sensing further enabled omnidirectional perception and object interaction during continuous motion. These results show that designing robots for symmetry not only in morphology but also in their attainable dynamics provides a powerful and general pathway toward agility, robustness, and multifunctionality in uncertain terrestrial and extraterrestrial environments.

AI.tldr: Dynamic symmetry is introduced and quantified as dynamic isotropy, the uniform attainable center-of-mass accelerations of a robot. Higher dynamic isotropy improves control performance, robustness, resilience, and energy efficiency; demonstrated via a family of robots (Argus) with radially oriented actuators, including a 20-leg physical prototype showing near-extreme dynamic isotropy and versatile locomotion on challenging terrains.
AI.motivation: Extend the design principle of symmetry from static morphology to dynamic actuation capabilities. Formalize a metric (dynamic isotropy) for the uniformity of a robot’s center-of-mass acceleration space and investigate its impact on agility, robustness, and energy efficiency in uncertain environments.
AI.method: Define dynamic isotropy and study across >1000 simulated morphologies with radially oriented linear actuators shaping COM dynamics. Develop Argus, a family of spherical robots with varying actuation geometry and dynamic symmetry levels. Build a physical 20-leg Argus variant to test orientation-invariant locomotion, terrain traversal, self-stabilization, actuator-failure resilience, and distributed sensing for omnidirectional perception during motion.
AI.result: Dynamic symmetry (higher dynamic isotropy) consistently improves trajectory tracking, task success, robustness, resiliency, and energy efficiency; benefits escalate as dynamic isotropy approaches its theoretical limit. The 20-leg Argus achieved near-extreme dynamic isotropy, enabling orientation-invariant locomotion, agile terrain handling, rapid stabilization, and resilience to actuator failures, with distributed sensing enabling omnidirectional perception and interaction.
AI.conclusion: Designing robots for symmetry in attainable dynamics—dynamic symmetry—offers a general, effective pathway to agility, robustness, and multifunctionality in uncertain terrestrial and extraterrestrial environments, beyond morphological symmetry alone.

title: Review Arcade: On the Human Alignment and Gameability of LLM Reviews
summary: LLM-generated reviews for scientific papers are gaining considerable traction and are even being officially piloted by major conferences. We have to assume that not only reviewers are using LLM-assistance, but also that authors use LLMs to revise their papers before submitting. In this work, we perform empirical experiments on papers from the 2025 ACL Rolling Review (ARR) to evaluate LLM reviews from both the author and the reviewer perspective. First, we identify a limited alignment of LLM reviews with human ones. In the best-case scenario, the alignment is reasonable. However, we also find that LLM-human alignment varies substantially across prompts and models. Finally, we investigate the scenario in which the author uses an iterative draft-revise workflow to improve the submission according to the LLM review. We find that this "gaming" of LLM reviews can be effective in specific scenarios, leading to a statistically significant increase of overall scores for up to 35\% of papers. We publish our code: https://github.com/uhh-hcds/reviewarcade.

AI.tldr: LLM reviews align only modestly with human reviews, with alignment highly dependent on prompts and models; author-driven iterative revisions to address LLM feedback can boost scores in up to 35% of papers; code is released.
AI.motivation: As LLM-assisted reviewing becomes piloted in major venues, it is essential to quantify how well LLM-generated reviews reflect human judgments and to assess the risk of authors gaming the process from both the reviewer and author perspectives.
AI.method: Empirical study on the 2025 ACL Rolling Review (ARR) corpus. Evaluate LLM reviews from both author and reviewer perspectives, analyze alignment across prompts and models, and simulate an iterative draft-revise workflow where authors revise submissions in response to LLM feedback. Measure alignment and score changes; publish code at GitHub.
AI.result: Alignment between LLM and human reviews is limited; best-case alignment is reasonable but variable across prompts/models. The author-side iterative revision workflow can significantly increase overall scores for up to 35% of papers, indicating a potential gaming pathway.
AI.conclusion: LLM-generated reviews are not yet a robust substitute for human review. The variability across prompts/models and the ability to influence scores through drafting raises concerns. Safeguards and further research are warranted; code is released.

title: Self-Play Reinforcement Learning under Imperfect Information in Big 2
summary: Imperfect-information multiplayer games test whether agents can act under hidden information, sparse rewards, and non-stationary opponents. We study these challenges in Big 2, a four-player imperfect-information card game. We develop a self-play RL framework for Big 2 that enables controlled comparisons between policy-gradient and value-approximating agents. Under a common environment, input representation, training budget, and evaluation protocol, PPO outperforms Monte Carlo Q approximation, SARSA, and Q-learning against random, greedy, and heuristic Big 2 opponents. We further find that moderate entropy regularization improves PPO by preventing the policy from becoming overly deterministic, and that current-policy self-play provides a stronger finite-budget curriculum than checkpoint self-play or fixed-opponent training. Together, these results show that Big 2 is a useful controlled setting for studying deep RL under imperfect information, multiplayer interaction, delayed rewards, and variable action sets.

AI.tldr: PPO-based policy-gradient learning with self-play outperforms value-based methods in Big 2 under common experimental settings; entropy regularization and current-policy self-play improve performance; Big 2 serves as a controlled testbed for imperfect-information, multiplayer RL with delayed rewards.
AI.motivation: Investigate RL under hidden information, sparse/delayed rewards, and non-stationary opponents; enable controlled comparisons between policy-gradient and value-approximation methods in a representative imperfect-information multiplayer game.
AI.method: Introduce a self-play RL framework for Big 2; standardize environment, input representation, budget, and evaluation; compare PPO, Monte Carlo Q, SARSA, Q-learning; assess different self-play curricula (current-policy, checkpoint, fixed opponents); analyze entropy regularization.
AI.result: PPO achieves superior performance against random/greedy/heuristic opponents; moderate entropy improves PPO; current-policy self-play yields a stronger finite-budget curriculum than other strategies.
AI.conclusion: Big 2 is a useful controlled environment for studying deep RL in settings with imperfect information, multiplayer interaction, delayed rewards, and varying action sets.

title: Orthogonal Concept Erasure for Diffusion Models
summary: Concept erasure has emerged as a promising approach to mitigate undesired or unsafe content in diffusion models, yet existing methods still face significant limitations. While training-based methods are effective, their high computational cost limits scalability. Editing-based methods are more efficient and deployment-friendly, yet they struggle to simultaneously achieve precise concept erasure and preserve overall generative capacity. We identify this core limitation of the editing-based methods as reliance on additive parameter updates. Our empirical analysis reveals that concept semantics primarily depend on neuron direction rather than neuron magnitude, while overall generative capacity relies on the angular geometry of neurons. As additive updates inherently entangle direction, magnitude, and angular geometry, they inevitably introduce unintended interference between concept erasure and overall generation performance. To address this, we propose Orthogonal Concept Erasure (OCE), which reformulates editing-based erasure as multiplicative parameter updates from a geometric perspective. Specifically, OCE applies layer-wise orthogonal transformations derived from a closed-form solution to the parameters, enabling precise concept erasure while preserving the neuron magnitude and angular geometry. Furthermore, to address conflicting constraints in multi-concept erasure, OCE introduces a subspace-level objective with structured subspace manipulation, yielding a more effective and scalable erasure. Extensive experiments on single- and multi-concept erasure demonstrate that OCE outperforms existing methods in concept erasure and non-target preservation, erasing up to 100 concepts in 4.3 s. Code: https://github.com/HansSunY/OCE.

AI.tldr: Orthogonal Concept Erasure (OCE) reformulates editing-based concept erasure as layer-wise orthogonal, multiplicative parameter updates to preserve neuron magnitude and angular geometry, enabling precise, scalable erasure of many concepts with minimal interference.
AI.motivation: Address the core limitation of additive updates in editing-based concept erasure, which entangle neuron direction, magnitude, and angular geometry, leading to interference with overall generative capacity; leverage the insight that concept semantics depend on neuron direction while capacity depends on angular geometry.
AI.method: Introduce layer-wise orthogonal transformations derived from a closed-form solution to model parameters, enabling multiplicative updates that preserve magnitude and angular geometry; reformulate erasure as a geometry-preserving operation; add a subspace-level objective with structured subspace manipulation to handle multi-concept erasure.
AI.result: Outperforms existing methods in both single- and multi-concept erasure, preserves non-target generation, and can erase up to 100 concepts in 4.3 seconds; code available at provided repository.
AI.conclusion: OCE delivers a precise, efficient, and scalable concept erasure framework for diffusion models by orthogonally reparametrizing updates, maintaining magnitude and geometry, and using subspace-aware constraints to mitigate interference with generative capacity.

title: BEAMS: Benchmarking and Evaluating AI for Modeling and Simulation
summary: AI tools to support real world decision making must be able to build simulation models that inform their recommendations and render them interpretable. Tools that can automate aspects of modeling practice must complement human expertise, not replace it. The BEAMS Initiative aims to guide the development of AI tools for modeling and simulation toward forms that are responsible and ethical by establishing benchmarks for human centered modeling and simulation practices. The initiative uses open digital and organizational infrastructure to collaboratively evaluate AI tools for modeling and simulation. The open source sd ai project hosted by the initiative establishes transparency and enables contributions to be shared broadly. A steering group focuses on prioritizing potential benchmarks, while a technical group focuses on implementing the benchmarks in the form of automated tests. Tests for several distinct categories of evaluation have been implemented and applied to AI tools that support qualitative model building, quantitative model building, and model discussion. These include tests for causal translation, model iteration, causal reasoning, conformance, model behavior explanation, suggested model building steps, and suggested model fixes. When engines from the sd ai project are coupled with different LLMs, their performance on these evaluations reveals variability across different AI tools. The evaluations implemented by the initiative demonstrate that AI enabled modeling tools perform better at discussion and basic qualitative tasks than with causal reasoning and quantitative error fixing. No single LLM dominates across engine types, highlighting the importance of specific tasks and tradeoffs between speed and accuracy. Ongoing efforts of the initiative aim to incorporate benchmarks that address concerns about bias by considering alternative perspectives and human centered use cases.

AI.tldr: Open benchmarks and tests for AI modeling tools show variability across models; human-centered, transparent, open infrastructure; stronger in discussion/qualitative tasks; need bias safeguards.
AI.motivation: Ensure AI-assisted modeling remains responsible, interpretable, and complementary to human expertise; establish benchmarks to evaluate and compare tools.
AI.method: Create open infrastructure (BEAMS sd ai), steering and technical groups; automated tests for qualitative and quantitative model building and model discussion; evaluate multiple LLMs; tests include causal translation, model iteration, causal reasoning, conformance, explanations, suggested steps and fixes.
AI.result: Evaluation shows AI tools excel at discussion and basic qualitative tasks but struggle with causal reasoning and quantitative error fixing; no single LLM dominates; performance varies by engine type; results inform task-specific tradeoffs and speed/accuracy considerations.
AI.conclusion: Open benchmarks and tests support responsible AI modeling; highlights importance of bias considerations, alternative perspectives, and human-centered use cases; ongoing efforts to incorporate benchmarks addressing bias and beyond.

title: TaxDistill: Improving Metagenomic Taxonomic Annotation via Distilled Genomic Foundation Models
comment: The manuscript contains 14 pages, 7 figures, and 3 tables
summary: Metagenomic taxonomic annotation aims to identify the microbial origins of DNA fragments in environmental samples. Traditional methods that rely on sequence similarity are often constrained by the high microbial diversity and the incompleteness of reference databases, which has motivated the development of learning approaches such as Taxometer that perform post hoc correction to learn more informative metagenomic sequence representations. However, these methods typically rely on labels derived from similarity search tools during training, which inevitably introduces noise that can impair representation learning and degrade classification performance. To address this issue, we propose TaxDistill, a knowledge distillation framework for metagenomic classification. We introduce GenomeOcean, a 500M parameter genomic foundation model, as the teacher network to extract deep semantic features and generate soft labels based on confidence. By distilling this soft label information into a lightweight student network, TaxDistill effectively reduces the label noise introduced by initial retrieval tools. Comprehensive experiments on seven diverse CAMI2 datasets demonstrate that TaxDistill outperforms existing baselines in most scenarios. For instance, on the Gastrointestinal dataset, it improves the F1 score of MMseqs2 from 0.763 to 0.941, outperforming the Taxometer baseline. Overall, TaxDistill provides a reliable method for label correction in complex metagenomic analysis.

AI.tldr: TaxDistill introduces a knowledge-distillation framework for metagenomic taxonomic classification, using GenomeOcean as a teacher to produce soft labels, which are distilled into a lightweight student to reduce label noise from similarity-based annotations, achieving superior performance on CAMI2 datasets (e.g., Gastrointestinal F1: 0.941 vs 0.763).
AI.motivation: Address label noise from retrieval-based labels and incomplete reference databases that hamper representation learning in metagenomics; improve robustness and accuracy of taxonomic predictions.
AI.method: Teacher-student distillation: GenomeOcean (500M params) as teacher to extract semantic features and soft labels; train student network with soft labels to learn robust representations; evaluation on seven CAMI2 datasets.
AI.result: TaxDistill outperforms baselines in most scenarios; notable improvement on Gastrointestinal dataset with F1 increase from 0.763 to 0.941 over MMseqs2 and Taxometer baselines.
AI.conclusion: TaxDistill provides a reliable label-correction approach for complex metagenomic analysis and enhances metagenomic classification under noisy labels.

title: A Progress-Aware Leader-Follower Midair Docking System for Dual-Drone Aerial Manipulation
comment: This paper has been accepted for publication in the Proceedings of the 2026 IEEE 22nd International Conference on Automation Science and Engineering (CASE 2026), August 17-21, 2026, Shenyang, China
summary: Reliable midair docking between small unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) is essential for modular aerial cooperation and manipulation, but it requires precise relative-pose control and repeatable platform under tight thrust and payload constraints. We present a dual-drone docking platform where two quadrotors operate in a leader-follower formation and dock using a lightweight modular frame with passive magnetic latching. A progress-aware mission supervisor manages phase transitions: approach, alignment, capture, and settle. This platform integrates a complete hardware-software stack (ROS 2 with Crazyflie/PX4 interfaces) and synchronized logging for benchmark evaluation. We evaluate the platform in simulation and real-world experiments using quantitative metrics such as formation error, baseline and yaw consistency, docking success rate, time-to-dock, and failure-mode statistics. The platform enables statistically grounded comparison of docking supervision and synchronization strategies and provides a practical testbed for modular aerial cooperation and repeatable midair aerial manipulation.

AI.tldr: Dual-drone docking platform with leader-follower quadrotors and passive magnetic latch; progress-aware supervisor manages approach, alignment, capture, and settle; ROS 2 integration with Crazyflie/PX4; validated in simulation and real experiments; provides a benchmark for docking strategies and modular aerial cooperation.
AI.motivation: Enable reliable midair docking for modular aerial cooperation/manipulation under tight thrust and payload constraints; current methods lack repeatability and quantitative benchmarking.
AI.method: Hardware: two quadrotors with a lightweight modular frame and passive magnetic latch; control in leader-follower mode; mission supervisor orchestrates four phases (approach, alignment, capture, settle); software stack: ROS 2 with Crazyflie/PX4 interfaces; synchronized logging for benchmark evaluation; evaluation in simulation and real-world experiments using metrics.
AI.result: Demonstrates a platform that enables statistically grounded comparison of docking supervision and synchronization strategies; provides a practical testbed for modular aerial cooperation and repeatable midair manipulation; quantitative metrics reported (formation error, baseline/yaw consistency, docking success rate, time-to-dock, failure modes).
AI.conclusion: A complete hardware-software stack for repeatable midair docking and modular aerial cooperation, offering a benchmark/testbed to evaluate docking strategies and synchronization approaches.

title: Adopt $\neq$ Adapt: Longitudinal Analyses of LLM Conversations in the Wild
summary: Although a growing body of research has begun to describe user--LLM interactions, the picture it paints is largely static; little is known about how individual users change their behavior over time. To address this gap, we analyze the conversational trajectories of $\sim$12,000 randomly sampled Microsoft Bing Copilot users and compare these with data from WildChat-4.8M. While the Copilot data contains significant population-level trends, we find that trends in individual user trajectories are much weaker; user habits prove to be overwhelmingly sticky. We also find stark differences between users of different activity levels: more active users have more successful conversations and use the LLM for more complex and professionally oriented tasks. Some user trends also appear in WildChat-4.8M, but we find evidence that this dataset is significantly skewed towards highly proficient "power" users. Ultimately, our results suggest that existing user behavior is difficult to change and demonstrate the extent of user heterogeneity. Our comparison between datasets highlights that WildChat does not represent typical user-AI interactions, an important caveat for downstream uses of the data.

AI.tldr: Individual user trajectories in LLM interactions are highly stable (sticky) and vary with activity level; WildChat data shows some trends but is biased toward power users, limiting generalizability; overall user behavior is heterogeneous and difficult to change; dataset representativeness matters for downstream use.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in longitudinal understanding of user–LLM interactions and assess generalizability across datasets by comparing long-run user trajectories in Bing Copilot with WildChat-4.8M.
AI.method: Longitudinal analysis of conversational trajectories from two large-scale datasets: ~12,000 randomly sampled Bing Copilot users and WildChat-4.8M; examine population-level vs. individual-level trends; compare user activity levels and task complexity; assess dataset representativeness and heterogeneity.
AI.result: Population-level trends exist for Copilot, but individual trajectories show weak change; user habits are highly sticky; more active users achieve more successful conversations and tackle more complex, professional tasks; WildChat trends appear but the dataset is skewed toward high-skill 'power' users; WildChat may not reflect typical user–AI interactions; substantial user heterogeneity persists across datasets.
AI.conclusion: User behavior in human–LLM interactions is resistant to change and highly heterogeneous; careful consideration is required when generalizing findings across datasets, and when using WildChat for downstream tasks due to representativeness issues.

title: Eulerian Gaussian Splatting using Hashed Probability Pyramids
summary: We introduce a probabilistic splat-based radiance field framework that retains the fast rasterization and test-time efficiency of 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) while replacing heuristic primitive manipulation with gradient-based optimization of a volumetric probability density. Rather than relocating, splitting, or culling Gaussians via hand-tuned densification (e.g., ADC), we treat primitive locations as samples drawn from a persistent, learnable density. We instantiate this density using a novel, memory-efficient multi-scale hierarchical grid that enables end-to-end gradient-based optimization. To stabilize the optimization, we derive an unbiased gradient estimator with control variates that markedly reduces variance. By allowing probability mass to flow to where the loss demands, our framework eliminates brittle priors and naturally explores the volume, achieving state-of-the-art reconstruction quality on mip-NeRF 360 while preserving 3DGS-level rendering speed.

AI.tldr: A probabilistic, differentiable radiance-field framework that replaces hand-tuned Gaussian manipulations with gradient-optimized, learnable volumetric density, using a memory-efficient multi-scale grid to achieve state-of-the-art mip-NeRF 360 results with fast rendering.
AI.motivation: To overcome brittle, heuristic Gaussian operations (splitting, culling, densification) in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) and to enable end-to-end optimization of primitive locations by modeling them as samples from a persistent learnable density.
AI.method: Introduce a memory-efficient, multi-scale hierarchical grid to instantiate a persistent volumetric density. Primitive locations are treated as samples drawn from this learnable density. Apply end-to-end gradient-based optimization with an unbiased gradient estimator using control variates to reduce variance. Allow probability mass to flow to regions that minimize loss, enabling natural exploration of the volume.
AI.result: Claims state-of-the-art reconstruction quality on mip-NeRF 360 while preserving the fast rendering speed characteristic of 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS). Demonstrates improved stability and reduced reliance on brittle priors.
AI.conclusion: The approach unifies fast, differentiable radiance field optimization with probabilistic splatting by replacing heuristic Gaussian edits with a learnable density, yielding higher-quality reconstructions at 3DGS-like speeds and with more robust optimization.

title: 3DVLA: Enhancing Vision-Language-Action Models via 3D Spatial and Instance Understanding
summary: Vision-Language-Action models have achieved remarkable progress in robotic manipulation, yet they suffer from a critical limitation: a lack of 3D scene understanding. This deficiency manifests as three intertwined challenges: weak extraction of 3D spatial positions without enforcing multi-view consistency, inadequate 3D instance understanding, and fragile reasoning under occlusion. Although mature 3D perception methods exist, their direct integration into VLA pipelines is hindered by architectural incompatibility and by heavy reliance on costly instance-level annotations. To address the above challenges, we propose 3DVLA, a plug-and-play framework that injects robust 3D reasoning into pretrained VLAs without requiring extra manual labels or discarding VLM priors. Specifically, 3DVLA tackles the three challenges through: (1) pervasive 3D feature encoding with explicit multi-view consistency constraints across all modalities and a Spatially-Conditioned Geometry Aggregation method, (2) an instance estimation module with high-level instance tokens for 3D instance awareness, and (3) a masked self-supervised 3D encoding branch that retains its predictor for visual token completion to handle occlusions. We integrate 3DVLA with multiple VLA baselines and evaluate on LIBERO-Plus and RoboTwin 2.0. Results show consistent and significant gains in manipulation performance, validating both the effectiveness and plug-and-play compatibility of our approach.

AI.tldr: 3DVLA adds plug-and-play robust 3D reasoning to Vision-Language-Action models by introducing pervasive 3D feature encoding with multi-view consistency, 3D instance awareness via high-level instance tokens, and a masked self-supervised 3D encoding branch to handle occlusions, yielding significant gains on LIBERO-Plus and RoboTwin 2.0 without extra labels or discarding VLM priors.
AI.motivation: VLA models lack solid 3D scene understanding, manifested as weak 3D spatial estimation with poor multi-view consistency, weak 3D instance comprehension, and vulnerability to occlusion; existing 3D perception methods are hard to integrate due to architectural mismatches and heavy annotation needs. A plug-and-play 3D reasoning module is needed to improve 3D understanding without extra supervision.
AI.method: 3DVLA introduces: (1) pervasive 3D feature encoding with explicit multi-view consistency constraints across all modalities and a Spatially-Conditioned Geometry Aggregation; (2) an instance estimation module using high-level instance tokens for 3D instance awareness; (3) a masked self-supervised 3D encoding branch that preserves a predictor for visual token completion to handle occlusions. It is designed as a plug-in that preserves VLM priors and works with pretrained VLAs.
AI.result: When integrated with multiple VLA baselines, 3DVLA yields consistent and significant manipulation performance gains on LIBERO-Plus and RoboTwin 2.0, validating effectiveness and plug-and-play compatibility.
AI.conclusion: 3DVLA successfully injects robust 3D reasoning into pretrained VLAs, addressing key 3D perception challenges in manipulation tasks without extra labeling, and demonstrating broad compatibility and performance gains across baselines and benchmarks.

title: When Models Disagree: Rethinking LLM Evaluation for Public Comment Analysis
summary: Federal agencies are deploying large language models (LLMs) to categorize public comment corpora, where the model's organization of the record shapes what policymakers see and which arguments register. Standard evaluation, anchored on stance accuracy against a small validated set, cannot detect when different models produce materially different categorizations of the same public input. We propose an Interpretive Audit Pipeline that treats multi-model disagreement as diagnostic of interpretive complexity and directs human review toward genuinely ambiguous public input. Analyzing 1,260 public comments on a federal USDA docket across four LLMs, we find that inter-model thematic divergence exceeds within-model prompt variation, and that an expert rubric suppresses deep interpretive disagreement without resolving it. In a two-stage labeling study on a stratified 40-comment subsample, four LLMs and a human annotator labeled independently and then revised after seeing the others' labels. Revision behavior varied across labelers, and the human annotator's revisions frequently introduced framings absent from the ensemble's collective output. We argue disagreement-based evaluation is a necessary complement to accuracy metrics for LLM-assisted interpretive coding.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an interpretive audit pipeline for evaluating multi-LLM interpretive coding of public comments, arguing that disagreement among models reveals interpretive complexity beyond accuracy metrics; a two-stage labeling study shows human revisions shape framing not captured by ensemble outputs; thus disagreement-based evaluation complements accuracy for LLM-assisted interpretive coding.
AI.motivation: To address limitations of accuracy-centric evaluation in LLM-assisted interpretive coding of public comments, where how records are organized affects policy insights and important arguments may be overlooked due to model-driven categorizations.
AI.method: 1) Apply four LLMs to 1,260 USDA docket comments; analyze inter-model thematic divergence vs. within-model prompt variation. 2) Conduct a two-stage labeling study on a stratified subsample (40 comments) with four LLMs and a human annotator labeling independently, then revising after seeing others’ labels. 3) Compare human revisions, ensemble outputs, and framings.
AI.result: Inter-model thematic divergence exceeds within-model prompt variation; expert rubrics reduce some deep interpretive disagreement but do not resolve it. Human revisions introduce framings absent from the ensemble’s output. Disagreement-based evaluation reveals interpretive complexity not captured by accuracy alone.
AI.conclusion: Disagreement among models is a diagnostic feature of interpretive complexity and should be used alongside accuracy metrics in evaluating LLM-assisted interpretive coding, with an interpretive audit pipeline guiding human review toward genuinely ambiguous input.

title: ElegantVLA: Learning When to Think for Efficient Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are a powerful paradigm for generalist robotic control. However, their high computational cost and limited control frequency hinder real-time robotic manipulation, especially when large vision-language backbones and iterative action heads run at every control step. Existing VLA acceleration methods often optimize individual components or rely on fixed acceleration rules, treating different control steps with largely fixed computation and overlooking the non-uniform reasoning demands of sequential embodied control. Inspired by human motor control, where cognitive and feedback resources concentrate on goal-sensitive stages, we argue that VLA models should learn when to invest full computation and when to reuse prior computation. We propose ElegantVLA, a plug-in phase-adaptive inference framework that accelerates VLA models through intra-model dynamic compute scheduling. ElegantVLA introduces a lightweight scheduler that observes temporal representation similarity, robot-motion cues, and episode progress to jointly allocate computation across the vision encoder, LLM, and action head. For perception-language reasoning, the scheduler selects a five-level Vision-LLM compute mode, from full recomputation to multi-step temporal reuse, based on visual-language representation stability. For action generation, it selects a three-level denoising mode, reusing intermediate denoising states during stable motion while preserving full refinement for goal-sensitive stages. By coordinating these decisions, ElegantVLA offers a general acceleration framework for modern VLA pipelines with explicit action-generation modules, without modifying or retraining the base model. Experiments on GR00T and CogACT achieve up to 2.55x and 3.77x speedup, and on six real-world GR00T tasks ElegantVLA cuts computation by 2.18x while raising control frequency from 13.8 Hz to 26.3 Hz.

AI.tldr: ElegantVLA is a plug-in, phase-adaptive inference framework that dynamically allocates compute in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models to speed up real-time robotic control without retraining the base model.
AI.motivation: VLA models deliver powerful embodied reasoning but are computationally heavy, limiting control frequency; real-time manipulation requires non-uniform, goal-sensitive computation across time steps. Inspired by human motor control, adaptive compute can focus resources where needed.
AI.method: Introduce a lightweight scheduler that monitors temporal representation similarity, robot-motion cues, and episode progress to allocate compute across the vision encoder, language model, and action head. For perception-language reasoning, use five compute modes (full recomputation to multi-step temporal reuse). For action generation, use three denoising modes (full refinement to reuse of intermediate states). The framework is plug-in and does not retrain or modify the base model.
AI.result: On GR00T and CogACT benchmarks, up to 2.55x and 3.77x speedups were achieved. Across six real-world GR00T tasks, computation was reduced by 2.18x while raising control frequency from 13.8 Hz to 26.3 Hz.
AI.conclusion: ElegantVLA provides a general, plug-in acceleration framework for modern VLA pipelines with explicit action-generation modules, enabling real-time performance gains without altering the base model.

title: Mind Your Tone: Does Tone Alter LLM Performance?
comment: 10 pages, 6 tables, 1 figure. Accepted as a full paper at the Thirty-second Americas Conference on Information Systems (AMCIS 2026), Reno. Follow-up to arXiv:2510.04950
summary: The use of Large Language Models (LLMs) is proliferating, yet their performance is observed to vary based on prompting styles and tones. In this study, we investigate both whether and how tonal variations in prompts lead to disparate LLM accuracy for objective multiple-choice questions. We use two datasets: a 50-base question dataset with five tone variants and a 570-base question MMLU subset spanning 57 subjects with seven tone variants. Experiments were conducted to evaluate the performance of four cost-efficient, popular LLMs: ChatGPT-4o, ChatGPT-5-nano, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite. Across models, tonal effects are systematic but highly model-dependent. Some models show small, yet statistically significant, shifts, while others exhibit large accuracy swings across tones. Further, we identify subject-level differences in tone sensitivity and present a routing framework to explain how tones may attune internal reasoning modes. Our findings caution users against assuming tone-robust reliability in LLM deployments.

AI.tldr: Tone in prompts systematically influences LLM accuracy on objective MCQs in a model-dependent way; some tones cause significant shifts, others are minor; subject-level sensitivity exists; a routing framework is proposed to explain tone-induced changes; caution advised against assuming tone robustness in practice.
AI.motivation: Understand how tonal variations in prompts affect LLM accuracy across models and domains, to assess reliability and guide safe deployment of prompt engineering practices.
AI.method: Two datasets: a 50-question base with five tone variants; and a 570-question MMLU subset across 57 subjects with seven tone variants. Evaluate four cost-efficient LLMs (ChatGPT-4o, ChatGPT-5-nano, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite). Measure accuracy across tones; conduct statistical tests for tone effects; analyze subject-level tonal sensitivity; propose a routing framework to connect tones to internal reasoning modes.
AI.result: Tone effects are systematic but highly model-dependent; some models show small but statistically significant tone-induced shifts, others show large accuracy swings across tone variants. Subject-level differences in tone sensitivity observed. A routing framework is proposed to explain how tones may tune internal reasoning modes. Overall, tone-robust reliability cannot be assumed in LLM deployments across models and domains.
AI.conclusion: Practitioners should be cautious about tonal prompting as a variable affecting performance. More work is needed to uncover mechanisms, validate across more models/datasets, and develop robust prompting guidelines that account for tone sensitivity.

title: Practitioner Beliefs and Behaviors in AI-Enhanced Education: DOT Framework Survey Evidence
summary: This study reports findings from a cross-sectional survey (n = 72) of higher education practitioners examining beliefs, behaviors, and institutional conditions related to artificial intelligence (AI) integration in teaching and learning. Grounded in the DOT Framework, which integrates design thinking and open systems theory, the study investigates AI familiarity, usage patterns, design-oriented practices, and pedagogical beliefs. Exploratory factor analysis of 19 belief items identified a three-factor structure: AI Functional Capabilities, Oversight and Governance, and Instructor Collaboration and Planning (α = .90). Results indicate that practitioners hold favorable views of AI as a pedagogical support while maintaining strong commitments to human oversight and critical evaluation. Reported practices emphasize iterative prompting and content generation, with less consistent use of needs assessment and feedback loops. Institutional barriers including limited policy, training, and infrastructure were widely reported. These findings provide preliminary empirical support for the DOT Framework as a descriptive model of practitioner beliefs and practices, while also highlighting gaps between design-oriented theory and current implementation. The study contributes an initial measurement structure and identifies directions for confirmatory validation and outcome-based research linking AI-supported design practices to instructional quality.

AI.tldr: Cross-sectional survey (n=72) of higher education practitioners finds preliminary support for the DOT Framework as a descriptive model of AI integration in teaching, revealing a three-factor belief structure, favorable views of AI with strong human oversight, practice emphasis on iterative prompting/content generation, and notable barriers; calls for validation and outcome-based research to link AI design practices to instructional quality.
AI.motivation: To understand beliefs, practices, and institutional conditions surrounding AI adoption in higher education and to test the DOT Framework as a descriptive model while developing a measurement structure for AI-supported design practices.
AI.method: Cross-sectional survey of 72 higher education practitioners; grounded in the DOT Framework (Design Thinking + Open Systems theory); exploratory factor analysis of 19 belief items (α = .90); assessment of AI familiarity, usage patterns, design-oriented practices, and pedagogical beliefs.
AI.result: Identified a three-factor belief structure: AI Functional Capabilities, Oversight and Governance, and Instructor Collaboration and Planning; practitioners view AI as a pedagogical aid but maintain human oversight and critical evaluation; practices emphasize iterative prompting and content generation, with inconsistent needs assessment and feedback loops; institutional barriers include limited policy, training, and infrastructure.
AI.conclusion: DOT Framework appears to be a descriptive model of practitioner beliefs and practices; the study provides an initial measurement structure and identifies directions for confirmatory validation and outcome-based research that links AI-supported design practices to instructional quality; highlights gaps between design-oriented theory and actual implementation.

title: Towards the automated segmentation of epicardial and mediastinal fats: A multi-manufacturer approach using intersubject registration and random forest
summary: The amount of fat on the surroundings of the heart is correlated to several health risk factors such as carotid stiffness, coronary artery calcification, atrial fibrillation, atherosclerosis, cancer incidence and others. Furthermore, the cardiac fat varies unrelated to the overall fat of the subject, and, therefore, it reinforces the quantitative analysis of these adipose tissues as being essential. Clinical decision support systems are computer programs capable of evaluating information and providing a corresponding diagnosis or data to complement the physicists' analyses. The aim of this work is to propose a method capable of fully automatically segmenting two types of cardiac adipose tissues that stand apart from each other by the pericardium on CT images obtained by the standard acquisition protocol used for coronary calcium scoring. Much effort was devoted to promote minimal user intervention and ease of reproducibility. The methodology proposed in this work consists of a registration, which will roughly adjust input images to a standard, an extraction of features related to pixels and their surrounding area and a segmentation step based on data mining classification algorithms that define if an incoming pixel is of a certain type. Experimentations showed that the achieved mean accuracy for the epicardial and mediastinal fats was 98.4% with a mean true positive rate of 96.2%. In average, the Dice similarity index was equal to 96.8%.

AI.tldr: An automatic CT-based segmentation method for epicardial and mediastinal adipose tissue using image registration, neighborhood feature extraction, and data-mining pixel classification; achieves high accuracy (98.4%), TPR (96.2%), and Dice (0.968).
AI.motivation: Cardiac adipose tissue quantification is clinically relevant due to associations with vascular and cancer risk factors and its dissociation from overall body fat; automating segmentation supports decision systems with reduced user input.
AI.method: A pipeline consisting of (1) registration to a standard frame, (2) feature extraction from pixels and surrounding neighbourhood, and (3) a data-mining classification step to label pixels as epicardial or mediastinal fat; emphasis on minimal user intervention.
AI.result: Mean accuracy for epicardial and mediastinal fats: 98.4%; mean true positive rate: 96.2%; Dice similarity index: 0.968.
AI.conclusion: The method enables highly accurate automatic segmentation of two cardiac adipose tissue types, supporting precise quantification and potential integration into clinical decision support with reproducible results.

title: Hallucination Mitigation with Agentic AI, Nested Learning, and AI Sustainability via Semantic Caching
summary: Hallucination remains a major reliability barrier for production LLM systems, particularly in multi-agent pipelines where unsupported claims can propagate unchecked across stages. This paper adapts a HOPE-inspired Nested Learning architecture with Continuum Memory Systems (CMS) and semantic similarity caching to a hybrid benchmark of 310 prompts combining 217 epistemic-uncertainty prompts and 93 fabrication-induction stress-test prompts. A three-stage agentic pipeline orchestrated via the Open Floor Protocol (OFP) is evaluated with five KPIs -- FCD (Factual Claim Density), FGR (Factual Grounding References), FDF (Fictional Disclaimer Frequency), ECS (Explicit Contextualization Score), and OSR (Observability Score Ratio) -- aggregated into THS (Total Hallucination Score) across five weighting configurations to study mitigation-observability trade-offs. FDF, ECS, OSR, and FGR are subtracted as mitigation signals, so that a more negative THS indicates stronger mitigation. The FrontEndAgent is configured as a high-stochasticity generator (temperature = 1.0) to produce a realistic hallucination baseline, while the SecondLevelReviewer and ThirdLevelReviewer operate as progressive correctors. This asymmetric design yields end-to-end THS reductions of -31.3% to -35.9% across five weighting configurations. Semantic caching achieves 440 cache hits over 930 potential calls (47.3% hit rate), reducing LLM invocations to 490, lowering energy and CO2e footprint, and making multi-stage review pipelines operationally viable at production scale. ExtremeObservability attains the most negative final THS (-0.0709), confirming that observability-heavy configurations reinforce rather than compromise mitigation. These findings suggest that memory-augmented multi-agent designs can jointly improve factual reliability, operational efficiency, and auditability without model retraining.

AI.tldr: Memory-augmented multi-agent architecture reduces hallucinations in a 310-prompt hybrid benchmark, using CMS and semantic caching; achieves ~-31% to -36% THS reductions, ~47% cache hit rate, and energy savings without retraining.
AI.motivation: Hallucination is a major reliability barrier in production LLM pipelines; memory augmentation, caching, and multi-agent oversight offer a path to improved factual grounding and auditability without model retraining.
AI.method: HOPE-inspired Nested Learning architecture with Continuum Memory Systems (CMS) and semantic similarity caching; a 3-stage agentic pipeline orchestrated via Open Floor Protocol (OFP); evaluation on 310 prompts (217 epistemic-uncertainty and 93 fabrication-induction stress-test); five KPI measures (FCD, FGR, FDF, ECS, OSR) aggregated into THS across five weighting configurations; FrontEndAgent with temperature=1.0; SecondLevelReviewer and ThirdLevelReviewer as progressive correctors; semantic caching yielding 440 cache hits out of 930 calls; energy and CO2e footprint considerations; ExtremeObservability attaining the most negative THS (-0.0709).
AI.result: End-to-end THS reductions of -31.3% to -35.9% across five weighting configurations; 440 cache hits (47.3% of potential calls); LLM invocations reduced to 490; final THS -0.0709 under ExtremeObservability; memory-augmented design reduces hallucination, improves efficiency and auditability without retraining.
AI.conclusion: Memory-augmented multi-agent designs can jointly improve factual reliability, operational efficiency, and auditability without retraining; observability-focused configurations further strengthen mitigation.

title: How to Relieve Distribution Shifts in Semantic Segmentation for Off-Road Environments
comment: 8 pages, 6 figures. Accepted to IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters (RA-L). \c{opyright} 2025 IEEE. Personal use of this material is permitted. Permission from IEEE must be obtained for all other uses
summary: Semantic segmentation is crucial for autonomous navigation in off-road environments, enabling precise classification of surroundings to identify traversable regions. However, distinctive factors inherent to off-road conditions, such as source-target domain discrepancies and sensor corruption from rough terrain, can result in distribution shifts that alter the data differently from the trained conditions. This often leads to inaccurate semantic label predictions and subsequent failures in navigation tasks. To address this, we propose ST-Seg, a novel framework that expands the source distribution through style expansion (SE) and texture regularization (TR). Unlike prior methods that implicitly apply generalization within a fixed source distribution, ST-Seg offers an intuitive approach for distribution shift. Specifically, SE broadens domain coverage by generating diverse realistic styles, augmenting the limited style information of the source domain. TR stabilizes local texture representation affected by style-augmented learning through a deep texture manifold. Experiments across various distribution-shifted target domains demonstrate the effectiveness of ST-Seg, with substantial improvements over existing methods. These results highlight the robustness of ST-Seg, enhancing the real-world applicability of semantic segmentation for off-road navigation.

AI.tldr: ST-Seg proposes a distribution-shift robust semantic segmentation framework for off-road navigation by combining style expansion (SE) and texture regularization (TR) to broaden source-domain styles and stabilize texture representations; demonstrated improvements across shifted target domains.
AI.motivation: Off-road perception faces domain discrepancies and sensor corruption that cause distribution shifts, degrading semantic segmentation and navigation reliability.
AI.method: ST-Seg expands the source distribution via style expansion (generating diverse realistic styles) and stabilizes local textures through texture regularization using a deep texture manifold, providing an explicit approach to handle distribution shifts rather than relying on fixed-source generalization.
AI.result: Experiments show substantial improvements over existing methods across various distribution-shifted target domains, indicating improved robustness.
AI.conclusion: ST-Seg enhances robustness and real-world applicability of semantic segmentation for off-road navigation by explicitly addressing distribution shifts with SE and TR.

title: Bridging the Sim-to-Real Gap in Reinforcement Learning-Based Industrial Dispatching through Execution Semantics
comment: Accepted for publication at the 24th IEEE International Conference on Industrial Informatics (INDIN 2026), held from 26 to 29 July 2026 in Melbourne, Australia
summary:
Event-driven scheduling policies are increasingly deployed in industrial environments, where decisions are made under asynchronous and partially observed system states. As a result, decision states are not temporally consistent, action admissibility is not explicitly defined, and the origin of execution errors remains ambiguous. These issues limit both reliability and interpretability.
  To address this gap, a policy-neutral execution and measurement layer is proposed to mediate between scheduling policies and the industrial execution environment. The layer constructs decision-valid snapshots from asynchronous event streams, defines a standardized execution contract with explicit action admissibility, and records outcomes as divergences between policy intent, transactional outcomes, physical execution, and human intervention. This enables a separation between decision semantics and execution behavior and makes deployment mismatch observable and structurally attributable.
  The proposed framework is evaluated using a discrete-event simulation. The results show analytical benefits across all observation lag regimes, as undifferentiated execution failures are transformed into structured, typed outcomes with full attribution coverage. Operational benefits are strongest under low observation lag, where avoidable execution errors can be prevented before commitment. Overall, the layer turns execution uncertainty into supervisory data for evaluation and policy refinement.

AI.tldr: Proposes a policy-neutral execution/measurement layer that bridges scheduling policies and industrial execution by creating decision-valid snapshots, enforcing an explicit action admissibility contract, and recording divergences between policy intent, outcomes, execution, and human intervention. Evaluated via discrete-event simulation showing structured attribution of failures and stronger benefits at low observation lag.
AI.motivation: Event-driven scheduling under asynchronous and partially observed states leads to inconsistent decision states, undefined action admissibility, and opaque error origins, hindering reliability and interpretability. A layer that decouples decision semantics from execution can improve observability and policy refinement.
AI.method: Introduce a policy-neutral execution and measurement layer that derives decision-valid snapshots from asynchronous event streams, enforces an explicit execution contract with action admissibility, and records divergences among policy intent, transactional outcomes, physical execution, and human intervention. Evaluate via discrete-event simulation to assess attribution coverage and outcome structuring across observation lags.
AI.result: Simulation shows that undifferentiated execution failures are transformed into structured, typed outcomes with full attribution coverage. Benefits are observed across lag regimes, with strongest operational gains at low observation lag where errors can be prevented before commitment.
AI.conclusion: The layer converts execution uncertainty into supervisory data for evaluation and policy refinement, enabling observable, structurally attributable mismatches and supporting more reliable and interpretable deployment of event-driven scheduling policies.

title: Toward Ethical Facial Age Estimation: A Generalized Zero-Shot Benchmark Without Training on Children's Data
summary: Age estimation from facial images typically relies on training data that includes images of minors, a practice that raises serious ethical, legal, and privacy concerns. In this work, we propose a generalized zero-shot benchmark for facial age estimation that explicitly excludes children's data during training while still assessing model performance on younger populations. We revisit six widely used datasets and introduce standardized splits with strict age-group separation: samples aged 18-59 for training, validation, and testing; samples under 18 reserved exclusively for zero-shot evaluation; and samples 60+ as an unseen validation set for model selection under distribution shift. For datasets with identity annotations, subject-exclusive splits prevent identity leakage and better reflect real-world deployment conditions. Evaluating nine state-of-the-art age estimation methods under this protocol reveals that all evaluated methods consistently fail to generalize to unseen age groups, suffering substantial performance degradation -- on average 46.4%, and up to 52.8% -- relative to the supervised baseline. Moreover, models do not simply degrade: they systematically anchor predictions for unseen ages to nearby seen classes, a manifestation of the well-known seen-class bias in generalized zero-shot learning. By formalizing age estimation without children's data as a generalized zero-shot benchmark on existing datasets, this work highlights a critical gap between current modeling practices and real-world ethical constraints. Our benchmark provides a principled basis for evaluating models under restricted data regimes and encourages the development of methods that are robust to distribution shift and aligned with responsible data use.

AI.tldr: Generalized zero-shot benchmark for facial age estimation excluding minors during training; reveals substantial generalization gaps and a seen-class bias to unseen ages; proposes standardized dataset splits and evaluation protocol to align with ethical data use.
AI.motivation: Address ethical/legal/privacy concerns of training on minor faces and assess how models perform under restricted data regimes and distribution shifts common in real-world deployment.
AI.method: Revisit six widely used age-estimation datasets; construct standardized splits with strict age-group separation (18-59 for training/validation/testing; under-18 for zero-shot evaluation; 60+ as unseen validation). Apply identity-exclusive splits where available; evaluate nine state-of-the-art methods under this protocol.
AI.result: All methods fail to generalize to unseen age groups, with performance degradation averaging 46.4% and up to 52.8% relative to the supervised baseline; unseen ages are systematically anchored to nearby seen classes due to seen-class bias in generalized zero-shot learning.
AI.conclusion: Formalizes age estimation without children's data as a generalized zero-shot benchmark, highlighting a gap between ethical data-use constraints and current modeling; provides a principled evaluation framework under distribution shift and encourages development of robust, ethically-aligned methods.

title: The Importance of Out-of-Band Metadata for Safe Autonomous Agents: The Redpanda Agentic Data Plane
comment: 6 pages, 1 figure. Published at SAO '26 (co-located with ACM CAIS '26)
summary:
AI agents are increasingly expected to operate as digital employees: accessing enterprise data, making decisions, and taking actions autonomously. But agents are simultaneously less predictable than humans -- prone to hallucination, misinterpretation, and adversarial manipulation -- and more technically capable: with deep system knowledge and high-throughput interfaces cascading damage at machine speed. This combination makes it unsafe to rely on agents to faithfully interpret or propagate security-critical metadata such as access policies, data classifications, and behavioral constraints.
  We present the Redpanda Agentic Data Plane (ADP), an architecture built around out-of-band metadata channels: infrastructure pathways that carry security context, policy signals, and audit trails deterministically, entirely outside the agent's read and write path and across heterogeneous infrastructure. These channels enforce governance at every stage of the agent lifecycle -- scoping data access on the way in, constraining actions during execution, and capturing tamper-proof transcripts on the way out.
  We demonstrate ADP with a multi-agent portfolio rebalancing system in which autonomous agents monitor markets, make trade decisions, and execute orders across isolated client accounts -- with per-client data scoping, trade approval thresholds, and tamper-proof audit trails all enforced by out-of-band channels the agents can neither see nor bypass.

AI.tldr: Redpanda ADP uses out-of-band metadata channels to govern autonomous AI agents, isolating security context from agents to enforce data scoping, action constraints, and tamper-proof audit trails.
AI.motivation: Autonomous agents are powerful but unsafe: prone to hallucination, misinterpretation, and adversarial manipulation. Reliable, enforceable security metadata propagation is needed beyond the agents' read/write path.
AI.method: Architectural approach: an out-of-band data plane carrying security context, policy signals, and audit trails across heterogeneous infrastructure, entirely outside the agent's read/write path, enforcing governance across data ingress, execution, and egress.
AI.result: Demonstrated with a multi-agent portfolio rebalancing system where per-client data scoping, trade-approval thresholds, and tamper-proof audit trails are enforced by the out-of-band channels that agents cannot see or bypass.
AI.conclusion: Out-of-band governance channels provide deterministic enforcement of data access, action constraints, and auditability across the agent lifecycle, enabling safer deployment of autonomous enterprise agents.

title: FLIP: Real-Time and Resilient Formation Planning for Large-Scale DIstributed Swarms via Point Cloud Registration
summary: Traditional large-scale formation planning either oversimplify the formation representation which leads to poor performance, or they employ complete collaborative relationships, which results in excessive computational load. To achieve high-performance and large-scale formation planning, we transform the Optimal Formation Position Sequence \cite{c1} (OFPS) calculation problem into a spatiotemporal Point Cloud Registration (PCR) problem. Each agent derives its OFPS by distributively computing the matching result between current positions and the desired formation positions of all other agents. Then each agent optimizes the cooperative formation trajectory by using OFPS. We leverage the PCR method with outlier rejection to rapidly perform large-scale formation position registration. This prevents suboptimal trajectories and failed agents from propagating through the cooperative network and affecting more agents. Consequently, we uniformly achieve resilient, efficient, and distributed trajectory planning for large-scale swarms. The effectiveness and the superiority of the proposed method are demonstrated through large-scale simulations of 120-drone formation, and rigorous benchmarking against state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods.

AI.tldr: Transforms OFPS computation into a spatiotemporal PCR framework with outlier rejection to enable scalable, distributed, and resilient trajectory planning for large swarms (e.g., 120 drones), outperforming state-of-the-art methods.
