The advantage persists under both parameter-matched and compute-matched controls, demonstrating a Pareto improvement. We perform careful pretraining ablations and identify a novel phenomenon whereby Muon unlocks the capacity of Parallax. To our knowledge, this is the first empirical demonstration of strong architecture-optimizer codesign for attention mechanisms in the architecture research literature.

AI.tldr: Parallax is a scalable, parameterized Local Linear Attention that overcomes LLA's scaling and numerical stability issues, enabling pretraining of LLMs (0.6B and 1.7B) with consistent perplexity gains and strong architecture-optimizer co-design for attention. It achieves higher arithmetic intensity, outperforms FlashAttention variants in decoding, and exhibits a Pareto-improving trade-off under different resource matches.
AI.motivation: Fill the gap of High-Bias/Variance tradeoffs and numerical instability in Local Linear Attention to scale LLA to large language models, achieving better bias-variance characteristics and efficiency over standard softmax attention.
AI.method: Introduce Parallax, a parameterized Local Linear Attention that removes the numerical solver of LLA and adds a query-like projector to probe KV covariance. Place within a broader family of attention mechanisms defined by bandwidth, probe construction, and affine structure. Develop a hardware-aware algorithm to increase arithmetic intensity (compute-bound regime) and implement a decode kernel competitive with FlashAttention 2/3. Pretrain at 0.6B and 1.7B scales with ablations, and investigate Muon as a capacity enhancer; compare architecture-optimizer co-design under parameter-matched and compute-matched controls.
AI.result: Parallax yields consistent perplexity improvements during pretraining across the tested scales, with gains transferring to downstream benchmarks. Decode kernel matches or surpasses FlashAttention 2/3 across batch sizes and context lengths. The approach demonstrates Pareto-improving performance under both parameter- and compute-matched baselines; Muon further unlocks Parallax capacity in ablations.
AI.conclusion: This work delivers the first empirical evidence of strong architecture-optimizer co-design for attention mechanisms in the architecture research literature, showing that scalable, parameterized Local Linear Attention can outperform existing efficient attention variants and integrate effectively into LLM pretraining.

title: DefSynUS: Real-time Patient-specific Intrahepatic Vessel Identification via Deformation-Aware CT-US Domain Adaptation
summary: Purpose: Laparoscopic ultrasound (LUS) enhances the safety of liver surgery by visualizing intrahepatic vessels in real-time. Still, vessel identification remains difficult due to probe constraints, complex vascular structure, and tissue deformation. This work aims to enable real-time, patient-specific vessel identification that remains robust under deformation through deformable ultrasound augmentation. Methods: Preoperative CT vessel annotations are used to generate synthetic ultrasound data via optimized physics-based rendering, coupled with domain adaptation to intraoperative ultrasound. The rendering is trained end-to-end for vessel identification and patient-specificity, eliminating the need for preoperative ultrasound. A deformation-aware augmentation simulates realistic intraoperative motion and tissue deformation within the rendering pipeline. Results: In abdominal phantom and limited clinical feasibility experiments (single-case clinical evaluation), the framework achieved real-time intrahepatic vessel-branch identification, maintaining performance under new patient poses. Conclusion: The framework enables real-time vessel identification without preoperative ultrasound and supports technical feasibility, but multi-patient validation is still needed for generalizability and clinical feasibility.

AI.tldr: Deformable ultrasound augmentation enables real-time, patient-specific intrahepatic vessel identification using synthetic ultrasound rendered from preoperative CT with domain adaptation to intraoperative ultrasound; demonstrated in phantom and one clinical case; needs multi-patient validation.
AI.motivation: Address safety-critical need to identify hepatic vessels in real-time during surgery under deformation and probe constraints, reducing reliance on preoperative ultrasound.
AI.method: Generate synthetic ultrasound data from preoperative CT vessel annotations via optimized physics-based rendering; train rendering end-to-end for vessel identification and patient-specificity; apply domain adaptation to intraoperative US; incorporate deformation-aware augmentation to simulate realistic motion and tissue deformation; eliminates need for preoperative US.
AI.result: Real-time intrahepatic vessel-branch identification achieved; robust under new patient poses; demonstrated in abdominal phantom and a single-case clinical feasibility evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Framework enables real-time vessel identification without preoperative US and shows technical feasibility; multi-patient validation is needed to assess generalizability and clinical feasibility.

title: Aligned but Fragile: Enhancing LLM Safety Robustness via Zeroth-Order Optimization
summary:
Safety alignment for large language models (LLMs) aims to reduce harmful or unsafe behavior while preserving general utility. However, recent findings reveal that alignment effects can be fragile: lightweight post-alignment manipulations, such as parameter noise, activation noise, or quantization, can easily weaken the intended safety behavior. Prior efforts to improve robustness have primarily focused on data curation, modified alignment objectives, and safety-critical parameter identification, leaving the role of the optimizer itself largely unexplored.
  In this paper, we are the first to study the robustness of safety alignment from the perspective of the base optimizer. This optimizer-centric view naturally points to zeroth-order optimization, which provides a robustness-oriented signal by evaluating safety alignment under perturbations. Based on this insight, we propose a hybrid framework that first performs standard first-order safety alignment and then applies zeroth-order refinement to improve robustness. Both theoretically and empirically, we show that only a few zeroth-order refinement steps can enhance robustness while preserving safety alignment. We further improve the efficiency of zeroth-order refinement by exploiting its inherent perturbation-based evaluations to estimate layer-wise robustness sensitivity, enabling the refinement process to concentrate updates on robustness-critical layers with modest training overhead.

AI.tldr: A novel optimizer-centric approach to safety alignment robustness: combine first-order alignment with a few steps of zeroth-order refinement to harden safety behavior against perturbations, using layer-wise robustness sensitivity to focus updates and improve efficiency.
AI.motivation: Safety alignment can be fragile under small post-alignment perturbations (noise, quantization). The optimizer (base training process) has been underexplored as a factor in robustness. A zeroth-order perspective can reveal robustness signals via perturbation responses.
AI.method: A two-stage framework: (1) perform standard first-order safety alignment; (2) apply zeroth-order refinement using perturbation-based evaluations to improve robustness. Estimation of layer-wise robustness sensitivity from perturbations guides focused updates on robustness-critical layers, reducing overhead. The approach has theoretical and empirical support.
AI.result: Few zeroth-order refinement steps significantly enhance robustness while preserving safety alignment; layer-wise sensitivity estimation improves efficiency by concentrating updates where they matter most, with modest additional training cost.
AI.conclusion: Optimizer-centric robustness via a hybrid first-order plus zeroth-order refinement is effective for strengthening safety alignment against perturbations, suggesting perturbation-aware optimization as a viable path for robustness in LLM safety.

title: PROTOCOL: Late Interaction Retrieval for Protein Homolog Search
summary: Protein homology search underlies function annotation, structure prediction, and evolutionary analysis, but remains challenging in the "twilight zone," where global sequence similarity is weak and classical alignment methods lose sensitivity. Protein language models provide context-aware representations that could improve alignment sensitivity in this regime. However, prior protein embedding-based retrieval pipelines often pool these representations into a single vector, potentially obscuring local motifs, domains, or conserved residues that reveal remote homology. We introduce ProtoCol, a model which represents proteins as sets of residue embeddings and uses ColBERT-style late interaction to test whether residue-level comparison improves homolog retrieval. ProtoCol encodes proteins independently, keeps candidate representations pre-computable, and scores candidates with MaxSim over residue embeddings. On SCOPe superfamily and Pfam clan benchmarks, ProtoCol outperforms sequence-composition, alignment-based, pooled PLM, and trained single-vector baselines, supporting late interaction as an effective retrieval layer for remote homology search.

AI.tldr: ProtoCol uses residue-level embeddings and ColBERT-style late interaction to improve remote homology retrieval, outperforming baselines on SCOPe and Pfam benchmarks while allowing precomputable, independent encodings.
AI.motivation: Improve remote homology search in the twilight zone by preserving local motifs/domains that single-vector embeddings may obscure.
AI.method: Represent proteins as sets of residue embeddings; apply ColBERT-style late interaction (MaxSim over residues) to score candidate homologs; encode proteins independently with precomputable representations; compare residue-level embeddings rather than pooled vectors.
AI.result: ProtoCol outperforms sequence-based, alignment-based, pooled PLM, and single-vector baselines on SCOPe superfamily and Pfam clan benchmarks, validating late interaction for remote homology retrieval.
AI.conclusion: Late interaction over residue embeddings is effective for remote homology search; ProtoCol demonstrates practical gains by enabling residue-level comparisons with precomputable representations.

title: Optimizing Latent Representations for Robust Building Damage Assessment Onboard Earth Observation Satellites
comment: IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition Workshops (CVPRW 2026), Jun 2026, Denver, United States
summary: Rapid identification of damaged buildings after natural disasters or on war areas is crucial to support emergency response and prioritize interventions. Earth Observation constellations provide timely, large-scale coverage, but actionable information is often delayed by data downlink constraints, on-ground processing, and human interpretation. Reducing this latency is essential to improve decision-making responsiveness. In this work, we propose an original AI-based system that enables object-level building damage assessment (localization and damage classification) directly onboard satellites from pre-disaster and post-disaster highresolution optical imagery. Available pre-disaster images are encoded on ground into compact latent representations, transmitted to the satellite, and compared on-board with newly acquired post-event observations. Leveraging AI interpretation capabilities and increasing processing capabilities on-board satellites, the proposed design enables processing directly at the data source, reducing the amount of information to be downlinked while preserving task-relevant content and improving overall system responsivity. We explore the design space through a systematic benchmark of onboard-compatible variants, analyzing the impact of siamese processing, cross-attention, latent-space compression, and robustness-oriented data augmentation. Experiments on xBD dataset demonstrate reliable and robust damage assessment under misalignment, with minimal performance degradation under strong compression.

AI.tldr: An onboard AI system performs object-level building damage assessment directly on satellites by matching pre-disaster latent representations of buildings with post-disaster imagery, reducing data downlink and latency; validated on xBD with robustness to misalignment and compression.
AI.motivation: To accelerate post-disaster response by minimizing data transfer and processing latency, leveraging on-board AI to perform damage localization and classification.
AI.method: Onboard siamese/attention-based processing using latent-space compression. Pre-disaster images are encoded on ground into compact latent representations, transmitted to the satellite, and compared with post-event high-resolution imagery to localize and classify damage.
AI.result: xBD experiments show reliable, robust damage assessment under misalignment; performance degrades minimally under strong compression.
AI.conclusion: Onboard processing at data source is feasible and beneficial, enabling rapid, action-ready damage information while reducing downlink requirements; the approach is robust to common degradation factors and offers a design space for onboard-compatible variants.

title: Architecture-Sensitive Supervised Fine-Tuning for Screen-Conditioned Action Prediction: A PiSAR Benchmark
comment: 14 pages, 7 figures, 2 tables. PiSAR corpus and fine-tuned weights are proprietary to AprioriLabs; methodology and recipe released
summary: We benchmark three supervised fine-tuned models against frontier zero-shot baselines on a 661-row held-out slice of PiSAR (Persona, intent, Screen, Action, Rationale), a 12,929-tuple corpus of screen-anchored behavioural rationales curated from public app-store reviews, Pew American Trends Panel demographics, and the OPeRA shopper traces. Every model, frontier or fine-tuned, is evaluated on the same 661-row slice with the same scoring pipeline. Two findings. First, frontier zero-shot baselines (Claude Opus 4.7 and GPT-5.5) reach sem_sim 0.459 and 0.482 respectively; a fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct reaches 0.783 and clears sem_sim >= 0.7 on 79% of rows, against 1-2% for either frontier baseline, a gap of 0.30 absolute on the same test set. Second, the same training data and recipe on Gemma-4-26B-A4B-IT scores only 0.441, in the same band as the frontier zero-shot baselines rather than the fine-tuned Qwen. We read this as a recipe-vs-model mismatch: the reasoning-tuned high-parameter model resists displacement and would likely need either more data or a stronger fine-tuning method.

AI.tldr: Fine-tuned models outperform frontier zero-shot baselines on PiSAR for reasoning tasks, but not uniformly: a high-parameter, reasoning-tuned model shows strong gains while Gemma-based results align with baselines, indicating a recipe-vs-model mismatch needing more data or stronger fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Assess whether supervision/fine-tuning yields gains over frontier zero-shot baselines on a large corpus of screen-anchored behavioral rationales (PiSAR) and to understand where fine-tuning helps or fails.
AI.method: Compare three supervised fine-tuned models against frontier zero-shot baselines on a fixed 661-row held-out PiSAR slice, using the same scoring pipeline across all models.
AI.result: Frontier baselines (Claude Opus 4.7, GPT-5.5) achieve sem_sim 0.459 and 0.482. A fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct reaches 0.783 and exceeds sem_sim 0.7 on 79% of rows. This yields an absolute gap of ~0.30 over baselines. The same training data with Gemma-4-26B-A4B-IT scores 0.441, comparable to baselines.
AI.conclusion: There is a recipe-vs-model mismatch: a high-parameter, reasoning-tuned model resists displacement, suggesting the need for more data or different fine-tuning strategies to surpass frontier baselines consistently across models.

title: Evolutionary Refinement of Generative Graph Topologies: A Hybrid WGAN-GA Approach
comment: 6 pages, 4 Figures, 4 Tables, IEEE World Congress on Computational Intelligence
summary: Generating realistic graph-structured data is challenging due to discrete connectivity, varying graph sizes, and class-specific structural patterns. Recent Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN)-based graph generation methods improve edge modelling by learning connectivity and matching class-specific density distributions. However these models still exhibit noticeable deviations such as in degree and spectral distribution when compared to real graphs, indicating that important structural properties are not fully preserved. This work aims to reduce these deviations by refining the graphs produced by an existing GAN-based graph generator framework with a Genetic Algorithm (GA). In the GAN framework, the generator produces both node features and connectivity patterns, while a GNN-based critic evaluates graph realism and class consistency to ensure global structural and class alignment. Building on this foundation, we apply a GA to refine the edges of generated graphs. The refinement process guides synthetic graphs toward closer agreement with real data, while preserving diversity and novelty. Experimental results show that the GA refinement consistently lowers combined Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) compared to the base model, leading to graphs that more closely match real structural patterns. This demonstrates that evolutionary refinement is an effective and flexible way to correct residual structural deviations in GAN-based graph generators, improving their suitability for realistic graph synthesis and data augmentation.

AI.tldr: GA-based refinement improves GAN-generated graphs by evolutionary edge optimization, reducing deviations in degree and spectral distributions and enhancing realism for data augmentation.
AI.motivation: GAN-based graph generators struggle to preserve key global and spectral properties of real graphs; residual structural deviations (e.g., degree distribution, spectral characteristics) reduce realism and utility.
AI.method: Extend a GAN-based graph generation framework where the generator creates node features and connectivity and a GNN critic enforces realism and class consistency; apply a Genetic Algorithm to refine generated edges post-generation to align with real data distributions (measured by MMD) while preserving diversity.
AI.result: GA refinement consistently reduces the combined Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) compared with the base GAN model, producing graphs whose structural properties more closely match real graphs.
AI.conclusion: Evolutionary refinement via GA is an effective and flexible approach to correct residual structural deviations in GAN-based graph generators, improving realism and suitability for graph synthesis and data augmentation.

title: Mitigating State Aliasing in Vision-Language-Action Models via Inverse Dynamics Learning
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a promising framework that unifies perception, reasoning, and control for robot manipulation by adapting pretrained vision-language models (VLMs) to action prediction. However, VLM-derived representations are often insensitive to subtle visual distinctions required for low-level control, causing state aliasing between visually similar states that require substantially different actions. Prior VLA studies improve visual understanding by generating visual or reasoning outputs, such as future frames, 2D grounding points or traces, or intermediate spatial reasoning steps, but these objectives typically shape the vision encoder only indirectly through end-to-end prediction and do not explicitly analyze state aliasing in the learned visual feature space. To mitigate state aliasing, we introduce inverse dynamics learning as an auxiliary objective that directly supervises the VLA vision encoder. By predicting the action between current and future observations, our objective encourages the encoder to capture fine-grained visual distinctions that determine low-level actions. We further use pseudo-reversed supervision to expose the encoder to a broader range of action directions and improve generalization under limited robot demonstrations. Our method applies to diverse VLA baselines, uses only standard observation-action pairs without additional annotations, and preserves the original inference pipeline at test time. Experiments on CALVIN ABC-D and SimplerEnv show consistent gains across diverse VLA baselines. Frozen-encoder probing and state-feature alignment analyses further show that our method learns state-discriminative visual representations that reduce state aliasing and better align with robot state changes.

AI.tldr: Proposes inverse dynamics learning as an auxiliary objective for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models to reduce state aliasing and improve low-level action prediction, using pseudo-reversed supervision; yields consistent gains on CALVIN ABC-D and SimplerEnv, with analyses showing more discriminative state representations.
AI.motivation: VLA representations suffer from state aliasing: visually similar states lead to different low-level actions. Existing methods improve perception but do not explicitly enforce fine-grained, state-discriminative encodings. An auxiliary inverse dynamics objective directly ties visual features to actions, encouraging distinctions necessary for control.
AI.method: Integrate inverse dynamics learning as an auxiliary objective by training the VLA vision encoder to predict the action between current and future observations. Employ pseudo-reversed supervision to cover broader action directions. Applicable to diverse VLA baselines, using standard observation-action pairs without extra annotations, preserving the original inference pipeline at test time.
AI.result: Experiments on CALVIN ABC-D and SimplerEnv show consistent performance gains across diverse VLA baselines. Frozen-encoder probing and state-feature alignment analyses indicate learned representations become more state-discriminative, reducing state aliasing and better aligning with robot state changes.
AI.conclusion: Inverse dynamics supervision effectively mitigates state aliasing in VLA models, improving low-level action accuracy while maintaining compatibility with existing pipelines and demonstrating general applicability.

title: When Does Persona Prompting Actually Help? A Retrieval and Metric Analysis of Expert Role Injection in LLMs
comment: 6 pages, 2 figures. Submitted for peer review
summary: Persona prompting is widely used to steer large language models, yet its practical value remains unclear. Prior work often evaluates persona prompting using aggregate scores, making it difficult to determine whether expert-role prompting consistently improves response quality or instead changes responses along different quality dimensions. We study this question through a controlled comparison of four prompting conditions across 1,140 open-ended questions spanning 38 expert roles and six domains: no role prompt, a generic domain-expert prompt, embedding-based role retrieval, and a hybrid retrieval method combining embedding search with LLM-based role selection. Aggregate results show only small overall differences between conditions. However, metric-level analysis reveals a consistent tradeoff that aggregate averages obscure: role prompting systematically increases expertise depth while reducing clarity. These effects are highly conditional rather than universal. Role prompting performs best on advisory questions and in domains such as medicine and psychology, where structured expert framing and risk communication are intrinsically valuable. In contrast, baseline prompting performs better on conceptual and explanatory questions in finance, legal, science, and technology domains, where concise plain-language explanation is more important. We further show that hybrid retrieval significantly improves over embedding-only role selection, although better role retrieval does not eliminate the broader expertise-depth versus clarity tradeoff. Overall, our findings suggest that persona prompting primarily reshapes response characteristics rather than broadly improving capability, and that multi-metric evaluation is necessary for understanding its effects.

AI.tldr: Persona prompting yields depth-versus-clarity tradeoffs; small overall gains; hybrid retrieval helps role selection but does not eliminate tradeoff; multidimensional evaluation is necessary.
AI.motivation: To determine whether expert-role or persona prompts improve response quality or merely shift quality dimensions, across domains and roles.
AI.method: Controlled experiment across four prompting conditions: no role prompt, generic domain-expert prompt, embedding-based role retrieval, and a hybrid retrieval method combining embedding search with LLM-based role selection; 1,140 open-ended questions spanning 38 expert roles and six domains; compare aggregate results and per-metric effects; evaluate role retrieval methods.
AI.result: Aggregate differences are small; but role prompting consistently increases depth of expertise and reduces clarity; effects are highly conditional. Best performance on advisory questions and in medicine/psychology. Baseline prompting performs better on conceptual/explanatory questions in finance/legal/science/technology. Hybrid retrieval improves over embedding-only role selection but does not remove depth-clarity tradeoff.
AI.conclusion: Persona prompting primarily reshapes response characteristics (depth vs clarity) rather than broadly improving capability; multi-metric evaluation is necessary to understand effects; domain- and task-specific design choices are essential.

title: Probabilistic bias adjustment of seasonal forecasts using generative machine learning: A case study of Arctic sea ice predictions
summary: Seasonal climate predictions support planning and risk management by offering early information of the most likely-to-occur climate conditions in the coming months, and associated uncertainties. Ensemble forecasts enable this by simulating many plausible outcomes, allowing predictions to be expressed as usable probabilities. Large ensembles and high-resolution forecasts strengthen this guidance by better sampling uncertainty and capturing finer-scale processes but come with significant computational cost. Moreover, forecast ensembles drift and exhibit systematic biases and spatio-temporal errors that grow with lead time, requiring careful post-processing and calibration. A probabilistic post-processing framework based on conditional Variational Autoencoders (cVAEs) was developed at the Canadian Center for Climate Modeling and Analysis to generate large ensembles of bias adjusted seasonal predictions of Arctic sea ice. The generative model was designed to learn the observational distribution conditioned on the biased model prediction. This enables generation of arbitrarily large ensembles of well-calibrated, bias corrected forecasts with improved skill. Here, we extend this framework to address the loss of fine-scale energy and the characteristic blurriness in predictions, a known limitation of standard cVAEs. Specifically, we employ a generator in place of the Gaussian parametrized decoder in the cVAE and use Continuous Ranked Probability Score in the objective function instead of the Mean Square Error. We further use a higher resolution target dataset compared to the raw forecast. We show that the adjusted forecasts are better calibrated, more consistent with the observational distribution, and exhibit smaller errors than benchmark predictions, while also enhancing the resolution of the raw forecasts and improving sharpness and spectral power relative to the standard cVAE.

AI.tldr: Extends a conditional VAE framework for Arctic sea-ice forecasts by replacing the Gaussian decoder with a generator, using CRPS loss, and higher-resolution targets to reduce blurriness, improve sharpness, and calibration of large ensembles.
AI.motivation: Mitigate ensemble forecast issues (drift, biases, spatio-temporal errors) and address cVAE limitations (loss of fine-scale energy/blurriness) to deliver better-calibrated, higher-resolution forecasts.
AI.method: Incorporates a generator in place of the Gaussian-parametrized decoder within the cVAE; uses Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS) loss instead of MSE; trains on higher-resolution target data; conditions on biased model predictions to generate large, bias-corrected ensembles.
AI.result: Forecasts are better calibrated and more consistent with observations, exhibit smaller errors than benchmarks, and have higher resolution, sharper predictions with enhanced spectral power relative to standard cVAE.
AI.conclusion: The extended framework improves calibration, resolution, and predictive skill for seasonal Arctic sea-ice forecasts, addressing key limitations of standard cVAE approaches.

title: ReactBench: A Cause-Driven Benchmark for Multimodal Hallucination via Systematic Evaluation
summary: While multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved rapid progress in vision-language understanding, they remain prone to multimodal hallucinations, producing responses that are inconsistent with the visual input. Existing benchmarks predominantly focus on detecting hallucination outcomes rather than evaluating the underlying causes of these failures. Moreover, many benchmarks rely on simplistic scenarios and limited evaluation formats that no longer challenge state-of-the-art models. To address these limitations, we introduce ReactBench, a cause-driven hallucination benchmark featuring multiple tasks and an exam-style evaluation format. By generating adversarial images and hallucination-inducing queries, ReactBench introduces four targeted tasks: Relational Erasure, Counterfactual Attribute, Alteration Tracing, and Dense Counting. These tasks systematically expose co-occurrence bias, language priors, cross-image comparative perception deficiencies, and fine-grained perceptual bottlenecks. Beyond standard accuracy-based evaluation, we leverage Chain-of-Thought reasoning to identify fine-grained sub-causes of hallucination within each task. Extensive evaluations reveal that current MLLMs remain notably vulnerable to cause-specific hallucination triggers, demonstrating the value of ReactBench as a systematic and interpretable testbed for diagnosing and improving multimodal model robustness. The project page is available at https://reactbench.github.io/.

AI.tldr: ReactBench is a cause-driven benchmark for multimodal LLMs that reveals cause-specific hallucinations via adversarial tasks (Relational Erasure, Counterfactual Attribute, Alteration Tracing, Dense Counting) and uses chain-of-thought to diagnose sub-causes; demonstrates current models' vulnerability and provides an interpretable testbed.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks detect hallucinations but do not diagnose their underlying causes; many rely on simplistic scenarios and fail to challenge state-of-the-art models. There is a need for a cause-oriented, interpretable, exam-style evaluation that exposes co-occurrence bias, language priors, cross-image perception gaps, and perceptual bottlenecks.
AI.method: Develop four targeted tasks with adversarial images and queries: Relational Erasure, Counterfactual Attribute, Alteration Tracing, and Dense Counting. Use exam-style evaluation and chain-of-thought to identify fine-grained sub-causes of hallucination. Evaluate across multiple multimodal LLMs to diagnose vulnerability to specific triggers.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations show current MLLMs remain vulnerable to cause-specific hallucination triggers. ReactBench serves as a systematic, interpretable testbed for diagnosing and improving multimodal model robustness beyond traditional accuracy metrics.
AI.conclusion: ReactBench provides a cause-focused, interpretable benchmark for diagnosing and improving multimodal model robustness, with the project page available at the provided URL.

title: ReasonLight: A Multimodal Foundation Model-Enhanced Reinforcement Learning Framework for Zero-Shot Traffic Signal Control
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has shown promise in traffic signal control (TSC). However, its reliance on predefined states limits responsiveness to observable open-world events that are absent from training data. IoT-enabled intersections provide heterogeneous observations from roadside sensors and cameras, creating opportunities to improve RL adaptability to such events. To this end, we propose ReasonLight, a multimodal foundation model-enhanced RL framework for zero-shot TSC. ReasonLight integrates three sources of information: structured traffic measurements, multi-view camera observations, and candidate phase decisions from a pre-trained RL controller. Given an RL-proposed phase, ReasonLight extracts visual semantics from multi-view images and aligns them with compact sensor-derived scene descriptions. This alignment enables a semantic-guided refinement module to either preserve or adjust the proposed action according to traffic rules and event semantics. To ensure operational reliability, refined actions are constrained by the set of available phases. Any invalid decision is rejected, and the system falls back to the original RL action. We evaluate ReasonLight on two types of rare events not seen during RL training: emergency vehicle priority and temporary traffic regulation. Experimental results show that ReasonLight achieves zero-shot adaptation without retraining. It reduces emergency vehicle waiting time by up to 88.7% compared with the RL-only backbone while preserving comparable routine traffic performance.

AI.tldr: A multimodal foundation-model–enhanced RL framework (ReasonLight) for zero-shot traffic signal control that fuses structured sensor data and multi-view cameras with an RL proposer, refining actions semantically under traffic rules; demonstrates zero-shot adaptation to emergency-vehicle priority and temporary regulations, with up to 88.7% reduction in emergency-vehicle waiting time and preserved routine throughput.
AI.motivation: RL-based TSC often relies on predefined state representations and lacks adaptability to unseen real-world events. IoT-enabled intersections provide heterogeneous data sources (sensors, cameras) that, if integrated, can improve responsiveness to rare events without retraining.
AI.method: ReasonLight integrates three information sources: structured traffic measurements, multi-view camera observations, and candidate phase decisions from a pre-trained RL controller. For a given RL-proposed phase, it extracts visual semantics from multi-view images and aligns them with compact sensor-derived scene descriptions. A semantic-guided refinement module then preserves or adjusts the proposed action according to traffic rules and event semantics, with refinements constrained to valid phases; invalid decisions are rejected and the system falls back to the original RL action.
AI.result: Evaluated on two rare, unseen events: emergency vehicle priority and temporary traffic regulations. Demonstrates zero-shot adaptation without retraining. Achieves up to 88.7% reduction in emergency-vehicle waiting time compared with the RL-only backbone, while maintaining comparable performance for routine traffic.
AI.conclusion: ReasonLight enables zero-shot adaptation to rare traffic events through multimodal semantic reasoning anchored to safety constraints, offering substantial improvements for emergency responsiveness without retraining. Further work could address broader event coverage, robustness to sensor failures, and real-world deployment considerations.

title: TIMEGATE: Sustainable Time-Boxed Promotion Gates for Continual ML Adaptation Under Resource Constraints
summary: As machine learning(ML) systems evolve to continual adaptation, each re-training cycle uses compute, annotation, and energy. We introduce TIMEGATE, a policy layer managing adaptation by budgeting time, labeling, training, and evaluation. TIMEGATE emits a metric-availability signal M for partial vs. full-evaluation decisions. We validate: (i) labeling outperforms training by 2.3x on Adult tabular; (ii) it transfers to LLaMA-3.1-8B + QLoRA on SST-2 (accuracy 0.80 to 0.96; M =1 in 35/36 runs); (iii) M is informative, 28-cell sensitivity shows M drops to 0.81 at tight thresholds; (iv) 100-cycle simulation achieves 66% evaluation-compute savings with no silent mis-promotions; (v) 10%-slice evaluation on LLaMA uses 89% less wall-clock and energy on a single H200 (ratios agree to 0.2%).

AI.tldr: TIMEGATE introduces a policy layer that budgets adaptation steps (time, labeling, training, evaluation) using a metric-availability signal M to guide partial versus full evaluation. Empirically, labeling outperforms training in several settings, M transfers across models, and significant compute/energy savings are achieved with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Continual adaptation incurs substantial costs (compute, labeling, energy) for retraining. A principled budgeting mechanism and an informative evaluation signal are needed to balance accuracy and resource use.
AI.method: Develop TIMEGATE, a policy layer that budgets adaptation steps and emits a metric-availability signal M to decide partial vs full evaluation. Validate on tasks: Adult (tabular), LLaMA-3.1-8B + QLoRA on SST-2, with 28-cell sensitivity analysis, 100-cycle simulation, and 10%-slice evaluation on LLaMA with H200 hardware.
AI.result: (i) Labeling outperforms training by 2.3x on Adult. (ii) M transfers to LLaMA-3.1-8B + QLoRA on SST-2, improving accuracy from 0.80 to 0.96; M = 1 in 35/36 runs. (iii) M is informative; 28-cell sensitivity shows M drops to 0.81 at tight thresholds. (iv) 100-cycle simulation achieves 66% evaluation-compute savings with no silent mis-promotions. (v) 10%-slice evaluation on LLaMA yields 89% less wall-clock and energy on a single H200; ratios corroborate around 0.2.
AI.conclusion: TIMEGATE provides an effective budgeting mechanism and an informative M signal enabling efficient continual adaptation. Labeling can outperform retraining under budget constraints, and cross-task/model transfer of M supports broader applicability with substantial compute and energy savings.

title: BitC-3DGS: High-Capacity 3D Gaussian Splatting Watermarking via Bit Compression
summary: High-capacity watermarking is necessary for 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) assets to embed rich information (e.g., ownership, provenance, and authentication codes), enabling reliable identification and integrity verification in large-scale 3D asset pipelines. Existing bit-to-token watermarking methods based on a pre-trained text encoder are limited to 77-bit messages due to CLIP's fixed 77-token context length, as tokens beyond this limit are unsupported by learned positional embeddings. To address this limitation, we introduce BitC-3DGS, a bit-compression framework that encodes multiple message bits per token. It employs a bit-compressed tokenization scheme that encodes multiple bits within the same chunk into a single semantic token. To enable recovery of the compressed information, it further introduces a dual-branch architecture for joint chunk decompression and bit decoding, along with a hard-message sampling strategy to improve combinatorial coverage during decoder training. Extensive experiments on the Blender and LLFF datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of BitC-3DGS for high-capacity watermarking, achieving high message recovery accuracy and rendering fidelity. For example, it supports 128-bit message capacity with recovery accuracy comparable to that of 64-bit messages in recent state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: BitC-3DGS provides a bit-compression framework for 3DGS watermarking, surpassing the 77-bit limit of CLIP-based methods by encoding multiple bits per token, with a dual-branch decompression/decoding architecture and hard-message sampling. It achieves 128-bit capacity with high recovery fidelity on Blender/LLFF while preserving rendering quality.
AI.motivation: Necessity for high-capacity watermarking in large-scale 3D asset pipelines to embed ownership, provenance, and authentication codes without compromising rendering fidelity. Existing methods are limited by CLIP’s fixed 77-token context, hindering payload size.
AI.method: Introduce a bit-compressed tokenization that packs multiple bits into the same semantic token; employ a dual-branch architecture for joint chunk decompression and bit decoding; use hard-message sampling to improve decoder training coverage across bit combinations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on Blender and LLFF show effective high-capacity watermarking with strong message recovery and rendering fidelity. Demonstrates 128-bit capacity with recovery accuracy comparable to 64-bit messages in recent SOTA methods.
AI.conclusion: BitC-3DGS successfully extends watermark capacity beyond the fixed token-context limit, enabling scalable high-capacity watermarking for 3DGS assets while maintaining fidelity; further validation on robustness and generalization would strengthen practical deployment.

title: Towards Human-Like Interactive Speech Recognition With Agentic Correction and Semantic Evaluation
summary: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) is a core component of human--computer interaction and an increasingly important front-end for LLM-based assistants and agents. However, most current ASR systems still follow a single-pass paradigm, which is poorly aligned with human communication, where misunderstandings are resolved through iterative clarification and refinement. This mismatch makes it difficult to correct meaning-critical errors once they occur. Meanwhile, token-level metrics such as WER or CER cannot adequately reflect such a problem. To address these limitations, we formulate \emph{Interactive ASR} as a multi-turn refinement task and propose \textbf{Agentic ASR}, a closed-loop framework that combines a single-pass ASR front-end with semantic correction, intent routing, and reasoning-based editing. We further introduce the \textbf{Sentence-level Semantic Error Rate} ($S^2ER$), an LLM-based semantic evaluation metric, together with an \textbf{Interactive Simulation System} for scalable and reproducible benchmarking. Experiments on multilingual, named-entity-intensive, and code-switching benchmarks show that iterative interaction consistently reduces semantic errors, with much larger gains in $S^2ER$ than in conventional token-level metrics. Human--AI alignment and ablation studies further validate the reliability of the semantic judge and the robustness of the proposed framework. The code is available at: https://interactiveasr.github.io/ and the live demo is available at https://i-asr.sjtuxlance.com/

AI.tldr: Agentic ASR: a closed-loop, multi-turn refinement framework that augments a single-pass ASR with semantic correction, intent routing, and reasoning-based editing. It introduces the Sentence-level Semantic Error Rate (S^2ER) and an Interactive Simulation System for scalable benchmarking, showing iterative interaction improves semantic accuracy beyond token-level metrics on multilingual, named-entity-heavy, and code-switching data.
AI.motivation: Current ASR largely uses a single-pass paradigm and optimizes token-level metrics (WER/CER), which poorly reflect semantic correctness and user misunderstandings. There is a need for iterative clarification and a semantic-level evaluation framework to align ASR outputs with user intent and meaning.
AI.method: Propose Agentic ASR, a closed-loop pipeline that combines a one-pass ASR front-end with semantic correction, intent routing, and reasoning-based editing. Introduce S^2ER, a sentence-level semantic error rate metric based on an LLM semantic judge, and an Interactive Simulation System for scalable benchmarking. Evaluate on multilingual, named-entity-heavy, and code-switching benchmarks with repeated interactions.
AI.result: Iterative interaction consistently reduces semantic errors, yielding larger gains in S^2ER than in token-level metrics (WER/CER). Ablation and Human–AI alignment studies validate the semantic judge and framework robustness. Code and live demo provided.
AI.conclusion: Interactive ASR with semantic-level evaluation and simulation can substantially improve semantic fidelity and user alignment in complex ASR tasks, suggesting the approach generalizes to diverse languages and domains and enabling more reliable human–AI communication.

title: Influence-Guided Symbolic Regression: Scientific Discovery via LLM-Driven Equation Search with Granular Feedback
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a promising avenue for scientific discovery, yet their application to symbolic regression is often constrained by inefficient search strategies and coarse feedback signals. Current methods typically guide LLMs using scalar metrics (e.g., global Mean Squared Error), which fail to identify which components of a proposed equation are driving performance or causing error. We introduce \textit{Influence-Guided Symbolic Regression} (IGSR), a method that frames equation discovery as an iterative two-step process combining diverse term generation with rigorous selection: an LLM generates candidate basis functions $ψ_j(\mathbf{x})$ for a linear model, which are then evaluated using granular influence scores $Δ_j$. These scores quantify each term's marginal contribution to generalization accuracy, enabling an influence-guided pruning process that systematically refines the model structure. Integrating this mechanism into a Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) enables navigating the combinatorial search space while balancing exploration of novel functional forms with exploitation of high-influence components. We demonstrate IGSR's effectiveness on a diverse suite of benchmarks, including LLM-SRBench, pharmacological PKPD models, an epidemiological simulation, and real-world genomic data. Notably, we validate the framework's capacity for genuine discovery in a case study using a high-dimensional biological dataset, in which IGSR identified a novel relationship between DNA methylation and RNA Polymerase II pausing; a hypothesis that was subsequently supported via wet-lab experimentation.

AI.tldr: IGSR introduces influence-guided symbolic regression: an LLM proposes basis functions for a linear model, which are scored by granular influence metrics Δ_j that reflect each term’s contribution to generalization. An MCTS-based search uses these scores to explore and prune the function space, enabling discovery across diverse benchmarks and a wet-lab–supported case study.
AI.motivation: To overcome the reliance on scalar global metrics in LLM-guided symbolic regression by providing granular, term-level feedback that reveals which components drive performance or error, enabling principled pruning and more reliable discovery.
AI.method: Iterative two-step framework: (1) LLM generates candidate basis functions ψ_j(x) for a linear model; (2) evaluate each ψ_j with granular influence scores Δ_j that quantify marginal generalization contribution. Use influence-guided pruning to refine the model; embed this in a Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to navigate the combinatorial search space, balancing exploration of new forms with exploitation of high-influence components.
AI.result: Demonstrated across diverse benchmarks: LLM-SRBench, pharmacological PKPD models, an epidemiological simulation, and real-world genomic data. A high-dimensional biological dataset case study revealed a novel relationship between DNA methylation and RNA Polymerase II pausing, later supported by wet-lab experiments.
AI.conclusion: IGSR provides a scalable, interpretable framework to discover governing equations with granular, term-level feedback and principled search, delivering genuine scientific discovery in challenging domains.

title: Brain-IT-VQA: From Brain Signals to Answers
summary: Decoding visual content from fMRI signals recorded while a person views images, and specifically answering questions about the seen images, is a long-standing challenge. While significant progress has been made in recent years in visual question answering (VQA) from fMRI, performance remains limited. Moreover, although recent models can make increasingly accurate predictions, they have rarely been used as tools for understanding the structure of visual representations in the brain. We present Brain-IT-VQA, a framework for visual question answering from fMRI. Building on the Brain Interaction Transformer (Brain-IT), our method decodes language tokens from brain activity and integrates them with a language model to answer visual questions. Our model substantially outperforms previous fMRI-based captioning and VQA approaches. We further introduce NSD-VQA, a new dataset and benchmark for visual question answering from fMRI. Unlike existing image-fMRI VQA datasets, which typically provide only a few broad and weakly controlled questions per image, NSD-VQA provides on average 20 question-answer pairs per image across 20 controlled question categories that disentangle multiple levels of visual understanding. This enables more reliable and interpretable evaluation despite limited fMRI test data. Together, Brain-IT-VQA and NSD-VQA provide both a strong predictive framework and a tool for studying brain representations. Using this benchmark, we quantify which forms of visual and semantic information can be reliably decoded from fMRI responses to natural images. We further analyze the contributions of different brain regions across question types.

AI.tldr: Brain-IT-VQA decodes language from fMRI to answer visual questions; NSD-VQA provides a dense, category-structured fMRI-VQA benchmark; together they improve decoding performance and illuminate brain representations.
AI.motivation: Address limited decoding accuracy and interpretability in fMRI-based VQA; provide a robust benchmark to study how visual and semantic information are represented in the brain.
AI.method: Extend Brain Interaction Transformer to map fMRI to language tokens and integrate with a language model for VQA; create NSD-VQA dataset with ~20 Q-A pairs per image across 20 categories; analyze contributions of brain regions across questions.
AI.result: Significant improvement over prior fMRI captioning/VQA methods; clearer evaluation via NSD-VQA; quantified decodable information and region contributions.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a powerful tool for both high-performance VQA from brain signals and neuroscientific insight into visual-semantic representations.

title: CrystalXRD-Bench: Benchmarking Vision-Language Models for XRD Peak Indexing Across Diverse Crystalline Materials
summary: Miller-index identification from powder XRD patterns requires capabilities untested by existing multimodal benchmarks: the model must read a narrow peak location from a rendered scientific curve and then connect that observation to multi-step crystallographic reasoning. We introduce CrystalXRD-Bench, a 250-sample benchmark built from 10 public crystallographic databases for a single task: recover the full set of HKLs contributing to the highest-intensity peak in an XRD pattern. Each sample pairs the rendered XRD image with the source CIF text and chemical formula, so visual extraction errors and reasoning errors can be examined side by side. We evaluate seven vision-language models. The best Jaccard score is 0.5888 (GPT-5.4) with an exact-match rate of 37.6%, yet six of seven models remain below Jaccard 0.50; the task is far from solved. Error patterns vary systematically: double-peak cases are especially brittle, recall-heavy models gain coverage by over-predicting HKLs, and access to CIF text does not close the gap in crystallographic calculation. Alongside model rankings, the benchmark identifies the conditions under which current VLMs fail on quantitative scientific figures. All data and evaluation code will be publicly available.

AI.tldr: A 250-sample CrystalXRD-Bench benchmark for HKL identification from the highest-intensity XRD peak, linking rendered XRD images to CIF text, evaluated across seven vision-language models with limited success.
AI.motivation: Fill a gap in multimodal benchmarks for crystallography by requiring readers to connect a narrow peak location in a rendered XRD curve to multi-step crystallographic reasoning and HKL identification.
AI.method: Construct a dataset from 10 public crystallographic databases. Each sample provides a rendered XRD image, the source CIF text, and chemical formula. Task: recover the full set of HKLs contributing to the highest-intensity peak. Evaluate seven vision-language models on visual extraction plus crystallographic reasoning; analyze error patterns and the impact of CIF access.
AI.result: Best Jaccard score 0.5888 (GPT-5.4) with 37.6% exact-match. Six of seven models have Jaccard < 0.50. Double-peak cases are brittle; recall-heavy models over-predict HKLs; CIF text access does not bridge the gap in crystallographic calculation.
AI.conclusion: The task remains far from solved; current VLMs struggle with quantitative scientific figures and multi-step crystallographic reasoning. The benchmark reveals systematic failure modes and provides data/code for future research.

title: When RL Suppresses Its Own Vocabulary: Recovering Reasoning Diversity in Puzzle-to-Math Transfer
summary: Reinforcement learning using verifiable rewards (RLVR) improves LLM reasoning, but the conditions under which it transfers across domains -- and why it does so -- remain under-explored. We study cross-domain transfer in a 7B model whose SFT and RL post-training stages use only constraint-satisfaction puzzles, with no mathematics problems in the post-training data. To analyze how transfer emerges, we introduce a reasoning primitive-level framework that combines a 9-class span classifier with motif extraction, allowing us to segment chain-of-thought traces into primitive motifs and track their evolution across training stages and domains. We find that puzzle SFT induces a reasoning-primitive vocabulary, yielding a $+7$pp \texttt{pass@32} gain on OlymMATH-Hard. Vanilla GSPO then composes these primitives into longer compute-verify chains, adding a further $+6$pp. However, this RL stage also suppresses exploratory primitives such as \textit{hypothesize} and \textit{backtrack}. To address this, we introduce a novelty bonus that rewards diverse correct rollouts, using perplexity under the reference model as a signal. This restores recovery primitives during RL and adds a further $+7$pp \texttt{pass@32} relative to vanilla GSPO. Finally, the end-to-end recipe raises the hard-math capability ceiling from $16.0\%$ at the OLMo3-7B-Instruct-SFT base to $36.0\%$, without adding any mathematics problems during the SFT or RL stages.

AI.tldr: Cross-domain transfer in RL-based reasoning for LLMs is analyzed by training a 7B model on constraint-satisfaction puzzles, introducing a primitive-level reasoning framework to track motifs in chain-of-thought. Puzzle SFT seeds a primitive vocabulary; GSPO composes them; RL with a novelty bonus maintains exploratory primitives. End-to-end training boosts hard-math accuracy from 16% to 36% without math data in SFT/RL.
AI.motivation: To understand when and why reinforcement-learning-with-verifiable-rewards (RLVR) transfers across domains in LLM reasoning, especially when post-training data exclude math problems, and to identify mechanisms enabling cross-domain generalization.
AI.method: Train a 7B model with supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and RL post-training using only constraint-satisfaction puzzles. Develop a reasoning-primitive framework: a 9-class span classifier plus motif extraction to segment chain-of-thought into primitive motifs. Track motif evolution across training stages and domains. Evaluate transfer on OlymMATH-Hard. Introduce a novelty bonus based on perplexity under a reference model to reward diverse correct rollouts during RL (GSPO).
AI.result: Puzzle SFT yields +7 percentage points (pp) pass@32 on OlymMATH-Hard. Vanilla GSPO adds +6pp by composing primitives into longer compute-verify chains. RL stage suppresses exploratory primitives like hypothesize and backtrack. Novelty bonus restores recovery primitives and adds +7pp relative to vanilla GSPO. End-to-end approach raises hard-math capability ceiling from 16.0% (OLMo3-7B-Instruct-SFT base) to 36.0%, without adding mathematics problems during SFT or RL.
AI.conclusion: A primitive-level analysis reveals how cross-domain transfer arises: puzzle-grounded SFT builds a primitive vocabulary; RL can both advance and suppress certain primitives; introducing a novelty signal maintains exploratory and recovery motifs, yielding substantial gains in hard-math performance without math data in post-training. The approach offers a concrete mechanism for promoting transfer through structured reasoning primitives and novel signals during RL.

title: Non-Forgetting Knowledge Allocation with Bi-level Competition for Class-Incremental Learning
summary: Class-Incremental Learning (CIL) with pre-trained models (PTMs) aims to sequentially adapt PTMs to new categories without forgetting old knowledge. Built upon PTMs, existing adapter-based methods mainly train models via distinct task-specific adapters, and present a uniform knowledge allocation for each adapter during inference. However, this allocation mechanism ignores the nature of task discrepancy and leads to suboptimal utilization of adapters. Also, under CIL constraint, an allocator is prone to forgetting when tasks evolve. To address these issues, we propose a Non-Forgetting Allocation with Bi-Level Competition (NoFA-BC). NoFA-BC constructs a non-forgetting allocator (NFA) by transforming the allocator training into a recursive least-squares problem and achieves an allocator equivalent to that trained with all data. Based on the NFA, a Bi-Level Competition (BLC) including an intra-task level Winner-Takes-All (WTA) mechanism and inter-task Last-Ones-Fall (LOF) elimination is proposed to provide better allocation of adapter knowledge. WTA extracts the most significant logit within a task to represent the adapter's contribution and LOF suppresses the irrelevant adapters. With BLC, participation ratio of each adapter can be tailored for each input. Moreover, a Stability Enhancement (SE) process is incorporated to further improve the performance of old tasks.

AI.tldr: NoFA-BC for class-incremental learning with pre-trained models: a non-forgetting allocator via recursive least squares and a bi-level competition for adapter allocation (WTA intra-task, LOF inter-task), plus stability enhancement to tailor adapter participation per input.
AI.motivation: In CIL with PTMs, uniform adapter allocation ignores task heterogeneity and adapters' forgetting risk; need a mechanism that preserves old knowledge while adapting to new tasks.
AI.method: Develop NFA by formulating allocator training as a recursive least-squares problem to achieve an allocator equivalent to using all data; introduce Bi-Level Competition (BLC) with intra-task Winner-Takes-All to identify the main contributor per task and inter-task Last-Ones-Fall to suppress irrelevant adapters; allow per-input adapter participation ratios; add Stability Enhancement (SE) to boost old-task performance.
AI.result: The approach aims to improve adapter utilization and reduce forgetting, yielding task-aware allocation and improved stability; empirical details are not provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: NoFA-BC provides a principled, dynamic allocation scheme for PTM-based CIL that balances plasticity and stability via non-forgetting allocation and bi-level competition, with SE to further protect old tasks.

title: VitalAgent: A Tool-Augmented Agent for Reactive and Proactive Physiological Monitoring over Wearable Health Data
summary: Wearable devices enable continuous monitoring of physiological signals such as ECG and PPG, but existing mHealth systems are largely limited to task-specific prediction pipelines or reactive question answering over static summaries. They lack the ability to support temporal reasoning, persistent physiological context, and proactive monitoring over long-term signal streams. We propose VitalAgent, a tool-augmented agentic framework for ECG/PPG-based mHealth that supports both reactive question answering and proactive monitoring. VitalAgent is built on a longitudinal physiological memory and a tool-augmented reasoning interface that enables dynamic computation over raw signals. We further introduce VitalBench, a longitudinal physiological monitoring benchmark dataset comprising 1,862 QA pairs for reactive question answering and 90.2 hours of continuous ECG/PPG recordings for proactive monitoring, covering cardiac, physical activity, and stress-related tasks. Experiments demonstrate that VitalAgent achieves over 30% improvement over prompt-based and ReAct baselines in reactive evaluation and supports proactive alert monitoring over long-term physiological signals, highlighting the importance of dynamic tool use and long-term physiological monitoring.

AI.tldr: VitalAgent introduces a tool-augmented agent framework for ECG/PPG-based mHealth offering reactive QA and proactive long-term monitoring, supported by the VitalBench benchmark; it outperforms prompt-based and ReAct baselines by over 30% in reactive tasks and enables alert monitoring across extended physiological streams.
AI.motivation: Current mHealth systems are largely task-specific, lack temporal reasoning and persistent physiological context, and cannot effectively perform proactive monitoring over long-term signal streams.
AI.method: Develop VitalAgent with a longitudinal physiological memory and a tool-augmented reasoning interface enabling dynamic computation over raw ECG/PPG signals; accompany with VitalBench containing 1,862 QA pairs (reactive) and 90.2 hours of ECG/PPG for proactive monitoring; evaluate reactive QA and proactive monitoring performance.
AI.result: VitalAgent achieves >30% improvement over prompt-based and ReAct baselines in reactive QA; demonstrates capability for proactive alert monitoring over long-term signals, highlighting effective tool use and long-term context integration.
AI.conclusion: A unified framework enabling reactive and proactive mHealth analysis via memory-based reasoning and tool augmentation; VitalBench provides a comprehensive benchmark for longitudinal physiological monitoring and QA tasks.

title: Stochastic Lifting for Generating Trajectories of Stochastic Physical Systems
summary: Many stochastic physical systems evolve smoothly over time in the sense that the distribution of states changes regularly across time steps. The transition from current state to the next state can often be modeled as the combination of a smooth map and an explicit source of randomness. Stochastic Lifting exploits this structure by attaching an independent, high-dimensional random label to each state transition in the training data and fitting a transition map from the current state and label to the next state using a standard regression loss. The labels act as auxiliary coordinates that let the model represent multiple plausible next states from similar current states, avoiding collapse to a mean prediction in the finite-sample size regime. At inference, fresh labels are sampled at each time step and the learned map is rolled forward autoregressively, generating diverse trajectories with a single network evaluation per time step.

AI.tldr: Stochastic Lifting uses high-dimensional random labels attached to state transitions to learn a regression-based transition map, enabling multi-modal next-state predictions and autoregressive generation of diverse trajectories with a single network evaluation per step.
AI.motivation: Capture multi-modal, smoothly evolving dynamics without collapsing to a mean prediction in finite data. Leverage stochasticity as auxiliary coordinates to represent multiple plausible futures.
AI.method: Assign an independent, high-dimensional random label to each observed state transition during training and fit a regression model that maps (current state, label) to the next state. At inference, sample new labels at each step and roll out trajectories autoregressively with a single forward pass per time step.
AI.result: The approach enables representing multiple plausible next states for similar current states and generates diverse trajectories without needing multiple network evaluations or complex multi-branch architectures; it avoids mean collapse in finite-sample regimes.
AI.conclusion: Stochastic Lifting is a simple, effective method to model multi-modal sequential dynamics by treating randomness as auxiliary coordinates, yielding diverse future trajectories with minimal inference cost.

title: CogniVerse: Revolutionizing Multi-Modal Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Cognitive Reflection and Geometric Reasoning
summary: Multi-modal Retrieval-Augmented Generation (MMRAG) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for enhancing Multimodal Large Language Models in knowledge-intensive question answering by integrating external visual, textual, and structural knowledge. However, existing MMRAG frameworks suffer from critical limitations, including noisy and irrelevant retrieval, cross-modal semantic misalignment, lack of adaptive reasoning, and incoherent generation across local and global contexts. We introduce \textbf{CogniVerse}, a novel MMRAG framework that addresses these challenges through a cognitive-inspired, mathematically rigorous approach. Drawing from human-like reasoning, CogniVerse integrates three synergistic components: (1) a Cognitive Reflection Module that dynamically assesses retrieval necessity and filters relevant multi-modal content, reducing noise and computational overhead; (2) a Multi-modal Retrieval Module that aligns embeddings in a Riemannian manifold using information geometry and refines knowledge graphs via spectral graph theory, ensuring precise and coherent retrieval; and (3) a Hierarchical Generation Module that employs an optimal transport-based loss to balance token-level accuracy and global semantic coherence. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CogniVerse significantly outperforms state-of-the-art systems in both accuracy and coherence, while reducing retrieval latency.

AI.tldr: CogniVerse is a cognitive-inspired MMRAG framework with three modules (cognitive reflection, multi-modal retrieval on a Riemannian manifold with spectral graph refinement, and hierarchical generation with an optimal-transport loss) that achieves higher accuracy and coherence with lower retrieval latency than state-of-the-art models.
AI.motivation: Current MMRAG systems suffer from noisy/irrelevant retrieval, cross-modal semantic misalignment, lack of adaptive reasoning, and incoherence across local/global contexts. A cognitively-inspired, mathematically rigorous framework is proposed to address these issues.
AI.method: 1) Cognitive Reflection Module dynamically assesses retrieval necessity and filters relevant multi-modal content; 2) Multi-modal Retrieval Module aligns embeddings on a Riemannian manifold using information geometry and refines knowledge graphs via spectral graph theory; 3) Hierarchical Generation Module uses an optimal transport-based loss to balance token-level accuracy and global semantic coherence.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show CogniVerse outperforms state-of-the-art in accuracy and coherence while reducing retrieval latency.
AI.conclusion: CogniVerse offers a cognitively motivated, rigorous approach to MMRAG that addresses core challenges and delivers improved performance and efficiency.

title: The Curse of Helpfulness: Inverse Scaling Law in Robustness to Distractor Instructions via DistractionIF
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in agentic and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems, where they must execute user-specified tasks over externally provided reference text. In practice, such context is often unstructured and contaminated with benign but instruction-like semantic noise, such as editorial comments and system traces, which should be treated strictly as data. We introduce DistractionIF, a benchmark designed to evaluate robustness against such distractor instructions in reference text. Across a broad range of models, we observe a consistent inverse scaling phenomenon: larger models are often less robust, with performance dropping by up to 30 points as scale increases. Mechanistically, our perplexity analysis reveals that scaling erodes the probabilistic boundary between robust and distracted behaviors, making models increasingly prone to over-interpreting noise as instructions. To address this, we demonstrate that reinforcement learning, specifically Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), can restore this boundary, improving robustness by up to 15.5% without compromising general instruction-following capability. Our findings highlight a critical instruction-following robustness gap in reference-grounded tasks and establish reinforcement learning as a promising path for enforcing strict data-instruction separation at scale.

AI.tldr: DistractionIF benchmarks robustness of LLMs against distractor instructions in reference text for RAG/agentic tasks. Finds inverse scaling: larger models are less robust, with up to ~30-point drops. Perplexity analysis suggests larger models blur the line between robust and distracted behaviors. GRPO reinforcement learning can restore the boundary, boosting robustness by ~15.5% without hurting general instruction-following.
AI.motivation: To address contamination of reference context with benign but instruction-like noise (editorial comments, system traces) in retrieval-grounded generation, and to quantify and mitigate its impact on instruction-following when models scale.
AI.method: Introduce DistractionIF benchmark; evaluate a broad spectrum of models on tasks requiring following user-specified instructions over noisy reference text; analyze model perplexity to study robustness boundaries; apply Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) reinforcement learning to enforce data-instruction separation and measure robustness gains while retaining general instruction-following performance.
AI.result: Across models, robustness degrades with scale: larger models experience up to ~30-point performance drops under distractor conditions. Perplexity reveals scaling erodes the probabilistic boundary between robust and distracted responses. GRPO-based RL recovers the boundary, yielding up to ~15.5% improvement in robustness without compromising standard instruction-following.
AI.conclusion: There exists a critical robustness gap in reference-grounded tasks as models scale. Reinforcement learning, particularly GRPO, is a promising approach to enforce strict data-instruction separation at scale while maintaining broad instruction-following capabilities.

title: Auditing Training Data in Generative Music Models via Black-Box Membership Inference
comment: The paper has been accepted for presentation at the workshop ArtSec 2026: Workshop on Artwork Security and Provenance in the Age of AI
summary: Recent advances in text-to-music generation enable high-fidelity synthesis of structured musical audio, raising growing concerns about data provenance, consent, and training transparency. These models are typically trained on large-scale corpora with little disclosure, leaving no practical mechanism to verify whether a particular audio sample was included in training. In this paper, we investigate black-box membership inference for generative music models, aiming to determine whether a candidate music sample was used during training, given only query access to the deployed system. Our key insight is that training membership induces systematically stronger semantic and structural alignment between a candidate sample and the model's generation conditioned on its caption. We query the target model with the associated caption and measure the relationship between the candidate audio and the generated output in a learned feature space. To capture features that separate members from non-members, we construct paired examples consisting of each track and its caption-conditioned generation from shadow models, and train a music auditor to classify membership. The auditor captures alignment patterns characteristic of training membership and generalizes to unseen target models in a fully black-box setting without access to model parameters or training metadata. Across multiple state-of-the-art music generators, our method achieves up to 98.6% accuracy, with false-positive and false-negative rates as low as 1.9% and 1.0%, demonstrating that reliable training-data auditing is feasible in realistic deployment scenarios.

AI.tldr: A black-box membership-inference framework for generative music models that uses shadow models and a learned music auditor to detect whether a candidate track was part of training, achieving high accuracy in black-box settings.
AI.motivation: Growing concerns about data provenance, consent, and training transparency in text-to-music generation; there is no practical mechanism to verify training data membership for a given audio sample.
AI.method: Construct paired examples from candidate tracks and their caption-conditioned generations produced by shadow models; query the target model with the same captions to obtain generations; extract a learned feature space to measure semantic/structural alignment between candidate audio and the generated output; train a music auditor in a fully black-box setting to classify membership based on patterns of alignment; the auditor generalizes to unseen target models.
AI.result: Across multiple state-of-the-art music generators, the approach achieves up to 98.6% membership accuracy, with false-positive rates as low as 1.9% and false-negative rates as low as 1.0%.
AI.conclusion: Reliable training-data auditing for music-generation models is feasible in realistic deployments and can generalize to unseen systems.

title: Learning Context-Conditioned Predicate Semantics via Prototype Feedback
summary: In scene graph generation, a central challenge is modeling polysemous predicates whose meanings shift across contexts. Prior approaches address this issue by decomposing predicates into multiple static prototypes or retrieving semantically similar exemplars. However, these strategies keep predicate representations static and cannot reorganize semantics to reflect image-specific evidence, leading to systematic confusions in ambiguous contexts. We propose AlignG, which learns context-conditioned predicate semantics via prototype feedback. AlignG infers context-conditioned predicate semantics from the relation candidates within each image and feeds the adapted semantics back to recalibrate relation representations. The learning objective anchors this adaptation to global semantic centers, preventing semantic drift while still allowing selective reorganization when the scene provides consistent relational cues. Experiments on VG-150 and GQA-200 show consistent improvements over state-of-the-art baselines, with F@100 improvements of +1.4 on VG-150 and +2.7 on GQA-200 under SGDet. We further visualize per-image prototype similarity shifts and observe coherent context-dependent reorganization where prototypes selectively merge or separate predicates according to scene evidence. The code is available at https://github.com/Namgyu97/AlignG-SGG.pytorch.

AI.tldr: AlignG learns context-conditioned predicate semantics via prototype feedback to adapt SGG predicates per image, addressing polysemy and improving SGDet metrics.
AI.motivation: Polysemous predicates exhibit context-dependent meanings; static prototype-based representations fail to reorganize semantics according to image evidence, causing systematic confusions in ambiguous contexts; need dynamic semantics with global semantic anchors to prevent drift.
AI.method: Propose AlignG: infer context-conditioned predicate semantics from relation candidates within each image and feed adapted semantics back to recalibrate relation representations. The learning objective anchors adaptation to global semantic centers to prevent semantic drift while allowing selective reorganization when scene evidence is consistent.
AI.result: Empirical results on VG-150 and GQA-200 show improvements over state-of-the-art baselines; F@100 gains of +1.4 on VG-150 and +2.7 on GQA-200 under SGDet; per-image prototype similarity shifts reveal coherent context-dependent reorganization where prototypes merge or separate predicates according to scene cues; code available at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: AlignG effectively addresses polysemous predicate semantics in scene graph generation by leveraging context-conditioned semantics and prototype feedback, achieving consistent accuracy gains and offering interpretable reorganization aligned with image evidence.

title: Xetrieval: Mechanistically Explaining Dense Retrieval
summary: Explaining why dense retrievers assign high relevance scores remains challenging because retrieval decisions are made through opaque high-dimensional embeddings. Existing explanations often focus on surface signals, such as lexical matches, token alignments, or post-hoc textual rationales, and thus provide limited insight into the latent factors that shape dense retrieval behavior at the embedding level. We propose \textit{Xetrieval}, an embedding-level mechanistic framework for explaining dense retrieval. \textit{Xetrieval} first introduces a lightweight reasoning internalizer that approximates Chain-of-Thought reasoning directly in the embedding space with a single forward pass, enriching sentence embeddings with reasoning-oriented information while avoiding expensive autoregressive generation. It then decomposes these reasoning-enhanced embeddings into sparse, human-interpretable features, each associated with a coherent natural language description. By aggregating sparse feature overlaps across multiple document-side views, \textit{Xetrieval} provides feature-level explanations of individual retrieval decisions. Experiments on diverse retrievers and benchmarks show that \textit{Xetrieval} uncovers coherent interpretable features, yields stronger pair-level intervention effects, and supports task-level feature steering. The project page and source code are available at https://hihiczx.github.io/Xetrieval .

AI.tldr: Xetrieval offers an embedding-space, mechanism-level explanation for dense retrieval by inserting a reasoning-like internalizer into embeddings, then decomposing into sparse, human-interpretable features with natural-language descriptions to explain decisions across document views.
AI.motivation: Dense retrievers rely on opaque high-dimensional embeddings, making it difficult to explain why a given relevance score is assigned. Existing explanations focus on surface signals (lexical matches, token alignments, or post-hoc rationales) and fail to reveal latent embedding-level factors shaping retrieval.
AI.method: Introduce Xetrieval: a lightweight reasoning internalizer that approximates chain-of-thought reasoning directly in the embedding space with a single forward pass, enriching sentence embeddings with reasoning-oriented information.
