summary: We study minimal attention-only transformers under all-token corruption and show they admit a two-stage empirical Bayes interpretation. A single attention step computes a kernel-weighted posterior mean with respect to the empirical distribution defined by the context. Depth refines this distribution through particle dynamics (Stage 1), while a long-range skip-connection carries the noisy input as a query for posterior inference (Stage 2), revealing distinct statistical roles for depth and attention residuals. The framework isolates a minimal setting in which the context itself induces a depth-dependent energy landscape governing in-context inference. We show that effective denoising can emerge without an explicit noise schedule: a fixed kernel bandwidth and finite integration horizon suffice, yielding a principled depth-noise relationship. We further establish a posterior-mean recovery guarantee for a class of well-behaved priors, where the empirical estimator converges to the Bayes-optimal predictor under asymptotic conditions. Connecting these dynamics to reverse-diffusion limits, our results provide a statistical interpretation of attention as in-context inference via sample-based posterior estimation, without explicit density modeling.

AI.tldr: Two-stage empirical Bayes interpretation of minimal attention-only transformers under all-token corruption, with depth refining the posterior via particle dynamics and attention (long-range skip) querying a fixed-kernel posterior mean; no explicit density modeling, with recovery guarantees and links to reverse-diffusion.
AI.motivation: Provide a principled statistical understanding of how depth and attention contribute to in-context inference in transformer architectures under noisy contexts.
AI.method: Propose a two-stage empirical Bayes framework: Stage 1 (depth) refines the posterior via particle dynamics; Stage 2 (attention as long-range skip) uses the noisy input as a query to perform posterior inference. Uses a fixed kernel bandwidth and finite integration horizon; connects to reverse-diffusion limits. Derives a posterior-mean recovery guarantee for well-behaved priors.
AI.result: Shows a depth-dependent energy landscape induced by the context; distinct statistical roles for depth and attention residuals; enables effective denoising without a noise schedule; establishes convergence of the empirical posterior mean to the Bayes-optimal predictor under asymptotics.
AI.conclusion: Attention can be interpreted as in-context, sample-based posterior estimation without explicit density modeling; clarifies the statistical roles of depth and attention in minimal transformer settings and connects to diffusion-style dynamics.

title: GeoMag: Geometric-Aware Video Motion Magnification via State Space Model
summary: Video Motion Magnification (VMM) reveals imperceptible dynamics but often suffers from structural inconsistencies under complex geometric transformations. Existing learning-based methods generally face a trade-off between the limited global context of CNNs and the high computational cost of Transformers. In addition, current training protocols, largely dominated by simple linear motion, fail to capture the geometric and imaging complexities encountered in real-world videos. To address these issues, we propose GeoMag, a geometric-aware VMM framework built upon State Space Models to achieve globally consistent motion amplification with linear complexity. We further construct Geo-200K, a large-scale synthetic dataset that introduces rich geometric transformations together with sensor-realistic degradations, improving the diversity and realism of training signals. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks show that GeoMag consistently outperforms prior methods in visual fidelity and computational efficiency, while producing fewer artifacts and better structural consistency.

AI.tldr: GeoMag introduces a geometry-aware video motion magnification framework based on State Space Models, with a large synthetic Geo-200K dataset, achieving globally consistent amplification with linear complexity and improved fidelity.
AI.motivation: Address structural inconsistencies under complex geometric transformations in VMM, and overcome the trade-off between CNN global context and Transformer cost; capture real-world geometric/imaging complexities beyond simple linear motion.
AI.method: Geometric-aware VMM built on State Space Models to enable global context with linear cost; training on Geo-200K, a synthetic dataset with rich geometric transformations and sensor-realistic degradations.
AI.result: Outperforms prior methods on synthetic and real benchmarks in visual fidelity and computational efficiency, with fewer artifacts and better structural consistency.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that geometry-aware global context via State Space Models improves VMM; Geo-200K provides diverse training signals to bridge synthetic-real gaps and enable scalable, robust motion amplification.

title: HiKEY: Hierarchical Multimodal Retrieval for Open-Domain Document Question Answering
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for document-based Open-domain Question Answering (ODQA) on large-scale industrial corpora faces two critical bottlenecks: routing failure in locating the correct document and evidence fragmentation in integrating scattered information. Existing approaches relying on flat text chunks or page-level images inherently struggle to (i) precisely pinpoint the target document among thousands of candidates and (ii) organically connect multimodal evidence, such as tables and figures, within a limited token budget. To address these challenges, we propose HiKEY, a hierarchical tree-based multimodal retrieval framework that elevates document hierarchy to a first-class retrieval signal. Instead of simple chunking, HiKEY reconstructs a logical heterogeneous graph via Document Hierarchical Parsing (DHP), explicitly encoding parent-child relationships. Adopting a hierarchical coarse-to-fine strategy, the framework (1) performs global routing to rapidly prune the search space using hierarchical indexing, and (2) conducts fine-grained retrieval to rank sections by employing a multimodal fusion strategy that captures the most discriminative evidence. Finally, HiKEY assembles a token-efficient evidence subgraph via a hybrid structural-semantic packing strategy. Experiments on ODQA benchmarks demonstrate that HiKEY significantly outperforms page- and chunk-based baselines, improving retrieval recall by up to 12.9% and end-to-end QA performance by up to 6.8%.

AI.tldr: HiKEY introduces a hierarchical, graph-based retrieval framework for document-level, multimodal ODQA, using Document Hierarchical Parsing to build a heterogeneous tree, then a coarse-to-fine retrieval with multimodal evidence fusion and sparse packing, yielding significant gains in retrieval recall and end-to-end QA.
AI.motivation: Address two bottlenecks in retrieval-augmented generation for ODQA on large corpora: (i) routing failure in locating the correct document among thousands, and (ii) evidence fragmentation when integrating scattered multimodal evidence within tight token budgets. Traditional flat chunking or page-level approaches struggle to locate the target document precisely and connect multimodal cues (tables, figures).
AI.method: Construct a logical heterogeneous graph via Document Hierarchical Parsing (DHP) that encodes parent–child relationships; apply hierarchical coarse-to-fine retrieval: (1) global routing with hierarchical indexing to prune search space, (2) fine-grained retrieval with multimodal fusion to rank sections and select discriminative evidence; assemble a token-efficient evidence subgraph using a hybrid structural-semantic packing strategy.
AI.result: On ODQA benchmarks, HiKEY outperforms page- and chunk-based baselines, achieving up to 12.9% higher retrieval recall and up to 6.8% higher end-to-end QA performance.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical, graph-based, multimodal retrieval improves routing and evidence integration in RAG for large industrial corpora, offering token-efficient evidence packing and demonstrable gains in retrieval and QA metrics; the approach highlights the value of leveraging document hierarchy and heterogeneous evidence in multimodal ODQA.

title: Neural-Behavioral Representation of Natural Whole-body Movement in Monkeys
summary: Understanding how cortical activity represents natural whole-body behaviors in primates remains challenging. Limited by the diversity of movements and inaccessibility of large-scale neural representation of whole-body kinematics, previous motor decoding studies focused on constrained tasks and limited limb movements. Here, we present a neural-behavioral recording and modeling framework for freely moving monkeys, combining large-scale epidural cortical signals from distributed sensory- and motor-related areas with synchronized multi-view motion capture through a custom-made data collection platform. We reconstructed whole-body monkey kinematics and learned a compact behavior prior using an autoregressive encoder-decoder model. Conditioned on neural signals, the model decoded accurate and realistic whole-body movement without explicit physical constraints. Our results provide a novel proof-of-concept approach for decoding natural whole-body movements in primates using large-scale intracranial neural activity.

AI.tldr: Framework to decode natural whole-body movements in freely moving primates from large-scale epidural cortical signals using an autoregressive encoder–decoder with a learned behavior prior.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of task-constrained studies and restricted access to broad, natural whole-body neural representations by enabling decoding of full-body kinematics from distributed cortical activity.
AI.method: Combine large-scale epidural signals from distributed sensory- and motor-related areas with synchronized multi-view motion capture; reconstruct whole-body kinematics; learn a compact behavior prior with an autoregressive encoder–decoder; decode movements conditioned on neural activity without explicit physical constraints.
AI.result: Demonstrates accurate and realistic whole-body movement decoding conditioned on neural signals in freely moving monkeys, providing a proof-of-concept for natural movement decoding from large-scale intracranial activity.
AI.conclusion: Shows feasibility of decoding natural whole-body primate movements and offers a data collection and modeling framework for future brain–behavior studies and brain–machine interfaces targeting full-body control.

title: Improving Collaborative Storytelling with a Multi-Agent Framework Based on Large Language Models
summary: The topic of Co-creation, i.e., AI agents interacting with humans to generate outputs (e.g., art), has gained significant attention recently. However, most studies focus on adult-human interactions in a digital setting. This paper explores a novel ludic co-creation scenario involving children and Large Language Models (LLMs) interacting through a physical board game to create written stories. Our goal is to develop a multi-agent framework capable of producing high-quality narratives suitable for young players. At the core of our approach is an iterative Writer-Editor process in which one LLM generates stories while another evaluates them and provides feedback for refinement. Through a simulation study involving multiple LLMs, we show that this iterative interaction consistently improves the perceived quality of generated stories across successive loops. The results indicate that a small number of refinement steps may be sufficient to achieve high-quality outputs in interactive storytelling systems.

AI.tldr: An iterative Writer-Editor LLM framework for child-directed ludic co-creation improves AI-generated storytelling quality, with a small number of refinement steps sufficient in a board-game setting.
AI.motivation: To extend co-creative AI research to child-friendly, physical-board-game storytelling, addressing the gap from adult-digital interactions and evaluating whether multi-agent feedback loops can enhance narrative quality for young players.
AI.method: Core Writer-Editor loop where one LLM writes stories and another evaluates and provides feedback; evaluated via a simulation study with multiple LLMs to assess quality improvements across successive refinement iterations.
AI.result: Iterative interactions consistently improved the perceived quality of stories across loops, with only a small number of refinement steps needed to achieve high-quality outputs in interactive storytelling.
AI.conclusion: A multi-agent Writer-Editor framework is effective for producing high-quality, child-appropriate narratives in interactive storytelling, suggesting practical design guidance that a few refinement cycles can yield strong outputs in ludic co-creation scenarios.

title: Solving Integer Linear Programming with Parallel Tempering
summary: Integer Linear Programming (ILP) serves as a versatile framework for modeling a wide range of combinatorial optimization problems, typically addressed by sophisticated exact solvers or heuristics. While learning-based approaches have recently shown their effectiveness, they suffer from poor generalization to out-of-distribution instances and inherent dependence on external solvers. In this work, we propose a solver-free, sampling-based optimization framework for ILP that directly explores discrete feasible regions without training or external solvers. Exploiting the linear structure of ILP, we employ a Locally-Balanced Proposal to construct a transition kernel, thereby avoiding the gradient approximation. To overcome the highly multimodal nature of ILP energy landscapes, we integrate Parallel Tempering. In addition to standard temperature tempering, we introduce penalty tempering, which modulates constraint barriers while preserving the objective landscape over feasible solutions. Empirically, our method consistently outperforms SCIP across all four benchmarks, matches or exceeds Gurobi on two of four tasks within a 200-second budget, and is substantially more robust to distribution shift than learning-based methods. Furthermore, on MIPLIB 2017 instances, our framework remains competitive with classical solvers without any problem-specific tuning.

AI.tldr: A solver-free, sampling-based ILP framework that uses locally-balanced proposals and parallel/penalty tempering to search discrete feasible regions without training or external solvers, achieving strong performance and robustness.
AI.motivation: Overcome generalization issues and solver-dependence in learning-based ILP approaches by providing a solver-free, broadly applicable optimization framework.
AI.method: Constructs a transition kernel via Locally-Balanced Proposal to explore feasible ILP regions without gradients; employs Parallel Tempering to handle multimodality; introduces penalty tempering to soften constraint barriers while preserving the objective landscape; operates purely by sampling without training or external solvers.
AI.result: Empirically outperforms SCIP on four benchmarks; matches or exceeds Gurobi on two of four tasks within a 200-second budget; more robust to distribution shift than learning-based methods; competitive with classical solvers on MIPLIB 2017 without problem-specific tuning.
AI.conclusion: A competitive, solver-free alternative for ILP that addresses generalization and robustness concerns of learning-based methods and demonstrates strong performance across diverse benchmarks without per-instance tuning.

title: Improving CLIP Adaptation by Breaking Tail Alignment for Source-Free Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) such as CLIP demonstrate strong zero-shot generalization, but their performance significantly degrades in cross-domain scenarios with scarce target-domain training data (Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning, CDFSL). In this paper, we focus on the target-domain few-shot finetuning in the CLIP-based CDFSL task. Prevailing finetuning paradigms uniformly align all image patch tokens with their corresponding textual embeddings. However, we find a counterintuitive phenomenon: actively pushing away certain low-similarity image tokens, termed "tail tokens", from their textual embeddings consistently improves target-domain performance. We delve into this phenomenon and provide a novel interpretation: under great domain shifts and scarce training data, the model can hardly extract semantic information from visual inputs; therefore, the common belief of alignment is valid only for tokens already containing sufficient semantic information; for tail tokens, forcing the alignment would lead to excessive overfitting to the scarce training, while breaking the alignment is more useful. Motivated by this, we propose Adaptive Tail-Head Alignment (ATHA), a novel fine-tuning strategy for CLIP that transforms the conventional uniform alignment paradigm to an adaptive alignment paradigm, with both alignment strengthening and weakening. Extensive experiments on four challenging CDFSL benchmarks validate our state-of-the-art performance. Our code is available at https://github.com/shuaiyi308/ATHA.

AI.tldr: Adaptive Tail-Head Alignment (ATHA): a CLIP fine-tuning strategy that adaptively strengthens or weakens token-wise alignment to tail tokens, improving cross-domain few-shot generalization.
AI.motivation: In cross-domain few-shot learning with CLIP, uniformly aligning all image patch tokens to text leads to overfitting on scarce target data; many tail tokens contain little semantic information under large domain shifts, so forcing their alignment harms generalization.
AI.method: ATHA converts uniform alignment into an adaptive scheme that simultaneously strengthens high-similarity (head) tokens and weakens low-similarity (tail) tokens during CLIP fine-tuning. It introduces token-wise alignment weights, computed from token-text similarity distributions, to modulate the CLIP loss and gradient flow. The approach is model-agnostic within the CLIP finetuning framework and aims to reduce overfitting to tail tokens while preserving useful semantic signals from head tokens.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on four challenging CDFSL benchmarks shows state-of-the-art performance, outperforming standard uniform alignment baselines and other CLIP-based fine-tuning strategies.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive Tail-Head Alignment provides a simple yet effective mechanism to cope with domain shift and data scarcity in CLIP-based CDFSL by tailoring token-level alignment strength, yielding robust target-domain generalization and practical training efficiency.

title: Beyond Attack Success Rate: Temporal Logit Observability for LLM Safety Failures
summary: Attack Success Rate (ASR) evaluates each jailbreak with a single yes/no label at the end of generation, telling us whether a failure happened but not how it unfolded. Two attacks that produce equally harmful outputs may have followed completely different paths, and ASR cannot tell them apart. We make those hidden paths observable from logits alone. Temporal Logit Observability (TLO) is a training-free diagnostic that watches a compliance-refusal margin during decoding and places each model-attack condition on a calibrated 2D plane. By design, this plane is most informative exactly where ASR is least informative: among attacks that succeed for genuinely different reasons. Across four aligned LLMs and three jailbreak paradigms, attacks with nearly identical ASR land at clearly different points on the plane: the same model can fail through different temporal patterns. The geometry matches refusal-direction probes from hidden states on most conditions, with one model showing the limit of our fixed-lexicon approach. A simple early-stop rule derived from TLO cuts successful jailbreaks by more than half, without false alarms on plain benign queries. Safety evaluation should report when and how a failure unfolds, not only whether it occurred. TLO makes the first two observable from logits alone.

AI.tldr: Temporal Logit Observability (TLO) uses decoding-time logit dynamics to diagnose jailbreak failures, distinguishing why similar ASR successes occur, enabling an early-stop defense and richer safety evaluation.
AI.motivation: ASR conflates distinct failure modes; need to observe how and why a jailbreak succeeds to inform mitigation and safe design.
AI.method: Training-free diagnostic that tracks compliance-refusal margin during decoding, maps model-attack conditions to a calibrated 2D plane, and compares across models and jailbreak paradigms. Derives an early-stop rule from TLO.
AI.result: Attacks with near-identical ASR land at different TLO locations; same model can fail for different reasons; TLO aligns with hidden-state probes; early-stop reduces jailbreak success by >50% with no benign false alarms.
AI.conclusion: Safety evaluations should report failure unfolding, not only occurrence. TLO provides the first logits-only observability of failure dynamics and yields a practical defense.

title: Deep Adaptive Dimension Reduction for Bayesian Inference in Inverse Problems
summary: Solving high-dimensional PDE-governed inverse problems is often challenging due to complex non-Gaussian posterior distributions, expensive forward model evaluations, and misspecified prior information. To address these issues, we propose a deep adaptive dimension-reduction Bayesian inference framework based on the Variational Flow (VF) model. Since standard normalizing flows are restricted by bijective mappings and cannot directly reduce dimensions, VF overcomes this limitation by integrating VAE-based nonlinear dimension reduction with dual normalizing flows for the latent prior and encoder. This design provides a strictly higher evidence lower bound than VAE and allows more flexible approximation of complex posterior distributions. We further introduce an iterative prior updating strategy that gradually moves the prior mean toward high-probability posterior regions, avoiding manual prior tuning. These components form a closed adaptive loop together with an adaptively fine-tuned Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) surrogate: VF generates posterior-concentrated samples to refine the surrogate, while the updated surrogate further improves posterior inference. Numerical experiments on a 100-dimensional Rosenbrock problem and three standard PDE-governed inverse problems show that our method delivers competitive or superior accuracy compared with MCMC, UKI, and SVGD baselines across all tested configurations, with the most pronounced advantages emerging in challenging scenarios such as high-noise observations and high-dimensional parameter spaces.

AI.tldr: Introduces a deep adaptive dimension-reduction Bayesian inference framework (Variational Flow) that couples VAE-based nonlinear dimension reduction with dual normalizing flows, plus an iterative prior updating loop and adaptively trained Fourier Neural Operator surrogates, achieving competitive or superior performance on high-dimensional PDE inverse problems.
AI.motivation: Address the challenges of high-dimensional, non-Gaussian posteriors, expensive forward solves, and misspecified priors in PDE-governed inverse problems by enabling flexible posterior approximation and efficient forward surrogates.
AI.method: Develop a Variational Flow (VF) that integrates VAE-based nonlinear dimension reduction with dual normalizing flows for latent prior and encoder, yielding a higher evidence lower bound than VAE. Introduce an iterative prior updating strategy to steer the prior toward high-probability posterior regions. Employ an adaptively fine-tuned Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) surrogate that is refined by VF-generated samples in a closed adaptive loop.
AI.result: Demonstrates competitive or superior accuracy compared with MCMC, UKI, and SVGD baselines across 100-dimensional Rosenbrock and three standard PDE-governed inverse problems, with pronounced advantages in high-noise and high-dimensional regimes.
AI.conclusion: The VF framework with adaptive prior updating and a co-trained FNO surrogate provides effective, scalable Bayesian inversion for challenging PDE problems, improving posterior approximation while controlling computational costs.

title: Fewer Steps, Better Performance: Efficient Cross-Modal Clip Trimming for Video Moment Retrieval Using Language
summary: Given an untrimmed video and a sentence query, video moment retrieval using language (VMR) aims to locate a target query-relevant moment. Since the untrimmed video is overlong, almost all existing VMR methods first sparsely down-sample each untrimmed video into multiple fixed-length video clips and then conduct multi-modal interactions with the query feature and expensive clip features for reasoning, which is infeasible for long real-world videos that span hours. Since the video is downsampled into fixed-length clips, some query-related frames may be filtered out, which will blur the specific boundary of the target moment, take the adjacent irrelevant frames as new boundaries, easily leading to cross-modal misalignment and introducing both boundary-bias and reasoning-bias. To this end, in this paper, we propose an efficient approach, SpotVMR, to trim the query-relevant clip. Besides, our proposed SpotVMR can serve as plug-and-play module, which achieves efficiency for state-of-the-art VMR methods while maintaining good retrieval performance. Especially, we first design a novel clip search model that learns to identify promising video regions to search conditioned on the language query. Then, we introduce a set of low-cost semantic indexing features to capture the context of objects and interactions that suggest where to search the query-relevant moment. Also, the distillation loss is utilized to address the optimization issues arising from end-to-end joint training of the clip selector and VMR model. Extensive experiments on three challenging datasets demonstrate its effectiveness.

AI.tldr: SpotVMR trims untrimmed videos for video moment retrieval by learning a language-conditioned clip search and using low-cost semantic indexing features, acting as a plug-and-play module that improves efficiency while preserving retrieval accuracy via distillation training.
AI.motivation: VMR on long, untrimmed videos suffers from heavy computation and boundary/reasoning biases due to downsampling into fixed-length clips. A method that selectively searches query-relevant regions can reduce wasted computation and mitigate boundary errors.
AI.method: Introduce a clip search model conditioned on the natural language query to identify promising video regions to search. Use low-cost semantic indexing features to capture contextual cues about objects and interactions guiding where to search. Employ a distillation loss to stabilize end-to-end training of the clip selector and the VMR model. Design SpotVMR as a plug-and-play module that can augment current VMR methods.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on three challenging datasets demonstrate SpotVMR’s effectiveness, showing improved efficiency and maintained (or enhanced) retrieval performance compared with baseline VMR approaches.
AI.conclusion: SpotVMR effectively trims query-relevant clips, reducing boundary and reasoning biases caused by naive downsampling while delivering practical efficiency gains and compatibility with existing VMR systems.

title: VikingMem: A Memory Base Management System for Stateful LLM-based Applications
summary: Large Language Models have revolutionized interactive applications; however, their finite context windows pose a critical data management challenge for maintaining stateful, long-term interactions. Existing memory approaches often rely on simplistic extraction methods that lead to incomplete memories or use rigid, single-purpose memory extraction prompts tailored to a single use case, such as chatbots. Consequently, they lack generalizability and perform poorly across diverse downstream tasks. To bridge this gap, we introduce the Memory Base, a novel data management paradigm for managing the persistent state of long-term interactions. It is characterized by three core principles: selective extraction of high-value memories from raw information streams; inherent statefulness and evolution, where memory content is progressively summarized, corrected, and temporally weighted to prioritize recent interactions; and a generalizable abstraction paradigm designed for robust transferability across diverse applications, including education, recommendation, and agent memory. Building on this foundation, we present VikingMem, an end-to-end Memory Base Management System implemented on the VikingDB vector engine. VikingMem materializes this paradigm through interconnected event and entity abstractions. It features event-centric memory extraction to selectively handle complex information streams, while entities are dynamically updated by events to achieve stateful evolution. Using temporal compression via a topic-wise timeline and time-weighted recall, the system progressively produces high-level summary memories, prioritizes recent items, and compresses and fades older ones. Extensive evaluations on long-term memory benchmarks demonstrate that VikingMem outperformes baselines by up to 30% in memory retrieval effectiveness while maintaining the low latency essential for interactive applications.

AI.tldr: Memory Base and VikingMem offer a generalizable memory management system for long-term LLM interactions, achieving up to 30% retrieval improvement with low latency.
AI.motivation: Finite context windows hinder stateful, long-term interactions; existing memory methods are fragile, non-generalizable, and task-specific. A selective, evolving, transferable memory paradigm is needed.
AI.method: Introduce Memory Base with selective extraction of high-value memories, stateful evolution through summarization/correction/temporal weighting, and a generalizable abstraction. Implement VikingMem on VikingDB with event- and entity-centric abstractions; use event-centric extraction, dynamic entity updates, temporal compression via topic-wise timelines, and time-weighted recall to produce high-level memories.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on long-term memory benchmarks shows VikingMem improves memory retrieval effectiveness by up to 30% over baselines while preserving low latency.
AI.conclusion: Memory Base enables robust, transferable memory management across diverse applications (education, recommendation, agent memory); VikingMem demonstrates effective, scalable long-term memory handling with selective, evolving memories.

title: TRACER: Persistent Regularization for Robust Multimodal Finetuning
summary: Mainstream strategies for finetuning pretrained multimodal models often degrade out-of-distribution (OOD) robustness, a phenomenon known as catastrophic forgetting. In this paper, we develop a theoretical framework for multimodal contrastive finetuning, yielding closed-form solutions and a geometric decomposition for each strategy. This framework shows that self-distillation is more effective than other regularization approaches to retain the knowledge of the pretrained model. Our analysis reveals a largely overlooked limitation: standard Exponential Moving Average (EMA) teachers, widely used in robust finetuning, suffer from collapse. To solve this, we prove that a Weighted Moving Average (WMA) teacher maintains a persistent regularizing force over finite horizons and yields bias-free convergence in the task subspace while preserving orthogonal knowledge. These insights motivate **TRACER** (**T**rajectory-**R**obust **A**nchoring for **C**ontrastive **E**ncoder **R**egularization), which combines contrastive learning with WMA-guided multi-perspective distillation. Extensive experiments on CLIP finetuning demonstrate consistent OOD accuracy and calibration gains across three backbone architectures, and comprehensive ablations confirm that TRACER is both principled and robust to hyperparameter choices. Code is available at [https://github.com/HesamAsad/TRACER](https://github.com/HesamAsad/TRACER).

AI.tldr: A theoretical framework for multimodal contrastive finetuning reveals EMA collapse; introduces Weighted Moving Average (WMA) teachers and TRACER, a distillation-augmented contrastive method that yields consistent OOD robustness and calibration improvements in CLIP finetuning.
AI.motivation: OOD robustness often degrades during finetuning of pretrained multimodal models (catastrophic forgetting). There is a need for principled analysis of finetuning strategies and robust regularization to preserve pretrained knowledge.
AI.method: Develop a theoretical framework for multimodal contrastive finetuning with a geometric decomposition; derive closed-form solutions; analyze self-distillation vs regularization; identify EMA collapse and propose WMA as a persistent regularizer; introduce TRACER that combines contrastive learning with WMA-guided distillation; validate via experiments.
AI.result: Closed-form solutions and a geometric view; self-distillation outperforms other regularizers; EMA teachers collapse, while WMA provides bias-free convergence and preserves orthogonal knowledge; TRACER yields consistent OOD accuracy and calibration gains across three backbones; ablations show robustness to hyperparameters; code released.
AI.conclusion: TRACER offers a principled, robust regularization framework for multimodal finetuning that mitigates catastrophic forgetting by using a WMA teacher; this enhances OOD robustness and calibration in CLIP finetuning.

title: Low-Magnification SEM May Suffice: Interpretable Deep Learning for Multi-Scale Fracture-Cause Classification in Zirconia-Toughened Alumina
summary: Reliable identification of fracture origins in alumina matrix composite hip and knee implants is critical for quality assurance and patient safety, yet current fractographic workflows are time-consuming, partly subjective, and reliant on high-magnification scanning electron microscopy (SEM). We present an interpretable vision-transformer (ViT) workflow for automated classification of fracture causes in an alumina matrix composite (BIOLOX delta, CeramTec GmbH) widely used in total joint replacements. A dataset of 8,493 SEM images (50x-10,000x) was curated from five years of in-production burst and proof tests and annotated into three defect categories defined along the manufacturing chain: green body, hard machining, and material defects. Under severe class imbalance, the fine-tuned ViT reached an accuracy of 0.907 and a macro-F1 of 0.888 in stratified five-fold cross-validation, with a two-stage perceptual-hash/SSIM leakage audit confirming negligible specimen overlap. Notably, performance at low magnification (50x) was comparable to that at high magnification (1k-10kx), indicating that macro-scale features - mirror geometry and hackle line fields - already encode sufficient diagnostic signal. Grad-CAM attributions consistently localised on canonical fractographic cues (mirrors, hackles, pores, machining marks), aligning with established fractographic criteria. Together, these results position interpretable ViTs as a complementary tool for ceramic-implant quality assurance, enabling low-magnification pre-screening and reducing reliance on time-intensive high-magnification inspection.

AI.tldr: Interpretable vision-transformer workflow for automated fracture-cause classification in BIOLOX delta alumina matrix composite implants. Uses 8,493 SEM images across three defect categories; achieves high accuracy (0.907) and macro-F1 (0.888) with stratified 5-fold CV. Low-magnification 50x performance rivals high magnification. Grad-CAM attributes align with fractographic cues. Enables low-magnification pre-screening and reduces reliance on high-magnification inspection.
AI.motivation: Reliable identification of fracture origins is crucial for quality assurance and patient safety in ceramic-on-ceramic joint implants. Traditional fractography is time-consuming, partly subjective, and relies on expensive, high-magnification SEM.
AI.method: Fine-tuned vision transformer (ViT) trained on a dataset of SEM images (8,493 images, 50x–10,000x) from five years of production/debug tests, annotated into three defect categories (green body, hard machining, material defects). Addressed severe class imbalance. Evaluation used stratified five-fold cross-validation with a two-stage perceptual-hash/SSIM leakage audit to ensure no specimen overlap. Additional analyses included low-vs-high magnification performance and Grad-CAM attributions to verify alignment with fractographic cues.
AI.result: Test accuracy: 0.907; macro-F1: 0.888. Leakage audit confirmed negligible specimen overlap. Low magnification (50x) performance comparable to high magnification (1k–10k). Grad-CAM localised on canonical fractographic cues (mirrors, hackle lines, pores, machining marks). Demonstrates viability of interpretable ViTs as a QA aid for ceramic implants, enabling pre-screening at lower magnification and reducing reliance on high-magnification inspection.
AI.conclusion: Interpretable ViTs offer a complementary QA tool for ceramic implants, supporting low-magnification pre-screening and potentially shortening turnaround while maintaining diagnostic relevance through interpretable attribution to established fractographic cues.

title: LLM-Evolved Domain-Independent Heuristics for Symbolic AI Planning
summary: Heuristic search is the dominant paradigm in symbolic AI planning, and the strongest heuristics are the result of decades of work by planning researchers. Recent work has shown that large language models (LLMs) can design heuristics for individual planning domains, but no LLM-generated heuristic has so far worked on arbitrary planning tasks. In this paper, we use evolutionary search to produce the first LLM-generated domain-independent heuristics that exceed the hand-engineered state of the art. We let an LLM mutate parent heuristics written in C++, store candidates in a MAP-Elites archive keyed on informedness and speed and calculate fitness scores by blending coverage with solving time. To place the evolved programs in context, we additionally benchmark a broad set of hand-engineered heuristics on their informedness-speed tradeoff, which to our knowledge has not been done before. On unseen testing domains, our best evolved heuristic solves more tasks than even the strongest baseline, with our full heuristic suite spanning the Pareto frontier of said tradeoff. We also find that seeding evolution from the trivial blind heuristic outperforms seeding from the strong FF heuristic, even when the resulting program is itself an FF variant, and that LLM reasoning effort affects how often candidates compile much more than the quality of those that do. Because the evolved programs are plain C++, they slot into existing planners as drop-in replacements and inherit the soundness and completeness guarantees of the underlying search.

AI.tldr: LLM-generated, domain-independent planning heuristics produced via evolutionary search surpass hand-engineered baselines; they are portable C++ drop-ins and exhibit Pareto-optimal informedness–speed tradeoffs.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitation of LLM-designed heuristics being domain-specific and to create domain-independent, performant heuristics for symbolic AI planning.
AI.method: Mutate LLM-written C++ heuristics; store candidates in MAP-Elites archive keyed by informedness and speed; fitness blends coverage with solving time; benchmark against diverse hand-engineered heuristics on informedness–speed tradeoff; compare seeding strategies (blind vs FF).
AI.result: On unseen domains, the best evolved heuristic solves more tasks than strong baselines; the full suite spans the Pareto frontier; seeding from blind heuristic outperforms FF seeding; LLM reasoning effort correlates with compilation frequency; evolved programs are plain C++ and slot into planners with soundness/completeness preserved.
AI.conclusion: LLM-powered evolutionary search can yield portable, competition-leading domain-independent heuristics for planning, with practitioner-friendly integration and predictable performance tradeoffs.

title: Not All Inputs Are Valid: Towards Open-Set Video Moment Retrieval Using Language
summary: Video Moment Retrieval (VMR) targets to retrieve the specific moment corresponding to a sentence query from an untrimmed video. Although recent works have made remarkable progress in this task, they implicitly are rooted in the closed-set assumption that all the given queries as video-relevant\footnote{In this paper, we treat ``video-relevant query'' as ``in-distribution (ID) query'' and ``video-irrelevant query'' as ``out-of-distribution (OOD) query''.}. Given an OOD query in open-set scenarios, they still utilize it for wrong retrieval, which might lead to irrecoverable losses in high-risk scenarios, \textit{e.g.}, criminal activity detection. To this end, we creatively explore a brand-new VMR setting termed Open-Set Video Moment Retrieval (OS-VMR), where we should not only retrieve the precise moments based on ID query, but also reject OOD queries. In this paper, we make the first attempt to step toward OS-VMR and propose a novel model \textbf{OpenVMR}, which first distinguishes ID and OOD queries based on the normalizing flow technology, and then conducts moment retrieval based on ID queries. Specifically, we first learn the ID distribution by constructing a normalizing flow, and assume the ID query distribution obeys the multi-variate Gaussian distribution. Then, we introduce an uncertainty score to search the ID-OOD separating boundary. After that, we refine the ID-OOD boundary by pulling together ID query features. Besides, video-query matching and frame-query matching are designed for coarse-grained and fine-grained cross-modal interaction, respectively. Finally, a positive-unlabeled learning module is introduced for moment retrieval. Experimental results on three VMR datasets show the effectiveness of our OpenVMR.

AI.tldr: Proposes OpenVMR for Open-Set Video Moment Retrieval (OS-VMR): learn ID query distribution with normalizing flow (MV Gaussian), detect OOD queries via an uncertainty boundary, and perform ID-based moment retrieval with coarse/fine cross-modal interactions plus positive-unlabeled learning; evaluated on three VMR datasets and shows effectiveness.
AI.motivation: Current VMR methods assume in-distribution (ID) queries and fail on out-of-distribution (OOD) queries, risking high-stakes errors in scenarios like crime detection. OS-VMR aims to both retrieve ID moments and reject OOD queries.
AI.method: Model ID distribution with a normalizing flow and assume MV Gaussian for ID queries. Compute an uncertainty-based boundary to separate ID/OOD. Refine the boundary by pulling ID features together. Use video-query and frame-query matching for coarse and fine cross-modal interaction. Apply positive-unlabeled learning for moment retrieval.
AI.result: Experimental results on three VMR datasets demonstrate effectiveness of OpenVMR in identifying ID vs OOD queries and retrieving corresponding moments.
AI.conclusion: First attempt to Open-Set Video Moment Retrieval. OpenVMR shows feasibility of rejecting OOD queries while accurately retrieving moments for ID queries, highlighting a new direction for VMR under open-set conditions.

title: Think Fast, Talk Smart: Partitioning Deterministic and Neural Computation for Structured Health Text Generation
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly being used to generate health text from structured records such as wearable time series, biomarkers, vitals, and care-management logs. For recurring health outputs, fluency is not enough: systems must remain faithful to source data, ground explanatory claims in available evidence, follow stated policies, emit machine-readable outputs, and run cheaply enough for repeated use. We ask which responsibilities in structured health generation should be deterministic computation rather than runtime LLM prompting.
  We introduce Think Fast, Talk Smart, a sleep-health insight pipeline in which deterministic code performs recurring analysis before one bounded LLM writer call. Across 280 user-nights and six models, achieves lower numeric error, lower instruction-compliance error, and lower end-to-end cost than structured zero-shot and few-shot one-call baselines.
  Layer replacement reveals contract-specific failures: LLM comparison raises numeric error, LLM ranking degrades policy selection, LLM attribution increases unsupported causal language, and an LLM-generated writer interface reintroduces errors even after upstream facts are deterministic. The results support a broader design rule: let code own recurring analysis, and let LLMs express verified facts within bounded interfaces.

AI.tldr: A deterministic-analysis plus bounded-LMM writer pipeline for sleep-health insights improves accuracy and cost vs one-shot LLM baselines, with layer replacements exposing where LLMs fail; advocates separating deterministic computation from LLM-based articulations.
AI.motivation: Ensure fidelity to structured health data, ground claims, follow policies, produce machine-readable outputs, and reduce costs in repetitive health text generation; determine which tasks should be deterministic vs prompting.
AI.method: Deterministic code performs recurring analyses on structured records; only one bounded LLM writer call per user night; evaluation on 280 user-nights, six models; compare to structured zero-shot and few-shot baselines; layer replacement experiments to pinpoint failure modes.
AI.result: Lower numeric error, lower instruction-compliance error, lower end-to-end cost compared to baselines; layer replacements reveal LLMs can cause numeric errors, policy-selection degradation, increased incorrect causal language, interface reintroduction of errors; suggests code should own recurring analysis and LLMs should express verified facts within bounded interfaces.
AI.conclusion: Promotes architectural principle: separate deterministic processing from LLM generation; rely on code for recurring analysis, LLM to produce verified facts within a constrained interface; improves reliability and cost-efficiency.

title: Fairness Beyond Demographics: Optimizing Performance Across Appearance-Based Hidden Cohorts in Medical Imaging
comment: Pre-review version submitted to MICCAI 2026. 10 pages, 5 figures
summary: Medical image analysis models can exhibit performance disparities across patient subgroups, threatening clinical safety and fairness. Existing methods typically address this issue by optimizing accuracy and fairness metrics for visible demographic attributes (e.g., sex or age) considered in isolation. This strategy not only overlooks potentially more informative latent stratifications, which may reveal deeper sources of model error and inequity, but also fails to scale when multiple demographic attributes are considered simultaneously due to the resulting sparsity of training data within each subgroup. We deal with these issues by introducing the label-free hidden-cohort fairness (LHCF) training paradigm that instead of maximizing fairness over visible demographic attributes, it optimizes fairness across latent subpopulations discovered from image appearance. By clustering images into K appearance-based cohorts and applying fairness optimization over them, LHCF uncovers underlying sources of model error and avoids the combinatorial sparsity of multi-demographic attributes, reducing disparities across both single and multiple demographic attributes. We demonstrate on our proposed fairness benchmark, HIDFairBench, that LHCF provides state-of-the-art fairness results on single and multiple demographic attributes, despite never using demographic labels for training. Our results position hidden-cohort fairness as a practical, scalable, and robust alternative to demographic-based fairness optimization for trustworthy medical image analysis.

AI.tldr: Label-free hidden-cohort fairness (LHCF) clusters images into appearance-based cohorts to optimize fairness, achieving state-of-the-art results on HIDFairBench without demographic labels.
AI.motivation: Address performance disparities in medical image analysis across subgroups while avoiding sparsity and scalability issues from multi-attribute demographic labels.
AI.method: Cluster images into K appearance-based cohorts and optimize fairness across these hidden cohorts rather than over explicit demographic attributes; evaluate on HIDFairBench; no demographic labels used during training.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art fairness for single and multiple attributes on HIDFairBench; reduces disparities and demonstrates robustness and scalability; demonstrates a practical alternative to demographic-based fairness.
AI.conclusion: Hidden-cohort fairness offers a scalable, label-free approach for trustworthy medical image analysis, resolving combinatorial sparsity and revealing latent sources of bias.

title: PTCG-Bench: Can LLM Agents Master Pokémon Trading Card Game?
summary: Given a strategically complex board game, human players can quickly learn to devise strategies after playing a few rounds. Autonomous agents require similar capabilities in realistic interactive environments, yet existing agent benchmarks often fail to fully capture such strategic and evolving decision-making scenarios. We present PTCG-Bench, a benchmark built on the Pok'{e}mon Trading Card Game (PTCG) that evaluates LLM agents at two complementary levels: (1) their decision-making performance within a single complex environment, and (2) their ability to self-evolving through accumulated experience. We further include a modular harness ablation to better interpret agent performance without conflating it with model capability. Our experiments show that, although LLM agents can achieve non-trivial gameplay performance, sustained and stable self-evolution remains challenging, and performance is sensitive to harness design. We hope that PTCG-Bench will facilitate future research on harness-aware and self-evolving agents in realistic interactive environments.

AI.tldr: Introduces PTCG-Bench to benchmark LLM agents in a Pokemon TCG environment along two dimensions: in-environment decision-making and self-evolution through experience, with a modular harness ablation to separate model capability from harness design. Findings: LLMs reach non-trivial gameplay but struggle with sustained self-evolution; performance is highly affected by harness design.
AI.motivation: There is a need for benchmarks that capture strategic decision-making and long-term adaptation in realistic interactive settings; existing agent benchmarks often miss evolving strategic behavior, hindering development of autonomous agents in dynamic environments.
AI.method: Construct PTCG-Bench using the Pokemon Trading Card Game; evaluate LLM agents on (1) single-environment decision-making performance and (2) self-evolution through accumulated experience; incorporate a modular harness ablation to parse effects of harness design from model capability.
AI.result: LLM agents demonstrate non-trivial gameplay; however, achieving sustained and stable self-evolution remains difficult; agent performance is sensitive to harness design choices.
AI.conclusion: PTCG-Bench is a step toward harness-aware and self-evolving agents in realistic interactive environments and should catalyze further research in this area.

title: Rethinking Post-Training Recipes for Multimodal Time-Series Forecasting
summary: Time-Series Foundation Models (TSFMs) excel at zero-shot unimodal forecasting using numerical data, but unlike LLMs they cannot consume multimodal, non-numerical context that often shape real-world trajectories. In this work, we bridge this gap and argue for a multimodal time-series forecasting approach that post-trains LLMs to act as context-guided revisors over strong numerical TSFM priors. We introduce PostTime, a post-training recipe combining Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), along with a methodology to generate automated reasoning traces for forecast revisions. PostTime teaches an LLM to generate context-conditioned forecast interventions -- decisions to revise, preserve, or ignore the TSFM prior based on the multimodal context. We evaluate this approach on the TimesX multimodal forecasting benchmark using a Gemma-3-4B LLM and TimesFM-2.5 TSFM, and show that it significantly outperforms standalone TSFMs, LLM-only baselines, and existing multimodal forecasting approaches.

AI.tldr: PostTime: a post-training pipeline that adapts LLMs to multimodal time-series forecasting by using context-guided forecast revisions of strong TSFM priors; combines supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards to generate context-conditioned interventions, yielding improvements on TimesX with Gemma-3-4B and TimesFM-2.5.
AI.motivation: TSFMs are strong for zero-shot unimodal numerical forecasting but struggle with multimodal, non-numerical context typical in real-world trajectories; bridging this gap requires leveraging multimodal signals to guide revisions to numerical priors.
AI.method: PostTime trains an LLM via Supervised Fine-Tuning and Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards to generate reasoning traces and context-conditioned forecast interventions (revise, preserve, or ignore) for a given multimodal context, effectively acting as a context-guided reviser over a TSFM prior.
AI.result: On the TimesX multimodal forecasting benchmark using a Gemma-3-4B LLM and TimesFM-2.5 TSFM, PostTime significantly outperforms standalone TSFMs, LLM-only baselines, and existing multimodal forecasting approaches.
AI.conclusion: Integrating LLM-based context-guided revision with strong numerical TSFM priors is an effective strategy for multimodal time-series forecasting, enabling better utilization of non-numerical context to revise forecasts.

title: Parameter-Efficient Subspace Decoupling ViT for Mitigating Multi-Task Negative Transfer in Histological Scoring
comment: 6 pages, 5 figures, 2 tables. Accepted by IEEE ICME 2026. Camera-ready version
summary: Histological scoring is essential for diagnosing Non-Alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease (NAFLD), yet its automation remains challenging due to the high annotation cost and negative transfer among the strongly correlated NAFLD Activity Score (NAS) indicators in multi-task learning. To address this issue, we propose a subspace-decoupled multi-task Vision Transformer (ViT) that integrates lightweight task-specific Adapters with orthogonality-based constraints. This design constructs independent feature subspaces for steatosis, ballooning, and inflammation, effectively reducing task interference while retaining shared representations. We further construct a curated multi-task mouse NAFLD histology dataset with expert annotations for all NAS components. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method improves multi-task stability and generalization with substantially reduced computational cost compared to training separate single-task models. The code and the curated dataset have been prepared and will be made publicly available upon acceptance to support reproducibility.

AI.tldr: A subspace-decoupled multi-task Vision Transformer with adapters and orthogonality constraints separates NAS-related histology features to improve multi-task learning for NAFLD scoring, validated on a curated mouse dataset; code and data will be released for reproducibility.
AI.motivation: NAFLD histology scoring (NAS) is costly to annotate and suffers from negative transfer among correlated NAS components in multi-task learning. A method that decouples task-specific features while preserving shared representations could improve accuracy and efficiency.
AI.method: A subspace-decoupled multi-task Vision Transformer (ViT) with lightweight task-specific adapters constrained by orthogonality to form independent feature subspaces for steatosis, ballooning, and inflammation. A curated multi-task mouse NAFLD histology dataset with expert NAS annotations is used for training and evaluation.
AI.result: Experiments show improved multi-task stability and generalization with substantially reduced computational cost compared to training separate single-task models.
AI.conclusion: The proposed subspace-decoupled MT ViT effectively mitigates task interference in NAS component scoring, offering a more efficient and generalizable solution for automated NAFLD histology assessment; code and dataset will be publicly released to support reproducibility.

title: TRACE: Toulmin-based Reasoning Assessment through Constructive Elements for LLM CoT Evaluation
summary: Evaluating open-ended outputs from large language models (LLMs) remains challenging due to the absence of ground truth. Existing metrics rely on final-answer accuracy or surface-level statistics, leaving the reasoning process itself unexamined. We introduce TRACE (Toulmin-based Reasoning Assessment through Constructive Elements), a metric that analyzes Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning processes. Rather than judging outcomes, TRACE inspects how arguments are constructed by integrating Toulmin's argumentation theory with Flavell's metacognitive framework to assess reasoning structure. Experiments on 26.3K QA samples across 7 reasoning models show strong correlation with benchmark accuracy (r=0.74). Furthermore, TRACE is effective as a reinforcement learning reward signal, outperforming accuracy-only baselines. Together, these results indicate that logically sound reasoning leads to higher-quality answers. TRACE thus serves as a complementary metric for evaluating open-ended outputs. Code is available at https://github.com/hyyangkisti/trace.

AI.tldr: TRACE evaluates chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning using Toulmin's argumentation theory and Flavell's metacognition to assess reasoning structure, not just outcomes. It correlates with accuracy and serves as a reinforcement learning (RL) reward signal.
AI.motivation: Grounded in the need to evaluate reasoning processes in open-ended LLM outputs, where final answers alone are insufficient and ground-truth CoT is unavailable. TRACE aims to quantify the quality of argument construction within CoT.
AI.method: Apply Toulmin's argument elements (claim, evidence, warrant, backing, rebuttal, qualifier) and Flavell's metacognitive components to CoT segments. Analyze 26.3K QA samples from 7 LLMs to derive structural scores, then correlate with benchmark accuracy; also test as a reinforcement learning reward signal.
AI.result: TRACE shows a strong correlation with benchmark accuracy (r = 0.74) across models and outperforms accuracy-only baselines when used as an RL reward signal. It generalizes across 7 reasoning models and 26.3K QA samples.
AI.conclusion: A structurally sound CoT, as captured by TRACE, aligns with higher-quality answers. TRACE complements existing metrics by focusing on reasoning construction and can guide RL optimization for better open-ended outputs.

title: Building and Road Recognition in Dense Urban Informal Settlements: A Dataset and Benchmark
summary: As a widespread form of informal settlements, urban villages present significant challenges for sustainable urban development and governance. Precise mapping of their infrastructure is essential, however, existing remote sensing datasets primarily focus on formal urban environments, lacking fine-grained annotated data for the high-density building patterns and narrow road networks typical of urban villages. To address this gap, we introduce the \textit{DenseUIS} dataset, the first high-resolution remote sensing dataset specifically designed for building and road extraction in extremely dense urban informal settlements, covering 126 urban villages across Shenzhen and Guangzhou in China. Furthermore, we conduct a comprehensive evaluation of state-of-the-art deep learning models on this dataset. Experimental results reveal the limitations of existing methods in handling the unique morphological patterns of dense informal settlements, underscoring the need for specialized approaches. \textit{DenseUIS} therefore provides a robust benchmark for advancing fine-grained urban mapping in complex and high-density informal environments. The dataset is publicly available at https://github.com/rui-research/DenseUIS.

AI.tldr: DenseUIS is a high-resolution remote sensing dataset for building and road extraction in extremely dense urban informal settlements (urban villages), serving as a new benchmark and revealing gaps in current models.
AI.motivation: There is a critical need for fine-grained mapping of dense informal settlements to support sustainable urban development and governance; existing datasets focus on formal urban areas and lack detailed annotations for high-density morphologies and narrow roads.
AI.method: Creation and annotation of DenseUIS: a high-resolution remote sensing dataset covering 126 urban villages in Shenzhen and Guangzhou, plus evaluation of state-of-the-art deep learning models on this data to benchmark performance.
AI.result: Current leading models struggle to capture the unique morphological patterns of dense informal settlements, highlighting limitations and the need for specialized approaches.
AI.conclusion: DenseUIS provides a robust benchmark for fine-grained urban mapping in complex, high-density informal environments and is publicly available for researchers via GitHub.

title: GRASP: Gated Regression-Aware Skill Proposer for Self-Improving LLM Agents
summary: LLM agents acting in structured environments fail in operational rather than conversational ways, and reliability depends on procedural knowledge of the environment. Prior self-improvement methods accumulate natural-language guidance without checking that each new item preserves previously correct behavior, so a note that fixes one trajectory can silently regress another. We introduce GRASP (Gated Regression-Aware Skill Proposer), which treats agent improvement as a sequence of edits to a bounded skill library, admitting each candidate only if it produces a net improvement on a balanced held-out probe under a hard regression budget. We evaluate GRASP across five base models (gpt-oss-120b, DeepSeek V4 Flash, Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite, GPT-4.1, GPT-5.4) on two FHIR-based clinical benchmarks. On MedAgentBench, GRASP lifts gpt-oss-120b from 40.6% to 88.8%, exceeds the strongest of five self-improvement baselines by 21.0 points, and improves every other base model by 17.2 to 40.3 points. Ablations attribute the gain to comparative proposal generation, the acceptance gate, and the hard regression budget rather than to skill writing itself, which without validation is no better than using no skills. The mechanism generalizes beyond the clinical domain, improving agents on three of four non-clinical environments and remaining flat only where the action space is open-ended. Frozen libraries transfer across models, where skills from a stronger model improve weaker executors beyond what they learn for themselves while the reverse does not, an asymmetry that no ungated baseline reproduces.

AI.tldr: GRASP improves LLM-based agents by editing a bounded skill library with a regression budget and a balanced held-out probe, yielding large gains on clinical benchmarks and generalizing to several non-clinical tasks; gains stem from proposal generation, an acceptance gate, and regression controls rather than skill-writing alone.
AI.motivation: LLM agents operating in structured environments exhibit operational failures; naive self-improvement via natural-language guidance can cause silent regressions. A mechanism is needed to validate improvements and preserve existing correct behavior across updates.
AI.method: Introduce GRASP: a pipeline that edits a bounded skill library through candidate proposals. Each candidate is accepted only if it yields a net improvement on a balanced held-out probe under a hard regression budget. Frozen libraries enable cross-model transfer. Evaluation spans five base models on two FHIR-based clinical benchmarks (MedAgentBench) and includes three of four non-clinical environments to test generality; ablations identify the key roles of comparative proposal generation, the acceptance gate, and the regression budget.
AI.result: On MedAgentBench, gpt-oss-120b improves from 40.6% to 88.8%; surpasses the strongest self-improvement baseline by 21.0 points; other base models improve by 17.2–40.3 points. Ablations show gains primarily from proposal generation, acceptance gating, and the hard regression budget. Without validation, skill-writing alone is no better than no skills. GRASP generalizes to 3/4 non-clinical environments; performance saturates in open-ended action spaces. Frozen libraries transfer across models, enabling stronger-model skills to boost weaker executors; reverse transfer is not observed; ungated baselines fail to reproduce the gains.
AI.conclusion: GRASP delivers reliable, transferable improvements for LLM agents in structured domains by combining gated, regression-aware edits with controlled evaluation. The approach preserves prior correctness while enabling net improvements, and its benefits extend beyond clinical tasks. Key factors are the acceptance gate and the fixed regression budget; limitations include dependence on hold-out probes and potential scaling challenges in highly open-ended environments.

title: The Good, the Bad, and the Ugly of Markov Boundary for Tabular Prediction
summary: Under standard graphical assumptions, the Markov boundary of a target variable is the smallest set of features that renders every other feature redundant. Once the boundary is observed, the target is conditionally independent of the rest of the table. This is a tempting object for tabular prediction, since it names exactly the columns a model should need. Yet modern regressors are still trained on the full feature set. We ask whether the Markov boundary is genuinely useful for prediction on SCM3K, a 3,450-task synthetic SCM benchmark with feature counts from 40 to 1000 and six SCM families, evaluated with six regressors. The answer is more nuanced than the theory suggests. Restricting a regressor to the oracle boundary often improves prediction substantially, and the improvement grows as the feature space becomes larger and sparser. But the natural pipeline of recovering the boundary with causal discovery and training on the recovered mask does not deliver. Existing estimators exhaust the compute budget before reaching the regime where the boundary helps most, and even where they run they rarely beat the full feature set. We trace this to three causes. Discovery optimizes structural recovery rather than prediction. False negatives and false positives carry sharply asymmetric predictive cost. The exact boundary is only one of many feature sets that beat all features. We then develop what these facts imply for prediction-aligned feature selection and for tabular models that learn to use causal structure.

AI.tldr: The Markov boundary can help prediction, but causal-discovery-based boundary recovery often fails to beat full feature sets; restricting to the boundary helps as features grow, yet boundary discovery is misaligned with predictive goals, and many feature sets beyond the exact boundary can beat all features.
AI.motivation: Assess the practical value of the Markov boundary for tabular prediction on a large, high-dimensional benchmark, and diagnose why boundary-based discovery pipelines underperform.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation on SCM3K (3,450 tasks; 40–1000 features; 6 SCM families; 6 regressors). Compare predictors using only the Markov boundary (oracle boundary) against full feature sets, and analyze causal-discovery pipelines that recover the boundary.
AI.result: Oracle boundary restriction often yields substantial predictive gains, especially as feature dimensionality grows and sparsity increases. However, pipelines that recover the boundary via causal discovery and train on the recovered mask generally do not outperform the full feature set; computation is a bottleneck and predictive advantage is not realized in practice.
AI.conclusion: Prediction-aligned feature selection should consider multiple viable feature sets beyond the exact boundary, and models could benefit from leveraging causal structure without strictly recovering the true boundary. Further work should align discovery objectives with predictive performance and address asymmetric costs of false positives/negatives.

title: How Much Is a Dataset Worth? Scaling Laws, the Vendi Score, and Matrix Spectral Functions
summary: Neural scaling laws appraise data through dataset size, while the Vendi Score uses quantum entropy to measure dataset value. We show both that common neural-scaling-law objectives and the Vendi Score are submodular. We further show that the Vendi Score is a special case of a broader class of submodular objectives that we call matrix spectral functions. This also includes determinantal (DPP) objectives, as well as many others. We also introduce weakly matrix monotone functions and show how they lead to weakly submodular matrix spectral functions, yielding a broad family of practical objectives for data appraisal. We develop secular-equation-based updates that avoid repeated eigendecompositions during greedy optimization, reducing marginal-gain evaluation for $m$-dimensional embeddings by an $O(m)$ factor relative to oracle queries. This yields an average empirical speedup of about 35,000x, making direct optimization of the Vendi Score feasible on ImageNet-1K-scale datasets. Thus enabled, we compare how well several objectives predict the value of training subsets for held-out test performance under fixed-size, class-balanced, and fixed training-budget regimes, including the Vendi Score, DPPs, facility location, and three new matrix spectral variants. Across multiple datasets, facility location performs the best. Direct optimization also reveals that, while the Vendi Score is predictive over moderate score ranges, pushing the objective to higher values can make it a poor downstream performance proxy. We also find that uniformly at random fixed-size subsets, both unconstrained and class-balanced, are remarkably concentrated in both appraisal scores and held-out performance. Finally, we show that size, class balance, and training budget do not alone determine data value: even when controlling for these factors, performance ranges smoothly from good to bad.

AI.tldr: Neural scaling objectives and the Vendi Score are submodular; the Vendi Score is a special case of a broader class called matrix spectral functions, unifying several data-valuation objectives. The paper introduces weakly matrix monotone functions, yielding weakly submodular matrix spectral functions, and develops secular-equation-based updates to dramatically accelerate greedy optimization. Empirically on ImageNet-scale data, they find facility location often performs best; Vendi Score is predictive only within moderate score ranges, and random fixed-size subsets show strong concentration. Data value depends on more than size, balance, or budget.
AI.motivation: To unify and scale data-valuation objectives under a common submodular matrix-spectral framework, enabling principled data appraisal and scalable optimization for large datasets and practical downstream performance assessment.
AI.method: (1) Prove submodularity of common neural-scaling-law objectives and the Vendi Score; (2) define matrix spectral functions and show DPPs as special cases; (3) introduce weakly matrix monotone functions and derive weakly submodular matrix spectral functions; (4) develop secular-equation-based updates to avoid repeated eigendecompositions in greedy optimization; (5) compare several objectives (Vendi Score, DPPs, facility location, and matrix-spectral variants) on ImageNet-1K-scale datasets under fixed-size, class-balanced, and fixed-budget regimes.
AI.result: The Vendi Score is shown to be a special case of matrix spectral functions; a broad framework including DPPs is established; secular-equation updates achieve an average ~35,000× speedup in marginal-gain computation for m-dimensional embeddings; in empirical evaluations, facility location consistently performs best across datasets; the Vendi Score’s predictive power is limited to moderate score ranges; random fixed-size subsets exhibit strong concentration in both appraisal scores and held-out performance; data value is not determined solely by size, class balance, or training budget.
AI.conclusion: Matrix spectral functions provide a unifying and practical family of data-valuation objectives with scalable optimization for large datasets. Facility location emerges as a robust benchmark, while the Vendi Score should be used with caution at high scores. Data value is multifactorial beyond size, balance, and budget.

title: NICE: A Theory-Grounded Diagnostic Benchmark for Social Intelligence of LLMs
summary: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied in social contexts such as emotional companionship and customer service, measuring their social intelligence has become critical to the quality and safety of human-AI interaction. However, existing social intelligence benchmarks lack a unified framework that organizes social abilities into a unified structure, and therefore cannot enable fine-grained diagnosis. To build the first holistic diagnostic evaluation grounded in social theory, we first construct a social intelligence framework through a literature review and multi-stage expert validation guided by psychometric principles. The resulting framework includes 4 categories and 11 dimensions, each further specified by fine-grained capability facets. Building on this framework, we introduce NICE (Norm, Interaction, Cognition, Experience), a diagnostic benchmark of 137 items operationalized through representative Chinese contexts. Across 5 frontier LLMs and a human reference group, models score higher in aggregate accuracy yet show a consistent weakness in Communication, which the framework localizes to 3 specific capability facets: multi-turn communication, nonverbal communication, and synchrony. NICE thus reframes social intelligence evaluation toward theory-grounded diagnosis of socially consequential weaknesses in LLMs.

AI.tldr: Proposes NICE: a theory-grounded diagnostic benchmark for social intelligence in LLMs, with a 4-category, 11-dimension framework (137 items) tested on 5 frontier LLMs and humans; finds consistent weakness in Communication across facets.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of a unified, theory-grounded framework for diagnosing social intelligence in LLMs, enabling fine-grained assessment beyond aggregate accuracy.
AI.method: Literature review + multi-stage expert validation guided by psychometric principles to build a 4-category, 11-dimension social intelligence framework with fine-grained facets; operationalize 137 items in Chinese contexts; evaluate 5 frontier LLMs and a human reference group.
AI.result: The NICE benchmark shows models achieve higher aggregate accuracy but consistently underperform in Communication, localized to three capability facets: multi-turn communication, nonverbal communication, and synchrony.
AI.conclusion: NICE reframes social intelligence evaluation as a theory-grounded diagnostic tool to identify specific socially consequential weaknesses in LLMs and supports more targeted improvements.

title: Mitigating Hallucination in Vision-Language Models through Barrier-Regulated Adaptive Closed-form Steering
summary: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) often hallucinate objects that are not present in the input image, largely because visual grounding weakens as decoding progresses. Existing inference-time mitigation methods modify logits or hidden states throughout generation, but they suffer from three key limitations: they lack an explicit grounding objective, intervene even when the model is already well-grounded, and use fixed correction strengths that do not adapt to the severity of grounding failure. We propose BRACS (Barrier-Regulated Adaptive Closed-form Steering), a training-free steering framework that addresses these issues through barrier-regulated adaptive closed-form steering. BRACS monitors the model's own attention to measure visual grounding and applies corrections to the hidden states only when grounding deteriorates. The corrective update is computed analytically in closed form, requiring no training of auxiliary networks or model retraining. Experiments on LLaVA-1.5-7B and Qwen-VL-Chat show that BRACS consistently outperforms prior methods on hallucination benchmarks, reducing CHAIR$_s$ by 9.4 points and improving POPE F1 by 2.7 points, while matching or improving performance on four general multimodal benchmarks. BRACS also remains efficient, operating at 80% of greedy decoding throughput and achieving 1.3 times higher speed on average than the baselines.

AI.tldr: BRACS is a training-free, barrier-regulated, adaptive closed-form steering method to reduce hallucinations in large vision-language models by monitoring attention-based grounding and applying selective hidden-state corrections only when grounding deteriorates; it improves hallucination metrics with minimal throughput impact.
AI.motivation: LVLMs frequently hallucinate objects not in the image due to diminishing grounding during decoding. Prior inference-time fixes lack explicit grounding objectives, intervene unnecessarily, and use fixed strengths. A responsive, training-free approach that adapts to grounding failure is needed.
AI.method: BRACS meters grounding by analyzing the model's attention signals during generation. When grounding deteriorates, it applies a closed-form, barrier-regulated adjustment to hidden states to steer the model toward better grounding. The update is analytical and does not require training or auxiliary networks.
AI.result: On LLaVA-1.5-7B and Qwen-VL-Chat, BRACS reduces CHAIR_s by 9.4 points and improves POPE F1 by 2.7 points, while maintaining or improving performance on four other multimodal benchmarks. It operates at ~80% of greedy decoding throughput and is about 1.3x faster on average than baselines.
AI.conclusion: BRACS is efficient, training-free, and adaptive, providing effective grounding-aware corrections that reduce hallucinations without retraining, while preserving or enhancing overall multimodal performance.

title: Reliable Reasoning with Large Language Models via Preference-Based Maximum Satisfiability
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) excel at understanding natural language but struggle with optimisation tasks involving multiple constraints and user-defined preferences, which commonly arise in domains such as robotics. We propose a hybrid reasoning approach in which LLMs externalise reasoning through code generation. Given a natural language problem description, an LLM generates Python code that encodes user-defined constraints and preferences as a preference-based Maximum Satisfiability (MaxSAT) problem, which is then solved by an exact MaxSAT solver. To ensure correctness, solutions returned by the model-generated code are independently verified for feasibility and optimality against a canonical MaxSAT encoding, allowing for different encodings and multiple optimal solutions.
