We evaluate our approach using both open-source and closed-access LLMs on three families of preference-based reasoning tasks, and compare it against direct-answer, chain-of-thought, and program-of-thought baselines using the same models. While these baselines rarely produce feasible solutions, the MaxSAT-based pipeline achieves substantially higher acceptance rates, in some cases exceeding 80%. Our results demonstrate that LLM-driven code generation combined with preference-based MaxSAT enables solver-verifiable optimisation with respect to generated encodings, and substantially improves correctness under independently verified reference semantics.

AI.tldr: LLMs, by generating code that encodes user preferences as a MaxSAT problem, coupled with an exact MaxSAT solver and independent verification, achieve higher feasible and optimal solutions for constraint-rich tasks than direct or chain-of-thought baselines.
AI.motivation: LLMs are strong in natural language but struggle with optimisation under multiple, user-defined constraints. A formal, verifiable optimization pipeline can improve solution correctness and reliability, especially in robotics and similar domains where constraints matter.
AI.method: An LLM generates Python code that encodes the problem as a preference-based MaxSAT formulation. An exact MaxSAT solver solves the encoded problem. Solutions are independently verified against a canonical MaxSAT encoding to ensure feasibility and optimality, allowing for different encodings and multiple optima. Evaluation compares MaxSAT-driven code against direct-answer, chain-of-thought, and program-of-thought baselines across several LLMs and task families.
AI.result: The MaxSAT-based pipeline significantly increases feasible/optimal solutions; in some cases acceptance rates exceed 80%, while baselines rarely produce feasible results.
AI.conclusion: LLM-driven code generation for encoding into a formal MaxSAT problem, combined with solver verification, enables solver-verifiable optimisation and substantially improves correctness relative to reference semantics.

title: A Full-Pipeline Framework for Evaluating Membership Inference Attacks in Machine Learning
summary: While Membership Inference Attacks (MIAs) are the prevailing method for identifying training data, their application has expanded into privacy auditing and machine unlearning. Nevertheless, the field lacks a systematic framework for evaluating how different contexts affect MIA efficacy. Without such a characterization, practitioners risk deploying algorithms that perform well on benchmarks but become statistically irrelevant when faced with the nuances of specific, real-world datasets. To bridge this gap and provide actionable insights, we introduce a comprehensive evaluation framework that systematically characterizes privacy risks across the entire machine learning pipeline, spanning data, architectures, algorithms, and post-training modules. Designed to inherently capture diverse operational contexts, our framework rigorously evaluates state-of-the-art MIAs across a broad spectrum of training configurations. To account for varying misclassification costs in real-world deployments, we employ three complementary metrics: Balanced Accuracy for symmetric costs, alongside TPR at low FPR (or TNR at low FNR) for asymmetric scenarios where false alarms or missed detections are strictly penalized. Furthermore, recognizing that existing MIAs assume divergent adversary capabilities, we formalize two standardized threat models and adapt these attacks into corresponding variants to ensure an equitable benchmark. Extensive empirical evaluations demonstrate that the efficacy of specific MIA methodologies is highly sensitive to the assumed threat models and chosen evaluation metrics. Ultimately, we distill these findings into actionable guidelines and provide a ready-to-use auditing toolkit, empowering practitioners to conduct better privacy assessments.

AI.tldr: Proposes a comprehensive, context-aware evaluation framework for Membership Inference Attacks (MIAs) that spans the ML pipeline, introduces two threat models with variants, uses multiple metrics (Balanced Accuracy and TPR at low FPR), and delivers guidelines and an auditing toolkit.
AI.motivation: Current MIA evaluations lack systematic coverage of how privacy risks vary across data, architectures, algorithms, and post-training modules; inadequate for real-world decision-making with asymmetric misclassification costs.
AI.method: Systematic, large-scale evaluation of state-of-the-art MIAs across diverse datasets, architectures, training configurations, and post-training modules; formalizes two standardized threat models with corresponding attack variants; benchmarks using Balanced Accuracy and TPR@low FPR (equivalently TNR@low FNR).
AI.result: Demonstrates that MIA efficacy is highly sensitive to the assumed threat model and the chosen evaluation metrics; provides actionable guidelines and an auditing toolkit for practitioners to assess privacy risk.
AI.conclusion: A robust, extensible framework for realistic privacy risk characterization in MIAs, facilitating better privacy auditing and informed unlearning verification.

title: FHRFormer: A Self-Supervised Masked Transformer Framework for Fetal Heart Rate Time-Series Inpainting and Forecasting
comment: Submitted to Frontiers in Digital Health. arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2509.20852
summary: Approximately 10% of newborns require assistance to initiate breathing at birth, and around 5% need ventilation support. Fetal heart rate (FHR) monitoring plays a crucial role in assessing fetal well-being during prenatal care, enabling the detection of abnormal patterns and supporting timely obstetric interventions to mitigate fetal risks during labor. Applying artificial intelligence (AI) methods to analyze large datasets of continuous FHR monitoring episodes with diverse outcomes may offer novel insights into predicting the risk of needing breathing assistance or interventions. Recent advances in wearable FHR monitors have enabled continuous fetal monitoring without compromising maternal mobility. However, sensor displacement during maternal movement, as well as changes in fetal or maternal position, often lead to signal dropout, resulting in gaps in recorded FHR data. Such missing data limits the extraction of meaningful insights and complicates automated (AI-based) analysis. Traditional approaches to handling missing data, such as simple interpolation techniques, often fail to preserve the spectral characteristics of the signals. In this paper, we propose a masked transformer-based autoencoder approach to reconstruct missing FHR signals by capturing both local temporal and frequency components of the data. The proposed method demonstrates robustness across varying durations of missing data and can be used for signal inpainting and forecasting. The proposed approach can be applied retrospectively to research datasets to support the development of AI-based risk algorithms. In the future, the proposed method could be integrated into wearable FHR monitoring devices to achieve earlier and more robust risk detection.

AI.tldr: Masked transformer autoencoder for FHR signal inpainting to address missing data in wearable monitoring, preserving temporal and spectral features for AI-based risk prediction.
AI.motivation: Fetal well-being monitoring via FHR is prone to data gaps due to movement; preserving spectral characteristics during imputation is essential for reliable AI analyses and risk prediction of breathing assistance or interventions.
AI.method: A masked transformer-based autoencoder designed to reconstruct missing FHR data by capturing local temporal and frequency components, enabling signal inpainting and forecasting.
AI.result: The approach shows robustness across varying lengths of missing data and is suitable for retrospective dataset analysis and future wearable integration for earlier risk detection.
AI.conclusion: Masked transformer autoencoders offer a robust method for FHR data completion in wearable monitoring, supporting AI-driven risk algorithms and real-time detection once deployed.

title: Honest Lying: Understanding Memory Confabulation in Reflexive Agents
comment: Accepted to ICML 2026 Workshop "Failure Modes in Agentic AI"
summary: Reflexion-style agents rely on self-generated reflections as memory, implicitly assuming that agents can accurately diagnose their own failures.We show that this assumption can fail systematically: across ALFWorld and HumanEval, agents store confident but incorrect interpretations of the task and continue acting on them across trials,even though the environment resets to the correct task each time. We call this failure mode memory confabulation and introduce the Reflection Repetition Rate (RRR), a log-based metric that detects repeated reliance on incorrect reflective content.Using RRR, we identify 16 frozen environments in ALFWorld, where 0 of 121 reflections mention the correct target object, and 4 analogous cases in HumanEval. Our mitigation replaces open-ended self-diagnosis with programmatic extraction of trajectory-level failure signals, increasing correct object mention from 0% to 86%, reducing RRR from 0.64 to 0.10, and solving 3 of 16 frozen ALFWorld environments, suggesting that reflective memory can reinforce false beliefs rather than correct them.

AI.tldr: Memory confabulation in reflexion-style agents; introduced Reflection Repetition Rate (RRR) to detect repeated reliance on incorrect reflections; identified frozen tasks where reflections never mention the correct target; mitigation via trajectory-level failure signals improves performance, reducing RRR and increasing correct object mentions.
AI.motivation: Assess reliability of self-generated reflections as memory in reflexion-style agents; quantify and mitigate systematic errors where agents persist incorrect interpretations across task resets.
AI.method: Analyze ALFWorld and HumanEval datasets; define RRR as a log-based metric capturing repeated reliance on incorrect reflective content; identify frozen environments where reflections omit correct targets; implement mitigation by replacing open-ended self-diagnosis with programmatic extraction of trajectory-level failure signals; evaluate before/after on object mention rate, RRR, and task solvability.
AI.result: Found 16 frozen ALFWorld environments with 0/121 reflections mentioning the correct target; 4 analogous cases in HumanEval. After mitigation, correct object mentions rose from 0% to 86%; RRR decreased from 0.64 to 0.10; 3/16 frozen ALFWorld environments were solved.
AI.conclusion: Reflective memory can fortify false beliefs if not grounded in failure signals; using trajectory-level failure signals is an effective mitigation, enabling reflective systems to correct and avoid confabulation.

title: Train the Agent, Not the Expert: Learning to Harness Heterogeneous Experts for Multi-Turn Visual Reasoning
summary: Recent progress in computer vision has produced a wide range of powerful specialized models for detection, segmentation, counting, and other visual tasks. However, these models are usually optimized for isolated task formulations, making it difficult to directly support general-purpose visual intelligence, especially when a task requires complex language understanding and dense small-object perception. In this paper, we propose VisHarness, a trainable visual agent that decouples high-level perception, reasoning, and decision-making from low-level task execution. Instead of training a model to solve a specific visual task, VisHarness learns to harness a set of carefully designed heterogeneous visual experts. This paradigm preserves the general intelligence of the agent while fully leveraging the precision advantages of specialized visual models in concrete visual tasks. With only lightweight training, VisHarness learns a generalizable visual expert-harnessing policy and can solve common fundamental vision tasks under various complex conditions through multi-turn interactions with visual expert models. To enable efficient on-policy reinforcement learning training in a live environment, we introduce dynamic visual memory archiving, which mitigates the rapidly accumulating visual-token overhead caused by multi-turn interactions with visual expert models. Experiments on four representative benchmarks covering reasoning segmentation, generalized referring segmentation, dense small-object detection, and referring counting demonstrate that VisHarness substantially outperforms existing general-purpose models and achieves competitive or superior performance compared with task-specific models.

AI.tldr: VisHarness is a trainable visual agent that decouples high‑level perception, reasoning, and decision-making from low‑level task execution, orchestrating heterogeneous visual experts via a learned policy and memory‑augmented reasoning to enable efficient, multi‑turn interaction across tasks.
AI.motivation: There is a need for general-purpose visual intelligence that can handle complex language understanding and dense small-object perception, beyond isolated, task‑specific models.
AI.method: Proposes VisHarness, a trainable agent that decouples perception/reasoning/decision from execution and learns a generalizable expert‑harnessing policy to orchestrate heterogeneous visual experts. Introduces dynamic visual memory archiving to mitigate token overhead from multi‑turn expert interactions, enabling efficient on‑policy RL in live environments.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on four benchmarks—reasoning segmentation, generalized referring segmentation, dense small‑object detection, and referring counting—show VisHarness substantially outperforms existing general‑purpose models and is competitive or superior to task‑specific models.
AI.conclusion: VisHarness demonstrates that general-purpose visual intelligence can be achieved by learning to orchestrate specialized visual experts with memory‑augmented, multi‑turn reasoning, achieving strong cross-task performance with efficient training.

title: Beyond Trajectory Rewards: Step-level Credit Assignment for Agentic Search via Graph Modeling
summary: In Agentic Search, trajectory-level outcome rewards fail to quantify the behavioral contributions of individual steps, while existing step-level reward methods typically rely on costly tree sampling. We view world knowledge as a latent world graph and each IS task as search within a latent task graph, where effective steps should make graph progress toward the answer node. Based on this prior, we propose Graph-Distance Contribution Reward (GDCR), a step-level process reward that scores newly-retrieved and newly-cited entities by their distance to the answer node in a training-time Entity-Relation (ER) graph. We further propose Step Advantage Policy Optimization (SAPO), which converts GDCR into step-level advantages and combines them with trajectory-level outcome advantages. Experiments on four challenging benchmarks validate the effectiveness of our method.

AI.tldr: GDCR and SAPO provide step-level rewards and advantages for Agentic Search by leveraging a latent ER graph to measure progress toward the answer, improving performance on four benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Trajectory-level rewards fail to attribute credit to individual steps; step-level rewards often require expensive tree sampling. A latent world/task graph perspective motivates step-level credit assignment via graph distance to the answer.
AI.method: Propose Graph-Distance Contribution Reward (GDCR), a step-level process reward scoring newly retrieved/cited entities by distance to the answer node in a training-time ER graph. Propose Step Advantage Policy Optimization (SAPO) to convert GDCR into step-level advantages and combine with trajectory-level outcome advantages.
AI.result: Experimental results on four challenging benchmarks demonstrate the method’s effectiveness, outperforming baselines.
AI.conclusion: GDCR enables fine-grained credit assignment for agentic search by measuring progress in a latent graph, and SAPO effectively integrates step-level and trajectory-level signals for improved decision making.

title: Composing Non-Conjugate Factor Graphs with Closed-Form Variational Inference
summary: Stacking probabilistic building blocks into deeper architectures typically breaks closed-form inference. We show that closed-form inference can be preserved. We identify five factor-graph primitives: a bilinear factor, an exponential link, a Gamma prior, a Gaussian likelihood, and an equality node, and prove that any model composed from them admits closed-form variational message passing. The construction works because each primitive preserves a small set of message families: under mean-field factorization, messages on Gaussian variables remain Gaussian and messages on precision variables remain Gamma, while the only non-conjugate interface, the exponential link, remains tractable through the Gaussian moment-generating function and the sufficient statistics of the Gamma family. We demonstrate composition at increasing depth, from static ensembles through input-dependent gating to split-branch routing, and show that stacking routing layers encodes arbitrary decision trees, establishing universal function approximation with closed-form inference. Applied to ensemble time-series forecasting, the framework yields a Bayesian mixture of experts in which gating functions are inferred rather than learned, providing calibrated uncertainty over expert selection across five benchmark datasets.

AI.tldr: Identifies five factor-graph primitives enabling closed-form variational inference in deep probabilistic architectures, enabling universal function approximation with stacked routing layers, and applying to Bayesian mixture-of-experts for time-series forecasting with inferred gating and calibrated uncertainty.
AI.motivation: To preserve tractable inference when stacking probabilistic building blocks by exploiting conjugacy properties and a tractable non-conjugate interface, enabling deeper models with closed-form updates.
AI.method: Define bilinear factor, exponential link, Gamma prior, Gaussian likelihood, and equality node as primitives; show mean-field messages preserve Gaussian/Gamma families; handle exponential link via Gaussian MGF; demonstrate composition from static ensembles to gating to routing; show universal function approximation; apply to ensemble time-series forecasting as Bayesian mixture of experts with gated routing.
AI.result: Demonstrates closed-form variational message passing for composite models built from the primitives; deeper architectures perform as universal function approximators; gating-inferring Bayesian mixture of experts yields calibrated uncertainty over expert selection across five datasets.
AI.conclusion: Provides a principled, tractable framework for deep probabilistic models with provable inference guarantees and practical uncertainty quantification in forecasting, via five primitive factors and closed-form message passing.

title: CityGen: Structure-Guided City-Style Synthesis for Cross-City Autonomous Driving
summary: Autonomous driving systems are commonly trained and evaluated within limited geographic regions, which hinders their scalability when deployed in new cities. However, significant domain shifts in appearance, road topology, and traffic patterns often cause severe performance degradation under cross-city deployment. Existing approaches based on domain adaptation, data augmentation, or synthetic data generation typically rely on labeled target data, city-specific annotations, or task-specific designs, limiting their scalability and effectiveness for holistic evaluation. In this paper, we introduce CityTransfer-Bench, a geographically disjoint benchmark for evaluating cross-city generalization across perception, segmentation, and planning, and propose CityGen, a diffusion-based generative framework that performs zero-label city adaptation via HD-map-conditioned synthesis guided by city-level visual prompts. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CityGen consistently improves cross-city robustness across multiple tasks, establishing a scalable and label-efficient foundation for generalizable autonomous driving.

AI.tldr: Benchmarks cross-city generalization for autonomous driving and a diffusion-based zero-label city adaptation method.
AI.motivation: Overcome performance drop when deploying autonomous driving to new cities due to domain shifts in appearance, topology, and traffic patterns; existing methods require labeled target data or city-specific designs, hindering scalability.
AI.method: CityTransfer-Bench: a geographically disjoint benchmark evaluating cross-city generalization across perception, segmentation, and planning. CityGen: a diffusion-based generator conditioned on HD-map data and city-level prompts to enable zero-label city adaptation.
AI.result: CityGen improves cross-city robustness across multiple tasks in extensive experiments, offering a scalable, label-efficient foundation for generalizable autonomous driving.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a scalable evaluation protocol and a diffusion-based adaptation framework that enhances cross-city generalization without labeled target data.

title: BitTP: The Lightweight Trajectory Prediction Model with BitLLM for Edge-Devices
comment: Camera-ready version. Accepted as a findings paper at CVPR 2026. 8 pages, 4 figures
summary: Trajectory prediction is a fundamental task for autonomous systems, requiring complex reasoning about multi-agent interactions and intents. Large language models (LLMs) have recently been adopted for this task, as they provide strong contextual reasoning and interpretable, language-based trajectory representations. However, these LLM-based predictors are extremely memory- and compute-intensive, making them difficult to deploy on resource-constrained edge devices such as on-board computers in autonomous robots. To bridge this gap, we propose BitTP, which converts an LLM-based trajectory predictor into a lightweight bitlinear architecture. We demonstrate that weight-only quantization to 1.58-bit (BitTP-Weight) is optimal. Crucially, activations must remain in full precision, as quantizing them leads to severe degradation and instability in spatio-temporal reasoning. Empirically, BitTP-Weight not only preserves but improves prediction quality over the full-precision (BF16) LLM baseline, reducing ADE by 14.29% and FDE by 20.97% on average, while simultaneously reducing memory usage and inference latency relative to other quantization methods. These results demonstrate that carefully designed quantization acts as an effective regularizer, enabling the practical deployment of sophisticated LLM-based reasoning on edge devices. Code is available at: https://github.com/MintCat98/BitTP.

AI.tldr: BitTP introduces weight-only quantization to 1.58-bit for LLM-based trajectory predictors, keeping activations full-precision, yielding better ADE/FDE and lower memory/latency on edge devices; quantization acts as a regularizer; code is released.
AI.motivation: Enable deployment of LLM-based trajectory prediction on resource-constrained edge devices. Standard LLMs are memory- and compute-intensive; weight quantization to 1.58-bit with full-precision activations aims to preserve accuracy while reducing resource usage; acts as a regularizer.
AI.method: Convert the LLM-based trajectory predictor to a lightweight bitlinear architecture (BitTP). Apply weight-only quantization to 1.58-bit (BitTP-Weight) while keeping activations in full precision. Compare against BF16 baseline and other quantization methods on trajectory prediction tasks, reporting ADE/FDE and efficiency.
AI.result: BitTP-Weight preserves and even improves prediction quality over the full-precision BF16 LLM baseline, reducing ADE by 14.29% and FDE by 20.97% on average; also reduces memory usage and inference latency versus other quantization approaches.
AI.conclusion: Carefully designed weight quantization can enable efficient deployment of sophisticated LLM-based reasoning for trajectory prediction on edge devices, with quantization acting as an effective regularizer; BitTP provides a practical pathway with open-source code.

title: Access Sets Matter: Budgeting Expert Reads for Scalable Weight-Space Model Merging
comment: ICML 2026 Workshop on Weight-Space Symmetries: from Foundations to Practical Applications
summary: Weight-space model merging is usually formulated as an algebraic operation on checkpoints, yet at LLM scale the limiting resource is often the set of expert weights that must be read. We introduce MergePipe, a budget-aware execution layer that casts LLM merging as an \emph{expert access-set} problem: given a merge operator and a checkpoint family in a shared weight coordinate system, choose which expert delta blocks to access under an explicit I/O budget. MergePipe indexes parameter blocks, builds deterministic access plans, and executes the induced budgeted merge with replayable manifests. The plan is budget-sound by construction and recovers the full-read merge at full budget; for fixed-coefficient additive operators, the omitted-update error is bounded by the norm of omitted deltas. Across Qwen and Llama merging workloads, MergePipe reduces expert-read I/O by up to an order of magnitude and achieves up to $11\times$ speedups. Representative budget sweeps show $O(10^{-3})$ parameter deviation from full-read merges and no monotonic degradation on downstream benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Budget-aware expert-access layer for LLM model merging; MergePipe minimizes I/O by planning which expert delta blocks to read under a budget, yielding up to 11x speedups with bounded error.
AI.motivation: LLM-scale model merging is dominated by reading expert weights; a budgeted I/O approach can substantially speed up merges without sacrificing reproducibility.
AI.method: Index parameter blocks; build deterministic, budgeted access plans; execute budgeted merges with replayable manifests. For fixed-coefficient additive operators, omit-update error is bounded by norm of omitted deltas; full-read merge recovered at full budget.
AI.result: Across Qwen and Llama merging workloads, reduces expert-read I/O by up to an order of magnitude and achieves up to 11× speedups. Budget sweeps show parameter deviation on the order of 10^-3 with no monotonic degradation on downstream benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: MergePipe provides a budget-sound execution layer for scalable LLM merging, delivering reproducible results with bounded error and significant I/O and time savings across representative workloads.

title: Mesh-Aware Epipolar Matching for Multi-View Multi-Person 3D Pose Estimation in Basketball
summary: Multi-view multi-person 3D pose estimation in team sports scenarios remains challenging due to player occlusions, appearance similarity caused by team uniforms, and the scarcity of annotated multi-view data, all of which limit the effectiveness and generalization capability of learning-based methods. In contrast, the performance of training-free approaches is inherently constrained by the accuracy of 2D keypoint detection and the robustness of cross-view association. To address these challenges, we propose Mesh-Aware Epipolar Matching (MAEM), a training-free framework for multi-view multi-person 3D pose estimation. Our method employs a monocular 3D human mesh recovery model as the frontend and introduces a two-stage epipolar matching strategy based on the recovered mesh outputs. Specifically, the proposed framework combines disjoint-set-union-based clustering with per-joint triangulation to achieve robust cross-view association and accurate 3D pose reconstruction. Experiments on two public multi-view basketball datasets demonstrate that MAEM consistently outperforms existing training-free association baselines while achieving competitive RGB-only performance in both indoor and outdoor basketball scenarios. MAEM achieves MPJPE/PA-MPJPE scores of 59.8/40.7 mm on SportCenter EPFL and 74.0/51.8 mm on Human-M3 Basketball, highlighting the effectiveness of dense mesh geometry for cross-view association without requiring target-domain training or fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: MAEM is a training-free framework for multi-view multi-person 3D pose estimation in sports, using monocular mesh recovery and a two-stage epipolar matching with DSU clustering and per-joint triangulation. It outperforms training-free baselines and yields competitive RGB-only results on two basketball datasets.
AI.motivation: To overcome challenges in team sports 3D pose estimation: heavy occlusions, appearance similarity from uniforms, and limited annotated multi-view data. Training-free methods are limited by 2D keypoint accuracy and cross-view association robustness, so a training-free approach leveraging dense mesh geometry could improve cross-view pose reconstruction.
AI.method: A monocular 3D human mesh recovery frontend is combined with a two-stage epipolar matching strategy. It uses DSU-based clustering for cross-view association and per-joint triangulation for 3D pose reconstruction, operating entirely without target-domain training.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on SportCenter EPFL and Human-M3 Basketball shows MAEM surpasses existing training-free association baselines and achieves competitive RGB-only performance in indoor and outdoor basketball. Reported MPJPE/PA-MPJPE: 59.8/40.7 mm (SportCenter EPFL) and 74.0/51.8 mm (Human-M3 Basketball).
AI.conclusion: Dense mesh geometry enables robust cross-view association and accurate 3D pose estimation without domain-specific training, validating the effectiveness of MAEM for multi-view basketball scenarios.

title: NaRA: Noise-Aware LoRA for Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning of Diffusion LLMs
summary: Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) have emerged as a promising non-autoregressive generative paradigm. Given the prohibitive computational cost of full fine-tuning, Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) has become the standard approach. However, existing PEFT methods (e.g., LoRA), originally tailored for autoregressive models, rely on static parameters that are agnostic to the noise level. Consequently, they ignore the intrinsic dynamics of the diffusion process, where input distributions and generation difficulty shift significantly along the denoising trajectory, rendering them suboptimal for dLLMs. To address this, we propose Noise-aware Low-Rank Adaptation (NaRA), which introduces a low-rank core matrix generated by a lightweight, globally shared hypernetwork conditioned on the noise level. This design enables the update matrices to vary continuously along the diffusion process while keeping parameter and latency overhead negligible. We provide a theoretical justification for the proposed NaRA framework and empirically demonstrate consistent improvements over noise-agnostic baselines across commonsense reasoning, mathematical reasoning, and code generation benchmarks. Our code is available at https://github.com/generaldi/NaRA.

AI.tldr: NaRA introduces a diffusion-aware, low-rank adapter for diffusion LLMs, generated by a lightweight hypernetwork conditioned on the noise level to create a dynamic update core. It achieves improvements with negligible overhead, validated on reasoning and code-generation tasks.
AI.motivation: Current PEFT methods (e.g., LoRA) are static and noise-agnostic, failing to capture the evolving noise and difficulty along the diffusion denoising trajectory in dLLMs.
AI.method: Propose Noise-aware Low-Rank Adaptation (NaRA): a low-rank core matrix produced by a lightweight, globally shared hypernetwork conditioned on the diffusion noise level. This enables update matrices to vary continuously along the diffusion process with minimal parameter/latency overhead. The paper provides theoretical justification and empirical validation across tasks.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over noise-agnostic baselines on commonsense reasoning, mathematical reasoning, and code generation benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: NaRA offers a principled, efficient way to integrate diffusion dynamics into PEFT for dLLMs, with theoretical support and public code availability.

title: Gradient Perturbation: Learning to Perturb Gradients for Adaptive Training
summary: Deep neural network training involves both forward propagation (from features through logits to loss) and backward propagation (from loss through gradients to parameter updates). While perturbations along the forward chain, including feature perturbation, logit perturbation, and label perturbation, have been extensively studied, the backward chain's gradient perturbation has received little systematic investigation. In this paper, we establish a unified framework for gradient perturbation, revealing that existing methods such as Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM), gradient clipping, and gradient noise injection can all be interpreted as imposing specific forms of gradient perturbation. Analogous to the recently proposed Logit Perturbation Learning (LPL), we conjecture that amplifying the gradient norm for a class acts as positive augmentation (enhancing learning), while dampening it acts as negative augmentation (suppressing overfitting). Based on these observations, we propose Learning to Perturb Gradients (LPG), which adaptively perturbs logit-level gradients at the class level to achieve category-aware training. We also establish theoretical connections between gradient perturbation bounds and generalization guarantees via PAC-Bayesian analysis. Experiments on balanced classification, long-tail classification, and noisy label learning demonstrate that LPG consistently outperforms existing methods and can be combined with them as a plug-in module.

AI.tldr: Proposes Learning to Perturb Gradients (LPG), a gradient-perturbation framework that views backward-pass perturbations as a general mechanism, unifying SAM, clipping, and noise injection; introduces class-level gradient perturbations (category-aware) and links to PAC-Bayesian generalization bounds; shows LPG improves performance across balanced, long-tail, and noisy-label tasks and can be used as a plug-in.
AI.motivation: Backward gradient perturbations have been underexplored; a unified, theoretically grounded framework is needed to understand and improve generalization via gradient perturbations; bridging to Logit Perturbation Learning (LPL).
AI.method: Define a gradient-perturbation operator at the class (logit) level; propose LPG to adaptively perturb per-class gradients during training to encourage category-aware learning; derive connections to PAC-Bayes bounds to link perturbation strength with generalization; show that existing methods (Sharpness-Aware Minimization, gradient clipping, gradient noise injection) are special cases of gradient perturbation; provide algorithmic implementation and compatibility with other methods.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on balanced classification, long-tail distribution, and noisy-label scenarios demonstrates LPG consistently improves performance over baselines and can be integrated as a plug-in with existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: Gradient perturbation on the backward chain is a unifying, effective tool for regularization and generalization; LPG provides a practical, theory-grounded approach; future work may optimize efficiency, extend to other modalities, and explore broader theoretical guarantees.

title: SwInception -- Local Attention Meets Convolutions
comment: International Conference on Pattern Recognition and Artificial Intelligence, 2024
summary: Sparse vision transformers have gained popularity as efficient encoders for medical volumetric segmentation, with Swin emerging as a prominent choice. Swin uses local attention to reduce complexity and yields excellent performance for many tasks but still tends to overfit on small datasets. To mitigate this weakness, we propose a novel architecture that further enhances Swin's inductive bias by introducing Inception blocks in the feed-forward layers. The introduction of these multi-branch convolutions enables more direct reasoning over local, multi-scale features within the transformer block. We have also modified the decoder layers in order to capture finer details using fewer parameters. We demonstrate a performance improvement on eleven different medical datasets through extensive experimentation. We specifically showcase advancements over the previous state-of-the-art backbones on benchmark challenges like the Medical Segmentation Decathlon and Beyond the Cranial Vault. By showing that the existing inductive bias in Swin can be further improved, our work presents a promising avenue for enhancing the capabilities of sparse vision transformers for both medical and natural image segmentation tasks. Code and pre-trained weights can be accessed at https://github.com/Eiphodos/SwInception.

AI.tldr: Enhances Swin-based sparse vision transformers for medical volumetric segmentation by incorporating Inception-like multi-branch feed-forward blocks and an optimized decoder, achieving state-of-the-art results on 11 medical datasets including Medical Segmentation Decathlon and Beyond the Cranial Vault.
AI.motivation: Small medical datasets cause overfitting in Swin's local-attention transformer. Strengthening inductive bias and enabling multi-scale local reasoning aims to improve segmentation performance while maintaining efficiency.
AI.method: Integrate Inception blocks (multi-branch convolutions) into the feed-forward layers of Swin Transformer blocks to enhance local multi-scale feature reasoning. Modify decoder to better capture fine details with fewer parameters. Evaluate on 11 medical segmentation datasets and benchmark against MSD and BCV; release code and pretrained weights.
AI.result: Reported performance gains across 11 datasets, with advances over prior state-of-the-art backbones on MSD and BCV benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that augmenting Swin with multi-branch convolutions can further strengthen inductive bias and improve performance for medical and potentially natural image segmentation, while maintaining efficiency.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Transfer Learning for Cross-Building Energy Forecasting: Toward Robust and Scalable District-Level Energy Management
comment: 5 pages, 3 figures, 2 tables. Accepted at BALANCES'26 (6th ACM International Workshop on Big Data and Machine Learning for Smart Buildings and Cities), Banff, Alberta, Canada, June 22, 2026. This is the author's accepted manuscript; final published version DOI will be activated after June 22, 2026
summary: Scaling data-driven energy forecasting to district level requires models that can be re-used across buildings with minimal target-domain data and honest uncertainty estimates. We present an uncertainty-aware transfer learning (TL) framework for cross-building energy forecasting based on the Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT), evaluated on a newly released high-resolution real sub-meter dataset: an educational building at Aalborg University, Denmark (source) and the multi-typology NEST building at EMPA, Switzerland (target). We introduce the Transfer Robustness Index (TRI), an architecture-agnostic metric for quantifying generalization quality across domain gaps. A four-strategy layer-freezing ablation shows that Probe-Only fine-tuning, updating only 455 output-layer parameters out of 806K, achieves the best transfer quality (TRI = 3,097), outperforming full fine-tuning and suggesting that TFT encoders learn transferable temporal representations. Monte Carlo Dropout yields a prediction interval coverage probability of 93.2%, close to the nominal 95% target. A data-scarcity analysis further shows monotonic improvement with increasing target-domain data, providing practical guidance for district energy deployment.

AI.tldr: Proposes an uncertainty-aware transfer-learning framework for cross-building energy forecasting using TFT, introducing the Transfer Robustness Index (TRI). Shows that probe-only fine-tuning (updating 455 of 806k params) yields best transfer quality; MC dropout achieves near-nominal PI coverage; data-scarcity analysis guides district-energy deployment.
AI.motivation: Need scalable, uncertainty-aware forecasting models that generalize across buildings with minimal target-domain data.
AI.method: Uncertainty-aware TL based on Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT). Introduces TRI to quantify cross-domain generalization. Conducts a four-strategy layer-freezing ablation (including Probe-Only fine-tuning). Uses Monte Carlo Dropout for predictive intervals. Evaluated on source: Aalborg University sub-meter dataset; target: NEST building at EMPA.
AI.result: Probe-Only fine-tuning yields the best transfer performance (TRI = 3,097), outperforming full fine-tuning. Monte Carlo Dropout achieves PI coverage of 93.2% (near 95%). Data-scarcity analysis shows monotonic improvement with more target-domain data.
AI.conclusion: TFT encoders learn transferable temporal representations enabling effective cross-building forecasting with minimal target data. TRI provides a metric for cross-domain generalization. The findings offer practical guidance for deploying district-energy forecasting with proper uncertainty quantification.

title: On-Policy Replay for Continual Supervised Fine-Tuning
summary: Continual supervised fine-tuning (SFT) is the de facto recipe for adapting large language models (LLMs) to a stream of downstream tasks, but it suffers from catastrophic forgetting of earlier capabilities. Recent work shows that on-policy signals -- training on the model's own outputs -- reduce forgetting more reliably than off-policy supervision. Existing on-policy methods route this signal through a new training objective (e.g., self-distillation losses with a teacher copy), inheriting an extra forward pass, schedule sensitivity, and stylistic drift from the teacher.We instead route the on-policy signal through the training data source. Our method, On-Policy Replay (OPR), rolls out the most recent checkpoint on a small budget of historical prompts, filters the generations by a task reward, and replays the surviving (prompt, model response) pairs as ordinary SFT examples. There is no teacher, no auxiliary loss, and no on-the-fly distillation. Across three 7--8B instruction-tuned backbones (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, Qwen3-8B, Llama3.1-8B-Instruct) on the TRACE continual-learning benchmark, OPR consistently reduces forgetting; on the sharpest stress test (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, Sequential SFT BWT -13.93), OPR lifts BWT to -0.65 at a 10% replay budget and to -2.29 at a 1% budget -- a 46% reduction in |BWT| over a tuned Vanilla Replay baseline, with 42--46% reductions observed across all three backbones. We give a KL-shrinkage interpretation that places OPR and prior on-policy distillation methods on a single axis, and we present a counterintuitive finding that explains why Vanilla Replay is already a strong baseline: low-score replay is uniformly worse than Vanilla Replay, demonstrating that the active ingredient in OPR is the on-policy distribution, not the response quality alone.Our code is available at https://github.com/Yancey2024/OnPolicyReplay.

AI.tldr: OPR uses on-policy replay of recent-checkpoint generations on historical prompts with a task-reward filter to create standard SFT data, avoiding teachers and extra losses; it reduces catastrophic forgetting across 7–8B models on TRACE with substantial BWT gains and consistent improvements, showing the on-policy distribution, not response quality, drives gains; code released.
AI.motivation: Alleviate catastrophic forgetting in continual SFT for LLMs; on-policy signals help but existing methods rely on teacher-based distillation with drawbacks; seek a simpler, data-driven on-policy method.
AI.method: Roll out the latest checkpoint on a small budget of historical prompts; generate responses; filter by a task reward; replay surviving (prompt, model response) pairs as standard SFT data; no teacher or auxiliary loss; thus no on-the-fly distillation.
AI.result: OPR reduces forgetting (BWT) across three backbones on TRACE; e.g., from -13.93 to -0.65 at 10% budget, to -2.29 at 1% budget; 46% reduction in |BWT| vs tuned Vanilla Replay; 42–46% reductions across backbones; KL-shrinkage interpretation; active ingredient is on-policy distribution; Vanilla Replay strong baseline.
AI.conclusion: OPR presents a simple, effective alternative to on-policy distillation for continual SFT, highlighting the importance of on-policy data distribution; supports broader applicability; code released.

title: EVL-ECG: Efficient ECG Interpretation With Multi-Aspect Heterogeneous Knowledge Distillation
comment: Accepted at the SD4H Workshop at ICML 2026. 11 pages, 3 figures
summary: High-fidelity ECG interpretation is increasingly reliant on massive foundation models, yet their deployment in clinical edge-care remains hindered by extreme computational demands. While knowledge distillation (KD) is a promising solution, traditional methods fail to capture the complex spatio-temporal dependencies of ECG signals when transferring knowledge across heterogeneous architectures. In this paper, we propose EVL-ECG, a framework specifically designed for cross-architecture distillation of cardiac diagnostic logic. EVL-ECG introduces three ECG-aware innovations: (1) Multi-Head Cross-Attention Alignment, which harmonizes architectural discrepancies to preserve fine-grained morphological features; (2) Optimal Transport-based Visual Feature Matching, utilizing optimal transport to maintain global structural relationships across ECG leads despite mismatched token representations; and (3) Geometric Intra-Architecture Relation Matching, which distills the latent diagnostic reasoning of the teacher model. Evaluations across ECG benchmarks demonstrate that EVL-ECG yields improvements of up to 2.4% AUC and 1.1% clinical accuracy over existing baselines. Notably, EVL-ECG establishes an efficient 2B-parameter ECG foundation model, suitable for resource-constrained clinical environments.

AI.tldr: EVL-ECG introduces three ECG-aware innovations for cross-architecture distillation: Multi-Head Cross-Attention Alignment, Optimal Transport-based Visual Feature Matching, and Geometric Intra-Architecture Relation Matching. It yields performance gains on ECG benchmarks and enables a compact 2B-parameter foundation model suitable for edge deployment.
AI.motivation: High-fidelity ECG models are computation-intensive, hindering edge deployment. Traditional knowledge distillation struggles to capture the complex spatio-temporal dependencies of ECG signals when transferring knowledge between heterogeneous architectures. There is a need for architecture-aware distillation methods that preserve morphological features and global lead relationships.
AI.method: 1) Multi-Head Cross-Attention Alignment to harmonize architectural discrepancies and preserve fine-grained ECG morphologies; 2) Optimal Transport-based Visual Feature Matching to maintain global structural relationships across ECG leads despite token representation mismatches; 3) Geometric Intra-Architecture Relation Matching to distill the teacher's latent diagnostic reasoning. The approach uses a teacher–student KD framework with these alignment losses to transfer diagnostic capability across architectures.
AI.result: On ECG benchmarks, EVL-ECG provides improvements up to 2.4% in AUC and 1.1% in clinical accuracy over baselines, and demonstrates an efficient 2B-parameter ECG foundation model suitable for resource-constrained clinical environments.
AI.conclusion: EVL-ECG shows that combining cross-attention alignment, optimal transport-based feature matching, and geometric relation distillation can effectively transfer ECG diagnostic logic across architectures, enabling compact yet capable foundation models for edge ECG interpretation.

title: Citation-Closure Retrieval and Per-Rule Attribution for Real-World Regulatory Compliance Question Answering
summary: Deploying Large Language Models (LLMs) for regulatory compliance demands rigorous traceability via comprehensive citations across multi-tiered authority structures. Unlike traditional multi-hop or legal QA, this task requires structured procedural lookups and evidence-set closure rather than entity resolution or case-law reasoning. Existing RAG systems struggle here due to flattened citation edges, fragmented retrieval expansions, and fragile post-hoc attribution. We formalize Regulatory Compliance QA with RegOps-Bench, a novel benchmark featuring an Operational Knowledge Graph derived from complex national R\&D regulations. To address these bottlenecks, we propose RefWalk, a unified framework driven by a shared topic anchor. RefWalk traverses cross-document citations, fuses multi-view candidates via max-based aggregation, and enforces per-rule attribution to explicitly map claims to sources. We establish a strong baseline with substantial improvements in retrieval recall and citation accuracy. Finally, a contrastive evaluation on a U.S. health compliance dataset (HIPAA) reveals that existing systems exhibit saturation on flat-structure rules, underscoring the need for RegOps-Bench. Our code is available at https://github.com/yeongjoonJu/RefWalk.

AI.tldr: Introduces RefWalk for Regulatory Compliance QA using an Operational Knowledge Graph (RegOps-Bench); achieves stronger retrieval recall and citation attribution by traversing cross-document citations with max-based candidate fusion and per-rule attribution, highlighting limitations of flat-structure baselines via HIPAA contrastive evaluation.
AI.motivation: Regulatory compliance QA requires traceable, source-backed evidence across multi-tier authority structures; traditional RAG and multi-hop QA falter due to flattened citation graphs, fragmented retrieval, and fragile attribution.
AI.method: RefWalk framework with a shared topic anchor that traverses cross-document citations, fuses multi-view candidates via max-based aggregation, and enforces per-rule attribution to map claims to sources; RegOps-Bench provides an Operational Knowledge Graph derived from complex national R&D regulations; strong baseline demonstrates improvements in retrieval recall and citation accuracy.
AI.result: Substantial improvements in retrieval recall and citation accuracy; RegOps-Bench exposes needs beyond flat-rule structures; HIPAA-based contrastive evaluation shows saturation of flat-structure rules in existing systems; code available at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: RegOps-Bench and RefWalk effectively address bottlenecks in regulatory QA by structured citation traversal and explicit attribution; establishes a new benchmark and baseline for regulatory compliance research.

title: Convex Basins in Single-Index Model Loss Landscapes: Applications to Robust Recovery under Strong Adversarial Corruption
summary: We study the problem of robustly learning Gaussian Single Index Models (SIMs) in the presence of heavy-tailed noise and a constant fraction of adversarially corrupted covariates and responses. Prior work on robust recovery has considered settings such as linear regression (Pensia et al., JASA 2024), strictly monotonic link functions (Awasthi et al., NeurIPS 2022), and phase retrieval (Buna and Rebeschini, AISTATS 2025). However, these techniques do not extend to generic asymmetric non-monotonic link functions such as \textsc{GeLU} and \textsc{Swish}, which arise naturally as scalar primitives in modern gated neural architectures. We close this gap by giving the first robust recovery algorithm with near-linear sample and time complexity for generic non-monotonic link functions, thereby establishing the first robust recovery guarantees for a broad family of nonlinear SIMs for which \textit{no guarantees were previously known}. Our central contribution is a new structural understanding of the Gaussian squared-loss landscape under adversarial contamination. Crucially, we prove that for a broad class of nonlinear non-monotonic SIMs, a dimension-independent, constant-radius convex basin exists around the ground truth and is efficiently reachable via robust spectral initialization even under adversarial contamination. Prior works fail to establish both guarantees simultaneously, thereby either breaking down under adversarial contamination or failing to handle generic non-monotonic link functions. Together, these structural insights yield a principled warm start for robust gradient descent that provably converges to a final estimation error of $O(σ\sqrtε)$ in $\tilde{O}(nd)$ time with $\tilde{O}(d)$ samples, where $ε$ is the contamination fraction.

AI.tldr: Robust learning of Gaussian SIMs with non-monotonic link functions under heavy-tailed noise and adversarial corruption; near-linear sample/time complexity; robust spectral initialization; convergence to O(σ√ε) error; first guarantees for GeLU/Swish-like links.
AI.motivation: Address gap where existing robust recovery results cover linear, monotone, or phase retrieval scenarios, but not generic non-monotonic links in SIMs; aim to handle GeLU/Swish and similar primitives in gated NN architectures.
AI.method: Analyze Gaussian squared-loss landscape under contamination; establish a constant-radius convex basin around ground truth that is dimension-independent; use robust spectral initialization to reach basin; apply robust gradient descent; achieve near-linear sample/time; derive error bound.
AI.result: Algorithm achieving final estimation error O(σ√ε) in ~O(nd) time with ~O(d) samples for contamination fraction ε; applies to broad class of nonlinear non-monotonic SIMs; first robust guarantees for such models.
AI.conclusion: Introduces structural insights enabling robust learning of nonlinear SIMs with non-monotonic links; closes gap and provides efficient, provable recovery guarantees; lays groundwork for robust learning in non-monotonic nonlinear models.

title: Genetically Aligned Patient Representations Improve Hematological Diagnosis
comment: Accepted for publication at the 29th International Conference on Medical Image Computing and Computer Assisted Intervention - MICCAI 2026
summary: Multimodal alignment of histopathology encoders with transcriptomic and genomic data has been shown to significantly improve performance in downstream diagnostic tasks. Hematological cytology is unique in that visual single-cell evaluation is often paired with cytogenetics and molecular genetics for blood cancer diagnosis. In this study, we present a framework to align single white blood cell images with chromosomal aberrations (karyotype) and somatic mutations from targeted gene panels. Our training strategy follows a two-stage approach: (i) self-supervised, vision-only pretraining of a transformer aggregator using an iBOT head on a cohort of over 1500 patients, and (ii) genetic alignment via supervised contrastive loss on acute myeloid leukemia patients. Our genetically aligned patient encoder improves hematological diagnostic tasks, outperforming slide-level histopathology foundation models. Additionally, the model provides off-the-shelf retrieval capabilities for diseases and genetic alterations. Incorporating genetic data into patient encoders increases the quality of patient representations, providing a framework that aligns with clinical diagnostic workflows and paves the way for future multimodal hematology-specific AI. The code and model weights are available at https://github.com/marrlab/GenBloom.

AI.tldr: Two-stage learning framework aligns histopathology images with genetic data (karyotype and targeted mutations) to improve hematology diagnostics and enable retrieval of diseases and genetic alterations; code released.
AI.motivation: Leverage multimodal data to improve diagnostic performance in hematology by combining image-based cytology with genomic/cytogenetic information, addressing a gap where imaging alone may miss genetic alterations.
AI.method: Stage 1: Self-supervised vision-only pretraining of a transformer aggregator using an iBOT head on >1500 patients to learn robust image representations. Stage 2: Genetic alignment via supervised contrastive loss using AML patients to align image representations with karyotype and targeted gene-panel mutations, producing a genetically aligned patient encoder.
AI.result: The genetically aligned encoder improves hematological diagnostic tasks relative to slide-level histopathology foundation models and enables off-the-shelf retrieval for diseases and genetic alterations.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating genetic data into patient encoders enhances representation quality and clinical relevance, aligning with diagnostic workflows and enabling future multimodal hematology AI; code and weights are available at the provided GitHub repository.

title: Why Specialist Models Still Matter: A Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Paradigm for Medical Artificial Intelligence
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026. 12 pages main text, 16 pages appendix
summary: The impressive performance of generalist large language models (LLMs) such as GPT and Claude in healthcare raises a critical question: will domain-specific medical specialist models become obsolete? We argue that the future of medical artificial intelligence (AI) lies not in building monolithic medical foundation models, nor in replacing human expertise, but in orchestrating collaboration among generalist LLMs, domain-specific specialist models, and clinicians. We propose HetMedAgent, a heterogeneous medical multi-agent framework that enables conflict-aware evidence fusion, uncertainty-based clinician intervention triggering, and adaptive threshold calibration. Experiments on three real-world clinical decision-making tasks demonstrate that the synergy between generalist LLMs and domain-specific specialist models significantly outperforms using either type of model alone, validating the irreplaceable value of specialist models in modality-specific analysis. HetMedAgent represents a shift from building medical LLMs or foundation models to multi-agent collaboration, achieving a balance between general reasoning capabilities and domain-specific precision.

AI.tldr: Generalist LLMs plus domain-specific medical specialist models in a heterogeneous multi-agent framework (HetMedAgent) outperform either type alone; the system enables conflict-aware evidence fusion, uncertainty-based clinician intervention, and adaptive thresholding, signaling a shift from medical LLMs to multi-agent collaboration.
AI.motivation: Large generalist LLMs show strong performance in healthcare, but domain-specific precision and modality-specific analysis by specialist models remain valuable. The work argues for orchestrating collaborations among generalist LLMs, specialists, and clinicians to balance reasoning and precision.
AI.method: Proposes HetMedAgent, a heterogeneous medical multi-agent framework that supports conflict-aware evidence fusion, uncertainty-based clinician intervention triggering, and adaptive threshold calibration. Empirical evaluation on three real-world clinical decision-making tasks to assess the synergy between generalist LLMs and domain-specific specialists compared to using either alone.
AI.result: The combination (synergy) of generalist LLMs and domain-specific specialist models significantly outperforms using either type of model alone, confirming the irreplaceable value of specialist models in modality-specific analysis.
AI.conclusion: Shifts the focus from building monolithic medical LLMs/foundation models to multi-agent collaboration. HetMedAgent demonstrates that orchestrating general reasoning with domain-specific precision achieves better performance and practical balance in medical AI.

title: Quotient DAGs for Off-Policy Evaluation:Forward-Flow Importance Sampling and Exact Slate Propensities
summary: Off-policy evaluation estimates how a target policy would perform using data collected by a different behavior policy, which is crucial when online testing is costly or risky, such as in recommendation or healthcare. Standard importance sampling reweights each logged trajectory, but it can treat details of the generation process as meaningful even when the evaluation target ignores them: for example, an autoregressive slate recommender may generate an ordered sequence of items while the reward and downstream estimator depend only on the unordered slate. This creates nuisance variance and a computational gap, since exact unordered slate propensities require summing over all generation orders. We introduce a quotient-DAG view that merges histories equivalent for evaluation and assigns weights using target-to-behavior forward-flow ratios on the merged graph. For slate recommendation under a set-sufficient next-item interface, this yields Forward-DP, a subset-DAG dynamic program that computes exact unordered propensities without factorial enumeration. The resulting propensity primitive enables practical propensity-based evaluation and model selection for context-dependent autoregressive slate loggers.

AI.tldr: Introduces a quotient-DAG framework to compute exact unordered propensities for off-policy evaluation in autoregressive slate loggers, avoiding factorial enumeration via Forward-DP on a merged histories graph; enabling practical propensity-based evaluation and model selection.
AI.motivation: Off-policy evaluation (OPE) is essential when online testing is costly or risky. In autoregressive slate settings, evaluation targets depend only on unordered slates, while generation histories depend on order, causing nuisance variance and a computational gap due to summing over all generation orders.
AI.method: Define a quotient-DAG by merging histories equivalent for evaluation, assign forward-flow ratios from target to behavior on the merged graph; for slate recommendation with a set-sufficient next-item interface, derive Forward-DP, a subset-DAG dynamic program that computes exact unordered propensities without factorial enumeration.
AI.result: Provides a practical propensity primitive enabling exact OPE and model selection for context-dependent autoregressive slate loggers.
AI.conclusion: The framework reduces nuisance variance and computational cost by focusing on evaluation-relevant histories and exact unordered propensities, making propensity-based OPE feasible for complex slate generation models.

title: FRUC: Feedforward Dynamic Scene Reconstruction from Uncalibrated Collaborative Driving Views
summary: We present FRUC, a feed-forward 3D Gaussian splatting framework for dynamic scene reconstruction from uncalibrated collaborative driving views. Existing multi-agent reconstruction frameworks are often hindered by rigid prerequisites, demanding precise spatial calibration and slow per-scene optimization. In this paper, we rethink this task by conceptualizing a distributed multi-vehicle network as a spatio-temporally unstructured ego-centric multi-camera system, where the core challenge lies in enhancing ego-centric occluded geometry through collaboration without degrading the ego's accurately observed visible geometry, while preserving reconstruction efficiency. For efficient reconstruction, FRUC is built upon a visual grounded geometric Transformer backbone to enable one-shot, calibration-free inference from a flexible number of multi-vehicle views. To achieve non-destructive geometric supplementation under uncalibrated cross-agent misalignment, FRUC first introduces an ego-centric causal occlusion field that explicitly derives occlusion evolution as latent priors by modeling agent-wise spatio-temporal correlations. Guided by these occlusion priors, it further formulates cross-agent integration as a deterministic residual denoising process via zero-initialized injection, turning challenging cross-agent fusion into bounded residual learning for robust collaborative blind-spot completion. Through extensive evaluations on the real-world V2XReal and UrbanIng-V2X datasets, FRUC is shown to be a new state-of-the-art for the scene reconstruction of dynamic collaborative driving environments, significantly outperforming existing methods in both rendering quality and efficiency.

AI.tldr: Calibration-free, ego-centric multi-vehicle 3D reconstruction via a Transformer-based framework with a causal occlusion field and residual cross-agent fusion, achieving state-of-the-art results on V2X datasets.
AI.motivation: Overcome reliance on precise cross-view calibration and slow per-scene optimization in multi-agent dynamic scene reconstruction by enabling calibration-free, scalable collaboration among uncalibrated ego-centric cameras.
AI.method: FRUC uses a visual-grounded geometric Transformer backbone for one-shot calibration-free inference from variable numbers of views; introduces an ego-centric causal occlusion field to model occlusion evolution; performs cross-agent integration as a deterministic residual denoising with zero-initialized injection to robustly complete occluded geometry.
AI.result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art scene reconstruction quality and efficiency on real-world V2XReal and UrbanIng-V2X datasets, outperforming existing methods in rendering quality and speed.
AI.conclusion: FRUC provides robust, non-destructive enhancement of ego-visible geometry through collaboration with uncalibrated agents, enabling efficient, blur-free dynamic scene reconstruction in multi-vehicle networks.

title: Benchmarking Positional Encoding Strategies for Transformer-Based EEG Foundation Models
summary: Electroencephalography (EEG) is a widely used non-invasive technique for measuring brain activity in brain-computer interface (BCI) applications. Supervised EEG decoding models often struggle to generalize across tasks, subjects, and datasets, motivating transformer-based EEG foundation models trained with self-supervised learning. Since transformers are permutation-invariant, they require explicit positional information. Unlike textual tokens, EEG electrodes are spatially distributed across the scalp, raising the question of how electrode positions should be encoded in transformer-based EEG models. In this study, we benchmark five positional encoding strategies within the CBraMod backbone and evaluate them under linear probing and fine-tuning protocols on motor imagery classification and emotion recognition. Our results show that no single strategy consistently outperforms across tasks. Spherical Positional Encoding (SPE) yields strong representations for motor imagery but underperforms on emotion recognition, while Asymmetric Conditional Positional Encoding (ACPE) demonstrates more consistent performance across tasks. These findings suggest that the optimal positional encoding strategy is task-dependent, with no universal solution across EEG decoding scenarios.

AI.tldr: No universal positional encoding works across EEG decoding tasks; SPE excels in motor imagery, ACPE is more consistent across tasks.
AI.motivation: EEG data are spatially distributed across the scalp, and transformers require positional information. The study benchmarks five positional encoding strategies within the CBraMod backbone to understand how encoding electrode positions affects transferability across tasks, subjects, and datasets.
AI.method: Benchmark five positional encoding strategies in the CBraMod transformer backbone using linear probing and fine-tuning on two EEG tasks: motor imagery classification and emotion recognition.
AI.result: There is no single encoding strategy that dominates across tasks. Spherical Positional Encoding (SPE) yields strong representations for motor imagery but performs poorly on emotion recognition, while Asymmetric Conditional Positional Encoding (ACPE) shows more consistent performance across tasks.
AI.conclusion: Positional encoding strategy performance is task-dependent in EEG decoding; no universal encoding exists. This has implications for designing EEG foundation models, suggesting a need for adaptive or task-aware encoding schemes.

title: K-FinHallu: A Hallucination Detection Benchmark for Multi-Turn RAG in Korean Finance
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have advanced financial automation through Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), yet hallucinations remain a critical barrier to deployment in high-stakes environments. Existing benchmarks focus on single-turn, English-centric tasks, leaving the multi-turn dynamics and linguistic-regulatory nuances of the Korean financial domain unaddressed. We introduce K-FinHallu, the first benchmark for hallucination detection in multi-turn Korean financial RAG. We construct multi-turn dialogues from authentic Korean financial documents and inject hallucinations under a proposed hierarchical taxonomy based on context answerability that explicitly accounts for justified abstention. Benchmarking frontier and open-source LLMs as hallucination detectors, we find that even the strongest models struggle with fine-grained financial diagnostics and refusal behavior. While fine-tuning an 8B model on our training split yields performance competitive with frontier LLMs, justified abstention remains the weakest axis across all evaluated models.

AI.tldr: Introduces K-FinHallu, a first multi-turn Korean financial RAG hallucination benchmark; uses authentic documents, a hierarchical taxonomy including justified abstention; evaluates frontier/open LLMs; fine-tuning an 8B model performs competitively but abstention remains weak.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap in benchmarks for multi-turn, Korean-language financial RAG and the handling of hallucinations and regulated content, which are critical for high-stakes deployment.
AI.method: Constructs multi-turn dialogues from authentic Korean financial documents; injects hallucinations following a hierarchical taxonomy based on context answerability that includes justified abstention; benchmarks frontier and open LLMs as hallucination detectors; experiments show fine-tuning an 8B model on training data approaching frontier performance.
AI.result: Top-performing models still struggle with fine-grained financial diagnostics and with managing refusal/abstention; the best 8B-tuned model approaches frontier models in overall performance, but justified abstention remains the weakest axis across models.
AI.conclusion: K-FinHallu fills a key benchmark gap for Korean financial RAG hallucination detection, revealing substantial room for improvement in refusal behavior; suggests that targeted fine-tuning can bridge some gap to frontier models, yet robust abstention mechanisms are essential for safe deployment.

title: LFQ: Logit-aware Final-block Quantization for Boosting the Generation Quality of Low-Bit Quantized LLMs
summary: As large language models continue to scale, low-bit weight-only post-training quantization (PTQ) offers a practical solution to their memory-efficient deployment. Although block-wise PTQ is capable of matching the full-precision (FP) baseline on basic language modeling and understanding, its quality is degraded for generative tasks -- especially at longer responses and extended chains of thought, which is critical in boosting task accuracy. We attribute this shortfall to two factors: (i) the omission of the unembedding layer (the LM head) in block-wise optimization and (ii) the reliance on the mean squared error (MSE) objective. Both factors cause the token probability distribution of the quantized model to misalign with that of the FP model, yielding notable accuracy drops on text generation benchmarks. To rectify the discrepancy, we introduce Logit-aware Final-block Quantization (LFQ), a simple yet effective enhancement to block-wise PTQ that quantizes the final Transformer block by minimizing the cross-entropy between the logits of the FP model and those of its quantized counterpart. By aligning token probabilities at the logit level in the final block, LFQ consistently improves the accuracy of complex generation tasks over state-of-the-art block-wise PTQ across diverse model families, while maintaining parity with FP baselines on language modeling and understanding.

AI.tldr: LFQ enhances block-wise post-training quantization by aligning the final block's logits with the full-precision model via cross-entropy minimization, addressing unembedding omission and MSE misalignment to boost generation quality while preserving FP accuracy on LM/understanding.
AI.motivation: Block-wise PTQ often omits the unembedding layer and relies on MSE, causing misalignment of token probabilities with FP models and degraded generation quality, especially for long responses and chains of thought.
AI.method: Quantize the final Transformer block by minimizing cross-entropy between FP logits and quantized logits, aligning token probabilities at the logit level in the final block within a block-wise PTQ framework.
AI.result: LFQ consistently improves generation task accuracy over state-of-the-art block-wise PTQ across diverse model families, while maintaining parity with FP baselines on language modeling and understanding tasks.
AI.conclusion: A simple, effective enhancement to block-wise PTQ; logit-level alignment in the final block is key to closing the gap in generative tasks without sacrificing LM/understanding performance.

title: Learning to Perturb Hidden Representations for Generalizable Deep Learning
summary: Deep neural networks process data through a cascade of representations: input features, hidden activations, logits, and loss. While perturbations at the input, logit, and label levels have been systematically studied, the intermediate hidden activations, which constitute the bulk of the network's computation, have received no unified perturbation analysis. In this paper, we establish a unified framework for hidden activation perturbation, revealing that Dropout, Manifold Mixup, adversarial feature perturbation, and related methods all impose specific forms of activation perturbation but with class-agnostic or random strategies. We conjecture that expansive perturbation (increasing activation norm) acts as positive augmentation, while contractive perturbation (decreasing activation norm) acts as negative augmentation, and that the perturbation layer determines whether the effect resembles input-level augmentation (shallow layers) or logit-level manipulation (deep layers). We propose Learning to Perturb Activations (LPA), which adaptively perturbs activations at a selected hidden layer with class-level perturbations learned via PGD. We further provide theoretical analysis connecting activation perturbation to flat minima and perturbation amplification through layers. Experiments on balanced classification, long-tail classification, and domain generalization demonstrate that LPA consistently outperforms existing methods and provides complementary benefits to logit perturbation methods such as LPL.

AI.tldr: Unified activation-perturbation framework; introduces Learning to Perturb Activations (LPA); provides theory linking perturbations to flat minima and layer-wise amplification; empirical gains across balanced, long-tail, and domain generalization, complementing logit perturbations like LPL.
AI.motivation: Hidden activations constitute the majority of neural computation and have lacked a unified perturbation analysis. The work seeks to unify perturbations from dropout, manifold mixup, and adversarial feature perturbations under a common activation-perturbation perspective, and to understand how activation-level perturbations affect generalization.
AI.method: Propose a generic activation-perturbation framework that characterizes perturbations as expansive (norm-increasing) or contractive (norm-decreasing) and analyzes the effect of the perturbation layer (shallow vs deep). Introduce Learning to Perturb Activations (LPA) that adaptively perturbs activations at a chosen hidden layer with class-level perturbations learned via PGD. Provide theoretical connections to flat minima and perturbation amplification through layers.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis links activation perturbations to flatter minima and cross-layer amplification. Empirical evaluation on balanced, long-tail, and domain-generalization benchmarks shows LPA consistently improves performance and provides complementary benefits to logit perturbation methods such as LPL.
AI.conclusion: Activation perturbation is a viable augmentation strategy for deep networks. LPA delivers adaptive, class-aware perturbations at latent layers, yielding generalization gains and complementing existing logit-based perturbation methods.

title: VisualThink-VLA: Visual Intermediate Reasoning for Effective and Low-Latency Vision-Language-Action Policies
summary: Recent work has begun to equip vision-language-action (VLA) policies with explicit intermediate reasoning. In embodied control, however, textual chain-of-thought is a poor fit: irrelevant or weakly textual information can interfere with action prediction, while autoregressive text decoding adds too much latency for real-time closed-loop execution. We present VISUALTHINK-VLA, a visual intermediate-reasoning framework for accurate, low-latency VLA policies. Our bootstrapping philosophy is to guide action with effective visual thinking: VISUALTHINK-VLA bootstraps action prediction through a compact visual-evidence interface that preserves spatial precision while avoiding decoding overhead. Besides, to further improve performance and efficiency, VISUALTHINK-VLA adopts a tailored selective routing mechanism to learn the visual evidence tokens, enabling low-latency inference while preserving high-capacity specialization. We also introduce VisualEvidence-Kit, a supervision-and-audit resource centered on a VisualEvidence-Agent that constructs a 754.7k VLA instructions VisualEvidence-Set for route supervision and counterfactual faithfulness tests. Across multiple benchmarks and real-robot evaluation, VISUALTHINK-VLA achieves the highest success rate on most benchmarks while reducing the multi-second latency of reasoning-augmented baselines to the sub-second regime. For example, on BridgeData V2, it reduces step latency from 8.377,s with ECoT to 0.367,s, achieving a 22.8 times speedup.

AI.tldr: VISUALTHINK-VLA introduces a visual intermediate-reasoning framework for vision-language-action policies that emphasizes a compact visual-evidence interface and selective routing to enable low-latency, accurate embodied control, complemented by a large supervision/audit resource (VisualEvidence-Kit).
AI.motivation: Textual chain-of-thought is ill-suited for embodied control due to interference from irrelevant textual information and the latency of autoregressive decoding; a spatially precise, visual reasoning paradigm can provide fast, actionable cues.
AI.method: Propose a visual-evidence interface to bootstrap action predictions, with a tailored selective routing mechanism to learn visual evidence tokens. Introduce VisualEvidence-Kit and a VisualEvidence-Agent that constructs a 754.7k-visual-instruction VisualEvidence-Set for route supervision and counterfactual faithfulness tests.
AI.result: On multiple benchmarks and real-robot evaluation, achieves high success rates and reduces reasoning latency from multi-second to sub-second. In BridgeData V2, latency drops from 8.377 s (with ECoT) to 0.367 s, a 22.8× speedup.
AI.conclusion: Visual intermediate reasoning with a compact visual-evidence interface and selective routing enables accurate, low-latency VLA policies in embodied tasks, supported by large-scale supervision and audit resources.
