title: From XXLTraffic to EvoXXLTraffic: Scaling Traffic Forecasting to Sensor-Evolving Networks
summary: Existing traffic forecasting benchmarks assume a fixed sensor set, but real road-sensor networks grow continuously as the road network changes year by year. We introduce the XXLTraffic dataset family, which spans up to 27 years of California PeMS and Transport for NSW data. The fixed-sensor subsets of XXLTraffic support extremely long forecasting with multi-year gaps and standard hourly / daily long-horizon forecasting. We extend it to EvoXXLTraffic, a sensor-evolving reorganization that exposes per-year active sensors, yearly traffic-flow matrices, and yearly graph snapshots across nine PeMS districts, with growth ratios ranging from +305% to over +10,000%. We define a yearly streaming forecasting protocol on EvoXXLTraffic in which each calendar year is a continual task, and benchmark a wide range of representative baselines drawn from static spatio-temporal GNNs, naïve online schemes, evolving-graph continual methods, and retrieval / test-time methods. We find that our ultra-large evolutionary dataset better reflects the real world, and many state-of-the-art (SOTA) results no longer work. Our dataset complements existing benchmarks by enabling more realistic forecasting under ultra-long evolutionary road networks.

AI.tldr: Ultra-large, evolving-graph traffic benchmarks (XXLTraffic and EvoXXLTraffic) enable ultra-long horizon forecasting with year-by-year sensor growth, revealing that many state-of-the-art models falter under realistic network evolution and data drift.
AI.motivation: Real-world road sensor networks continually grow as road networks change; static benchmarks miss long-horizon forecasting under evolving topology. The work provides a dataset family and a streaming evaluation protocol to study forecasting under sensor evolution and growth.
AI.method: Introduce XXLTraffic: a multi-decade dataset (up to 27 years) from California PeMS and NSW; include fixed-sensor subsets for long-horizon tasks. Extend to EvoXXLTraffic: sensor-evolving reorganization with per-year active sensors, yearly traffic-flow matrices, and yearly graph snapshots across nine PeMS districts, with growth ratios from +305% to >+10,000%. Define a yearly streaming forecasting protocol treating each year as a continual task. Benchmark baselines include static spatio-temporal GNNs, naïve online schemes, evolving-graph continual methods, and retrieval/test-time methods.
AI.result: The ultra-large evolutionary dataset better reflects real-world conditions; many current SOTA models no longer perform well under evolving sensor networks. The dataset complements existing benchmarks by enabling more realistic long-horizon forecasting under evolving road networks.
AI.conclusion: XXLTraffic/EvoXXLTraffic provide a more realistic, challenging benchmark for ultra-long, evolving-graph traffic forecasting, encouraging development of models robust to sensor growth, data drift, and year-by-year topology changes.

title: AsymVLM: Asymmetric Token Pruning for Efficient Vision-Language Model Inference
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) process thousands of visual tokens per image alongside comparatively few text tokens, yet existing compression methods treat both modalities uniformly. We observe that the two modalities have fundamentally different properties: vision tokens are spatially redundant and dominate prefill, while text tokens are causally dependent and accumulate during decoding. Based on this asymmetry, we propose and empirically evaluate AsymVLM, which applies aggressive pruning to vision tokens before prefill using a learned importance scorer with per-sample adaptive budgeting, and temporal threshold-based eviction to text tokens only when they exceed a fixed budget. Our experiments indicate that AsymVLM achieves the highest FLOPs savings (up to 54%) among state-of-the-art methods while outperforming existing approaches by 2--3% on document and chart understanding tasks where visual information is spatially localized and query-specific, and maintaining competitive accuracy on holistic benchmarks. In text-dominated scenarios, our eviction strategy substantially outperforms standard LLM cache compression methods by adapting to the short-context nature of VLM.

AI.tldr: AsymVLM introduces modality-aware compression for vision-language models by aggressively pruning vision tokens before prefill using a learned importance scorer with per-sample budgets, and applying fixed-budget, threshold-based eviction for text tokens during decoding, achieving significant FLOPs savings while maintaining or improving task accuracy on document and chart understanding and staying competitive on holistic benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Vision tokens are spatially redundant and dominate prefill; text tokens are causally dependent and accumulate during decoding. Uniform compression across modalities wastes efficiency and can hurt accuracy. A modality-tailored approach can better exploit the different natures of visual and textual information.
AI.method: Split compression policy by modality: aggressive vision-token pruning prior to prefill using a learned importance scorer with per-sample adaptive budgets; text tokens evicted only when their count exceeds a fixed budget via a temporal threshold-based scheme. Evaluate on document and chart understanding tasks and holistic benchmarks; compare against state-of-the-art compression methods.
AI.result: Up to 54% FLOPs savings, outperforming existing methods by 2-3% on document/chart understanding tasks; maintains competitive accuracy on holistic benchmarks; eviction strategy outperforms standard LLM cache compression in text-dominant scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Modality-aware compression for VLMs yields favorable efficiency-accuracy trade-offs. The approach highlights the benefit of exploiting cross-modal asymmetry and suggests directions for adaptive budgeting, more nuanced eviction policies, and broader evaluation across multimodal models.

title: DocRetriever: A Plug-and-Play Framework for Multimodal Document Retrieval with Comprehensive Benchmark
summary: Multimodal documents contain diverse elements, such as tables, figures, and layouts, which can complicate retrieval tasks. While current approaches typically combine dense visual embedding models with supervised rerankers to achieve high-precision retrieval, they face inherent limitations. First, the coarse-grained nature of dense embeddings tends to obfuscate explicit semantics, failing to leverage structurally salient information. Second, supervised reranking models suffer from generalization bottlenecks, as their performance heavily relies on domain-specific training data. Furthermore, existing benchmarks often lack diverse assessment dimensions and comprehensive relevance annotations, limiting reliable evaluation. To address these challenges, we propose DocRetriever, a plug-and-play framework. It enhances visual retrieval via a layout-aware sparse embedding technique, enabling effective hybrid encoding without the overhead of optical character recognition (OCR). We also introduce a generalizable reranker that leverages reasoning-augmented demonstrations and optimized sampling to improve accuracy in few-shot settings. Finally, we construct a new benchmark, MultiDocR, to enable more rigorous evaluation. Experiments across diverse benchmarks validate DocRetriever's superiority over state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: We propose DocRetriever, a layout-aware sparse-embedding retrieval framework with a generalizable reranker and a new benchmark MultiDocR, achieving superior results across diverse benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address key limitations in multimodal document retrieval: (1) dense embeddings obscure explicit structure and semantics; (2) supervised rerankers generalize poorly across domains due to domain-specific training data; (3) benchmarks lack diverse evaluation dimensions and comprehensive relevance annotations.
AI.method: Introduce DocRetriever, a plug-and-play framework that uses layout-aware sparse embeddings to enable effective hybrid encoding without OCR, and a generalizable reranker leveraging reasoning-augmented demonstrations with optimized sampling for few-shot settings; also present a new benchmark MultiDocR for robust evaluation.
AI.result: Empirical results across diverse benchmarks demonstrate DocRetriever's superiority over state-of-the-art methods in retrieval performance and robustness.
AI.conclusion: DocRetriever advances visual retrieval by integrating layout-aware sparse representations with a generalizable reranker, avoids OCR overhead, and provides a rigorous evaluation framework via MultiDocR.

title: Croissant Tasks: A Metadata Format for Reproducible Machine Learning Evaluations
summary: Reproducibility is fundamental to the scientific method, yet remains a critical challenge in machine learning. Contributing factors include underspecified execution details and brittle software environments. Human-centric remedies, such as checklists and manual verification, help but require intensive effort and fail to scale. To address this, we introduce Croissant Tasks: a declarative, machine-actionable metadata format that abstracts low-level implementation details into high-level specifications. This format enables conceptual reproducibility: verifying claims via independent, agent-generated implementations rather than brittle source code replication. We contribute: (1) the Croissant Tasks specification, formally decoupling task problem from solution; (2) an automated LLM pipeline that retrofits existing benchmarks into this format; and (3) empirical validation showing autonomous agents can ingest these specifications to generate functional, accurate reproduction pipelines from scratch. We envision this format as a new foundation for automated and conceptual reproducibility in machine learning.

AI.tldr: Proposes Croissant Tasks, a declarative, machine-actionable metadata format to enable conceptual reproducibility in ML by decoupling task specification from solution and enabling autonomous agents to generate reproduction pipelines from specs.
AI.motivation: Reproducibility in ML is hindered by underspecified execution details and brittle environments; human-centric checklists are costly and do not scale; need scalable, automated conceptual reproducibility.
AI.method: Define Croissant Tasks specification; retrofit benchmarks into the format via an automated LLM pipeline; validate by showing autonomous agents can construct functional reproduction pipelines from the specs.
AI.result: Benchmarks retrofitted to Croissant Tasks; autonomous agents can ingest specs to produce accurate reproduction pipelines from scratch.
AI.conclusion: Croissant Tasks offers a foundation for automated and conceptual reproducibility in machine learning, enabling scalable verification of claims through agent-generated implementations.

title: SCOPE: A Lightweight-training LLM Framework for Air Traffic Control Readback Monitoring
summary: Pilot readback of Air Traffic Control (ATC) voice instructions is a primary safeguard against miscommunication in air transportation. However, readback anomalies remain implicated in approximately 80% of aviation incidents. This vulnerability is further exacerbated by rising traffic volume and elevated cognitive workload, thereby motivating automated readback monitoring by machine. Traditional rule-based and machine learning approaches struggle to generalize across the highly variable and evolving phraseology of air traffic controller-pilot communications. While Large Language Models (LLMs) have opened a new avenue through their strong reasoning and generalization capabilities, existing approaches still face deployment and computational barriers in practice. In this work, we propose Semantic reasoning for Communication via Open-set Plug-in with Examples (SCOPE), a novel lightweight-training LLM framework that advances both the efficiency and accuracy of machine-based ATC readback monitoring. The core idea is to couple a plug-in open-set classifier with a carefully designed in-context learning mechanism on top of a frozen LLM. Extensive experiments on the semi-synthetic communication dataset show that SCOPE attains superior accuracy while delivering the low-latency response required for operational environments. Under a few-shot setting, SCOPE achieves 91.05% accuracy in open-set detection and corrects 96.63% of anomalous readbacks, thereby outperforming the strongest available baselines while providing explanations for its decisions. These findings demonstrate the potential of our framework as a practical pathway toward interpretable and controllable ATC readback monitoring.

AI.tldr: SCOPE introduces a lightweight training framework combining a plug-in open-set classifier with in-context learning on a frozen LLM for ATC readback monitoring, achieving high open-set detection accuracy and anomaly correction with low latency and explanations.
AI.motivation: Mitigate miscommunication in ATC readbacks under rising traffic and workload; overcome generalization gaps of rule-based/ML approaches; leverage LLM reasoning while maintaining efficiency and interpretability.
AI.method: Plug-in open-set classifier integrated with frozen LLM, guided by carefully designed in-context learning prompts; few-shot learning; semi-synthetic dataset; evaluation of open-set detection accuracy and anomaly correction; explanations provided.
AI.result: In few-shot setting, 91.05% open-set detection accuracy; 96.63% anomalous readback correction; outperforms baselines; low-latency; provides explanations.
AI.conclusion: SCOPE demonstrates a practical, interpretable, and controllable path for ATC readback monitoring with robust generalization to novel phrases and efficient inference; potential for operational deployment.

title: GenEraser: Generalizable Video Object Removal via Balanced Text-Mask Guidance and Decoupled Locator-Preserver
summary: Video object removal frequently struggles to simultaneously eliminate target objects and their associated physical effects (e.g., smoke, reflections, light, and ripples) in out-of-domain scenarios due to complex spatiotemporal ambiguities. While existing methods primarily rely on spatial masks, they often fail to capture weakly correlated effects, and the potential of explicit textual guidance remains underexplored. Furthermore, a fundamental optimization conflict exists in removal models between high-level semantic generalization and precise pixel-level background preservation. To address these challenges, we propose GenEraser, a novel framework for generalized and high-fidelity video object and effect removal. First, we introduce a Multi-Conditional Mixture-of-Experts (MC-MoE) paired with Bipartite Text guidance to fully exploit the multimodal priors of Diffusion Transformers, significantly enhancing the identification of complex effects. Second, a Learnable Deep ``CFG'' Fusion mechanism (LD-CFG) is developed to adaptively balance the relative dominance of mask and textual conditions across diverse scenarios. Finally, we propose a Decoupled Expert Architecture, comprising a Locator and a Preserver, to mitigate the inherent trade-off between semantic generalization and pixel alignment. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our GenEraser surpasses recent state-of-the-art approaches, achieving significant quantitative improvements (e.g., $2.16$ dB and $1.44$ dB on the ROSE Benchmark and VOR-Eval, respectively) while maintaining exceptionally robust generalization in open-world scenarios. https://cyqii.github.io/GenEraser.github.io/

AI.tldr: GenEraser delivers generalized, high-fidelity video object-and-effect removal by combining a multimodal, diffusion-based framework with learnable conditioning and a decoupled expert architecture for Locator and Preserver, achieving state-of-the-art results on ROSE and VOR-Eval with strong open-world generalization.
AI.motivation: Addresses: (1) difficulty removing objects and associated physical effects in out-of-domain videos due to spatiotemporal ambiguities; (2) overreliance on spatial masks and underutilization of textual guidance; (3) fundamental trade-off between semantic generalization and pixel-precise background preservation.
AI.method: 1) Multi-Conditional Mixture-of-Experts (MC-MoE) with Bipartite Text guidance to leverage multimodal priors of Diffusion Transformers for complex effects. 2) Learnable Deep CFG Fusion (LD-CFG) to adaptively balance mask vs. text conditions across scenarios. 3) Decoupled Expert Architecture with Locator and Preserver to separate semantic generalization from pixel alignment.
AI.result: Experimental results show significant improvements over recent SOTA (e.g., +2.16 dB on ROSE Benchmark and +1.44 dB on VOR-Eval); demonstrated robust generalization in open-world scenarios across diverse tasks.
AI.conclusion: GenEraser introduces a principled, modular framework that (i) exploits multimodal priors, (ii) adaptively fuses conditioning signals, and (iii) decouples semantic understanding from pixel-level preservation, yielding superior, generalized, high-fidelity video object and effect removal.

title: Certified Policy Optimisation for Nested Causal Bandits via PAC-Bayes Risk
summary: Critical sequential decisions are rarely single-timescale: a strategic decision causally shapes the context in which every subsequent tactical choice is made; standard bandit and reinforcement-learning theory does not capture this causal coupling between timescales. We formalise the problem class as Nested Contextual Causal Bandits (NCCBs), a hierarchical SCM where each level's action sets the next level's context distribution, and propose Nested Causal Thompson Sampling (NCTS), which draws one mechanism-factorised belief per episode and acts recursively under it. Our main theoretical result is a causal PAC-Bayesian excess-risk bound that certifies any candidate deployment policy from historic data alone, off-policy and anytime, answering the deployment question: can we trust this agent here, and at what risk? Experiments on a hierarchical SCM show that, against a matched RFF-GP joint regression on the same function class, the factorised SCM-mechanism posterior transfers significantly better zero-shot under exogenous distribution shifts, the recursive meta-to-inner commit significantly dominates the joint-commit alternative in distribution, and the certificate significantly contracts as offline data accumulates. Combining these results, we establish progressive certified handover, a safe-deployment method: each timescale flips from a legacy controller to NCTS when gains can be certified, independently of the others.

AI.tldr: NCCBs with Nested Causal Thompson Sampling (NCTS) and a causal PAC-Bayesian bound enabling offline-certified, multi-timescale decisions; supports progressive, safe handover from legacy controllers.
AI.motivation: Standard bandits/RL miss the causal coupling across timescales where outer actions shape future contexts; formal hierarchical SCM needed for safe, offline deployment in multi-scale decision processes.
AI.method: Define Nested Contextual Causal Bandits (NCCB) as a hierarchical structural causal model. Propose Nested Causal Thompson Sampling (NCTS) that draws one factorised mechanism-belief per episode and acts recursively. Derive a causal PAC-Bayesian excess-risk bound for offline deployment. Evaluate against a matched regression baseline on a hierarchical SCM.
AI.result: The bound certifies any deployment policy from historical data off-policy and offline; NCTS achieves better zero-shot transfer under exogenous distribution shifts; recursive meta-to-inner commitments dominate joint commitments; certificates contract as more offline data accumulates.
AI.conclusion: Enables progressive certified handover: each timescale switches from legacy controllers to NCTS once gains are certifiable, enabling safe deployment across hierarchical decision processes.

title: Singularity-aware Optimization via Randomized Geometric Probing: Towards Stable Non-smooth Optimization
summary: Deep learning optimization relies heavily on the assumption of smooth loss landscapes, a condition systematically violated by modern architectures due to non-smooth components such as ReLU activations and quantization operators. In such non-smooth regimes, adaptive optimizers such as Adam suffer from gradient chattering, violent oscillations caused by conflicting signals within the Clarke subdifferential, leading to poor convergence and suboptimal generalization. To address this, we introduce Singularity-aware Adam (S-Adam), a novel optimizer that stabilizes training by dynamically modulating step sizes based on local geometric instability. Our key contribution is the Local Geometric Instability (LGI) metric, a computationally efficient estimator of the Clarke subdifferential diameter derived from the variance of randomized directional derivatives. S-Adam incorporates an adaptive damping mechanism exp(-$λ$$ρ$) that decelerates updates in high-instability regions while preserving fast convergence in smooth basins. We provide a rigorous convergence analysis using differential inclusions, proving that S-Adam converges almost surely to ($δ$,$ε$)-Clarke stationary points at the optimal O(1/$\sqrt(T)$) rate. Empirical evaluations on Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) and high-noise small-batch learning demonstrate that S-Adam consistently outperforms AdamW and Prox-SGD, achieving accuracy gains of up to 6 percent on CIFAR-100 and 3 percent on TinyImageNet while effectively mitigating gradient oscillations.

AI.tldr: S-Adam introduces Local Geometric Instability (LGI) and a singularity-aware damping mechanism to stabilize optimization in non-smooth loss landscapes. It provides convergence guarantees to Clarke stationary points and demonstrates empirical gains over AdamW and Prox-SGD on quantization-aware training and noisy small-batch regimes, notably +6% on CIFAR-100 and +3% on TinyImageNet.
AI.motivation: Modern architectures with non-smooth components (e.g., ReLU, quantization) violate smoothness assumptions, causing gradient chattering in adaptive optimizers like Adam due to the Clarke subdifferential; there is a need for stable, geometry-aware optimization methods.
AI.method: Define Local Geometric Instability (LGI) as a computationally efficient estimator of the Clarke subdifferential diameter based on the variance of randomized directional derivatives. Introduce an adaptive damping factor exp(-λρ) that slows updates in high-instability regions while preserving fast progress in smooth basins. Provide a convergence analysis via differential inclusions showing almost sure convergence to (δ, ε)-Clarke stationary points at O(1/√T). Validate empirically on Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) and high-noise small-batch learning, comparing against AdamW and Prox-SGD.
AI.result: The method achieves theoretical convergence guarantees to Clarke stationary points with rate O(1/√T). Empirically, S-Adam yields accuracy gains up to 6 percentage points on CIFAR-100 and up to 3 on TinyImageNet relative to competitive baselines, while mitigating gradient oscillations.
AI.conclusion: S-Adam provides a principled, geometry-aware optimization framework for non-smooth training, combining a computationally efficient instability estimator with adaptive damping to improve convergence and generalization in non-smooth regimes; LGI enables rapid detection of instability without heavy cost.

title: Towards Consistent Video Geometry Estimation
summary: This work presents ViGeo, a feed-forward foundation model for recovering spatially dense and temporally consistent geometry from video sequences. Built upon a plain transformer architecture without task-specific architectural modifications, ViGeo supports streaming, full-sequence, and long-video inference within a unified model. The key design is dynamic chunking attention, which exposes the model to both bidirectional and causal temporal contexts during training and allows it to adapt its attention pattern at test time without retraining. To improve supervision quality, we further introduce a completion-based data refinement framework. This framework trains a video depth completion teacher that conditions on sparse and noisy annotations and exploits video/multi-view context to produce dense, temporally coherent, and geometrically reliable training targets. Beyond depth and point maps, ViGeo also predicts surface normals within the same framework. Trained solely on public datasets, ViGeo achieves state-of-the-art performance across online, offline, and long-video depth estimation, surface normal estimation, and video point map estimation.

AI.tldr: ViGeo is a transformer-based, streaming-capable, unified model for video geometry (depth, normals, point maps) that uses dynamic chunking attention and a completion-based data-refinement teacher. It achieves state-of-the-art results across online, offline, and long-video tasks on public data.
AI.motivation: The problem of recovering dense, temporally coherent 3D geometry from videos, especially for long sequences and streaming scenarios, requires high supervision quality and a unified multi-task approach beyond task-specific architectures.
AI.method: A plain transformer backbone with dynamic chunking attention that blends bidirectional and causal temporal contexts and adapts its attention at test time without retraining. A completion-based data refinement framework trains a video depth completion teacher, conditioned on sparse/noisy annotations and leveraging video/multi-view context to generate dense, temporally coherent targets. The model jointly predicts depth, video point maps, and surface normals.
AI.result: ViGeo achieves state-of-the-art performance across online, offline, and long-video depth estimation, surface normal estimation, and video point map estimation, trained exclusively on public datasets.
AI.conclusion: ViGeo demonstrates that a unified, streaming-capable transformer with dynamic attention and a data-refinement supervision loop can deliver dense, temporally consistent geometry across multiple tasks without task-specific architectures.

title: SkillsInjector: Dynamic Skill Context Construction for LLM Agents
summary: LLM agents now draw on growing skill libraries to handle complex tasks. However, injecting more skills does not always improve task completion and can even degrade it. Existing methods still treat skill injection as a static step, selecting skills with fixed criteria, fixing the budget in advance, and leaving descriptions unchanged. We argue that this static treatment can undermine the utility of skills, because which skills are exposed, how many are included, and how they are presented all affect downstream performance. We propose SkillsInjector, a two-stage adaptive method that jointly addresses these decisions. First, a context planner learns execution-grounded skill preferences and admits an adaptive number of skills for each task. A set-aware renderer then tailors how selected descriptions are presented relative to their co-injected neighbors. Across tau2-bench, SkillsBench, and ALFWorld, SkillsInjector achieves the highest score, improving over the strongest baseline by 3.9, 6.1, and 7.3 percentage points, respectively. Ablation studies show that skill selection, adaptive budgeting, and set-aware rendering each contribute to the gain. These results show that skill-augmented agents benefit from optimizing the injected context itself. Code will be released upon publication

AI.tldr: Adaptive SkillsInjector selectively exposes and presents skills for LLM agents, improving task performance by jointly optimizing skill selection, budgeting, and presentation.
AI.motivation: Static skill injection can misalign exposure, budget, and descriptions with task needs, potentially degrading performance; there is a need for adaptive, context-grounded skill management.
AI.method: Two-stage system: (1) a context planner that learns execution-grounded skill preferences and adaptively decides how many skills to admit per task; (2) a set-aware renderer that tailors how selected skill descriptions are displayed relative to co-injected partners.
AI.result: Across tau2-bench, SkillsBench, and ALFWorld, SkillsInjector achieves the top scores, outperforming the strongest baseline by 3.9, 6.1, and 7.3 percentage points respectively. Ablation studies show that skill selection, adaptive budgeting, and set-aware rendering each contribute to the gains.
AI.conclusion: Skill-augmented agents benefit from optimizing the injected context; adaptive, context-aware skill injection yields improved task performance. Code will be released upon publication.

title: Why Larger Models Learn More: Effects of Capacity, Interference, and Rare-Task Retention
summary: Larger models learn tasks smaller models do not. What drives this phenomenon? We develop a simple phenomenological argument that power-law scaling already suggests that a larger model will be able to learn a part of the data distribution that a smaller model fails to learn, even with infinite training data. To validate this claim and identify its causes, we study the effects of model scaling on a synthetic setup consisting of a mixture of tasks that show monotonic scaling curves. The results point to a data-induced competition over resources (neurons). Specifically, smaller models allocate their neurons to high frequency or low complexity tasks, and so they learn solutions that perform poorly on rare and complex tasks. Moreover, this happens even when solutions capable of expressing the desired task exist. We then assess how a larger model circumvents this data-centric bottleneck, finding that it traces to a reduced interference mechanism: larger models can allocate enough resources to common tasks that the gradient updates for those tasks become weak, which means that they do not overwrite rare-task features as they slowly accumulate. Finally, to further validate these claims, we pretrain OLMo models (4M to 4B parameters) on novel tasks of varying frequency and complexity. The results mirror those from our synthetic data experiments: only the larger OLMo models learn the infrequent and complex tasks, and these larger models embed more task features in their representations and show less gradient interference between tasks. Overall, we offer a data-centric account of why larger models learn tasks that smaller models fail to. This helps explain why larger models are better in practice, and it can inform practical questions concerning model sizing and training data mixtures.

AI.tldr: Larger models overcome a data-driven bottleneck by allocating resources more effectively, enabling learning of rare/complex tasks that smaller models miss. This is demonstrated via a data-centric, power-law scaling argument, synthetic mixtures of tasks, and large-scale OLMo pretraining (4M–4B params).
AI.motivation: Explain why larger models learn tasks that smaller models fail to, even with abundant data, by identifying data-driven mechanisms and resource allocation dynamics that scale with model size.
AI.method: Develop a phenomenological, power-law-based scaling argument; study a synthetic mixture-of-tasks with monotonic scaling; analyze neuron-resource competition and gradient interference; validate via pretraining OLMo models (4M–4B params) on tasks with varying frequency and complexity.
AI.result: In small models, neurons split between high-frequency/low-complexity tasks, causing poor learning of rare/complex tasks; larger models reduce interference, allocate sufficient capacity to common tasks so their gradients don't overwrite rare-task features; larger models learn infrequent/complex tasks and embed more task features with less gradient interference; OLMo experiments corroborate synthetic results across a wide parameter range.
AI.conclusion: A data-centric account explains the scaling advantages: larger models mitigate neuronic interference and can learn rare/complex tasks by better resource allocation. This informs practical choices on model size and data composition for training.

title: FakeVLM-R1: Internalizing Physical Laws via CoT for Synthetic Image Detection
summary: The development of generative artificial intelligence technologies has propelled the visual realism of synthetic images to an unprecedented level. Although current interpretable detection methods based on Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) have made certain progress, they still rely on imitation learning derived from massive volumes of forged data. Consequently, they lack genuine causal reasoning capabilities and are prone to explanatory hallucinations. To overcome this bottleneck, we propose FakeVLM-R1, aiming to endow the model with human-like critical thinking capabilities when performing synthetic detection tasks. Building upon Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), this framework integrates Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a Critical Thinking Chain-of-Thought (CoT) mechanism. During the inference phase, the model executes a "bidirectional dialectical reasoning" process: while proposing a forgery hypothesis, it must simultaneously invoke physical commonsense to construct an authenticity counter-proof. Furthermore, we constructed the FakeClue++ dataset with high-quality samples, which extensively introduces annotations guided by the physical laws of authentic images, providing a unified authenticity anchor for the model. Experiments confirm that FakeVLM-R1 achieves SOTA performance the evaluated models across multiple benchmarks. It not only achieves high-precision, logically interpretable detection but also resolves the over-rejection bias of existing methods against real images, demonstrating generalization and robustness against perturbations.

AI.tldr: A novel framework FakeVLM-R1 for detecting synthetic images using human-like critical thinking, combining supervised fine-tuning with GRPO and a Critical Thinking Chain-of-Thought, plus a physics-informed FakeClue++ dataset; achieves state-of-the-art, better interpretability, and robustness.
AI.motivation: Current LMM-based detectors rely on imitation learning from forged data and lack true causal reasoning, leading to explanatory hallucinations and bias against real images; there is a need for human-like critical thinking and physical commonsense in detection tasks.
AI.method: Extend SFT by integrating Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a Critical Thinking Chain-of-Thought mechanism. During inference, perform bidirectional dialectical reasoning: propose forgery hypotheses while constructing authenticity counter-proofs using physical commonsense. Introduce FakeClue++ dataset with physics-guided annotations to anchor authenticity.
AI.result: FakeVLM-R1 attains state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks, delivering high-precision, interpretable detection and reducing the over-rejection bias against real images, with demonstrated generalization and robustness to perturbations.
AI.conclusion: The approach endows LMM-based detectors with human-like critical thinking for synthetic image detection, improving accuracy, explainability, and robustness; the FakeClue++ dataset provides a solid authenticity anchor for future work.

title: MEMENTO: Leveraging Web as a Learning Signal for Low-Data Domains
summary: Real-world tasks often lack large labeled datasets, motivating extensive work on learning in low-data regimes. However, existing approaches such as few-shot prompting, instruction tuning, and synthetic data generation, continue to treat labeled or pseudo-labeled data as the primary learning signal. In contrast, human practitioners acquire expertise through repeated, self-directed interaction with the open web, progressively refining both domain knowledge and search strategies. We propose MEMENTO, a framework that treats the web as a learning signal rather than a stateless retrieval interface. MEMENTO operates at two levels: within each session, it conducts iterative web exploration via an Adaptive Exploration Tree (AET) that decomposes tasks into evolving questions and reflects on intermediate findings; across sessions, it accumulates experience through dual-channel memory, separating declarative knowledge (facts) from procedural knowledge (search strategies). This design enables agents to learn reusable research strategies and domain expertise from trajectories of web interaction without additional model training. We evaluate MEMENTO on two low-data professional domains: sales automation and legal research. Our empirical results show consistent improvements in performance over ReAct based baselines (+25.6% on sales automation and 36.5% on legal research), demonstrating that the web can serve as a scalable learning source for acquiring task-specific expertise in data-scarce settings.

AI.tldr: MEMENTO treats the web as a learning signal, using an within-session Adaptive Exploration Tree (AET) and cross-session dual-channel memory to acquire reusable research strategies and domain knowledge without extra model training.
AI.motivation: Real-world tasks often have scarce labeled data. Existing methods rely on labeled or pseudo-labeled data as the learning signal. The web and iterative exploration offer a human-like pathway to acquire domain expertise and effective search strategies in data-scarce settings.
AI.method: Within each session, AET decomposes tasks into evolving questions and reflects on intermediate findings to guide web exploration. Across sessions, a dual-channel memory stores declarative knowledge (facts) and procedural knowledge (search strategies). This framework enables learning from trajectories of web interaction without additional model training.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation in two low-data domains (sales automation and legal research) shows consistent improvements over ReAct baselines: +25.6% for sales automation and +36.5% for legal research.
AI.conclusion: Web-based interaction can serve as a scalable learning signal for acquiring task-specific expertise in data-scarce settings, enabling reusable research strategies and domain knowledge without new model training.

title: On the Construction and Implications of Low-Loss Valleys in LoRA-based Bayesian Inference
summary: While parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods like low-rank adaptation (LoRA) are standard for large language models, principled estimation of epistemic uncertainty remains challenging. Recent results in the LoRA regime suggest that discrete multi-mode approaches such as deep ensembles offer little benefit over single-mode methods. This contradicts broader observations in deep learning, where ensembling independent optima typically improves generalization, and linking these modes through continuous low-loss valleys further enhances Bayesian model averaging (BMA). Whether such structure exists in the LoRA space and whether it yields functional diversity missed by local or discrete methods has not been studied. We introduce LoRA-Curve, a segmented Bézier curve parameterization in the LoRA space, with two variants: a free configuration that jointly optimizes all control points, and an anchored configuration that connects independently fine-tuned LoRA optima. We prove pathwise continuity and Lipschitz regularity of the loss along the curve and empirically show, across reasoning and classification benchmarks with Qwen2.5 7B, that linear interpolation encounters loss barriers, while our anchored multi-segment curves connect independent optima through continuous low-loss valleys. Combined with flat-minima perturbations and a Jensen-Shannon divergence regularizer, LoRA-Curve yields measurably higher mutual information of the predictive distribution without sacrificing performance, and links continuous parameter-space traversal to functional diversity.

AI.tldr: LoRA-Curve: a segmented Bézier-curve in LoRA space to connect independently fine-tuned LoRA optima, enabling continuous low-loss valleys and improved Bayesian uncertainty without harming performance.
AI.motivation: Discrete multi-mode LoRA results show little benefit from ensembles, contrasting broader DL evidence that ensembles of independent optima improve generalization and Bayesian model averaging. The work investigates whether structure in the LoRA parameter space can yield functional diversity and uncertainty gains by continuous pathways.
AI.method: Introduce LoRA-Curve with two variants: a free configuration optimizing all control points jointly, and an anchored configuration connecting independently fine-tuned LoRA optima. Prove pathwise continuity and Lipschitz regularity of the loss along the curve. Empirically evaluate on reasoning and classification benchmarks with Qwen2.5 7B. Compare linear interpolation (which shows loss barriers) to anchored multi-segment curves that traverse continuous low-loss valleys. Combine with flat-minima perturbations and a Jensen-Shannon divergence regularizer to enhance predictive distribution diversity.
AI.result: Anchored multi-segment LoRA-Curve connects independent optima through continuous low-loss valleys, overcoming linear-interpolation barriers. The approach yields higher mutual information of the predictive distribution without sacrificing accuracy on reasoning/classification tasks, indicating that continuous LoRA-space traversal can induce functional diversity.
AI.conclusion: LoRA-space possesses meaningful continuous structure that can be exploited to improve uncertainty estimation and functional diversity. Anchored segment curves enable better Bayesian model averaging in the LoRA regime, suggesting a general strategy to explore model spaces with continuous paths rather than solely discrete ensembles.

title: Boosting Zero-Shot 3D Style Transfer with 2D Pre-trained Priors
summary: In this work, we focus on zero-shot 3D style transfer that can generate multi-view consistent stylized views of the 3D scene given an arbitrary style image. We primarily tackle the issue of data scarcity in 3D style transfer, which arises when each model is trained on only a single scene, thereby limiting the number of available content images. This scarcity significantly hampers stylization performance, as model optimization relies on a sufficient number of content-style image pairs to provide supervisory signals. Our core idea is to integrate a decoder pre-trained on large-scale 2D image datasets into the 3D style transfer pipeline, thereby leveraging the prior knowledge encoded in the decoder from learning over numerous content-style image pairs. Our method combines feature Gaussian splatting and deferred stylization, enabling high-quality stylization with the data-sufficient decoder network while ensuring view consistency by unifying view-dependent operations into a view-invariant process. Experiments demonstrate that our Data-Sufficient StyleGaussian (DS-StyleGaussian) model outperforms existing zero-shot 3D style transfer methods in terms of visual quality across various datasets. This work also suggests that 2D pre-training can serve as a strong enhancement for 3D tasks, bridging the data gap between 2D and 3D.

AI.tldr: Introduces DS-StyleGaussian, a zero-shot 3D style transfer method that leverages a 2D pre-trained decoder to mitigate data scarcity, using feature Gaussian splatting and deferred stylization for multi-view consistency.
AI.motivation: Zero-shot 3D style transfer is hampered by scarcity of content-style image pairs since each model is trained on a single scene. Leveraging large-scale 2D pre-training can inject prior knowledge and improve stylization quality and consistency across views.
AI.method: Integrate a decoder pre-trained on large-scale 2D datasets into the 3D style transfer pipeline; combine feature Gaussian splatting with deferred stylization; unify view-dependent operations into a view-invariant process to ensure multi-view consistency while benefiting from the 2D decoder's priors.
AI.result: DS-StyleGaussian outperforms existing zero-shot 3D style transfer methods in visual quality across multiple datasets.
AI.conclusion: 2D pre-training can serve as a strong enhancement for 3D tasks, bridging the data gap between 2D and 3D and improving zero-shot 3D style transfer performance.

title: SAAS: Self-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Over-Search Mitigation in Agentic Search
summary: Agentic search enables LLMs to solve complex multi-hop questions through iterative reasoning and external search. Despite the effectiveness, these systems often suffer from a critical limitation in practice: agents fail to recognize their own knowledge boundaries, blindly triggering searches when internal knowledge suffices and failing to terminate search even when adequate evidence has been collected. The lack of self-awareness leads to severe \textbf{over-search}, incurring substantial inference latency and prohibitive computational cost. To this end, we propose SAAS, a novel RL framework designed to cultivate dynamic self-awareness that precisely regulates search behavior without compromising accuracy. SAAS introduces three key components: (i) a search boundary modeling mechanism, which identifies the search boundary under the evolving policy by contrasting search-disabled and search-enabled rollouts; (ii) a boundary-aware reward module, which translates this boundary awareness into trajectory-level penalties, suppressing unnecessary and redundant searches; and (iii) a stage-wise optimization strategy, which leverages a sequential curriculum to prioritize reasoning over search regularization, thereby avoiding reward hacking. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SAAS substantially reduces over-search, while maintaining accuracy. Our code is anonymously released at https://github.com/XMUDeepLIT/SAAS.

AI.tldr: SAAS introduces a boundary-aware RL framework to curb over-search in agentic search for LLMs, balancing reasoning and search with boundary modeling, boundary-aware rewards, and staged optimization.
AI.motivation: Current agentic search suffers from self-knowledge boundaries being unrecognized, causing over-search and high costs; there is a need for dynamic self-awareness to regulate search without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.method: Three components: (i) boundary modeling by contrasting search-disabled vs search-enabled rollouts under evolving policy; (ii) boundary-aware reward providing trajectory-level penalties to suppress unnecessary searches; (iii) stage-wise optimization with a curriculum to prioritize reasoning over search regularization and avoid reward hacking.
AI.result: Experiments show substantial reduction in over-search while maintaining accuracy; code released at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: SAAS effectively reduces over-search through dynamic self-awareness, with a practical impact; boundary modeling and curriculum strategy facilitate robust search regulation; future work could explore broader task generalization and efficiency.

title: PEARL: Training Socratic Tutors with Pedagogically Aligned Reinforcement Learning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown promise as educational tutors, yet effective tutoring requires more than solving problems: it must provide progressive Socratic guidance and balance multiple pedagogical objectives across multi-turn interactions. However, training such tutors remains challenging due to limited-fidelity and weakly controllable student simulation, under-specified pedagogical reward modeling, and unstable multi-objective optimization. To overcome these limitations, we propose PEARL, a pedagogically aligned reinforcement learning framework for training Socratic tutoring agents, consisting of three key components. First, we introduce a controllable student simulator that decouples latent cognitive states from response generation to model diverse abilities and misconceptions. Second, we develop a generative reward model that jointly evaluates pedagogical quality and objective correctness for policy optimization. Finally, we propose a stable multi-objective RL scheme that discretizes rewards within each dimension and aggregates normalized advantages across dimensions, preventing high-variance objectives from dominating updates. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that PEARL achieves the best performance among open-source models and remains competitive with leading proprietary LLMs, despite using only a 30B policy model.

AI.tldr: PEARL is a reinforcement learning framework for Socratic tutoring that combines a controllable student simulator, a generative reward model, and a stable multi-objective RL scheme. It achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source models and competitiveness with proprietary LLMs using a 30B policy.
AI.motivation: Tackling challenges in training pedagogically adept tutors: limited-fidelity student simulations, weakly controllable/assembled pedagogical rewards, and unstable optimization when balancing multiple objectives in multi-turn tutoring.
AI.method: Three components: (1) a controllable student simulator that decouples latent cognitive states from response generation to model diverse abilities/misconceptions; (2) a generative reward model that jointly evaluates pedagogical quality and objective correctness for policy optimization; (3) a stable multi-objective RL scheme that discretizes rewards within each dimension and aggregates normalized advantages across dimensions to avoid dominance by high-variance objectives.
AI.result: Empirical benchmarks show PEARL achieves best performance among open-source models and remains competitive with leading proprietary LLMs, despite using only a 30B policy model.
AI.conclusion: PEARL demonstrates that modular, pedagogy-aligned RL can effectively train Socratic tutors with smaller models, offering improved pedagogical quality and training stability; its components—student simulator, reward model, and multi-objective RL scheme—address core challenges in educational AI.

title: Native Audio-Visual Alignment for Generation
summary: Joint audio-video generation aims to synthesize temporally synchronized and semantically coherent visual-acoustic content. However, existing open-source methods mainly rely on either dual-tower designs with posterior alignment or fully unified tri-modal designs that mix textual context, audio and video in one shared space. The former weakens fine-grained audio-video co-evolution, while the latter couples semantic conditioning with low-level synchronization. To address these limitations, we propose NAVA, a Native Audio-Visual Alignment framework for joint audio-video generation. NAVA is built upon context-conditioned native audio-visual alignment: it first establishes audio-video correspondence in a dedicated interaction space, and then uses external context to condition the joint denoising process. Specifically, NAVA is instantiated with an Align-then-Fuse MMDiT architecture, which transitions from modality-aware audio-video alignment to modality-shared joint denoising. Furthermore, we introduce Timbre-in-Context Conditioning to associate reference timbre cues with corresponding speech spans to achieve controllable speech timbre. Experiments on Verse-Bench and Seed-TTS, together with a user study, demonstrate that NAVA achieves superior video quality, precise audio-visual synchronization, competitive audio quality, and stronger reference-timbre controllability using only 6.3B parameters.

AI.tldr: NAVA introduces a native audio-visual alignment framework with an Align-then-Fuse MMDiT architecture and Timbre-in-Context Conditioning, achieving strong synchronization and controllable timbre with 6.3B parameters.
AI.motivation: Current methods either rely on dual-tower designs that weaken audio-video co-evolution or fully unified tri-modal spaces that entangle semantic conditioning with low-level synchronization; a dedicated interaction space for alignment can improve both synchronization and controllability.
AI.method: Align-then-Fuse MMDiT architecture first performs modality-aware audio-video alignment in a dedicated interaction space, then conducts modality-shared joint denoising conditioned by external context; Timbre-in-Context Conditioning links reference timbre cues to speech spans for controllable timbre; evaluated on Verse-Bench and Seed-TTS with a user study.
AI.result: NAVA achieves superior video quality, precise audio-visual synchronization, competitive audio quality, and stronger reference-timbre controllability, using 6.3B parameters.
AI.conclusion: Native audio-visual alignment is a viable framework for joint generation, offering improved co-evolution and controllability while maintaining parameter efficiency; the Align-then-Fuse strategy effectively transitions from alignment to denoising.

title: AgentDoG 1.5: A Lightweight and Scalable Alignment Framework for AI Agent Safety and Security
summary: Modern open-world agents such as OpenClaw exhibit powerful cross-environment execution capabilities yet introduce broad new safety risk sources. Meanwhile, advanced frontier AI models drastically lower attack barriers, rendering current agent alignment frameworks inadequate for real-world deployment. To tackle these emerging threats, we propose a lightweight and scalable agent safety alignment framework. Specifically, we update the agent safety taxonomy to accommodate emergent risks from Codex and OpenClaw execution scenarios. We further build a taxonomy-guided data engine with influence-function purification to train lightweight AgentDoG 1.5 variants (0.8B, 2B, 4B, and 8B parameters) using only around 1k samples, achieving comparable performance with leading closed-source models (e.g., GPT-5.4). Based on AgentDoG 1.5, we construct a highly efficient agentic safety SFT and RL training environment, which reduces deployment overhead in Docker-level environments by two orders of magnitude. Finally, we deploy AgentDoG 1.5 as a training-free online guardrail for real-time safety moderation. Extensive experimental results indicate that AgentDoG 1.5 achieves state-of-the-art performance in diverse and complex interactive agentic scenarios. All models and datasets are openly released.

AI.tldr: Proposes AgentDoG 1.5, a lightweight, taxonomy-guided safety alignment for cross-environment agents, achieving strong safety with data-efficient training and deployment-friendly guardrails; released openly.
AI.motivation: Emerging open-world agents and frontier AI models introduce new safety risks and misalignment challenges that outpace existing alignment frameworks and require scalable, data-efficient solutions suitable for real-time deployment.
AI.method: Update the safety taxonomy to include Codex/OpenClaw-like risks; build a taxonomy-guided data engine with influence-function purification; train AgentDoG 1.5 variants (0.8B, 2B, 4B, 8B) using ~1k samples; develop SFT and RL environments for lightweight deployment; deploy as a training-free online guardrail for real-time moderation.
AI.result: AgentDoG 1.5 achieves state-of-the-art safety performance in diverse interactive agentic scenarios, with performance comparable to leading closed-source models (e.g., GPT-5.4) using far fewer data and parameters; deployment overhead reduced two orders of magnitude in Docker environments; all models and datasets are openly released.
AI.conclusion: A scalable, data-efficient safety alignment framework for open-world agents that enables real-time moderation and practical deployment, with open resources to foster reproducibility and further development.

title: Cluster-Level Attention-Guided Parallel Decoding for Masked Diffusion Language Models
summary: Masked diffusion language models (MDLMs) enable parallel decoding by predicting all masked positions at each denoising step, yet existing training-free samplers usually decide which positions to commit at token-level granularity. We revisit this granularity and observe that reliable predictions often emerge as contiguous high-confidence spans, suggesting that the unit of parallel commitment can be larger than a single token. We first group adjacent high-confidence candidates into confidence-induced clusters (CICs) as span-level update units. We then use self-attention maps from the same forward pass to estimate inter-cluster dependencies, enabling conflict-aware selection of mutually compatible CICs for parallel commitment. This yields CLAD (Cluster-Level Attention-Guided Decoding), a training-free cluster-level decoder for MDLMs. Experiments on LLaDA and Dream model families across four reasoning and code-generation benchmarks show that CLAD achieves 1.77x--8.47x speedups over Vanilla decoding while maintaining broadly comparable task accuracy in most settings.

AI.tldr: Introduces CLAD, a training-free cluster-level decoding method for Masked Diffusion Language Models (MDLMs) that groups high-confidence adjacent tokens into confidence-induced clusters (CICs), uses forward-pass self-attention to estimate inter-cluster dependencies, and selects compatible CICs for parallel updates; achieves 1.77x–8.47x speedups with comparable accuracy on LLaDA and Dream models across reasoning and code-generation benchmarks.
AI.motivation: MDLMs enable parallel decoding but current training-free samplers select tokens individually, potentially underutilizing span-level structure. Observing that high-confidence predictions often form contiguous spans motivates a cluster-level approach to accelerate decoding without retraining.
AI.method: Identify high-confidence candidate tokens, group adjacent candidates into confidence-induced clusters (CICs) as span-level units; derive inter-CIC dependencies from self-attention maps in the same forward pass; perform conflict-aware selection of mutually compatible CICs for parallel commitment; implement as CLAD (Cluster-Level Attention-Guided Decoding).
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on LLaDA and Dream model families across four reasoning and code-generation benchmarks shows 1.77x–8.47x speedups over Vanilla decoding while maintaining broadly comparable task accuracy in most settings.
AI.conclusion: CLAD demonstrates that span-level, attention-informed cluster updates yield substantial decoding speedups for MDLMs with minimal accuracy loss, offering a training-free alternative to token-level samplers and highlighting the value of leveraging span structure in MDLM decoding.

title: Future Forcing: Future-aware Training-free KV Cache Policy for Autoregressive Video Generation
summary: Autoregressive (AR) video generation has emerged as a promising paradigm for long-horizon video synthesis, where each frame is generated conditioned on previously generated tokens. To accelerate inference, the KV cache is used to avoid redundant recomputation across generation steps. Nevertheless, its growth with generation length introduces increasing memory and error accumulation, limiting the scalability of AR models to even longer sequences. Existing KV cache compression methods mitigate this issue by selectively retaining only video tokens deemed important. However, most existing methods assess token importance using short-horizon signals derived from the current or historical generation context, making these methods prone to overlooking tokens that appear unimportant at early steps but later become critical for future frames. In this work, we identify an important property of trained AR video models: although RoPE-modulated queries evolve across autoregressive steps, the underlying canonical pre-RoPE query distribution remains remarkably stable throughout the video generation process. This approximate stationarity implies that future query distributions are estimable from historical statistics, enabling principled future-aware cache decisions without any additional training. Building on this insight, we propose Future Forcing, a training-free future-aware KV cache policy for AR video generation. Specifically, Future Forcing first constructs a future query proxy from historical statistics, then scores KV cache tokens by their importance under this proxy, and finally merges redundant token pairs within the affine subspace induced by the future query. Extensive experiments show that Future Forcing improves long-horizon consistency under limited KV caches, achieving up to 1.49 improvement in subject consistency on VBench-Long for 60s generation over existing AR video KV cache policies.

AI.tldr: Future Forcing is a training-free, future-aware KV cache policy for autoregressive (AR) video generation that leverages a stable pre-RoPE query distribution to estimate future token importance, enabling better long-horizon consistency under limited KV caches.
AI.motivation: To address memory growth and error accumulation in KV caches for long-horizon AR video generation, where short-horizon importance signals miss tokens that later become critical.
AI.method: Construct a future query proxy from historical statistics, score KV cache tokens by their importance under this proxy, and merge redundant token pairs within the affine subspace induced by the future query; this policy is training-free.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows improved long-horizon consistency under limited KV caches, with up to 1.49 improvements in subject consistency on VBench-Long for 60s generation compared to existing AR video KV cache policies.
AI.conclusion: Future Forcing provides a principled, training-free mechanism for future-aware KV caching, enhancing scalability of AR video generation to longer horizons.

title: PRAIB: Peer Review AI Benchmark of Behaviour of LLM-Assisted Reviewing
summary: The growing number of submitted papers has motivated the exploration of Large Language Models (LLMs) as a means to support and augment the peer review process, particularly in terms of improving its speed and scalability. Yet, it remains unknown whether LLMs engage with scientific manuscripts in the same manner as human reviewers, or whether they merely produce review-looking text. To address this, we introduce the Peer Review AI Benchmark (PRAIB), a novel framework comprising thoroughly defined metrics that measure review specificity, style, and behavior of engagement. To complement the PRAIB framework, we conduct a large-scale empirical study leveraging a dataset of 11,000 reviews generated by five proprietary and open-source models for 1,000 ICLR and NeurIPS papers. Spanning the 2021--2025 period, these machine-generated reviews are compared against original human feedback across diverse prompting strategies to identify systematic behavioral divergences. Our analysis reveals that the generated reviews diverge significantly from feedback provided by human reviewers: LLM ratings are less variable, positively biased, and overconfident, and their cross-reference patterns are model-dependent and distinct from human norms. Furthermore, when evaluated through PRAIB, we observe that LLMs tend to generate longer, more complex reviews, yet frequently overlook the atomic weaknesses noted by human reviewers. By characterizing where and how LLMs reviewing behavior departs from human norms, PRAIB provides the community with a diagnostic tool for identifying which aspects of the review process LLMs can reliably support today and which require further development before deployment.

AI.tldr: PRAIB reveals systematic divergences between LLM-generated reviews and human reviews; LLMs produce longer, more complex but less precise assessments, with overconfidence and model-dependent cross-references; PRAIB serves as a diagnostic for what LLMs can reliably support today and what requires further work.
AI.motivation: To determine whether LLMs engage with scientific manuscripts like human reviewers or merely produce review-like text, and to assess their reliability, biases, and safety in automated peer-review support.
AI.method: Large-scale empirical study using PRAIB: 11,000 reviews generated by five models for 1,000 ICLR/NeurIPS papers (2021–2025) across diverse prompting strategies; comparison against original human feedback; analysis focused on metrics of review specificity, style, and engagement.
AI.result: LLMs show less variability, positive bias, and overconfidence in ratings; cross-reference patterns are model-dependent and diverge from human norms; generated reviews tend to be longer/ more complex but miss atomic weaknesses noted by humans; PRAIB diagnostic identifies which aspects can be reliably supported today.
AI.conclusion: PRAIB provides a diagnostic framework to identify safe, reliable use-cases for LLM-assisted review today and highlights areas needing further alignment and development before deployment in real-world peer review.

title: MōLe-Λ: Learning the Coupled-Cluster Response State for Energies, Gradients, and Properties
summary: Coupled-cluster (CC) theory is often considered the gold standard of quantum chemistry, but its high computational cost limits routine access to accurate energies, forces and response properties. While the right-hand $T$-amplitudes determine the correlated wavefunction, many practically important observables additionally require the left-hand $Λ$-amplitudes. We introduce MōLe-$Λ$, an extension of Molecular Orbital Learning (MōLe) that predicts the full ground-state coupled-cluster singles and doubles (CCSD) response state by jointly learning right-hand amplitudes $(T_1,T_2)$ and left-hand amplitudes $(Λ_1,Λ_2)$ from localized Hartree--Fock molecular orbitals. Architecturally, MōLe-$Λ$ extends MōLe with $Λ_1$ and $Λ_2$ readouts that mirror the symmetry constraints of the $T_1$ and $T_2$ heads, while preserving the original equivariant orbital encoder, odd sign-equivariant decoding, locality and size-extensivity. The resulting model yields accurate CC-quality energies and forces, while simultaneously recovering dipoles, quadrupoles, polarizabilities, the electron density, and 2-electron observables such as the pair density. We show that MōLe-$Λ$ further extends the speed advantage of MōLe over full CCSD while substantially expanding the accessible properties, providing a route to wavefunction-level surrogate models for correlated quantum chemistry.

AI.tldr: MōLe-Λ extends Molecular Orbital Learning to predict full CCSD response states by jointly learning T and Λ amplitudes from localized HF orbitals, restoring CC quality energies/forces and a wide set of properties with improved speed over full CCSD.
AI.motivation: CCSD is highly accurate but computationally expensive; many observables require left-hand Λ amplitudes. A fast surrogate that yields energies, forces, and diverse response properties would greatly broaden practical access to wavefunction-level information.
AI.method: Architectural extension of MōLe by adding Λ1 and Λ2 readouts that mirror T1/T2 symmetry constraints, preserving the equivariant orbital encoder, odd-sign-equivariant decoding, locality, and size-extensivity. Trains on localized Hartree–Fock orbitals to jointly predict right-hand amplitudes (T1,T2) and left-hand amplitudes (Λ1,Λ2), yielding the CCSD response state and CC-quality observables.
AI.result: MōLe-Λ delivers accurate CC-quality energies and forces while simultaneously recovering dipoles, quadrupoles, polarizabilities, electron density, and 2-electron observables such as the pair density. It retains MōLe’s speed advantage over full CCSD and substantially expands the accessible properties, enabling wavefunction-level surrogate modeling for correlated quantum chemistry.
AI.conclusion: MōLe-Λ provides a scalable, property-rich surrogate for CCSD response states, offering CC-level accuracy with a broad set of observables and improved efficiency, thus broadening practical access to correlated quantum chemistry.

title: Geometry Matters: 3D Foundation Priors for Learning Semantic Correspondence
summary: Foundation features from self-supervised vision models and text-to-image diffusion models have proven effective for semantic correspondence estimation. However, because these features are learned primarily from 2D image objectives, they lack explicit 3D awareness and often confuse symmetric object sides, repeated parts, and visually similar structures that are distinct in 3D. We introduce a 3D-aware post-training framework that goes beyond available 2D foundation features by incorporating priors from 3D foundation models. Given an image, our method uses SAM3D to estimate object geometry and pose, and refines the pose through render-and-compare optimization. Subsequently, we render PartField descriptors from the reconstructed geometry into the image plane based on the estimated object pose. The resulting geometry-aware feature maps complement DINO and Stable Diffusion features, while geodesic distances on the reconstructed shapes enable reliable filtering of candidate correspondences. We use the filtered matches as supervision to train a lightweight adapter on top of DINO and Stable Diffusion for semantic correspondence. In contrast to prior post-training approaches that require pose annotations and rely on coarse spherical geometry, our method automatically obtains instance-specific 3D structure and uses it to guide correspondence learning. Experiments show that our approach improves semantic correspondence over the prior methods while reducing manual geometric supervision. Code and model can be found at https:/github.com/GenIntel/3D-SC.

AI.tldr: A 3D-aware post-training framework enhances semantic correspondence by integrating 3D priors from SAM3D and PartField descriptors with 2D foundation features (DINO/Stable Diffusion), using render-and-compare for pose refinement and geodesic-based filtering to supervise a lightweight adapter, reducing the need for explicit pose annotations.
AI.motivation: 2D foundation features often lack explicit 3D awareness, struggle with symmetric parts, repeated structures, and 3D-distinguishable shapes; incorporating instance-specific 3D structure can improve semantic correspondence without heavy geometric supervision.
AI.method: 1) Use SAM3D to estimate object geometry and pose; refine pose via render-and-compare optimization; 2) Render PartField descriptors from the reconstructed geometry into the image plane using the estimated pose; 3) Use geometry-aware maps to complement DINO and Stable Diffusion features; 4) Compute geodesic distances on reconstructed shapes to filter candidate correspondences; 5) Train a lightweight adapter on top of DINO and Stable Diffusion using the filtered matches as supervision; 6) No pose annotations required; instantiates 3D structure automatically; 7) Provide code and model at the given GitHub URL.
AI.result: The approach improves semantic correspondence relative to prior post-training methods and reduces the need for manual geometric supervision; it demonstrates the benefit of integrating 3D priors into 2D foundation-feature learning.
AI.conclusion: A 3D-aware post-training framework that leverages instance-specific 3D geometry guided by 3D foundation models can enhance semantic correspondence and reduce supervision, with a practical workflow and released code.

title: Harnessing non-adversarial robustness in large language models
summary: The work presents an approach for addressing the challenge of robustness in Large Language Models (LLMs) to alterations and potential errors caused by semantically similar but textually different prompts. Recent works have shown that these kinds of prompt variations can significantly impact the performance of LLMs on tasks. The central question is: can LLMs' robustness to semantically-neutral prompt alterations be acquired without expensive retraining of the entire model? We address this question both theoretically and through experiments. Our theoretical analysis reveals a crucial factor impacting model robustness - a systematic expected shift or perturbation-induced bias in neural network module outputs. Motivated by this analysis, we show that robustness can be achieved via a simple fine-tuning process: debiasing for robustness. We identify conditions when debiasing helps and when it does not, and demonstrate, through both theory and extensive experiments, that debiasing for robustness may indeed be a quick and efficient tool to enhance robustness and provide certification against random prompt perturbations.

AI.tldr: A lightweight debiasing-for-robustness approach that makes LLMs robust to semantically equivalent prompt changes, supported by theory and experiments; enables fast robustness gains and potential certification without full retraining.
AI.motivation: LLMs are sensitive to semantically-neutral prompt variations; achieving robustness without expensive full-model retraining is desirable.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis reveals a perturbation-induced bias in neural-module outputs. Based on this, a simple fine-tuning procedure—debiasing for robustness—is proposed. The work identifies conditions under which debiasing helps or does not and substantiates claims with theory and extensive experiments.
AI.result: Debiasing for robustness can be a quick, efficient tool to enhance robustness and provide certification against random prompt perturbations, under the identified conditions.
AI.conclusion: Debiasing-based robustness is a practical, low-cost approach to improve LLM reliability against prompt perturbations, with theoretical justification and empirical validation.

title: Relational Rank Geometry in Transformers: Detecting and Steering Hidden-State Relation Frames
summary: Transformer hidden states are often interpreted through local or low-order objects: neurons, sparse features, attention heads, residual-stream directions, or activation patches. This paper studies a complementary object: the rank-indexed geometry of relations among token tuples. I use Plucker sign entropy to test whether r-argument relations leave arity-matched orientation signatures in hidden-state space. Across Llama-family 8B, 70B, and 405B checkpoints, true relation tuples show stronger orientation-sign consistency at the expected rank k=r for r=3,...,6 than scrambled tuples under matched random-control audits. Multi-template audits show that the effects survive surface variation, with all tested 405B rows retaining positive expected-rank margins and 8B/70B retaining positive rows with constructor-specific mixed cells. I then ask whether the same relation geometry can be steered. In an edge-grid clean/corrupt intervention assay over 32 prompts, the row/column scaffold and answer format stay fixed while the YES/NO relation map changes, and the corrupt hidden-state relation frame is patched toward clean or placebo targets. In 70B and 405B, clean-targeted relation-frame paths recover clean-answer behavior and residual relation geometry, while centroid-only and equal-norm controls show negligible recovery. Site/order controls further separate marker-site importance from ordered clean-frame geometry: target clean shape and cross-prompt clean shape recover behavior and residual geometry at the marker interface, whereas corrupt-donor transfer, same-site permutation/reflection, wrong-site clean deltas, centroid-only motion, and equal-norm noise fail or remain far below clean-frame paths. The result is a controlled bridge from relation probing to relation-frame intervention: relation rank geometry can be detected, targeted, and behaviorally validated in transformer hidden states.

AI.tldr: Explores rank-indexed relation geometry in transformer hidden states using Plucker sign entropy; demonstrates detectability and steerability of relation frames across Llama models via targeted interventions.
AI.motivation: To move beyond local/low-order interpretables and examine higher-order relations among token tuples, assessing whether arity-r matched orientation signatures exist in hidden-state space and can be controlled.
AI.method: Compute Plucker sign entropy on r-arity relations for r=3..6; compare true vs scrambled tuples across Llama 8B, 70B, 405B; perform multi-template audits; edge-grid intervention with clean/corrupt targets across 32 prompts; control variants (centroid-only, equal-norm, marker-site, etc.); assess recovery of clean-answer behavior and geometry.
AI.result: True relation tuples show stronger orientation consistency at arity r; effects survive surface variation; 405B shows all positive margins; 8B/70B mixed; edge-grid interventions partially recover clean geometry and behavior when targets are clean; various controls fail to recover; marker-site controls reveal site vs geometry contributions.
AI.conclusion: Relation rank geometry is detectable, targetable, and behaviorally testable in transformer hidden states, enabling a bridge from probing to intervention and advancing interpretability of high-order relational structure in language models.

title: Evaluation of Conversational Agents: Understanding Culture, Context and Environment in Emotion Detection
summary: Valuable decisions and highly prioritized analysis now depend on applications such as facial biometrics, social media photo tagging, and human robots interactions. However, the ability to successfully deploy such applications is based on their efficiencies on tested use cases taking into consideration possible edge cases. Over the years, lots of generalized solutions have been implemented to mimic human emotions including sarcasm. However, factors such as geographical location or cultural difference have not been explored fully amidst its relevance in resolving ethical issues and improving conversational AI (Artificial Intelligence). In this paper, we seek to address the potential challenges in the usage of conversational AI within Black African society. We develop an emotion prediction model with accuracies ranging between 85% and 96%. Our model combines both speech and image data to detect the seven basic emotions with a focus on also identifying sarcasm. It uses 3-layers of the Convolutional Neural Network in addition to a new Audio-Frame Mean Expression (AFME) algorithm and focuses on model pre-processing and post-processing stages. In the end, our proposed solution contributes to maintaining the credibility of an emotion recognition system in conversational AIs.

AI.tldr: Multimodal emotion recognition in African context using speech and image data with an AFME algorithm and a 3-layer CNN to detect seven basic emotions (including sarcasm) with high accuracy (85-96%), focusing on ethics and cultural considerations in conversational AI.
AI.motivation: Address challenges and biases in emotion recognition within Black African society; improve ethical robustness and credibility of conversational AI.
AI.method: A multimodal approach combining speech and image data, implemented with a 3-layer convolutional neural network, augmented by a new Audio-Frame Mean Expression (AFME) algorithm, emphasizing pre-processing and post-processing stages.
AI.result: Achieves 85-96% accuracy in detecting seven basic emotions and sarcasm; proposes a solution aimed at maintaining credibility of emotion recognition in conversational AIs within the target context.
AI.conclusion: Contributes to understanding and mitigating context-specific challenges in emotion recognition, highlighting the need for culturally aware models and evaluation.

title: Quantifying and Optimizing Simplicity via Polynomial Representations
summary: Deep networks often exhibit a preference for "simple" solutions, and such a simplicity bias is widely believed to play a key role in generalization. Yet a broadly applicable, quantitative measure of simplicity remains elusive.
