AI.method: Empirical study across three creative task families comparing independent generation vs semantic direction stratification, with self-, peer-, and representative-anchor baselines under neutral and population-referential divergent instructions. Evaluation considers semantic diversity, quality proxies, and compute using full-pipeline token accounting.
AI.result: Population-referential divergence acts as a strong low-cost baseline, increasing semantic diversity while preserving quality proxies. Semantic direction stratification is stronger, with a single planning call organizing generations across broad semantic directions, yielding the best diversity–quality–compute frontier. Anchored regeneration can boost final-pool diversity, but its advantage shrinks under full-pipeline token accounting.
AI.conclusion: Anchorless baselines are practical and effective for open-ended LLM ideation, with semantic direction stratification offering the strongest performance when compute is accounted for.

title: Midpoint Generative Models
summary: We introduce Midpoint Generative Models (MGM), a principled framework for training one-step generative models. MGM is based on a simple symmetry of Flow Matching with linear interpolation: when the two endpoint distributions coincide, the corresponding drift field vanishes at the midpoint time, $t=1/2$. We show that the norm of this field defines a valid discrepancy between distributions, which we call the Midpoint Divergence. We extend this discrepancy beyond the midpoint by introducing randomly flipped interpolations and further generalize it by replacing deterministic linear Flow Matching interpolations with symmetric stochastic interpolants, yielding a generalized Midpoint Divergence. Finally, we derive a variational formulation of our generalized divergence, yielding a tractable objective for training a one-step generator. The resulting MGM algorithm offers an effective and theoretically grounded approach to generative modeling, achieving competitive performance against existing one-step generative modeling methods.

AI.tldr: MGM defines a Midpoint Divergence from the flow-field at t=1/2 in Flow Matching, and extends it with symmetric stochastic interpolants to train a one-step generator via a variational objective, achieving competitive performance among one-step models.
AI.motivation: To develop a principled, efficient one-step generative model by exploiting a symmetry at the flow-interpolation midpoint and formulating a tractable objective.
AI.method: Identify the drift field in Flow Matching; show its norm at t=1/2 serves as a valid distribution discrepancy (Midpoint Divergence). Extend to random flipped interpolations and symmetric stochastic interpolants to generalize the divergence; derive a variational objective that enables training of a one-step generator.
AI.result: Proposes MGM, a theoretically grounded one-step generative framework, with a tractable objective and competitive empirical performance against existing one-step methods.
AI.conclusion: MGM provides a unified, principled approach to one-step generation by leveraging midpoint symmetry and a generalized divergence, with a practical variational objective and competitive performance.

title: Veda: Scalable Video Diffusion via Distilled Sparse Attention
summary: Scaling Diffusion Transformers to generate high-resolution, long videos is constrained by the quadratic cost of self-attention, and existing sparse attention methods degrade under high sparsity. We show empirically that generation quality is determined not by the sparsity ratio itself, but by how well the sparse mask aligns with the tile-wise geometry of full attention. Based on this insight, we propose Veda, a distilled sparse attention framework that formulates tile selection as an explicit reconstruction problem from full attention. Veda integrates statistics-aware tile scoring with head-aware tiling to reduce estimation error and structural mismatch, enabling aggressive sparsity. A hardware-efficient tile-skipping kernel converts theoretical sparsity into practical wall-clock speedups. Experiments on large video diffusion models, including Waver and Wan2.1, demonstrate substantial acceleration with no noticeable degradation in generation quality. To generate 720P 10-second videos on Waver-T2V-12B, Veda achieves a 5.1$\times$ end-to-end speedup and a 10.5$\times$ self-attention speedup, reducing attention overhead from 92% to 50%. Notably, the gains increase with sequence length, indicating that Veda scales favorably with spatiotemporal resolution across models.

AI.tldr: Veda introduces a distilled sparse-attention framework for diffusion transformers that aligns sparse masks with tile-wise full-attention geometry, enabling aggressive sparsity and large-scale video generation with substantial speedups while preserving quality.
AI.motivation: Self-attention costs are quadratic and limit long-video generation; high sparsity can hurt quality; need mask-tile alignment and efficient hardware to scale diffusion models to high-resolution videos.
AI.method: Formulates tile selection as reconstruction from full attention; combines statistics-aware tile scoring and head-aware tiling; introduces a hardware-efficient tile-skipping kernel; end-to-end integration with large video diffusion models.
AI.result: Substantial speedups: 5.1x end-to-end and 10.5x self-attention on 720p 10s with Waver-T2V-12B; attention overhead reduced from 92% to 50%; gains scale with sequence length; no noticeable quality degradation.
AI.conclusion: Veda enables scalable, efficient sparse-attention for long videos; architecture yields favorable scaling with spatiotemporal resolution and can be broadly beneficial for large video diffusion models.

title: Temporal Stability and Few-Shot Prompting in Math Task Assessment
summary: As AI tools become increasingly integrated into educational contexts, questions arise about both their stability over time and their responsiveness to prompt engineering techniques. This longitudinal study focused on different AI tools' ability to use the Task Analysis Guide (TAG; Stein \& Smith, 1998) to classify the cognitive demand of mathematics tasks. In particular, it examined whether this classification ability changed with (1) model version updates over time and (2) few-shot prompting using exemplar tasks. We tested a general-purpose AI tool (Gemini) and an education-specific AI tool (Coteach). The specific tools were selected because of their relatively high performance on relevant published benchmarks and prior task-specific tests. Models were tested at baseline, retested with model version updates, and then tested again using few-shot prompting (two exemplar tasks for each cognitive demand category). Results revealed that newer model versions alone produced mixed effects: Gemini's accuracy remained stable at 58\%, while Coteach's accuracy decreased from 75\% to 50\%. However, few-shot prompting improved both models' performance: Gemini increased to 67\% and Coteach recovered to 75\% accuracy. These findings demonstrate that prompt engineering techniques can have larger and more reliable effects than passive model improvements, and that version updates may not always improve performance on specialized educational tasks. The study has important implications for how educators and researchers should approach AI tool selection, evaluation, and implementation in educational contexts.

AI.tldr: Few-shot prompting yields larger and more reliable gains in cognitive-demand classification of math tasks than model-version updates; version updates can be neutral or detrimental. Baselines: Gemini 58%, Coteach 75%; with prompts: Gemini 67%, Coteach 75%.
AI.motivation: Examine stability of AI tools over time and responsiveness to prompt engineering in classifying cognitive demand of math tasks using the Task Analysis Guide (TAG).
AI.method: Longitudinal study testing two AI tools (Gemini and Coteach). Measurements at baseline, after model-version updates, and after few-shot prompting with two exemplar tasks per cognitive-demand category.
AI.result: Version updates yielded mixed effects (Gemini stable at 58%; Coteach declined 75%→50%). Few-shot prompting improved both tools (Gemini 58%→67%; Coteach 50%→75%).
AI.conclusion: Prompt engineering can have larger, more reliable effects than passive model improvements; version updates do not universally improve performance on specialized educational tasks; implications for tool selection, evaluation, and implementation in education.

title: A Triple-Modal Contrastive Learning Framework with Sequence, Graph, and 3D Features for Drug-Target Interaction Prediction
summary: Accurate prediction of drug-target interactions (DTI) is critical for drug discovery. Existing methods often rely on single-modal representations (e.g., sequences or graphs) or combine only two modalities, overlooking 3D structural features. To address this challenge, we propose TriMod-DTI, a triple-modal contrastive learning framework that incorporates 1D sequences, 2D graphs, and 3D structures of drugs and proteins, obtaining the universal and complementary feature representations for DTI prediction. We design a Feature Extractor to capture drug and target features across the three modalities, thereby enriching their representations. We further propose a triple-modal contrastive learning strategy to align different modal representations of the same drug or protein in the latent space. By constructing cross-modal positive and negative sample pairs, this approach enhances the model's discriminative ability. Experiments on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that TriMod-DTI outperforms state-of-the-art methods. The ablation studies validate the contributions of each modality. Moreover, case studies highlight its practical potential for DTI prediction and drug discovery.

AI.tldr: TriMod-DTI introduces a triple-modal contrastive learning framework that integrates 1D sequences, 2D graphs, and 3D structures for both drugs and proteins to predict drug–target interactions, achieving state-of-the-art results on three benchmarks and with ablations confirming each modality's contribution.
AI.motivation: Current DTI models typically use a single modality (sequence or graph) or combine only two modalities, often neglecting 3D structural information, which limits predictive performance and generalization.
AI.method: A Feature Extractor captures drug and target representations across 1D, 2D, and 3D modalities. A triple-modal contrastive learning strategy aligns representations of the same molecule/protein across modalities by constructing cross-modal positive and negative pairs, thus enriching latent representations for DTI prediction.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on three benchmark datasets shows TriMod-DTI outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Ablation studies demonstrate the contribution of each modality, and case studies illustrate practical potential for DTI prediction and drug discovery.
AI.conclusion: Triple-modal integration with contrastive learning yields universal, complementary features for DTI, improving predictive accuracy and offering a robust framework for drug discovery involving multimodal data.

title: Meta-Cognitive Memory Policy Optimization for Long-Horizon LLM Agents
summary: Memory-augmented LLM agents tackle complex long-horizon tasks by recursively summarizing interaction trajectories into compact memory. However, existing approaches typically train these memory policies using outcome-based reinforcement learning, failing to localize where intermediate memory quality degrades. As interactions unfold, ambiguous recursive summaries progressively discard task-relevant information and introduce semantic noise. This exacerbates belief deviation, obscuring the agent's estimate of the latent task state and ultimately derailing long-horizon reasoning. We therefore argue that memory optimization should focus not merely on trajectory-level success, but on the clarity of the belief induced by intermediate summaries. To this end, we introduce Belief Entropy, a self-supervised proxy that probes how uncertain the model remains about the latent task state given its current memory. Based on this proxy, we propose Metacognitive Memory Policy Optimization (MMPO). Instead of relying only on sparse outcome-based signals, MMPO provides fine-grained, memory-specific supervision via explicitly penalizing summaries that induce high epistemic uncertainty. Experiments show that MMPO consistently outperforms existing methods on diverse long-horizon tasks, maintaining 97.1% performance even when scaled to 1.75M-token contexts.

AI.tldr: Introduces Belief Entropy and Metacognitive Memory Policy Optimization (MMPO) to improve memory quality in memory-augmented LLMs by minimizing epistemic uncertainty in intermediate summaries, outperforming outcome-based RL on long-horizon tasks and scaling to 1.75M-token contexts.
AI.motivation: Existing memory-augmented LLM agents rely on outcome-based RL to optimize memory, which does not localize where intermediate memory quality degrades. As summaries accumulate, semantic noise and belief drift reduce the agent's estimate of the latent task state, harming long-horizon reasoning. There is a need for memory optimization that directly improves the clarity of the agent's beliefs.
AI.method: Propose Belief Entropy as a self-supervised proxy that measures the model's epistemic uncertainty about the latent task state given its current memory. Develop Metacognitive Memory Policy Optimization (MMPO), which provides memory-specific supervision by penalizing summaries that induce high epistemic uncertainty, rather than relying solely on sparse outcome signals.
AI.result: MMPO consistently outperforms existing methods on diverse long-horizon tasks and maintains 97.1% performance even when scaling contexts to 1.75M tokens.
AI.conclusion: Optimizing memory for the clarity of the belief, via Belief Entropy and MMPO, yields more robust long-horizon reasoning than trajectory-level success optimization, enabling substantial gains in very long contexts.

title: Treatment-Conditioned Diffusion for Forecasting Neurodegenerative Disease Progression
summary: Forecasting the progression of neurodegenerative diseases, such as Parkinson's disease, is essential for effective long-term planning and personalized therapeutic intervention. Existing systems typically produce scalar clinical scores that ignore the rich structure of longitudinal neuroimaging, while traditional generative approaches suffer from a loss of anatomical details and blurring subtle progression patterns. To address this, we introduce a novel treatment-conditioned diffusion framework that predicts high-fidelity future brain states by conditioning the generative process on patients' screening DaTscan images and levodopa equivalent daily dose over one year. The pipeline uses a Transformer-based encoder to represent non-linear, time-dependent pharmacological dynamics and optimizes generation through a multi-weight region-of-interest mask that focuses on biologically critical areas. Experimental evaluation shows that our framework maintains sharp anatomical boundaries and significantly improves clinical fidelity relative to the baseline, achieving 14.0% lower MSE, 7.2% lower MAE, and 4.9% higher SSIM.

AI.tldr: A diffusion model conditioned on DaTscan and levodopa dose predicts high-fidelity 1-year brain-state trajectories for Parkinson's, using Transformer encoding of pharmacodynamics and ROI-focused training; outperforms baselines on MSE, MAE, and SSIM.
AI.motivation: Current approaches rely on scalar clinical scores and often produce blurred, anatomically imprecise generative predictions. There is a need to preserve rich longitudinal imaging structure and incorporate treatment dynamics for personalized prognosis.
AI.method: Treatment-conditioned diffusion framework. Uses a Transformer-based encoder to model non-linear, time-dependent pharmacodynamics from screening DaTscan images and levodopa dose. Generation is guided by a multi-weight region-of-interest mask focusing on biologically critical brain regions, predicting brain states over one year.
AI.result: Sharper anatomical boundaries and superior clinical fidelity with 14.0% lower MSE, 7.2% lower MAE, and 4.9% higher SSIM versus a baseline.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that conditioning diffusion models on treatment data and using ROI-aware training can yield high-fidelity, anatomically consistent forecasts of neurodegenerative progression, supporting personalized planning and intervention.

title: Colored Noise Diffusion Sampling
summary: Diffusion models achieve state-of-the-art image synthesis, with their generative trajectories fundamentally exhibiting a spectral bias, resolving low-frequency global structures early and high-frequency fine details later. Conventional stochastic differential equation (SDE) solvers fail to account for this dynamic, naively injecting uniform white noise throughout the entire process and misusing the finite energy budget. In this work, we establish a mathematical framework that reconsiders SDE inference as a targeted, frequency-decoupled energy transfer. Leveraging this framework, we introduce Colored Noise Sampling (CNS), a novel, training-free stochastic solver. Rather than injecting uniform white noise, CNS utilizes a dynamic, timestep- and frequency-dependent schedule that more efficiently allocates injected energy toward structurally unresolved frequency bands. By actively exploiting the model's inherent spectral bias, CNS systematically steers the generated distribution toward the true data manifold. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CNS significantly outperforms standard ODE and SDE baselines as a strictly plug-and-play, inference-time sampler substitution across diverse architectures (SiT, JiT, FLUX). Compared to standard sampling on ImageNet-256, CNS achieves substantial unguided FID reductions, improving from 8.26 to 6.27 on SiT-XL/2, 32.39 to 26.69 on JiT-B/16, and 11.88 to 8.31 on JiT-H/16, while yielding consistent relative FID improvements with Classifier-Free Guidance. Project page is available at https://hadardavidson.github.io/CNS/.

AI.tldr: Introduces Colored Noise Sampling (CNS), a training-free diffusion-sampling method that uses frequency-aware energy transfer to exploit spectral bias, achieving substantial FID gains over standard solvers across multiple architectures on ImageNet-256.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models show a spectral bias (global structures formed early, high-frequency details later). Conventional SDE solvers inject uniform white noise throughout, misallocating energy and hindering sample quality. A frequency-aware inference strategy could improve efficiency and fidelity.
AI.method: Develop a mathematical framework for frequency-decoupled energy transfer in SDE inference. Propose CNS — a dynamic, timestep- and frequency-dependent noise scheduler that allocates injected energy toward unresolved frequency bands. CNS is training-free and plug-and-play as an inference-time sampler across architectures (SiT, JiT, FLUX).
AI.result: CNS significantly outperforms standard ODE/SDE baselines. On ImageNet-256: SiT-XL/2 improves FID from 8.26 to 6.27; JiT-B/16 from 32.39 to 26.69; JiT-H/16 from 11.88 to 8.31. Also yields consistent gains with Classifier-Free Guidance.
AI.conclusion: CNS demonstrates that leveraging a model's inherent spectral bias via a frequency-aware, training-free noise schedule yields strong, plug-and-play improvements in diffusion-based image synthesis, with broad applicability across architectures and potential for further exploration.

title: BioRefusalAudit: Auditing Biosecurity Refusal Depth Using General and Domain-Fine-Tuned Sparse Autoencoders
comment: 21 pages, 2 figures, 3 tables. Apart Research AIxBio Sprint hackathon paper, April 2026 (Track 3: AI Biosecurity Tools). Code, eval set, and SAEs: github.com/SolshineCode/Deleeuw-AI-x-Bio-hackathon. Reviewer feedback: apartresearch.com/project/biorefusalaudit-auditing-biosecurity-refusal-depth-using-general-and-domainfinetuned-sparse-autoencoders-1fyk
summary: Biosecurity evaluations of language models typically ask whether models produce hazardous output. This paper asks a complementary question: when a model refuses, is that refusal structurally sound, or does it disappear under modest changes to prompt framing, formatting, or output length? Across five architectures, no model cleanly discriminated benign from hazard. Gemma 2 2B-IT never genuinely refused across 75 prompts, hedging on every hazard-adjacent query. Gemma 4 E2B-IT refused 65/75 prompts with chat-template formatting and 0/75 without it. Both Gemma models collapsed to 0% under an 80-token cap. Qwen 2.5 1.5B and Phi-3-mini over-refused, flagging 83-87% of benign biology as hazardous. Llama 3.2 1B showed the only meaningful tier gradient (61-point spread). To probe what drives such over-refusal, we tested a panel of Schedule I but biologically non-toxic compounds (notably psilocybin cultivation, with FDA Breakthrough Therapy status). Some models refused these at rates exceeding genuinely hazardous biology, suggesting refusal tracks legality and cultural salience over CBRN hazard. To measure the internal side, we introduce a divergence score D comparing a model's surface response label to its internal sparse autoencoder (SAE) feature activations. Full D was computed on Gemma 2 2B-IT (Gemma Scope 1) and Gemma 4 E2B-IT (author-trained bio SAE). Two fine-tuned Gemma 2 domain SAEs were released. On Gemma 4, comply and refuse responses separated by a 0.647-point gap with zero overlap (n=75), though this is preliminary, with a narrow catalog, within-sample calibration, and Gemma-family-only SAE coverage. Built over one hackathon weekend on consumer hardware (GTX 1650 Ti Max-Q, plus Colab T4 for SAE training), this preliminary evidence suggests activation-level auditing may surface failure modes invisible to behavioral evaluation, with substantial variation across architectures.

AI.tldr: LM refusals are not robustly distinguishable between benign/hazardous prompts across architectures; internal representations (SAEs) reveal potential divergence; architecture-specific behavior and prompt framing strongly affect refusals; activation-level auditing shows promise but limited by scope.
AI.motivation: To complement hazard-output tests by assessing the structural soundness of model refusals and to investigate internal representations underlying refusals, across several architectures.
AI.method: Evaluate five models (Gemma 2 2B-IT, Gemma 4 E2B-IT, Qwen 2.5 1.5B, Phi-3-mini, Llama 3.2 1B) on 75 prompts with and without chat-template formatting; apply an 80-token cap; test non-toxic compounds to probe refusal drivers; compute a divergence score D between surface labels and SAE activations; release Gemma SAEs; compare comply vs refuse patterns; conduct preliminary calibration.
AI.result: No clean discrimination between benign and hazardous prompts; Gemma 2 2B-IT rarely refused; Gemma 4 E2B-IT shows strong formatting-dependent refusals; 80-token cap yields 0% refusals; Qwen and Phi over-refused benign biology; Llama shows a gradient; non-toxic compounds trigger refusals linked more to legality/cultural salience; D reveals separation between comply and refuse in Gemma 4; preliminary, sample-limited SAE results; cross-architecture variation.
AI.conclusion: Activation-level auditing may reveal failure modes not captured by behavioral tests; substantial variation across architectures; need broader, more systematic SAE coverage and larger datasets; cautious interpretation due to limited scope.

title: CLUBench: A Clustering Benchmark
summary: Clustering is a fundamental problem in data science with a long-standing research history, yielding numerous insightful algorithms. Despite this progress, a systematic and large-scale empirical evaluation that jointly considers conventional algorithms, deep learning-based methods, and recent foundation model-based clustering remains largely absent, leading to limited guidance on algorithm selection and deployment. To address this gap, we introduce CLUBench, a comprehensive clustering benchmark comprising 24 algorithms of diverse principles evaluated on 131 datasets across tabular, text, and image data, involving 178,815 experiments. Importantly, our analyses of (i) the impact of hyperparameter tuning,(ii) the impact of data types and characteristics,(iii) the impact of pretrained embeddings,(iv) large language model-based clustering,(v) the similarity of algorithms, and (vi) the low-rank structures of performance matrices, yield meaningful insights and promising pathways for clustering research. For instance, our study reveals that: 1) All evaluated deep clustering methods do not exhibit a significant advantage compared with the top-performing conventional clustering algorithms (e.g., KMeans, SpeClu) in terms of average performance; 2) For image and text clustering tasks, combining pretrained embeddings with conventional clustering algorithms (e.g., KMeans, SpeClu) offers effective and efficient clustering; 3) Clustering remains a challenging and nontrivial problem, even in the era of increasingly dominant foundation models. Moreover, we propose to use the low-rank structure in cross-model performance matrices to efficiently approximate the overall performance evaluation in practical applications. We further demonstrate the feasibility of model selection based on the performance matrices across all hyperparameter configurations.

AI.tldr: A large-scale benchmark (CLUBench) evaluates 24 clustering algorithms across 131 datasets (tabular, text, image) involving 178k experiments; key findings show deep clustering offers little average advantage over strong conventional methods; combining pretrained embeddings with conventional clustering is effective for image/text; foundation-model-based clustering is explored but not decisively superior; low-rank structures enable efficient performance estimation and model selection.
AI.motivation: To provide a comprehensive, cross-modal, large-scale empirical evaluation of clustering algorithms, including conventional, deep-learning-based, and foundation-model-based methods, to guide algorithm selection and deployment.
AI.method: A benchmark suite (CLUBench) evaluating 24 clustering algorithms on 131 datasets spanning tabular, text, and image data; analyses include the impact of hyperparameter tuning, data type characteristics, pretrained embeddings, LLM-based clustering, algorithm similarity, and low-rank structures of performance matrices; proposes low-rank approximations to facilitate efficient evaluation and model selection across hyperparameters.
AI.result: Deep clustering methods do not show significant average gains over top conventional algorithms (e.g., KMeans, SpeClu); for image/text, using pretrained embeddings with conventional clustering is effective; foundation-model-based clustering is explored but not dominating; low-rank structure enables efficient approximation of overall performance and enables model selection based on cross-configuration performance matrices.
AI.conclusion: Clustering remains challenging despite advances; CLUBench offers a scalable evaluation framework and highlights practical guidance (e.g., leverage pretrained embeddings with conventional methods for image/text); the low-rank perspective provides a scalable path for future model selection and performance estimation.

title: REST3D: Reconstructing Physically Stable 3D Scenes from a Single Image
summary: Reconstructing physically stable 3D scenes from a single RGB image enables casual images to be converted into simulation-ready digital assets for applications such as immersive interaction and content creation. However, existing single-image reconstruction methods fall short in capturing the physical structure of a scene. As a result, they often produce geometrically plausible but physically inconsistent results, including object floating and penetration, which lead to unstable behavior in physics simulations. Image-conditioned scene generation methods improve physical plausibility but often rely on strong scene priors, yielding plausible yet inaccurate object arrangements that fail to match the input image. We propose REST3D, a single-image reconstruction framework that can reconstruct physically stable 3D scenes by integrating physical scene understanding with physics-constrained refinement. We first introduce an agentic physical scene understanding technique that constructs a scene-tree representation capturing object physical states and inter-object relationships from a gravity-support perspective, providing a structural prior for reconstruction. Leveraging this structure, we initialize the scene using image-to-3D models, followed by scene-tree-guided alignment and physics-constrained optimization to resolve physical violations while preserving visual consistency with the input image. Experiments show that our method significantly reduces physical errors and improves simulation stability on both synthetic and real-world datasets while maintaining strong reconstruction quality. We further demonstrate the reconstructed scenes in VR-based human-object interaction, showing their potential for immersive applications.

AI.tldr: REST3D introduces a single-image 3D reconstruction pipeline that enforces physical plausibility by combining a scene-tree prior of gravity-based object relations with physics-constrained refinement, yielding stable, simulation-ready scenes from a single RGB image.
AI.motivation: Single-image reconstructions often produce geometrically plausible but physically inconsistent results (e.g., floating objects, penetrations). Image-conditioned methods improve visual plausibility but can rely on strong priors that misplace objects. A method that jointly infers a physical structure and enforces physics constraints can produce both visually faithful and physically stable scenes.
AI.method: 1) Build an agentic physical scene understanding module that creates a scene-tree representing object physical states and inter-object gravity-support relationships from a gravity-centric perspective. 2) Initialize the scene with image-to-3D models. 3) Apply scene-tree-guided alignment and physics-constrained optimization to remove physical violations while preserving visual consistency with the input image.
AI.result: The approach reduces physical errors and improves simulation stability on synthetic and real data, while maintaining reconstruction quality, and enables VR-based human-object interaction demonstrations, showing practical application potential.
AI.conclusion: REST3D can reconstruct physically stable 3D scenes from a single image by integrating a scene-structure prior with physics-based refinement, enabling stable simulations and immersive applications.

title: Modularizing Educational LLM-Agency for Fostering Responsible Learning Assistance
comment: 12 pages, 2 figures (+ 2 in appendix), accepted at AISoLA 2025 (Track: Responsible and Trusted AI: An Interdisciplinary Perspective)
summary: The widespread adoption of AI chatbots in education will drastically change learning, making responsible deployment a critical concern. While large language models (LLMs) might have access to sources discussing insights from educational sciences, they are not particularly inclined to adhere to pedagogical concepts, risking negative effects on the learning process, such as a loss of transfer capabilities, critical thinking, or creativity. In this paper, we introduce an agentic AI chatbot architecture assisting students with exercise solving, specifically designed to contribute to more responsible AI use in education. We base our conceptual development on the identification of several desiderata for responsible LLM-based educational systems, argue for the structural shortcomings inherent in monolithic, out-of-the-box solutions, and instead suggest modularizing the agentic architecture. We propose specific modules for different stages of exercise solving, enabling incorporation of targeted pedagogical advice, guiding students through the learning process in a more controllable, transparent, and overseeable manner.

AI.tldr: Proposes a modular, agentic AI chatbot architecture for education to support responsible use of LLMs in solving exercises, embedding pedagogical guidance and transparency.
AI.motivation: To mitigate risks of LLM-based educational tools (e.g., reduced transfer, critical thinking, creativity) and address the inadequacies of monolithic, out-of-the-box solutions by enabling controllable, supervised learning experiences.
AI.method: Develop a modular architecture with specialized modules for different stages of exercise solving, each capable of incorporating targeted pedagogical advice; argue for a designer-oriented, transparent, and overseable system over monolithic LLMs; base the design on identified desiderata for responsible LLM-based educational systems.
AI.result: Conceptual framework and architectural proposal; no empirical results reported in the abstract; intended to guide future implementation and evaluation of responsible educational AI.
AI.conclusion: A modular, agentic design can address structural shortcomings of monolithic LLMs and support more responsible, transparent AI-assisted learning in education.

title: From Short Histories to Long Futures: Horizon-Aware Graph Neural Networks for Long Horizon Forecasting
comment: Accepted for International Conference on Pattern Recognition (ICPR) 2026
summary: Accurate long-range prediction of geophysical systems is difficult due to strongly nonlinear dynamics, the high computational cost of full-physics simulations, and the error accumulation that arise when one-step autoregressive surrogates are rolled out over decades. Deep neural network can serve as efficient emulators, but most are trained only for next-step prediction and often drift or become unstable as the forecast horizon grows. We propose a multi-horizon graph neural network emulator that learns state-to-state transitions from a single current time to multiple future lead times within one unified model. The physical domain is represented as a graph, where nodes correspond to spatial locations with time-varying geophysical attributes and edges encode local spatial interactions. Given the current graph state, the model predicts the future evolution of key fields, ice thickness and ice velocities at all nodes, using a shared graph backbone with separate output branches for each target variable. To improve stability, the network predicts state increments relative to the current state, which are then added back to reconstruct future states. Training jointly optimizes all lead times with a unified regression objective, and inference uses a coarse-to-fine rollout that advances with larger jumps and selectively refines with shorter jumps to reduce drift and avoid redundant computation. Experiments on multi-decadal Pine Island Glacier simulations show that our approach achieves higher long-range accuracy and improved stability than both (i) an initial-state baseline that predicts each future time directly from the starting state and (ii) a standard single-step autoregressive rollout, producing a more reliable emulator for downstream climate and sea-level studies.

AI.tldr: A multi-horizon graph neural network emulator predicts long-range geophysical evolution by modeling state-to-state transitions across multiple future lead times in one model, using a graph-based representation with increment predictions, unified training, and a coarse-to-fine rollout. Demonstrated on multi-decadal Pine Island Glacier simulations, it outperforms an initial-state baseline and a single-step autoregressive rollout in long-horizon accuracy and stability.
AI.motivation: Long-range geophysical forecasting is difficult due to strongly nonlinear dynamics, costly full-physics simulations, and error accumulation in one-step rollouts. A stable, efficient emulator capable of multi-horizon predictions would enable reliable climate and sea-level projections.
AI.method: Represent the physical domain as a graph (nodes = spatial locations with geophysical attributes; edges = local spatial interactions). Use a shared graph neural network backbone with separate heads for ice thickness and ice velocities. Predict state increments relative to the current state for future lead times, and reconstruct future states by adding increments. Train jointly across all lead times with a unified regression objective. Inference uses a coarse-to-fine rollout: advance with large jumps, then refine with shorter jumps to reduce drift and save compute. Evaluate on multi-decadal Pine Island Glacier simulations, comparing against an initial-state baseline (future state from the starting state) and a standard single-step autoregressive rollout.
AI.result: The approach achieves higher long-range accuracy and improved stability than both baselines, yielding more reliable emulator performance for downstream climate and sea-level applications.
AI.conclusion: A unified multi-horizon graph-based emulator with increment predictions and coarse-to-fine rollout provides more robust, scalable long-range forecasts for geophysical systems, with demonstrated benefits on Pine Island Glacier simulations and potential applicability to other systems.

title: Double-Edged Sword or Sharp Tool? Designing and Evaluating Triadic LLM-Teacher Collaboration for K-12 Writing at Scale
summary: The double-edged sword of integrating Large Language Models (LLMs) requires an effective triadic collaboration mechanism among LLMs, teachers and students, especially for K-12 education. By developing a triadic collaboration system to support K-12 writing learning, a multidimensional evaluation framework grounded in Systemic Functional Linguistics and the suggestion trajectory tracing pipeline, this paper contributes a large-scale empirical dataset involving $57,954$ essays from $10,195$ students across $120$ schools over two years. Our findings confirm the efficacy of this system in improving writing quality through a strategic labor division: the LLM serves as a generative engine to mitigate teacher burnout, and the teacher acts as a pedagogical gatekeeper and bridge to guarantee feedback quality. While both LLM and teacher are critical for skill improvement, we uncover a ceiling effect where excessive linguistic expansion yields diminishing marginal utility. These suggest a dynamically adaptive LLM-teacher collaboration as student proficiency increases.

AI.tldr: A triadic LLM–teacher–student framework for K-12 writing improves outcomes with large-scale data, but reveals a diminishing return from excessive linguistic expansion; adaptive collaboration is recommended as student proficiency grows.
AI.motivation: To enable effective LLM integration in K-12 writing by balancing generative assistance with teacher oversight, reducing teacher burnout, and ensuring feedback quality; grounded in Systemic Functional Linguistics (SFL) and trajectory tracing of suggestions.
AI.method: Develop a triadic collaboration system and a multidimensional evaluation framework based on SFL and a suggestion trajectory tracing pipeline; collect and analyze a large-scale dataset comprising 57,954 essays from 10,195 students across 120 schools over two years.
AI.result: The system improves writing quality; the LLM acts as a generative engine to alleviate teacher workload, while the teacher serves as gatekeeper to ensure feedback quality. Both LLM and teacher contribute to skill improvement, but there is a ceiling effect where excessive linguistic expansion yields diminishing marginal utility; results support dynamically adaptive LLM–teacher collaboration as proficiency grows.
AI.conclusion: A dynamically adaptive LLM–teacher collaboration is recommended to align with increasing student proficiency and to optimize the balance between generated content and instructional feedback.

title: A Fully Convolutional Approach to Denoising Structural Dynamics Data from X-Ray Photon Correlation Spectroscopy
summary: We present a fully convolutional denoising autoencoder (FC-DAE) for denoising two-time intensity-intensity correlation functions ($C_2$) in X-ray photon correlation spectroscopy (XPCS). Unlike conventional denoising autoencoders that are typically restricted to fixed input sizes, the FC-DAE accepts inputs of arbitrary dimensions while preserving correlation structures across diverse dynamical regimes. The model is trained using experimentally derived $C_2$ data collected at NSLS-II beamlines, with data augmentation applied to expand the diversity of the dataset and reduce overfitting. The FC-DAE successfully recovers intricate dynamical features in low signal-to-noise conditions while maintaining structural fidelity. To assess reconstruction reliability, we employ quantitative metrics to evaluate structural fidelity and identify potential model-induced bias. Our results demonstrate that the FC-DAE provides robust denoising performance with high computational efficiency, enabling recovery of XPCS dynamics under photon-limited and low-dose measurement conditions.

AI.tldr: A fully convolutional denoising autoencoder (FC-DAE) denoises two-time intensity-intensity correlation functions (C2) in XPCS, handling arbitrary input sizes and preserving dynamical correlation structures, trained on NSLS-II data with augmentation, achieving robust, efficient denoising under photon-limited conditions with quantitative fidelity and bias checks.
AI.motivation: Denoising C2 in XPCS across diverse dynamical regimes is challenging due to fixed input-size constraints of standard autoencoders and low SNR photon-limited data. A size-invariant, structure-preserving denoiser could improve reconstruction fidelity and enable low-dose measurements.
AI.method: Develop a fully convolutional denoising autoencoder (FC-DAE) that accepts inputs of arbitrary dimensions, train on experimentally derived C2 data from NSLS-II with data augmentation to diversify the dataset, and evaluate reconstruction fidelity using quantitative metrics and bias assessment.
AI.result: FC-DAE successfully recovers intricate dynamical features in low SNR conditions while preserving structural fidelity, demonstrates robust denoising with high computational efficiency, and enables XPCS dynamics recovery under photon-limited and low-dose conditions; reliability assessed via quantitative fidelity metrics and bias checks.
AI.conclusion: An FC-DAE provides robust, efficient denoising for C2 in XPCS across dynamical regimes, with verification of reconstruction reliability and potential bias assessment, facilitating photon-limited measurements.

title: GPIC: A Giant Permissive Image Corpus for Visual Generation
summary: Studying scalable methods for visual generative modeling requires large, accessible, and stable datasets. We introduce GPIC, a Giant Permissive Image Corpus of approximately 28 trillion pixels. GPIC comprises diverse internet images captioned by a state-of-the-art vision-language model, including 100M training, 200K validation, and 1M test examples. Moreover, all GPIC images are permissively licensed for both research and commercial use. GPIC is safety-filtered, deduplicated, and centrally hosted on Hugging Face. We provide a benchmarking protocol for generative modeling on GPIC. Finally, we provide a reference baseline for pixel-space flow matching on GPIC. Our dataset, benchmark, and models are available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/stanford-vision-lab/gpic. Evaluation toolkit and code are available at https://gpic.stanford.edu

AI.tldr: GPIC is a giant, permissively licensed image corpus (~28 trillion pixels) for scalable generative modeling, with 100M train, 200K val, and 1M test images captioned by a vision-language model; safety-filtered, deduplicated, and hosted on Hugging Face, plus a benchmarking protocol and a pixel-space flow-matching baseline.
AI.motivation: To enable scalable, diverse, and accessible data for training and evaluating generative models, addressing data availability, licensing, and evaluation needs in the community.
AI.method: Assemble a large-scale image corpus from diverse internet images, generate captions with a state-of-the-art vision-language model, deduplicate and apply safety filters, ensure permissive (research and commercial) licenses, and host centrally on Hugging Face. Develop a benchmarking protocol for GPIC and provide a reference baseline for pixel-space flow matching; release evaluation toolkit and code.
AI.result: GPIC provides a dataset (~28T pixels) with 100M training, 200K validation, and 1M test images, all captioned and under permissive licenses for research and commercial use. It is safety-filtered, deduplicated, and hosted on Hugging Face. A benchmarking protocol for generative modeling on GPIC is provided, along with a reference baseline for pixel-space flow matching; resources and code are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: GPIC offers a scalable, accessible, and license-friendly resource for training and evaluating generative models, supporting standardized benchmarks and enabling broader research and commercial use.

title: Improving Adversarial Robustness of Attribution via Implicit Regularization
comment: 39 pages, 22 figures, to be published in International Conference on Machine Learning 2026
summary: The adversarial robustness of attributions is a fundamental requirement for reliable explainability in deep learning, yet existing approaches typically rely on computationally expensive explicit regularization. In this work, we show that attribution robustness can arise implicitly from the learning dynamics of standard stochastic gradient descent. We theoretically motivate this effect through connections between parameter-space and input-space curvature, and validate it across architectures, datasets, and attribution methods, with negligible computational overhead. In contrast, we prove that such robustness gains often does not transfer to attention-based attribution under softmax normalization, due to inherent entropy constraints, and we validate this limitation experimentally. Finally, we show that replacing softmax attention with kernel-based attention restores the robustness gains in transformer models. Our results highlight learning dynamics as a principled and practical mechanism for robust explainability, and reveal fundamental limitations of attention-based attribution under normalization.

AI.tldr: Attributions achieve adversarial robustness as a byproduct of SGD learning dynamics without explicit regularization; softmax-based attention undermines robustness due to entropy constraints, while kernel-based attention restores it in transformers.
AI.motivation: To ensure reliable explainability in deep networks by achieving robust attributions with low computational cost, and to understand the role of normalization in attention on attribution robustness.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis linking parameter-space and input-space curvature; extensive empirical validation across architectures, datasets, and attribution methods; analysis of attention under softmax; experiments substituting kernel-based attention in transformers.
AI.result: Robust attribution emerges implicitly from SGD dynamics with negligible overhead across methods and data; robustness does not transfer to softmax-attention due to entropy constraints; kernel-based attention restores robustness in transformer models.
AI.conclusion: Learning dynamics provide a principled, practical mechanism for robust explainability; attention normalization introduces fundamental limitations for attribution; kernel-based attention offers a viable path to regain robustness.

title: When Should Models Change Their Minds? Contextual Belief Management in Large Language Models
summary: Long-horizon interactions require language models to manage accumulating information: when to update their state, when to preserve their state, and what to ignore. We study this challenge as \textbf{Contextual Belief Management (CBM)}: maintaining a predicted belief state aligned with formal evidence while isolating task-irrelevant noise. To make CBM measurable, we introduce BeliefTrack, a closed-world benchmark spanning Rule Discovery and Circuit Diagnosis, where a finite belief space and symbolic verifiers enable exact turn-level evaluation. BeliefTrack diagnoses three failures: Failed Stay, Failed Update, and Failed Isolation. Across multiple LLMs, vanilla models exhibit severe CBM failures, while explicit belief-tracking prompts provide limited gains. In contrast, reinforcement learning with belief-state rewards reduces failure rates by 70.9\% on average. Further probing reveals latent belief-state dynamics behind these failures, and representation-level steering reduces failure rates by 46.1\% across two tasks\footnote{Code is coming soon at https://github.com/zjunlp/CBM.

AI.tldr: Proposes Contextual Belief Management (CBM) and BeliefTrack to quantify how LLMs manage long-horizon beliefs; identifies three failure modes and shows RL with belief-state rewards and representation-level steering significantly improve performance.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon interactions require maintaining, updating, and filtering information; there is a need for a measurable framework to study belief management and isolate task-irrelevant noise.
AI.method: Introduce CBM as a framework and BeliefTrack as a closed-world benchmark spanning Rule Discovery and Circuit Diagnosis, with a finite belief space and symbolic verifiers enabling exact turn-level evaluation. Diagnose three failure modes: Failed Stay, Failed Update, and Failed Isolation. Evaluate vanilla LLMs, belief-tracking prompts, RL with belief-state rewards, and representation-level steering across multiple tasks.
AI.result: Vanilla LLMs show severe CBM failures; explicit belief-tracking prompts yield limited gains. RL with belief-state rewards reduces failure rates by 70.9% on average. Representation-level steering reduces failure rates by 46.1% across two tasks; latent belief-state dynamics are revealed through probing.
AI.conclusion: CBM can be substantially improved via reinforcement learning and representation-level interventions rather than relying solely on explicit belief prompts. The BeliefTrack benchmark enables precise, turn-level evaluation and analysis of CBM failures, pointing to directions for more robust long-horizon reasoning in LLMs.

title: MIC: Maximizing Informational Capacity in Adaptive Representations via Isotropic Subspace Alignment
comment: Accepted at the GlobalSouthML Workshop at ICML 2026. 13 pages, 2 figures
summary: Although multi-scales representation learning enables elastic-dimension embeddings, nested subspaces often suffer from dimensional redundancy and spectral collapse. To address this, we introduce MIC, a framework that optimizes the geometric landscape of multi-granular embeddings through isotropic subspace alignment. MIC employs Soft Collapse Regularization (SCR) to mitigate redundancy between prefix and residual subspaces via cross-correlation penalties, alongside Spectral Isotropy Regularization (SIR) to ensure hyper-spherical uniformity in low-dimensional prefixes. By unifying these strategies through a self-distillation objective, MIC generates semantically dense representations that maintain high discriminative power. Our experiments demonstrate that MIC significantly outperforms standard baselines, particularly in high-compression scenarios where maintaining informational capacity is most critical.

AI.tldr: MIC is a framework for multi-granular embeddings that reduces redundancy and spectral collapse via SCR and SIR, unified by self-distillation, yielding dense, discriminative representations, especially beneficial under high compression.
AI.motivation: Nested subspaces in multi-scale representations often suffer dimensional redundancy and spectral collapse, which degrade information capacity and discriminative power under compression. An isotropic alignment of subspaces is needed to preserve semantic content.
AI.method: Introduce MIC with Soft Collapse Regularization (SCR) that penalizes cross-correlations between prefix and residual subspaces to mitigate redundancy, and Spectral Isotropy Regularization (SIR) that enforces hyper-spherical uniformity in low-dimensional prefixes. These are unified via a self-distillation objective.
AI.result: MIC significantly outperforms standard baselines, with larger gains in high-compression scenarios where maintaining informational capacity is critical.
AI.conclusion: Isotropic subspace alignment through SCR and SIR under a self-distillation objective yields semantically dense, highly discriminative representations, particularly effective in high-compression settings.

title: YoCausal: How Far is Video Generation from World Model? A Causality Perspective
summary: As video diffusion models (VDMs) advance toward world models, a key question arises: do they truly understand causality, or merely overfit to statistical temporal patterns? Existing benchmarks mostly rely on synthetic data, limiting real-world generalization due to the sim-to-real gap. We present YoCausal, a two-level benchmark inspired by the Violation of Expectation (VoE) paradigm from cognitive science. By temporally reversing real-world videos at zero cost as natural counterfactual samples, YoCausal establishes an arbitrarily extensible evaluation protocol. Level 1 introduces the Reverse Surprise Index (RSI), quantifying arrow-of-time perception via denoising loss. Level 2 introduces the Causality Cognition Index (CCI), which leverages a VLM to stratify datasets into causal and non-causal subsets, disentangling genuine causal reasoning from temporal bias. Evaluation of 13 state-of-the-art VDMs reveals that perceiving the arrow of time does not imply understanding causality, and a significant gap persists relative to human-level causal cognition.

AI.tldr: YoCausal presents a two-level, real-world video benchmark to gauge causal understanding in video diffusion models, separating arrow-of-time perception from true causal reasoning; results show models still lag human-level causal cognition.
AI.motivation: Current video diffusion benchmarks rely heavily on synthetic data and fail to test real-world causal understanding, risking overfitting to temporal statistics. A cognitive-science–inspired, extensible benchmark with real counterfactuals addresses this sim-to-real gap.
AI.method: Two-level YoCausal benchmark. Level 1: Reverse Surprise Index (RSI) uses denoising loss to quantify arrow-of-time perception from temporally reversed real videos generated at zero cost. Level 2: Causality Cognition Index (CCI) uses a Vision-Language Model (VLM) to stratify datasets into causal vs non-causal subsets, separating genuine causal reasoning from temporal bias. Expands via zero-cost reversals to produce counterfactual samples.
AI.result: Evaluation of 13 state-of-the-art video diffusion models shows that arrow-of-time perception does not imply causal understanding, with a sizable gap relative to human causal cognition.
AI.conclusion: Perceiving the arrow of time is insufficient for true causality understanding in VDMs; YoCausal offers an extensible, real-world evaluation framework to better quantify and drive progress toward human-like causal cognition in video models.

title: mcp-proto-okn: Natural-language access to open scientific knowledge graphs through the Model Context Protocol
summary:
MCP Server Proto-OKN (mcp-proto-okn) is a Python-based Model Context Protocol server that enables AI assistants to discover, inspect, query and integrate scientific knowledge graphs through natural language. The server provides graph routing, schema inspection, SPARQL execution, ontology expansion, multi-graph querying, and transcript generation, lowering the barrier to cross-domain knowledge graph analysis for biomedical and scientific users.
  mcp-proto-okn is implemented in Python using the FastMCP framework and is available at https://github.com/sbl-sdsc/mcp-proto-okn. Documentation, client configuration instructions, and example analysis transcripts are provided in the GitHub repository.

AI.tldr: A Python-based MCP Server (Proto-OKN) enabling AI assistants to discover, inspect, query, and integrate scientific knowledge graphs via natural language, with graph routing, SPARQL, ontology expansion, multi-graph querying, and transcript generation; built on FastMCP and hosted on GitHub.
AI.motivation: Lower the barrier to cross-domain knowledge graph analysis for biomedical and scientific users by enabling natural-language interaction, discovery, and integration of graphs within AI assistants.
AI.method: Implemented in Python using the FastMCP framework; provides graph routing, schema inspection, SPARQL execution, ontology expansion, multi-graph querying, transcript generation; includes APIs, client configuration, and example transcripts in the repository.
AI.result: Enables discovery, inspection, querying, and integration of scientific knowledge graphs; supports SPARQL; supports transcript generation; facilitates cross-domain analysis for biomedical and scientific users.
AI.conclusion: Delivers an open-source tool that bridges AI assistants with scientific knowledge graphs, reducing integration effort and enabling rich, natural-language-based exploration; documentation and examples are provided at the GitHub repository.

title: Test Time Training for Supervised Causal Learning
summary: Supervised Causal Learning (SCL) has shown promise in causal discovery by framing it as a supervised learning problem. However, it suffers from significant out-of-distribution generalization challenges. We reveal three limitations of previous SCL practices: a significant performance gap between synthetic benchmarks and real-world data, fragility to distribution shifts, and failure in compositional generalization, collectively questioning its real-world applicability. To address this, we propose Test-Time Training for Supervised Causal Learning (TTT-SCL), a novel framework that dynamically generates training sets explicitly aligned with any specific test instance. We demonstrate the correlation between TTT-SCL and score-based methods, and design an efficient module for generating training sets based on the classic scoring function. Experiments on synthetic benchmarks, pseudo-real and real-world datasets demonstrate that TTT-SCL significantly outperforms existing SCL and traditional causal discovery methods.

AI.tldr: TTT-SCL introduces test-time training for supervised causal learning by dynamically generating training sets aligned with each test instance, addressing out-of-distribution and compositional generalization gaps and outperforming prior SCL and traditional causal discovery on synthetic and real data.
AI.motivation: SCL shows promise for causal discovery but suffers from severe out-of-distribution generalization, with performance gaps between synthetic benchmarks and real-world data, sensitivity to distribution shifts, and failure in compositional generalization, limiting real-world applicability.
AI.method: Propose Test-Time Training for Supervised Causal Learning (TTT-SCL): at test time, dynamically generate training sets specifically tailored to the test instance. The training-set generator is built around a classic scoring function, and, as shown, correlates with score-based causal-discovery approaches.
AI.result: Empirical results on synthetic benchmarks, pseudo-real, and real-world datasets show that TTT-SCL significantly outperforms existing SCL methods and traditional causal-discovery methods.
AI.conclusion: TTT-SCL mitigates key generalization issues hindering SCL, connects with score-based perspectives, and demonstrates strong potential for real-world causal discovery.

title: NeuROK: Generative 4D Neural Object Kinematics
summary: Data-driven approaches have revolutionized 3D vision, enabling transformers to effectively reconstruct and generate static 3D objects. However, generating simulative 4D dynamics -- realistic temporal deformations of static objects under various physical conditions -- remains challenging and often ad hoc, despite its importance in building comprehensive 3D world models. Most existing methods assume a predefined physical model and use system identification to estimate parameters, restricting these methods to specific categories and small-scale datasets. We propose that these restrictions can be overcome by learning a data-driven kinematic state parameterization for object-centric physical systems. Specifically, we learn both a latent space representing all possible states of the object and a decoder that maps any sampled latent to a plausibly deformed shape of the object. We refer to this parameterization as Neural Object Kinematics (NeuROK), and learn a transformer-based encoder-decoder model on a curated large-scale 4D dataset. This formulation and the learned model significantly simplify the generation of simulative dynamics since we only need to consider the dynamics within a low-dimensional latent space from the Lagrangian mechanics' perspective in classical physics. We demonstrate the effectiveness and generality of this neural simulation framework across diverse dynamic object types, showing clear advantages over prior works. Project page: https://chen-geng.com/neurok

AI.tldr: NeuROK learns a latent, transformer-based kinematic model to generate 4D object dynamics without explicit physics models.
AI.motivation: Overcome reliance on predefined physical models and small datasets for simulating 4D deformations; aim for a general, data-driven parameterization of object kinematics.
AI.method: Train a transformer encoder-decoder to learn a latent space of object states and a decoder that maps latent vectors to deformed shapes; trained on a large-scale 4D dataset; uses Lagrangian perspective for dynamics within latent space.
AI.result: Demonstrates effectiveness and generality across diverse dynamic object types; offers clear advantages over prior works in generating simulative dynamics.
AI.conclusion: NeuROK provides a scalable, data-driven framework for 4D object dynamics, enabling realistic deformations under varied conditions without predefined physical models; broad potential for 3D world modeling.

title: ProjectionBench: Evaluating Scientific Hypothesis Generation in LLMs Under Progressive Information Disclosure
summary: Scientific discovery is an inherently creative and uncertain process, requiring reasoning beyond the recall of known knowledge. While many benchmarks have been proposed to evaluate large language model (LLM) performance on deep research tasks via multi-hop retrieval, their innovative reasoning abilities essential for true scientific discovery remain largely untested. We introduce a benchmark framework for evaluating model performance in scientific discovery and reasoning, building up from a raw problem to the classical null hypothesis test. In our framework, models initially receive only the topic and research question from a recent paper, with technical details progressively revealed. At each stage of information disclosure, the model is tasked with generating hypotheses that address the research question, which is compared with the conclusions from the original paper and evaluated via automated semantic similarity of constituent atomic claims. This progressive evaluation of semantic divergence from ground-truth conclusions enables assessment of a model's innovativeness (under minimal information) to grounded reasoning capabilities (under full experimental details), both critical for using LLMs for scientific discovery purposes. Our framework provides a foundation for systematically evaluating scientific reasoning and discovery capabilities in LLMs, crucial for advancing the development of next-generation AI scientist/co-scientist systems. Specifically, here we evaluate GPT-5, GPT-5.4, Gemini 2.5 pro, and Gemini 3.1 pro preview across 45 papers spanning bioactive materials, mechanical materials, and nanomaterials. We find that GPT-5.4 and Gemini 3.1 pro outperform their previous generation counterparts as expected, and GPT-5.4 in particular maintains 0.7 F1 score alignment with ground truth conclusions even under minimal context.

AI.tldr: A progressive, ground-truth-aligned evaluation framework for LLMs' scientific discovery and reasoning, revealing information stepwise and comparing generated hypotheses to ground-truth conclusions via semantic similarity of atomic claims; applied to four models across 45 materials-related papers; shows improvements in newer models and partial alignment (0.7 F1) under minimal context.
AI.motivation: To test genuine scientific reasoning and innovativeness in LLMs beyond recall-based benchmarks, enabling evaluation of AI scientists in uncertain, discovery-driven tasks.
AI.method: Stage-wise information disclosure from topic/question to full experimental details. At each stage, models generate hypotheses addressing the research question. Hypotheses are compared to original paper conclusions using automated semantic similarity of atomic claims to measure semantic divergence from ground truth.
AI.result: GPT-5.4 and Gemini 3.1 pro outperform predecessors; GPT-5.4 achieves approximately 0.7 F1 alignment with ground-truth conclusions even with minimal context.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a foundational approach to systematically evaluate scientific reasoning and discovery in LLMs, supporting the development of AI scientist/co-scientist systems.

title: AdaState: Self-Evolving Anchors for Streaming Video Generation
summary: Autoregressive video diffusion models generate streaming video by producing frames sequentially, conditioning each chunk on previously generated content. These models are structurally anchored to the first frame: its key-value representation occupies a privileged position in the attention cache and serves as the primary scene reference throughout generation. As the cleanest and most error-free position in the cache, this anchor draws disproportionate attention, suppressing video dynamics, and locking scene composition to the initial viewpoint even as the scene naturally evolves. The result is a temporally shallow video in which motion, camera movement, and scene progression are dampened in favor of static consistency. To address this, we replace the static anchor with an adaptive state, a hidden latent that the model denoises alongside content at every chunk but never renders. Rather than referencing a frozen first frame, the model generates its own scene anchor at each step by attending to both the previous state and the current content, producing a reference that evolves with the generated content. Unlike standard video generation, which encodes an absolute notion of time, our formulation treats time as relative: every generation step sees the same positional structure regardless of how far generation has progressed, and the state transition is identical at every chunk. Together, these properties introduce a recurrence into the generation process, where denoising serves as the transition function, and the KV cache serves as the carrier, requiring no external module. Experiments demonstrate that the adaptive state substantially improves video dynamics, enabling richer motion and natural scene progression within generated videos.

AI.tldr: Replace the static, first-frame anchor in autoregressive video diffusion with an adaptive, recurrent latent state that denoises with content at every step, enabling a relative-time formulation and richer motion dynamics.
AI.motivation: In current autoregressive video diffusion, the first frame's key-value representation becomes an over-privileged anchor in the attention cache, suppressing motion and locking scene composition to the initial viewpoint, resulting in temporally shallow videos.
AI.method: Introduce an adaptive latent state that the model denoises alongside content at each chunk but does not render. The scene anchor is generated at each step by attending to the previous state and current content. Time is treated as relative, with identical state transitions at every chunk, forming a recurrence where denoising is the transition function and the KV cache is the carrier, requiring no external module.
AI.result: Experiments show the adaptive state substantially improves video dynamics, yielding richer motion and more natural scene progression.
AI.conclusion: An adaptive, recurrent latent state effectively replaces the static first-frame anchor, enabling dynamic, coherent video generation with a consistent transition function across steps.

title: MIRA: Mid-training Rubric Anchoring for Source-Aware Data Selection
summary: Mid-training has become an important stage in modern LLM development, using large-scale curated mixtures to strengthen capabilities before final post-training. Its data selection problem is distinct: the data are optimized under a pretraining-style objective at near-pretraining scale, but are curated toward downstream capabilities and drawn from heterogeneous sources with different formats and training roles. As a result, effective selection requires both scalability and source-adaptive semantic criteria. Existing model-based methods scale well, but provide only implicit quality signals. Semantic selection methods offer stronger judgments, but usually assume fixed rubrics or standardized data formats. To address this mismatch, we propose MIRA, a source-aware filtering framework based on self-anchored rubric discovery. The key idea is to make rubric construction part of data selection: MIRA first discovers what should be evaluated for each source group, then distills those judgments into scalable student scorers for full-corpus filtering. On code-oriented mid-training with 21 sources and 5 source groups, MIRA outperforms selection baselines across nine code benchmarks and matches the full-corpus run while using only half the tokens.

AI.tldr: MIRA introduces a source-aware data-filtering framework for mid-training that discovers rubrics per source group and distills them into scalable scorers; it matches full-corpus performance on code benchmarks while using half the tokens.
AI.motivation: Mid-training data selection must be scalable and semantically aware in the presence of heterogeneous sources. Existing model-based methods give implicit quality signals and semantic methods rely on fixed rubrics or standardized formats; a mismatch exists between scalable selection and source-adaptive semantics.
AI.method: MIRA integrates self-anchored rubric discovery into data selection. For each source group, it discovers what should be evaluated, then distills those rubrics into scalable student scorers for full-corpus filtering.
AI.result: In code-oriented mid-training with 21 sources and 5 source groups, MIRA outperforms selection baselines across nine code benchmarks and matches the full-corpus run using only half the tokens.
AI.conclusion: Source-aware rubric-discovery-based filtering is effective for mid-training data selection, enabling adaptable semantic criteria without sacrificing scalability.

title: Masked Diffusion Modeling for Anomaly Detection
summary: Anomaly detection aims to identify samples that deviate from the nominal data distribution and is central to many safety-critical applications. However, developing effective anomaly detection methods for categorical, mixed-type, and discrete sequence data remains challenging and relatively underexplored. Masked diffusion models provide a natural way to model such data by learning to recover masked values from the remaining visible context. In this paper, we propose Masked Diffusion for Anomaly Detection (MaskDiff-AD), a forward-only method based on masked diffusion models trained only on nominal data. Given a test sample, MaskDiff-AD constructs anomaly scores from the difficulty of reconstructing randomly masked coordinates, yielding a content-sensitive score that operates directly on discrete state spaces while avoiding reverse-time sampling. We also develop a non-parametric variant of MaskDiff-AD and provide theoretical guarantees by characterizing Type-I and Type-II errors under a fixed detection threshold. Experiments on fourteen categorical and mixed-type tabular datasets from ADBench and UADAD, as well as four text anomaly detection datasets from NLP-ADBench, show that MaskDiff-AD achieves competitive performance against classical, diffusion-based, and recent tabular/text anomaly detection baselines. Notably, MaskDiff-AD achieves the best overall average rank, outperforming all twelve tabular baseline methods.

AI.tldr: Masked Diffusion for Anomaly Detection (MaskDiff-AD) introduces a forward-only, nominal-data-trained method for discrete and mixed-type data by scoring anomalies via the difficulty of reconstructing randomly masked coordinates; it includes a non-parametric variant and offers theoretical Type-I/II guarantees, achieving top performance on tabular and text anomaly benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Anomaly detection on categorical, mixed-type, and discrete sequences is challenging and under-explored. Traditional diffusion models often rely on reverse-time sampling and continuous data, making discrete, mixed-type data difficult. A forward-only approach that operates on discrete state spaces can be more efficient and directly model nominal discrete distributions.
AI.method: Train MaskDiff-AD as a forward diffusion-like process learned only on nominal data. At test time, randomly mask coordinates of a sample and measure the difficulty of reconstructing them to obtain an anomaly score; this yields a content-sensitive score that operates on discrete state spaces without reverse-time sampling. Also propose a non-parametric variant and provide theoretical guarantees on Type-I and Type-II errors under a fixed detection threshold.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on 14 categorical/mixed-type tabular datasets (ADBench, UADAD) and 4 text anomaly datasets (NLP-ADBench) shows MaskDiff-AD achieving competitive performance against classical, diffusion-based, and recent baselines, with the best overall average rank among twelve tabular methods.
AI.conclusion: MaskDiff-AD provides an effective, forward-only, discrete-state anomaly detector with theoretical guarantees and strong empirical performance, including a non-parametric option for robustness and efficiency benefits by avoiding reverse-time sampling.

title: VideoMLA: Low-Rank Latent KV Cache for Minute-Scale Autoregressive Video Diffusion
summary: Long-rollout causal video diffusion has converged on a fixed-size sliding-window KV cache, with recent progress innovating within this layout by changing which tokens occupy the window or how their positions are encoded. The per-head KV layout itself, a dominant contributor to streaming memory and latency, has been mostly left unchanged. In this paper, we present the first study of Multi-Head Latent Attention (MLA) in video diffusion. VideoMLA replaces per-head keys and values with a shared low-rank content latent and a shared decoupled 3D-RoPE positional key, reducing per-token KV memory by 92.7% at every cached layer. We further investigate why MLA succeeds in video diffusion even though the spectral assumption often used to motivate it in language models does not hold: pretrained video attention is not low-rank, with 99%-energy effective rank far above any practical latent dimension. VideoMLA retains quality at compression ratios where direct spectral approximation would predict large reconstruction error. We show that the MLA bottleneck, rather than the pretrained spectrum, determines the effective rank: both spectral and random initialization occupy nearly the full rank budget from initialization, and training preserves this budget while adapting within it. On VBench, VideoMLA matches short-horizon streaming video diffusion baselines, achieves the best overall score at long horizons among evaluated methods, and improves throughput by 1.23x on a single B200.

AI.tldr: Introduces VideoMLA, the first study of Multi-Head Latent Attention in video diffusion. Replaces per-head keys/values with a shared low-rank content latent and a shared decoupled 3D-RoPE positional key, reducing per-token KV memory by 92.7% at cached layers. Demonstrates MLA works in video diffusion despite non-low-rank pretrained spectra, showing the bottleneck is MLA rather than spectrum. On VBench, VideoMLA matches short-horizon baselines, achieves best long-horizon score, and yields 1.23x throughput on a single B200.
AI.motivation: Reduce memory and latency costs of long-rollout causal video diffusion by modifying the KV attention mechanism, challenging the idea that video attention must be high-rank and per-head, and investigating why MLA works in non-LM spectra settings.
AI.method: Proposes VideoMLA: replace per-head keys/values with a shared low-rank content latent and a shared decoupled 3D-RoPE positional key; analyze spectral properties and effective rank of pretrained video attention; train and evaluate on VBench and B200 for throughput and quality.
AI.result: Per-token KV memory reduced by 92.7% at cached layers; quality preserved; matches short-horizon baselines; achieves best overall score at long horizons; 1.23x throughput improvement on a single B200.
AI.conclusion: MLA can dramatically reduce memory/latency in video diffusion without sacrificing quality, and its success is governed by the MLA bottleneck rather than the pretrained spectrum. Training preserves the latent budget while adapting within it, implying broad applicability for streaming video diffusion.

title: Demystifying Data Organization for Enhanced LLM Training
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized various fields, yet their training efficiency is heavily reliant on effective data curation. While data selection has been widely studied, the strategic data organization for enhanced training remains an underexplored area, particularly since current LLMs are often trained for only one or a few epochs. This paper systematically explores the influence of data organization on LLM training by reusing pre-computed sample-level scores originally generated for data efficiency, thereby incurring minimal additional computational overhead. We identify and formalize four key guidelines for optimizing data organization: Boundary Sharpening, Cyclic Scheduling, Curriculum Continuity, and Local Diversity. Guided by them, we introduce two novel data ordering methods termed STR and SAW. Extensive experiments across different model scales and data sizes, encompassing both pre-training and SFT stages, validate the effectiveness of our summarized guidelines. They also demonstrate the robustness of our proposed data ordering methods in enhancing the stability and performance of LLM training. Github Link: https://github.com/microsoft/data-efficacy/

AI.tldr: This work studies how data organization, not just data selection, affects LLM training efficiency by reusing precomputed sample-level scores; it formalizes four guidelines and introduces two data-ordering methods (STR and SAW), with extensive experiments showing improved stability and performance across scales and training stages.
