AI.motivation: To push beyond data selection by leveraging data organization as a lever for LLM training efficiency, especially since many LMs are trained for only a few epochs and existing scores can be reused to guide ordering with minimal overhead.
AI.method: Reuses precomputed sample-level scores from prior data-efficiency work to design data ordering. Formalizes four guidelines—Boundary Sharpening, Cyclic Scheduling, Curriculum Continuity, Local Diversity—and introduces two ordering algorithms, STR and SAW. Evaluates across multiple model scales, data sizes, and training stages (pre-training and SFT).
AI.result: The guidelines improve training stability and performance across different model sizes and data volumes. The STR and SAW methods demonstrate robust gains in both pre-training and SFT settings compared with baselines.
AI.conclusion: Data organization is a viable lever for improving LLM training; the proposed guidelines and ordering methods offer effective, low-overhead improvements through reusing existing sample-level scores, with demonstrated robustness across training regimes and scales.

title: Ridge Regression from Poisson Resetting: A Renewal Perspective on Spectral Regularization
summary: We connect stochastic resetting from non-equilibrium statistical physics with ridge regularization in statistical learning. For linear gradient flow, resetting to the origin at rate $r$ produces stationary mean $(X^\top X+rI)^{-1}X^\top y$, exactly the ridge estimator with penalty $λ=r$. This uses the known Laplace-transform relationship between ridge regression and exponential-time averaging of gradient flow, with the exponential time now interpreted as the stationary age associated with Poisson resetting. We then extend this identity to general renewal reset laws: the exponential reset time distribution is the unique renewal law whose stationary mean reproduces scalar ridge in every eigendirection as an exact filter identity for every positive curvature, while non-exponential renewal laws generate alternative spectral filters. At the fluctuation level, we study a separate additive Ornstein-Uhlenbeck extension with constant diffusion, interpreted as a stylized SGD approximation. In this setting, the equality holds only at the level of the mean, since the reset process has a nonzero stationary covariance from accumulated OU noise and reset-timing variance, whereas deterministic ridge is a fixed estimator with the same center. Stylized experiments compare the deterministic renewal-induced filters directly and illustrate when filters induced by non-exponential reset-time laws can differ predictively from ridge. The results for the stationary mean and the induced spectral filters are established for continuous-time gradient flow with isotropic resetting on quadratic objectives; the covariance and risk formulas additionally assume additive noise with state-independent covariance.

AI.tldr: Stochastic resetting of gradient flow reproduces ridge regression as the stationary mean; exponential resets uniquely yield ridge in all eigendirections via a Laplace transform link, while other renewal resets produce alternative spectral filters; adding OU noise yields mean-only equality and SGD-like behavior; results hold for quadratic objectives with isotropic resetting and state-independent noise.
AI.motivation: Bridge stochastic resetting from non-equilibrium statistical physics with ridge regularization in statistical learning, providing a spectral interpretation of ridge and a framework to compare resetting schemes as filters in gradient-based estimators.
AI.method: Analytical derivation for continuous-time gradient flow with resetting to the origin at rate r, yielding stationary mean (X^T X + rI)^{-1} X^T y (ridge with λ=r). Use Laplace-transform relation between ridge regression and exponential-time averaging of gradient flow. Extend to general renewal reset laws; show exponential reset is the unique renewal law that reproduces scalar ridge in every eigendirection as an exact filter identity across positive curvature. Introduce additive Ornstein-Uhlenbeck noise as a stylized SGD approximation and analyze mean vs covariance. Conduct stylized experiments comparing renewal-induced filters and ridge, and illustrate spectral filter differences under non-exponential resets.
AI.result: Derived that the stationary mean under Poisson (reset-to-origin) resets equals the ridge estimator with penalty λ=r. For general renewal resets, exponential resets uniquely recover ridge in all eigen-directions; non-exponential resets produce alternative spectral filters. With additive OU noise, equality holds only for the mean due to nonzero stationary covariance and reset-timing variance. Experiments demonstrate when renewal-induced filters align with or diverge from ridge. Theoretical results hold for continuous-time gradient flow with isotropic resetting on quadratic objectives; covariance/risk formulas require state-independent additive noise.
AI.conclusion: The work formalizes a precise link between stochastic resetting and ridge regularization, showing resetting time laws determine the resulting spectral filters. Exponential resets uniquely reproduce ridge in all directions, while other renewal laws yield different filters. Incorporating OU noise exposes mean-only equivalence and SGD-like dynamics. The results provide a rigorous bridge across physics and learning theory for quadratic objectives.

title: GMOS: Grounding Moving Object Segmentation in 3D Space and Time
summary: Moving Object Segmentation (MOS) aims to discover, segment, and track objects that move independently of the camera. Current MOS methods, however, exhibit two fundamental limitations: they rely on pre-computed 2D auxiliary modalities such as optical flow or point trajectories that lack 3D geometric information, and they treat motion as a sequence-level attribute, overlooking the instantaneous motion state of each object. We address both by grounding MOS in 3D space and time, and propose GMOS, a framework that operates directly on RGB video to produce 3D-aware, temporally fine-grained segmentation of multiple moving objects, alongside a foreground--background variant GMOS-S for faster deployment. To support training and evaluation in this regime, we curate GMOS-2K, a dataset of 2,210 real-world videos with per-object temporal motion annotations drawn from five established Video Object Segmentation (VOS) benchmarks, and formalise MOS-I ("I" for instantaneous), a temporally fine-grained evaluation protocol with three complementary metrics. GMOS achieves state-of-the-art results across MOS, MOS-I, and Unsupervised VOS benchmarks, while running significantly faster than prior multi-object MOS methods and supporting online inference for streaming deployment.

AI.tldr: GMOS introduces 3D-aware, temporally fine-grained moving object segmentation from RGB video, with a faster GMOS-S variant, a large GMOS-2K dataset, and MOS-I evaluation protocol; achieves state-of-the-art and online inference.
AI.motivation: Current MOS methods rely on 2D auxiliary cues (e.g., optical flow, point tracks) that lack 3D spatial context and treat motion as a sequence-level attribute. This limits accurate, instantaneous segmentation of multiple moving objects in 3D space and time.
AI.method: Ground MOS in 3D space and time by a framework (GMOS) operating directly on RGB video to produce 3D-aware, temporally fine-grained multi-object segmentation; includes a lighter GMOS-S variant for faster deployment; introduces training/evaluation data (GMOS-2K) and defines MOS-I for instantaneous, temporally fine-grained evaluation with three metrics.
AI.result: GMOS achieves state-of-the-art performance on MOS, MOS-I, and Unsupervised VOS benchmarks; runs faster than prior multi-object MOS methods and supports online inference for streaming deployment.
AI.conclusion: The work advances MOS by integrating 3D grounding and fine-grained temporal reasoning, offers a new dataset and evaluation protocol, and enables fast, online-capable deployment with superior performance.

title: Locally Coherent, Globally Incoherent: Bounding Compositional Incoherence in Multi-Component LLM Agents
comment: 25 pages, 7 figures, 24 tables. Preliminary versions to appear at the ICML 2026 Workshops on Combining Theory and Benchmarks (CTB), Statistical Frameworks for Uncertainty in Agentic Systems (AgenticUQ), and Failure Modes of Agentic AI (FAGEN)
summary: Multi-component LLM agents assemble probabilistic claims from components that each see only part of a joint problem; the composition can violate basic probability axioms even when every component is locally coherent. We formalise this locally coherent, globally incoherent failure via the compositional residual eps*, the L2 distance from the composed quote to the joint coherent polytope, computable at runtime from system output and the declared cross-component coupling constraints. A product-structure dichotomy characterises when local coherence suffices, and a Rayleigh-quotient prediction matches the observed residual within 7% on three of four relation classes. A hierarchical Boyle-Dykstra projection repairs the composition deterministically; an anytime-valid e-process gives sequential coherence monitoring. Across 1,876 ensemble cliques on a four-LLM mid-tier panel (frontier-panel rerun in Section 5.5), eps* > 0 on 33-94% of cliques, translating to +0.115 nats per bet of regret on 1,770 resolved bets under the proportional allocation rule (the gain collapses to +0.006 under bettors that themselves coherentise). Three intuitive LLM-side mitigations(retrieval, partition-aware prompting, aggregator-LLM) each fail or regress.

AI.tldr: Demonstrates that assembling probabilistic claims from partially observed LLM components often yields globally incoherent results; defines a compositional residual eps*, uses a product-structure dichotomy, Rayleigh-quotient predictions, hierarchical Boyle–Dykstra projection for repair, and e-process monitoring; empirical results show substantial residuals and limited efficacy of simple mitigations.
AI.motivation: Quantify and diagnose coherence failures in multi-LLM ensembles where components see only parts of a joint problem; establish principled measures and repair mechanisms to achieve global coherence.
AI.method: Define eps* as L2 distance from the composed claim to the joint coherent polytope; compute at runtime from system output and inter-component coupling constraints. Characterize when local coherence suffices via a product-structure dichotomy. Predict residual using Rayleigh quotient. Apply hierarchical Boyle–Dykstra projection to deterministically repair composition. Develop an anytime-valid e-process for sequential coherence monitoring. Empirically evaluate on 1,876 ensemble cliques from a four-LLM panel (frontier-panel rerun Sec. 5.5), report residuals and betting regret under proportional allocation; test three mitigation strategies (retrieval, partition-aware prompting, aggregator-LLM).
AI.result: Compositional residual eps* > 0 in 33–94% of cliques; translates to +0.115 nats per bet of regret on 1,770 resolved bets under proportional allocation (reduced to +0.006 when bettors themselves are coherent). Rayleigh-quotient predictions align with observed residual within 7% on three of four relation classes. Three intuitive LLM-side mitigations largely fail or regress.
AI.conclusion: Local coherence does not guarantee global coherence in multi-LLM ensembles. Significant residuals prevail across many cliques and betting scenarios, necessitating principled monitoring and deterministic repair via hierarchical projections and sequential tests. Simple mitigation strategies are insufficient.

title: A Predictive Law for On-Policy Self-Distillation From World Feedback
summary: Moving beyond simple scalar rewards toward richer world feedback is a natural path to more scalable RL post-training. On-policy self-distillation (OPSD) is a promising recent approach that uses arbitrary feedback as learning signal, yet its reliability compared to established methods, such as GRPO, remains unclear. We identify a strikingly consistent linear correlation between the initial student-self-teacher performance gap and the final performance improvement in OPSD. This relationship holds across context types and model families, providing a powerful predictive law for anticipating the outcome of an OPSD configuration without running the full training procedure. Interestingly, we show that this linear predictability holds with model scale, suggesting a potential basis for new empirical scaling laws on larger models with stronger in-context learning capabilities. In essence, our findings show that OPSD performance can be predicted and tuned before training, offering a principled way to incorporate world feedback as a first-class component of the post-training pipeline.

AI.tldr: OPSD outcomes are linearly predictable from the initial student–self–teacher gap across contexts and model scales, enabling pre-training tuning and suggesting new scaling laws.
AI.motivation: Investigate the reliability of on-policy self-distillation (OPSD) and whether world feedback can improve RL post-training; establish predictive guidance without full training runs.
AI.method: Empirical analysis across multiple context types and model families, measuring the initial student–self–teacher gap and the final OPSD-induced improvement; examine consistency of the linear relationship and its persistence with model scaling.
AI.result: A consistent linear correlation between the initial gap and final improvement in OPSD is observed across contexts, model families, and scales; this enables predicting OPSD outcomes prior to training and suggests potential empirical scaling laws for larger models with enhanced in-context learning.
AI.conclusion: OPSD performance can be forecast and tuned before training, allowing world feedback to be integrated as a formal component of the post-training pipeline, with implications for configuring OPSD without full retraining.

title: Tiny but Trusted: Efficient Vision-Language Reasoning for Time-Series Anomaly Detection
summary: Recent advances in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have achieved impressive performance across many tasks, yet prior studies report unsatisfactory performance when applying large language or multimodal models to finding abnormal patterns in sequential data. Public anomaly detection benchmarks typically provide interval annotations but not natural-language rationales, making it difficult to fine-tune VLMs to produce grounded, interpretable decisions. To address this gap, we construct VisAnomBench, a curated benchmark built from public time-series datasets and augmented with high-quality anomaly explanations selected from multiple large VLMs using fine-grained, task-specific rewards. Through fine-tuning on this benchmark, we develop VisAnomReasoner, a parameter-efficient VLM for time-series anomaly detection. Experimental results on VisAnomBench show that VisAnomReasoner achieves more accurate anomaly localization and consistently outperforms all baselines, with improvements of at least 21.23 and 23.87 percentage points in precision and F1, respectively. Additional experiments on the TSB-AD-U benchmark demonstrate strong cross-benchmark generalization, with VisAnomReasoner improving precision and F1 by 9.57 and 13.39 percentage points, respectively.

AI.tldr: Introduces VisAnomBench and VisAnomReasoner for time-series anomaly detection with grounded natural-language explanations; achieves strong anomaly localization and cross-benchmark generalization.
AI.motivation: Public anomaly benchmarks offer interval annotations but lack natural-language rationales; existing vision-language models struggle to produce grounded, interpretable decisions for sequential data; need a benchmark and a fine-tuned model to generate explanations.
AI.method: Create VisAnomBench from public time-series datasets; augment with high-quality anomaly explanations sourced from multiple large VLMs using task-specific rewards; fine-tune a parameter-efficient model, VisAnomReasoner, on the benchmark.
AI.result: On VisAnomBench, VisAnomReasoner yields precision and F1 improvements of at least 21.23 and 23.87 percentage points; on TSB-AD-U, improvements of 9.57 and 13.39 percentage points in precision and F1 respectively.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effective grounding of anomaly decisions with natural-language explanations, achieving accurate localization and strong cross-benchmark generalization while remaining parameter-efficient.

title: Q-ANCHOR: Federated Quantum Learning with ZNE-guided Correction
summary: Quantum Federated Learning (QFL) offers a promising framework to train quantum models across distributed clients while keeping data strictly local. Due to its simplicity and low communication overhead, Federated Averaging (FedAvg) is the standard aggregation choice in QFL literature. However, deploying QFL on practical hardware exposes a severe double-drift phenomenon: the global model is simultaneously derailed by client drift from non-IID data and hardware bias from noisy quantum gradient estimates. In this work, we first analyze the convergence of FedAvg under these realistic conditions, mathematically demonstrating that quantum hardware bias creates a persistent error floor that standard averaging cannot correct. To overcome this limitation, we propose Q-ANCHOR, a quantum-aware federated aggregation architecture that anchors server updates with zero-noise extrapolation while applying stateful client correction to suppress both client drift and hardware-induced bias. Our convergence theory proves that Q-ANCHOR successfully mitigates classical client drift while actively reducing the hardware-bias floor. Experimental results demonstrate that Q-ANCHOR achieves significantly more stable training than conventional FL baselines.

AI.tldr: QFL with FedAvg suffers from double drift due to non-IID data and hardware bias; Q-ANCHOR uses zero-noise extrapolation and stateful client correction to mitigate both and stabilize training.
AI.motivation: Address practical limitations of quantum federated learning by counteracting both classical client drift and quantum hardware-induced bias to enable reliable convergence.
AI.method: Analyze FedAvg convergence under non-IID data and hardware bias; design Q-ANCHOR as a quantum-aware aggregation using zero-noise extrapolation for server updates and stateful client correction; provide convergence theory and empirical evaluation comparing to baselines.
AI.result: Theoretical results show Q-ANCHOR mitigates the drift and reduces the hardware-bias floor; experiments demonstrate significantly more stable training than conventional federated learning baselines.
AI.conclusion: Q-ANCHOR offers a practical solution to the double-drift problem in quantum federated learning, improving convergence stability and enabling more reliable QFL deployment on noisy quantum hardware.

title: SchGen: PCB Schematic Generation with Semantic-Grounded Code Representations
summary: Printed circuit board (PCB) schematic design defines nearly all electronic hardware, but it remains manual and expertise-intensive. While generative AI has advanced digital and analog IC design, PCB schematic generation from natural-language intent is largely unexplored. This paper presents SchGen, the first large language model that generates editable PCB schematics from natural-language requests. The key challenge lies in the lack of an LLM-suited representation and a large-scale dataset. Current schematic formats are dominated by verbose, tool-specific syntax and geometry-heavy descriptions, making them difficult to generate reliably. We introduce a semantically grounded code representation that encodes schematic editing primitives with relative placement and pin-name-based wiring, transforming a geometry-driven generation problem into a semantics-driven matching task amenable to LLMs. We further construct a large-scale dataset of PCB schematics paired with user prompts via a human-agent collaborative pipeline that converts open-source hardware designs into our representation. Experiments show that SchGen significantly outperforms alternative representations and even larger general-purpose LLMs on wire connectivity accuracy and functional correctness. Our results highlight the critical role of representation design in enabling generative models for complex hardware design tasks.

AI.tldr: SchGen proposes a semantics-based, editable PCB schematic representation and a large-open dataset to enable NL-to-schematic generation with LLMs, achieving superior wire connectivity and functional accuracy over baseline representations and general LLMs.
AI.motivation: Fill the gap in NL-to-PCB schematic generation by designing an LLM-suited representation and a large-scale, human-annotated dataset to enable robust, editable schematic generation.
AI.method: Introduce a semantically grounded code representation encoding schematic editing primitives with relative placement and pin-name wiring; build a large dataset by converting open-source designs into this representation via a human-agent collaboration pipeline; evaluate LLM-based generation on wire connectivity and functional correctness.
AI.result: SchGen significantly outperforms alternative, geometry-heavy representations and larger general-purpose LLMs in connectivity accuracy and functional correctness.
AI.conclusion: Representation design is critical for enabling robust NL-to-hardware generation with LLMs; the approach demonstrates the viability of semantics-driven matching for complex hardware tasks and suggests a path for integrating NL requests into editable PCB schematics.

title: Distributionally Robust Set Representation Learning Under Inference-Time Element Corruption
summary: Standard Set Representation Learning methods typically excel on curated data but often overlook the challenge of inference-time element corruption. This refers to scenarios where deployed models encounter element-level degradations, such as outliers or missing components, that may distort set representation and degrade performance. We propose SW-DRSO, a distributionally robust optimization framework tailored for sets. Rather than minimizing loss solely on observed training data, SW-DRSO optimizes a tractable surrogate of the worst-case expected loss over a family of plausible inference-time variations. We introduce a barycentric adversary that approximates the intractable search over corrupted sets by a differentiable training-time optimization over simplex weights. Extensive experiments across four tasks demonstrate that SW-DRSO effectively enhances robustness against corruption while maintaining high overall performance.

AI.tldr: SW-DRSO introduces a distributionally robust optimization for set representations, using a barycentric adversary to approximate worst-case inference-time corruption, yielding improved robustness while maintaining performance across tasks.
AI.motivation: Inference-time element corruption (outliers, missing components) can distort set representations; standard methods trained on clean data may underperform under such perturbations.
AI.method: SW-DRSO defines a distributionally robust optimization framework for sets. It minimizes a tractable surrogate of the worst-case expected loss over a family of plausible corrupted sets. A barycentric adversary is proposed to approximate the intractable search over corrupted sets via differentiable training-time optimization over simplex weights.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across four tasks shows that SW-DRSO improves robustness to corruption while preserving high overall performance.
AI.conclusion: SW-DRSO provides an effective, tractable approach to robust set representation learning under inference-time element corruption by combining distributionally robust optimization with a differentiable barycentric adversary.

title: Physics Is All You Need? A Case Study in Physicist-Supervised AI Development of Scientific Software
comment: 10 pages, 2 figures, 2 tables, 1 physicist and a few AI agents. Accepted by ICML 2026 AI for Science Workshop. Code and development log are available at this repo: https://github.com/MinhMPA/clax-pt
summary:
Are AI agents tools, co-authors, or researchers? We present a quantified case study ($N=1$): a physicist supervising an AI coding agent (Claude Code, Sonnet and Opus models) over 12 work days and 57 sessions to build CLAX-PT, a differentiable one-loop perturbation theory module in JAX. We documented and classified 15 supervision events by intervention level.
  The agent resolved ten autonomously by iterating against oracle tests. Two more by the physicist's domain knowledge. The three it could not -- all evaded oracle detection -- share a common property: the agent treated symptom reduction as root-cause resolution. It spent 33 of the 57 sessions adjusting coefficients within a code architecture that could not represent the target physics, and could not re-evaluate its CLASS-PT branch choice even when prompted to reconsider; only an injected physics concept (anisotropic BAO damping) triggered the redesign. Separately, the agent committed a calibrated correction that passed all oracle tests but corresponded to no quantity in the theory, predicting wrong values at any other cosmology.
  The fudge factor was caught and replaced within the same session. Three supervision practices proved critical for catching what oracle tests missed: testing at diverse parameter points beyond the fiducial calibration; shared changelogs that surfaced stalled exploration across sessions; and an explicit rule against unphysical numerical patches. In this case, supervision design, not model capability, determined whether the agent's output was trustworthy. Closing the gap would require agents that propose architectural alternatives rather than optimize within a given structure, and distinguish predictive adequacy from explanatory correctness -- capabilities not exhibited here, not obviously addressed by scaling alone. [Abridged.]

AI.tldr: One-in-a-case study evaluating supervision of an AI coding agent by a physicist over 12 work days (57 sessions) to build CLAX-PT; most tasks solved autonomously; three failures due to symptom-reduction thinking; three key supervision practices enabled catching issues; conclusion emphasizes supervision design and need for architectural alternatives rather than scaling alone.
AI.motivation: Assess whether AI agents act as tools, co-authors, or researchers and examine trustworthiness through supervision; identify failure modes and requirements for reliable AI-assisted scientific coding.
AI.method: Single-case qualitative study (N=1): 57 sessions across 12 workdays; 15 supervision events classified by intervention level; use of oracle tests; domain-knowledge injections; analysis of failure modes and correction processes; evaluation of calibration patches.
AI.result: Agent autonomously resolved 10 tasks; 2 resolved with physicist guidance; 3 unresolved by the agent but detectable; one calibrated correction passed oracle tests but incorrect outside calibration; fudge-factor replaced within the same session; three supervision practices (diverse parameter testing, changelogs, explicit anti-patch rule) crucial for catching issues; architectural redesign triggered by injected physics concept; overall, supervision design determined trustworthiness.
AI.conclusion: Trustworthiness in AI-assisted scientific coding hinges on supervision design rather than model capabilities; to close the gap, agents must propose architectural alternatives and distinguish predictive adequacy from explanatory correctness; scaling alone is insufficient.

title: Chess-World-Model: A 10M-Game Benchmark for Exact State Tracking from Chess Move Sequences
summary: World models require state tracking, which is the ability to maintain a correct latent state across action sequences. Existing benchmarks are often synthetic or language-based, limiting their value as tests of structured state updates in realistic domains. We introduce Chess-World-Model, a large-scale state-tracking benchmark built from 10 million real chess games, where models predict the exact board state reached after a sequence of legal moves. Alongside a held-out real-game split, we include an out-of-distribution split from uniformly random legal play, which tests whether models learn the transition rules rather than shortcuts from common human positions. Prior theoretical and empirical work has shown that Transformers struggle to state-track, while input-dependent linear RNNs require expressive state-transition matrices to do so. We therefore benchmark a causal Transformer, block-diagonal SLiCE, Mamba-3, and Gated DeltaNet with negative eigenvalues under a matched interface and training protocol. The recurrent models strongly outperform the Transformer at 3 and 8 million parameters. Real-game performance saturates above 18 million parameters, but the random-uniform split remains discriminative up to 40 million, exposing failures otherwise hidden by scale. Additionally, ablations show that less expressive state-transition mechanisms reduce performance on the out-of-distribution split for all three recurrent models. Together, these results establish Chess-World-Model as a practical large-scale benchmark for state tracking that exposes failures model scale would otherwise conceal.

AI.tldr: A large-scale state-tracking benchmark (Chess-World-Model) built from 10M real chess games to predict post-sequence board states, with held-out real-game and random-ODD splits; recurrent models outperform Transformers and reveal scale-dependent limitations.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks are often synthetic or language-based, limiting evaluation of structured state updates in realistic domains. A real-game, large-scale benchmark tests whether models learn proper state-transition rules rather than shortcuts.
AI.method: Dataset derived from 10 million real chess games; models predict the exact board state after a sequence of legal moves. Includes a held-out real-game split and an out-of-distribution split from uniformly random legal play to stress transition-rule learning. Evaluates multiple models (causal Transformer, block-diagonal SLiCE, Mamba-3, Gated DeltaNet with negative eigenvalues) under a matched interface and training protocol.
AI.result: Recurrent models outperform the Transformer at 3M and 8M parameters; real-game performance saturates above 18M, but the random-uniform split remains discriminative up to 40M, exposing failures hidden by scale; ablations show less expressive state-transition mechanisms hurt OOD performance for all recurrent models.
AI.conclusion: Chess-World-Model provides a practical, large-scale benchmark for state tracking that reveals failures scale would otherwise conceal.

title: Convergence Theory for Iterative LLM-Based Neural Architecture Search: A Parametric Cross-Entropy Framework with Closed-Form Proxy Reliability
comment: 14 pages, 2 figures, 2 tables. Submitted to NeurIPS 2026
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as generators in iterative neural architecture search (NAS), yet no formal convergence theory exists for this class of algorithms. We model iterative LLM-NAS as a parametric Cross-Entropy (CE) method over executable programs and prove six results: (1) iterative LLM fine-tuning on elite architectures is equivalent to the CE update restricted to the LLM parametric family; (2) expected architecture quality is monotonically non-decreasing across cycles; (3) elite-set probability converges to a fixed point at a geometric rate C_t >= 1-(1-rho_0)^t; (4) delta-based generation achieves a strictly higher valid-generation rate than full-code generation under a first-order Markov token-error model; (5) the MinHash-Jaccard novelty filter prevents mode collapse; (6) proxy reliability admits the closed-form rho_S = (6/pi) arcsin(rho_P(SNR)/2), yielding the practical diagnostic sigma^2_arch >> sigma^2_noise as a necessary condition for trustworthy proxy-based rankings. Testing against a 22-cycle, three-LLM, six-dataset experiment with 3,300 generated architectures confirms two predictions quantitatively, two at direction-of-effect level, and explains the proxy-reliability ceiling effect previously reported empirically but left unexplained.

AI.tldr: Models iterative LLM-guided NAS as a parametric CE method; proves six theoretical results about convergence, generation strategies, novelty filtering, and proxy reliability, with partial empirical validation across cycles, LLMs, and datasets.
AI.motivation: There is no formal convergence theory for iterative LLM-NAS; understanding convergence, performance monotonicity, and proxy reliability is essential for trustworthy, scalable NAS with LLMs.
AI.method: Formal analysis of LLM-NAS as a parametric Cross-Entropy optimization over executable programs; derives equivalences and bounds for elite-set updates, monotone improvement, convergence rate, token-generation dynamics, novelty filtering effects, and a closed-form proxy reliability relation; validates predictions on a 22-cycle, 3-LLM, 6-dataset experiment (3,300 architectures).
AI.result: Six theoretical results: (1) fine-tuning on elite architectures equals CE update restricted to LLM family; (2) expected quality is non-decreasing per cycle; (3) elite-set probability converges to fixed point geometrically; (4) delta-based generation yields higher valid-generation rate than full-code under a Markov error model; (5) MinHash-Jaccard novelty filter prevents mode collapse; (6) closed-form proxy reliability rho_S = (6/pi) arcsin(rho_P(SNR)/2) yielding a practical diagnostic sigma^2_arch >> sigma^2_noise as necessary for trustworthy proxy rankings. Empirical testing confirms two quantitative predictions, two direction-level effects, and explains proxy-reliability ceiling observed previously.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a formal convergence framework for LLM-guided NAS, identifies key factors for monotone improvement and reliable proxy-based ranking, and offers practical diagnostics (proxy SNR condition and novelty filtering) to mitigate mode collapse and proxy unreliability; partial empirical validation supports several predictions and clarifies observed ceilings in proxy reliability.

title: VLA-Pro: Cross-Task Procedural Memory Transfer for Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-Language-Action~(VLA) models have shown strong potential for general-purpose robotic manipulation, yet they still struggle to generalize to unseen tasks that necessitate transferring relevant experience across objects, scenes, and action patterns. This paper proposes VLA-Pro, a plug-and-play framework designed to enhance cross-task generalization by storing task-relevant procedural memories at training time and transferring these memories during inference. Specifically, VLA-Pro stores task-specific LoRA adapters as parameterized procedural memories during training. At inference time, VLA-Pro retrieves relevant procedural memories based on the current multi-modal context and dynamically fuses these memories for generating the current action chunk. Experiments on RoboTwin, RLBench, and real-world manipulation tasks show that VLA-Pro consistently improves cross-task generalization across multiple backbones, achieving up to a 207% relative improvement in simulation and increasing real-world success rate from 5.8% to 65.0%. These results suggest that procedural memory retrieval and adaptation provide an effective mechanism for transferring manipulation experience to novel tasks while preserving modularity and execution stability.

AI.tldr: VLA-Pro is a plug-and-play framework that stores task-specific LoRA adapters as procedural memories during training and retrieves/fuses them at inference to improve cross-task generalization in Vision-Language-Action robotics, achieving significant improvements across RoboTwin, RLBench, and real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: Address the limited cross-task generalization of VLA models by enabling the transfer of relevant experience across objects, scenes, and action patterns through modular procedural memories.
AI.method: Store task-specific LoRA adapters as parameterized procedural memories during training; at inference, retrieve context-relevant memories using the current multi-modal context and dynamically fuse them to generate the current action chunk.
AI.result: Consistent cross-task generalization improvements across backbones; up to 207% relative improvement in simulation; real-world success rate increased from 5.8% to 65.0%; validated on RoboTwin, RLBench, and real-world manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: Procedural memory retrieval and adaptation provides an effective mechanism for transferring manipulation experience to novel tasks while preserving modularity and execution stability.

title: Striding Across Reynolds Numbers: Representation Geometry in Neural PDE Generalisation
summary: Cross-Reynolds generalisation in neural PDE solvers remains poorly characterised. On the canonical forced 2D Navier-Stokes benchmark, a trained Fourier Neural Operator reaches 46.68% relative L2 error under a 10x Reynolds-number shift, yet zero-forward-model retrieval baselines already improve to 41-42%. This suggests representation geometry as a major organising variable among the tested methods. We test this hypothesis through ConvAE-Relay, which matches states in a source-trained convolutional autoencoder latent space and borrows dynamics from a source-regime database, achieving 38.34+/-0.07% using only a source-regime database and no target-regime fitting, labels, or database entries. A 2x2 ablation isolates matching quality as dominant over the update rule. Oracle experiments confirm that source-regime dynamics directions remain transferable (cosine similarity ~0.84) when matching stays on-manifold; autoregressive drift is the primary bottleneck (~12 percentage points). From the learned-prediction side, a U-Net with multi-scale skip connections achieves 34.72+/-0.60%, consistent with the retrieval-side finding that local, multi-scale representations organise cross-Reynolds transfer among tested methods. All claims are scoped to this benchmark.

AI.tldr: Cross-Reynolds generalisation in neural PDE solvers is governed by representation geometry; source-regime latent matching with multi-scale features enables cross-Reynolds transfer with limited or no target data, while autoregressive drift is the primary bottleneck; results are demonstrated on a canonical 2D Navier–Stokes benchmark and are benchmark-scoped.
AI.motivation: To characterize and improve cross-Reynolds generalisation in neural PDE solvers, addressing the observed performance drop under Reynolds-number shifts and identifying the representational factors that enable transfer.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation on 2D forced Navier-Stokes using Fourier Neural Operators; introduce ConvAE-Relay that matches latent states from a source-trained Conv-AE and borrows dynamics from a source-regime database; perform ablations (2x2) to compare matching vs update rules; conduct oracle experiments to probe transferability of source-regime dynamics; evaluate a learned-prediction U-Net with multi-scale skip connections.
AI.result: FNO baseline: 46.68% relative L2 error under 10x Reynolds shift; zero-forward-model retrieval baselines: 41–42%; ConvAE-Relay: 38.34±0.07% using only source-regime data and no target data; 2x2 ablation shows matching quality dominates over update rule; oracle dynamics transfer cosine similarity ≈0.84; autoregressive drift is the main bottleneck (~12 percentage points); learned-prediction U-Net with multi-scale skips: 34.72±0.60%; findings align that local, multi-scale representations organize cross-Reynolds transfer; all claims scoped to the benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Representation geometry—particularly local, multi-scale latent structures—organises cross-Reynolds transfer more than the specific update dynamics; high-quality latent matching to a source-regime database enables substantial transfer even without target-regime data, though autoregressive drift remains a bottleneck; these conclusions are validated within the studied 2D Navier–Stokes benchmark.

title: Emergent Semantic Representations in World Models through Physical Interaction without Linguistic Supervision
summary: What does a world model learn from physical exploration, without any linguistic supervision? We argue the answer is organized by a single principle: the geometric structure of the physical world. Training a VAE-based world model on random embodied exploration, we find that its latent space develops spatial semantic structure that mirrors physical geometry -- direction accuracy 0.677+-0.029 versus 0.547 for a randomly initialized encoder, and position RSA 0.192+-0.047 versus 0.029 for random encoders (6.6x improvement), showing that training induces genuine structural organization beyond CNN inductive bias. Across 20 temporal checkpoints, prediction performance and semantic alignment co-improve (Spearman r=-0.61, p=0.004), consistent with the shared-driver account. We confirm this through a double knockout: standard KL regularization (beta=0.1) forces the encoder away from geometric structure, and both prediction performance and semantic alignment collapse simultaneously to near-chance by step 50,000 -- exactly as the shared-driver account predicts. Reducing beta to 0.001 restores geometric access and recovers both capabilities together. These findings establish physical world geometry as the organizing principle of world model representations, with direct implications for the design of semantically grounded embodied agents.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised VAE-based world modeling learns geometry-aligned latent structure from random embodied exploration; semantic alignment and predictive accuracy co-improve, and are causally tied to a shared geometric driver, as shown by KL-based ablations that disrupt/recover geometry.
AI.motivation: To determine what a world model learns in the absence of linguistic supervision and whether geometric physical structure governs its latent representations, beyond CNN inductive biases, with implications for semantically grounded embodied agents.
AI.method: Train a VAE-based world model on random embodied exploration. Assess latent geometry via direction accuracy and position RSA, compare against randomly initialized encoders, track 20 temporal checkpoints. Conduct a double knockout with KL regularization (beta=0.1) that disrupts geometry, then reduce beta to 0.001 to recover. Report predictive performance and semantic alignment (e.g., Spearman correlations) and p-values.
AI.result: Latent space develops spatial semantic structure mirroring physical geometry: direction accuracy 0.677±0.029 vs 0.547 for random encoder; position RSA 0.192±0.047 vs 0.029 (6.6x). Over 20 checkpoints, prediction performance and semantic alignment co-improve (Spearman r=-0.61, p=0.004). KL regularization (beta=0.1) pushes encoder away from geometry and collapses both capabilities to near-chance by step 50,000; lowering beta to 0.001 restores geometric access and recovers both abilities.
AI.conclusion: Physical world geometry is the organizing principle of world-model representations. This supports the shared-driver account and has implications for designing semantically grounded, geometry-aware embodied agents.

title: Evolving Features vs Evolving Entire Trees with GP for Interpretable Survival Analysis
summary:
Survival analysis concerns the task of predicting the time until an event occurs. Often used in the medical field, survival analysis deals with incomplete (i.e., censored) data, for instance, from patients who did not experience the event during the duration of the study. For practical use, both accuracy and interpretability are important.
  Survival trees are easy-to-follow survival models that split the patient cohort recursively into discrete patient groups. Whilst survival trees can capture complex relationships, they typically need to grow large, threatening interpretability. Moreover, survival trees are often built using greedy approaches that may overlook globally optimal split combinations, limiting predictive performance.
  Shallow survival trees require expressive, higher-order feature combinations to achieve competitive accuracy. We therefore use genetic programming to multi-objectively evolve inherently inspectable feature sets and study how they interact with different tree induction strategies. We further introduce an evolutionary approach that jointly optimises the survival tree structure and the non-linear split logic.
  Our findings demonstrate that evolutionary feature construction improves predictive performance across different tree induction strategies on two real-world datasets and two different survival tree depths. Full joint evolution has the overall highest potential to propose multiple inherently inspectable shallow survival trees of good performance.

AI.tldr: Evolutionary feature construction improves predictive performance and interpretability of survival trees; joint evolution yields best shallow models.
AI.motivation: Survival analysis requires both accuracy and interpretability. Greedy survival trees can be large and miss globally optimal split combinations, especially for shallow models where higher-order features are needed.
AI.method: A multi-objective genetic programming (GP) approach is used to evolve inherently inspectable feature sets and study their interaction with different tree induction strategies. An additional evolutionary approach jointly optimizes both the survival tree structure and the non-linear split logic. The methods are evaluated on two real-world datasets with two survival-tree depths.
AI.result: Evolutionary feature construction improves predictive performance across different tree induction strategies. Full joint evolution has the highest potential to produce multiple inherently inspectable shallow survival trees of good performance.
AI.conclusion: Joint evolution of tree structure and split logic with evolved features provides the strongest balance of interpretability and accuracy for shallow survival trees, and outperforms strategies that optimize features or structure separately.

title: Continuity and Ordinality Matter: Constraining Time Series Tokens for Effective Time Series Analysis with Large Language Models
summary: Token-based time series large language models (TS-LLMs) have emerged as a promising direction for time series analysis and reasoning. However, prior studies largely overlook the inherent continuity and ordinality of time series tokens, which substantially limits model performance. In this paper, we argue that preserving these properties in time series token embeddings is crucial for the effectiveness of token-based TS-LLMs. To this end, we propose COM (Continuity and Ordinality Matter), a continuity- and ordinality-aware strategy that integrates geometric constraints into both the initialization and training stages. Empirical results on multiple time series analysis benchmarks demonstrate that COM consistently improves the performance of token-based TS-LLMs, achieving competitive results and strong generalizability. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/COM .

AI.tldr: COM enforces continuity and ordinality in time-series token embeddings for TS-LLMs via geometric constraints in initialization and training, yielding consistent improvements across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing token-based TS-LLMs overlook the intrinsic continuity and ordinality of time-series tokens, which can degrade performance. Preserving these properties in embeddings should improve reasoning and generalization.
AI.method: Introduce COM, a continuity- and ordinality-aware strategy that imposes geometric constraints on token embeddings during both initialization and training to preserve temporal structure in the latent space.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations across multiple time-series benchmarks show consistent performance gains and strong generalization, with competitive results.
AI.conclusion: Preserving continuity and ordinality in time-series token embeddings is beneficial for TS-LLMs; COM provides an effective, generalizable approach; code is released.

title: Beyond MSE: Improving Precipitation Nowcasting with Multi-Quantile Regression
summary: Deep-learning precipitation nowcasting models are often optimized using pointwise losses such as mean squared error or mean absolute error, which can lead to overly smooth forecasts and poor representation of heavy rainfall. This study investigates whether the predictive performance of an established deterministic nowcasting architecture can be improved by reformulating training as a multi-quantile regression problem. Using SmaAt-UNet as a core model, we compare MSE, MAE, and multi-quantile pinball-loss training on radar precipitation nowcasting over the Netherlands. The results show that multi-quantile training improves the central deterministic forecast, decreasing test-set MSE by 8.6\% compared to a model trained using MSE, while also producing upper-quantile outputs that are useful for risk-sensitive prediction of heavy precipitation. These findings suggest that quantile regression provides a simple alternative to standard pointwise losses without requiring a new architecture or generative sampling procedure. The implementation of our models and training setup is available on \href{https://github.com/gijsvn/Multi-Quantile-Precipitation-Nowcasting}{GitHub}.

AI.tldr: Multi-quantile pinball-loss training on SmaAt-UNet improves the central forecast (MSE ↓ 8.6% vs MSE baseline) and yields useful upper quantiles for heavy rainfall without changing architecture; code available on GitHub.
AI.motivation: Pointwise losses (MSE/MAE) can produce overly smooth rainfall forecasts and fail to represent heavy rainfall. Multi-quantile regression can capture forecast distribution and provide risk-relevant upper quantiles.
AI.method: Empirical comparison using SmaAt-UNet for radar precipitation nowcasting over the Netherlands, evaluating MSE, MAE, and multi-quantile pinball-loss training.
AI.result: Multi-quantile training improves the central deterministic forecast (MSE reduction of 8.6% vs MSE-trained baseline) and yields informative upper quantiles for heavy rainfall predictions; no new architecture or generative sampling needed; GitHub implementation available.
AI.conclusion: Quantile regression offers a simple, effective alternative to pointwise losses for nowcasting, enabling sharper central forecasts and useful probabilistic information without architectural changes.

title: PrismFlow: Residual Dynamics for Flow Matching in Time-Series Generation
summary: Generating high-quality time-series data is challenging because real-world signals often exhibit multimodal patterns and multiscale dynamics, including oscillations and high-frequency variations. Flow Matching (FM) offers an efficient alternative to diffusion models, but practical implementations typically rely on a single finite-capacity global vector-field estimator. In such heterogeneous temporal distributions, distinct regimes may pass through nearby flow states while requiring incompatible conditional velocities. A monolithic estimator trained with the standard $\ell_2$ velocity-matching objective may therefore learn an overly smoothed approximation of the local transport field. This estimator-level smoothing can attenuate branch-specific dynamics, leading to spectral distortion and poor mode coverage. To address this, we propose PrismFlow, a new FM method with Koopman-inspired dynamical experts. Each expert learns residual corrections in a latent space where local nonlinear temporal evolution can be approximated by linear transitions. We further propose a confidence-aware Winner-Take-All (WTA) objective that updates only the expert best aligned with each sample while masking gradients to the others, encouraging mode-specific specialization. During sampling, the selected expert adds a residual dynamical correction to the global transport field, preserving FM stability while recovering fine-grained and high-frequency temporal structures. Across various benchmarks, PrismFlow effectively mitigates the spectral contraction in standard FM and achieves state-of-the-art performance, with a 15.6% gain in Context-FID and a 38.6% improvement in Discriminative Score, while remaining robust in low-data settings and effective for forecasting and imputation.

AI.tldr: PrismFlow introduces Koopman-inspired dynamical experts with a confidence-aware Winner-Take-All objective to address multimodal, multiscale temporal dynamics in Flow Matching, yielding improved mode coverage and reduced spectral contraction.
AI.motivation: Standard flow matching with a single finite-capacity global vector-field estimator tends to overly smooth local transport fields, causing spectral distortion and poor mode coverage in heterogeneous time-series with multiple regimes.
AI.method: PrismFlow deploys multiple latent-space linearizable experts that learn residual corrections to a global transport field. A confidence-aware Winner-Take-All objective updates only the best-aligned expert per sample (masking gradients for others). During sampling, the selected expert injects a residual dynamical correction, preserving FM stability while recovering fine-grained temporal structure.
AI.result: Empirical gains include Context-FID +15.6% and Discriminative Score +38.6% across benchmarks; robust in low-data settings and effective for forecasting and imputation; spectral contraction is mitigated and mode coverage improved, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.conclusion: PrismFlow successfully mitigates the limitations of monolithic flow-matching estimators by enabling regime-specific, latent-space linear dynamics through multiple experts and a selective updating rule, resulting in better temporal fidelity and performance.

title: Learning to Extrapolate to New Tasks: A Relational Approach to Task Extrapolation
summary: Modern learning systems excel at interpolation but struggle to generalize to unseen tasks outside the training distribution's support. This failure occurs even in simple settings, such as handling task parameters beyond the training range, and persists despite advances in foundation models. To this end, we develop the Relational Task Extrapolator (RTE), an algorithm designed to enable systematic extrapolation to novel tasks. The key observation is that extrapolation is inherently relational: extrapolating to unseen tasks requires learning how tasks transform into one another. If a model learns the transformation between tasks A and B during training, it can apply that same transformation to relate known tasks to unseen ones at test time. RTE operationalizes this idea by decomposing each target task into a known anchor task and a transformation linking the anchor and target. It then learns a relational operator, mapping an anchor-transformation pair to predictions for the target task. We instantiate RTE across multiple task extrapolation regimes in function prediction, e.g. where target tasks use out-of-range parameters (parameter extrapolation), have greater compositional depth (length extrapolation), and/or recombine function primitives in unseen ways (compositional extrapolation). We further extend RTE to sequence prediction, integrating it into fine-tuning algorithms for foundation models. Across empirical studies, we find that RTE substantially outperforms existing approaches on extrapolation to novel, unseen tasks.

AI.tldr: Relational Task Extrapolator (RTE) enables systematic extrapolation to unseen tasks by learning how tasks transform into one another and applying learned transformations to anchor tasks to predict target tasks; demonstrated across function and sequence prediction with various extrapolation regimes and extended to foundation-model fine-tuning, showing strong extrapolation performance.
AI.motivation: The problem is that modern learning systems interpolate well but struggle to generalize to tasks outside the training distribution, including beyond training task parameters. There is a need for a scalable, task-aware inductive bias for extrapolation.
AI.method: Decompose each target task into a known anchor task plus a transformation linking the anchor to the target. Learn a relational operator that maps an anchor-transformation pair to predictions for the target task. Instantiate this across parameter extrapolation, length (compositional depth) extrapolation, and compositional extrapolation in function prediction; extend to sequence prediction and integrate into fine-tuning for foundation models.
AI.result: Empirical studies show that RTE substantially outperforms existing approaches on extrapolation to novel, unseen tasks across multiple regimes.
AI.conclusion: Relational extrapolation provides a general, effective inductive bias for task extrapolation; RTE offers a practical framework applicable to function and sequence tasks and to fine-tuning foundation models, with broad potential impact on out-of-distribution generalization.

title: DAMEL: Dual-Axis Multi-Expert Learning for Class-Imbalanced Learning
summary: Various algorithms have been proposed to address the challenges posed by class-imbalanced learning from real-world data with long-tailed distributions. While these algorithms reduce prediction bias through rebalancing techniques, they often introduce increased prediction variance as a trade-off. Several multi-expert learning algorithms aim to address this variance but involve complex procedures. We propose a new multi-expert learning algorithm, called the dual-axis multi-expert learning (DAMEL), which reduces both bias and variance of predictions by using multiple experts along both representation and time axes. Along the representation axis, DAMEL concatenates the representations of multiple experts and trains an auxiliary balanced classifier simultaneously with the concatenated representations. Along the time axis, DAMEL aggregates network weights across training epochs, employing these aggregated weights during testing. Experimental results demonstrate that DAMEL reduces both bias and variance of predictions, highlighting its effectiveness in class-imbalanced learning.

AI.tldr: DAMEL is a dual-axis multi-expert framework for long-tailed learning that reduces bias and variance by combining multiple experts across representation and temporal axes, using concatenated representations with an auxiliary balanced classifier and epoch-weight aggregation at test time.
AI.motivation: Long-tailed data cause classifier bias toward majority classes and higher variance. Rebalancing helps bias but increases variance; existing multi-expert methods exist but are often complex. A simpler, effective dual-axis approach could better balance bias and variance.
AI.method: Construct DAMEL with two axes: (1) representation axis by training multiple experts to produce representations, concatenating these representations, and training an auxiliary balanced classifier on the concatenated features; (2) time axis by aggregating network weights across training epochs and using these averaged weights during testing.
AI.result: Experiments indicate that DAMEL reduces both bias and variance in predictions, outperforming baselines on long-tailed learning tasks.
AI.conclusion: DAMEL effectively mitigates the bias-variance trade-off in class-imbalanced learning by leveraging dual-axis multi-expert collaboration, providing a competitive and potentially simpler alternative to existing multi-expert methods.

title: Balancing Multimodal Learning through Label Space Reshaping
summary: Multimodal learning often suffers from modality imbalance, where modalities that converge faster dominate optimization while others remain undertrained. Existing approaches typically mitigate this issue by strengthening the weak modality or adjusting optimization gradients. However, such strategies mainly compensate for optimization rate discrepancies, often at the expense of the strong modality's optimization capacity, without analyzing how these discrepancies arise at the modality level. Based on theoretical insights and empirical observations, we argue that the discrepancy of learning pace arises from differences in the mapping difficulty between modality-specific feature space and the shared label space. To address this issue, we propose Balanced Multimodal Label Reshaping (BMLR), the first method that promotes multimodal balance from the label-side design. BMLR reshapes the cross-modal label space to equalize mapping difficulty across modalities, thereby facilitating modality interaction and injecting richer inter-class information into each modality. Extensive experiments across multiple architectures demonstrate that BMLR consistently improves multimodal performance and exhibits strong compatibility with diverse model designs. The source code will be released soon.

AI.tldr: Proposes Balanced Multimodal Label Reshaping (BMLR), a label-side approach to mitigate modality imbalance by reshaping cross-modal label space to equalize mapping difficulty across modalities, improving multimodal integration.
AI.motivation: Modality imbalance in multimodal learning arises from differences in how hard it is for each modality's features to map to the shared label space, leading to faster-learning modalities dominating and undertraining others.
AI.method: Introducing BMLR, which reshapes the cross-modal label space to balance mapping difficulty across modalities; aims to align label-space mappings to ensure consistent learning pace across modalities, and is reported to be compatible with diverse architectures.
AI.result: Empirical results across multiple architectures show consistent performance gains and strong compatibility; improvements observed across datasets/tasks, with code to be released.
AI.conclusion: BMLR is a novel label-side mechanism that supplements optimization-based remedies by equalizing cross-modal mapping difficulty, enabling richer inter-class information and improved multimodal collaboration.

title: Overcoming Forgetting in LLM Fine-Tuning with Evolution Strategies
summary: Evolution Strategies (ES) has recently emerged as a competitive alternative to reinforcement learning (RL) for large language model (LLM) fine-tuning, offering advantages through simplicity, scalability, and inference-only training. However, recent work suggests that ES fine-tuning on new tasks may induce forgetting of prior tasks. First, this paper shows that prior task forgetting (1) is better characterized as performance drift rather than irreversible forgetting, with prior-task performance often recovering during ES training; and (2) is not a specific failure mode of ES, but can also arise for fine-tuning with RL methods. Second, it analyzes when and why such drift arises, highlighting its dependence on ES training dynamics, particularly random walk behavior in weakly constrained directions of the weight space. Third, based on these insights, it introduces Anchored Weight Decay (AWD) as a parameter-space regularization technique that constrains optimization toward the initial model parameters. AWD effectively stabilizes prior-task performance while preserving target-task performance, achieving benefits comparable to large ES population sizes at much lower computational cost. Thus, contrary to previous beliefs, the paper shows that prior-task forgetting under ES is largely avoidable, positioning ES as a promising approach for continual learning in LLMs.

AI.tldr: ES fine-tuning can cause prior-task performance drift, not irreversible forgetting; drift stems from optimization dynamics and weakly constrained weight directions; Anchored Weight Decay (AWD) stabilizes prior tasks while preserving target-task gains, offering ES-level benefits at lower cost, suggesting ES viability for continual learning in LLMs.
AI.motivation: Address whether Evolution Strategies (ES) cause forgetting on previously learned tasks during continual fine-tuning of LLMs, compare with RL, and improve stability with a regularizer.
AI.method: Analyze prior-task performance during ES fine-tuning, characterize drift as performance drift due to random-walk dynamics in weakly constrained weight directions; compare with RL; introduce Anchored Weight Decay (AWD) as parameter-space regularization that anchors optimization to the initial parameters; evaluate AWD's ability to stabilize prior-task performance while maintaining target-task performance and its computational efficiency relative to large ES populations.
AI.result: Prior-task performance often recovers during ES training, indicating drift rather than irreversible forgetting; this drift is influenced by ES dynamics, especially random-walk-like movement in under-constrained weight directions; AWD stabilizes prior-task performance by anchoring updates to initial parameters, preserving target-task gains with competitive results to large population sizes but at lower computational cost.
AI.conclusion: Prior-task forgetting under ES is largely avoidable with appropriate regularization; ES remains a promising approach for continual learning in LLMs when equipped with methods like AWD.

title: Representation Alignment Rests on Linear Structure
summary: We investigate the Platonic Representation Hypothesis (PRH) through a tripartite statistical framework of representations: signal, bias, and noise. {1) Signal:} We propose that Platonic alignment arises from the universal relationship between objects and attributes, which is encoded linearly in representations according to the Linear Representation Hypothesis (LRH). We provide evidence that LRH helps explain PRH by extracting linear object-attribute features with sparse autoencoders and showing that these sparse representations often exhibit stronger cross-modal alignment than their dense counterparts. {2) Bias:} Models have different implicit biases due to the diverse architectures and training procedures used. We show that this difference can be partially mitigated. Centering and normalization consistently improve cross-model alignment. {3) Noise:} Finite-sample training leads to noise in representations. We provide evidence that representational noise is driven by data scarcity by revealing a strong and consistent positive correlation between word frequency and alignment in LLMs and text embedding models. Synthesizing signal, bias, and noise, we propose a statistical model that refines the Linear Representation Hypothesis and explains further phenomena related to the alignment of representations emerging from diverse modern AI architectures.

AI.tldr: A tripartite signal-bias-noise framework for the Linear Representation Hypothesis (LRH) to explain Platonic Representation Hypothesis (PRH), showing that sparse autoencoder-derived linear object-attribute features yield stronger cross-modal alignment, bias can be mitigated by centering/normalization, and noise from data scarcity correlates with word frequency; culminates in a refined LRH that accounts for alignment across modern AI architectures.
AI.motivation: To understand and generalize the Platonic Representation Hypothesis using a statistical framework that disentangles signal (object-attribute structure), bias (model-specific inclinations), and noise (finite-sample effects).
AI.method: - Propose Linear Representation Hypothesis (LRH) as the core signal mechanism. - Use sparse autoencoders to extract linear object-attribute features and compare alignment of sparse vs dense representations. - Examine how centering and normalization affect cross-model alignment across architectures. - Analyze finite-sample training to characterize representational noise and relate word frequency to alignment in LLMs/text embeddings. - Synthesize findings into a refined statistical model that extends LRH.
AI.result: - Evidence that LRH captures PRH; sparse representations show stronger cross-modal alignment than dense ones. - Centering and normalization consistently improve cross-model alignment, mitigating biases across architectures. - Representational noise from data scarcity correlates positively with word frequency and alignment in LLMs/text embeddings. - The synthesis yields a refined LRH that explains alignment phenomena across diverse AI architectures.
AI.conclusion: A cohesive tripartite model (signal-bias-noise) refining LRH explains how representations align across modern AI systems, offering practical steps (normalization) and theoretical insights (data-frequency driven noise) to unify diverse architectures under PRH.

title: RL2ML: Finite-Rollout Surrogate Objectives from Reinforcement Learning to Maximum Likelihood
summary: Correctness-based Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) trains language models from binary feedback on sampled outputs, but the objective optimized in expectation and the stochastic update geometry induced by finite rollout groups are often conflated. This paper develops RL2ML, a family of finite-rollout surrogate objectives with a closed-form, exactly unbiased gradient estimator. The family continuously connects standard reinforcement learning, maximum-likelihood-like training, and beyond-maximum-likelihood objectives while preserving estimator-objective alignment under a fixed rollout budget. We introduce the group-level update scale to characterize how a rollout group is reweighted after its empirical success count is observed, revealing a subcritical-supercritical update-scale transition that is hidden by population-level objective notation alone. Building on this distinction, calibrated metric-gain analysis and exact variance decomposition show that the best choice of surrogate objective is determined neither by proximity to maximum likelihood nor by the population-level weight alone. Instead, it depends jointly on the evaluation metric, local sensitivity, and estimator variance. The remaining degree of freedom in the surrogate objective family can therefore be formulated as a one-dimensional optimization problem rather than treated as an unconstrained hyperparameter.

AI.tldr: RL2ML proposes finite-rollout surrogate objectives with a closed-form, unbiased gradient estimator that continuously connects standard reinforcement learning, maximum-likelihood-like training, and beyond-maximum-likelihood objectives under a fixed rollout budget. It introduces a group-level update scale, revealing a subcritical-supercritical transition hidden by population-level objective notation. The optimal surrogate depends on evaluation metric, local sensitivity, and estimator variance; the remaining degree of freedom is a one-dimensional optimization problem rather than an unconstrained hyperparameter.
AI.motivation: Correctness-based RL with verifiable rewards often conflates the objective optimized in expectation with the stochastic update geometry induced by finite rollout groups. There is a need for unbiased gradient estimators under finite rollout budgets and a principled understanding of surrogate objective choice beyond heuristic hyperparameters.
AI.method: Define RL2ML as a family of finite-rollout surrogate objectives with a closed-form, exactly unbiased gradient estimator. Analyze group-level update scale to reweight rollout groups after empirical success counts, revealing a subcritical-supercritical transition. Use calibrated metric-gain analysis and exact variance decomposition to determine how surrogate choice depends jointly on evaluation metric, local sensitivity, and estimator variance. Argue that the remaining degree of freedom is a one-dimensional optimization problem.
AI.result: A closed-form, exactly unbiased gradient estimator for the RL2ML family; identification of a subcritical–supercritical transition in update-scale; demonstration that surrogate objective performance depends on a combination of evaluation metric, local sensitivity, and estimator variance; reduction of the surrogate-design problem to a single dimension.
AI.conclusion: Surrogate objective selection should be treated as a constrained one-dimensional optimization problem that jointly accounts for evaluation metrics, local sensitivity, and estimator variance. The RL2ML framework thus unifies RL and ML-like training under a fixed rollout budget and provides a principled basis for choosing surrogate objectives beyond naive ML proximity or population weights.

title: Context Distillation as Latent Memory Management
summary: Context distillation compresses contextual information into model parameters, yet existing methods often ignore how multiple distilled latent memories should be stored, retrieved, and safely activated in non-oracle settings. We formulate context distillation as a latent memory management problem. We distill each context into an independent LoRA adapter, forming a modular memory bank that enables explicit memory selection. Given a query, our framework retrieves candidate memories, routes the query to the most suitable adapter, and uses a Self-Gating mechanism to decide whether latent memory should be activated. To improve efficiency, we further introduce cache sharing to reduce management overhead during inference. Experiments show that our method substantially outperforms baselines with retrieval, while Self-Gating improves robustness by deactivate unnecessary latent memories.

AI.tldr: Modular memory bank for context distillation using independent LoRA adapters with retrieval, routing, self-gating, and cache sharing; improves efficiency and robustness.
AI.motivation: Address how to store, retrieve, and safely activate multiple distilled latent memories in non-oracle settings; improve efficiency and robustness of context distillation.
AI.method: Distill each context into an independent LoRA adapter forming a memory bank; retrieve candidate memories for a query; route to the most suitable adapter; apply a Self-Gating mechanism to decide activation; employ cache sharing to reduce inference overhead.
AI.result: Experiments show substantial performance gains over baselines with retrieval; Self-Gating improves robustness by deactivating unnecessary latent memories.
AI.conclusion: Latent memory management framework with a modular memory bank, retrieval, Self-Gating, and cache sharing effectively enhances context distillation in terms of accuracy, robustness, and efficiency.

title: On Distributional Reinforcement Learning in Chaotic Dynamical Systems
summary: Chaotic dynamical systems pose a fundamental challenge for Reinforcement Learning (RL): exponential sensitivity to initial conditions induces high-variance bootstrap targets and poorly conditioned gradient updates. Chaotic dynamics arise across scientific and engineering domains, from fluid flows and climate systems to multi-agent systems, where reliable learning is highly desirable. Standard RL methods optimise expected returns through scalar value functions, implicitly averaging over diverging trajectories and entangling trajectory level instability with the learning objective. We show that under mild statistical stability assumptions, the return distribution evolves more regularly than individual trajectories when measured under the $1$-Wasserstein metric, yielding a smoother distributional Bellman objective. By aligning optimisation with this measure level structure, distributional RL provides better conditioned learning. We offer a principled explanation for the advantages of distributional methods in chaotic systems and the geometries of RL objectives under chaos.

AI.tldr: In chaotic RL regimes, distributional RL using 1-Wasserstein yields smoother return distributions and better conditioned learning, explaining why distributional methods excel under chaos.
AI.motivation: Chaotic dynamics cause high-variance targets and unstable gradients; scalar value functions entangle trajectory instability with the objective; a measure-level approach may stabilize learning.
AI.method: The authors assume mild statistical stability; they show the return distribution evolves more regularly under the 1-Wasserstein metric than trajectories, leading to a smoother distributional Bellman objective; they propose aligning optimization with this structure and provide a principled explanation for distributional method advantages and the geometry under chaos.
AI.result: Under these assumptions, distributional RL yields better conditioned learning in chaotic systems; the distributional objective reduces sensitivity to trajectory-level chaos and clarifies the geometry of RL objectives in chaotic regimes.
AI.conclusion: Distributional RL, particularly under 1-Wasserstein, is advantageous in chaotic environments due to smoother measure-level dynamics; explains and justifies the empirical success of distributional methods in such settings; geometry of objectives is more favorable when focusing on distributions rather than trajectories.

title: CosmicFish-HRM: Adaptive Reasoning via Hierarchical Recurrent Mechanisms in Compact Language Models
comment: 17 pages, 4 figures. Exploratory study of adaptive reasoning depth in compact autoregressive language models. Code available at https://github.com/MistyozAI/CosmicFish-HRM
summary: Large language models have achieved strong reasoning capabilities, though often at the cost of massive parameter counts and expensive inference. In this work, we explore a different direction: adaptive reasoning depth in compact language models. We present CosmicFish-HRM, a compact language model built around a Hierarchical Reasoning Module (HRM) that dynamically allocates computational effort during inference. Instead of applying fixed computation to every input, the model iterates through high-level and low-level reasoning cycles and learns when to halt based on input complexity. CosmicFish-HRM combines this adaptive reasoning core with modern transformer components including Grouped Query Attention, RoPE, and SwiGLU activations. While the additional reasoning infrastructure introduces overhead at small scale, we hypothesize that this tradeoff becomes increasingly favorable as model size grows and the relative cost of the HRM core diminishes. Our results show that the model learns non-uniform reasoning behavior, allocating different numbers of reasoning steps across tasks and inputs. These findings suggest that adaptive reasoning depth may offer a promising alternative to relying solely on parameter scale for reasoning capability.

AI.tldr: Adaptive reasoning depth using a Hierarchical Reasoning Module enables dynamic computation in a compact language model, reducing dependence on scale.
AI.motivation: Reduce reliance on massive parameter counts for reasoning by enabling input-aware, adaptive computation; address inference costs while maintaining performance.
AI.method: CosmicFish-HRM is a compact LM built with a Hierarchical Reasoning Module (HRM) that iterates through high-level and low-level reasoning cycles and learns when to halt based on input complexity. It combines HRM with transformer components such as Grouped Query Attention, Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE), and SwiGLU activations. The design tolerates HRM overhead at small scales with the expectation that benefits grow with model size.
