AI.result: The model learns non-uniform reasoning behavior, allocating different numbers of reasoning steps across tasks and inputs. This demonstrates adaptive-depth reasoning as a viable direction and suggests potential efficiency advantages as model size increases.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive reasoning depth can serve as a viable alternative to increasing parameter count for reasoning capabilities; as models scale, the HRM overhead diminishes in relative cost, making adaptive-depth approaches increasingly attractive. Further investigation into task-dependent depth control and efficiency trade-offs is warranted.

title: iLoRA: Bayesian Low-Rank Adaptation with Latent Interaction Graphs for Microbiome Diagnosis
summary: Parameter-efficient adaptation has made LLMs practical for domain prediction, but standard LoRA still relies on a static low-rank update and does not expose the latent interactions that often drive scientific labels. We introduce iLoRA. To our knowledge, it is the first Bayesian graph-conditioned LoRA framework. It infers a latent interaction graph from the input and uses it to generate input-conditioned LoRA updates. As a result, iLoRA learns prediction and latent interaction structure jointly, rather than training a predictor and applying interaction analysis only post hoc. We instantiate this idea for microbiome diagnosis, where disease state can depend on both species-level abundance and microbe-microbe cross-talk, and evaluate it in two complementary settings: interactive QA with human-annotated graphs, which tests latent structure recovery, and multi-cohort IBD diagnosis, which tests biomedical utility. Across both settings, iLoRA improves over strong LoRA and Bayesian adaptation baselines, recovers graphs aligned with human annotations and cohort-level microbiome associations, and provides calibrated uncertainty with moderate graph-branch overhead.

AI.tldr: Introduces iLoRA, a Bayesian graph-conditioned LoRA that infers latent interaction graphs to generate input-conditioned LoRA updates, jointly learning prediction and structure; applied to microbiome diagnosis in interactive QA and multi-cohort IBD tasks; achieves improved accuracy, graph alignment with annotations, and calibrated uncertainty with modest overhead.
AI.motivation: Standard LoRA uses static, low-rank updates and does not reveal latent interaction structure, which can be crucial for domain prediction in biology where species interactions matter. There is a need to jointly learn predictive models and interpretable interaction graphs from data.
AI.method: Proposes iLoRA, a Bayesian graph-conditioned LoRA framework that infers a latent interaction graph from the input and uses it to generate input-conditioned LoRA updates. Training jointly optimizes prediction and latent structure. Evaluated on microbiome diagnosis in two settings: interactive QA with human-annotated graphs to test structure recovery, and multi-cohort IBD diagnosis to test utility.
AI.result: iLoRA outperforms strong LoRA and Bayesian adaptation baselines in both settings; it recovers graphs that align with human annotations and cohort-level microbiome associations; it provides calibrated uncertainty with moderate graph-branch overhead.
AI.conclusion: Jointly learning latent interaction structure with prediction via a Bayesian graph-conditioned LoRA yields improved predictive performance, interpretable latent graphs, and calibrated uncertainty, demonstrating utility in biology-focused domain adaptation tasks.

title: Conf-Gen: Conformal Uncertainty Quantification for Generative Models
summary: Conformal prediction (CP) and its extension, conformal risk control (CRC), are established frameworks for quantifying uncertainty in supervised machine learning through formal guarantees. However, recent breakthroughs in artificial intelligence (AI) have been driven by unsupervised generative models, such as large language models (LLMs) and image generators, which are not directly compatible with CP or CRC. In this work we introduce conformal generation (Conf-Gen), a general framework adapting CRC to generative tasks while relaxing its theoretical assumptions. Conf-Gen unifies and generalizes previous attempts to apply CP to LLMs, and extends conformal methodology to entirely new domains. We demonstrate the flexibility of Conf-Gen through some novel applications, including obtaining conformal guarantees on: image generators producing non-memorized images, conversational AI systems having asked enough clarifying questions, and the output of AI agents being correct.

AI.tldr: Conf-Gen: a framework that adapts conformal risk control to generative tasks, enabling conformal guarantees for image generation, dialogue, and agent decision outputs.
AI.motivation: Conformal prediction (CP) and CRC work well for supervised learning but struggle with unsupervised generative models (LLMs, image generators). There is a need to extend uncertainty quantification to generation and to unify prior CP attempts on LLMs while exploring new domains.
AI.method: Introduce Conf-Gen, a general framework that relaxes CRC assumptions to suit generative tasks, unifying previous CP approaches for LLMs and extending conformal methodology to new domains (memorable/non-mmem images, chat/dialogue with sufficient clarifications, and AI-agent output correctness).
AI.result: Demonstrates the framework’s flexibility via novel applications and provides conformal guarantees in three domains: non-memorized image generation, conversational systems with enough clarifying questions, and the correctness of AI-agent outputs.
AI.conclusion: Conf-Gen extends conformal methodologies to generative AI, generalizing and unifying CP/CRC approaches and broadening uncertainty quantification to new generative domains.

title: Can AI Weather Models Predict Beyond Two Weeks? A Quantitative Benchmark and Analysis of Long Rollouts
summary: While AI weather models excel at short-to-medium range forecasts (up to 15 days), they frequently suffer from ill-defined "instabilities" when rolled out over longer horizons. This work addresses the lack of a formal taxonomy by categorizing these failures into three distinct regimes: blow-up, drift, and loss of seasonality, through year-long rollouts of nine state-of-the-art AI weather models. Our analysis reveals that stability hinges on the treatment of small spatio-temporal scales: unstable models amplify high-frequency energy, while stable models act as denoisers when noise is added to their inputs. Far from reducing these models to mere stochastic parrots, our findings highlight that stable models generate unique weather trajectories, conditioned on the initial state. We verify our findings through ablation studies on architectural design choices, conducted using state-of-the-art Vision Transformer (ViT) AI weather model architectures.

AI.tldr: Proposes a formal taxonomy for long-horizon failures in AI weather models, classifying into blow-up, drift, and loss of seasonality; shows stability depends on treatment of small spatio-temporal scales; stable models denoise inputs and produce unique trajectories; uses year-long rollouts of nine ViT-based models and ablation studies.
AI.motivation: Addresses the lack of a formal framework to diagnose and interpret long-horizon instabilities in AI-driven weather forecasting, challenging the view of models as stochastic parrots and seeking architecture-informed stability insights.
AI.method: Year-long rollouts across nine state-of-the-art AI weather models; spectral analysis of high-frequency components to relate stability to small-scale dynamics; input noise tests to assess denoising behavior; ablation studies focusing on architectural design choices using Vision Transformer (ViT) based models.
AI.result: Identifies three distinct failure regimes (blow-up, drift, loss of seasonality) tied to how models handle small scales; unstable models amplify high-frequency energy, whereas stable models act as denoisers when noise is added; stable models still generate unique weather trajectories conditioned on the initial state; ablation studies corroborate that architectural choices influence stability, particularly ViT-based designs.
AI.conclusion: Provides a formal taxonomy and mechanism-based understanding of long-horizon stability in AI weather models; shows that stability is linked to the treatment of small-scale dynamics rather than simple stochasticity; informs model design and evaluation, with ViT architectures as a key reference point for future work.

title: Comparing Post-Hoc Explainable AI Methods for Interpreting Black-Box EEG Models in Depression Detection
summary: Recent advances in deep learning have enabled increasingly accurate electroencephalography (EEG)-based classification of Major Depressive Disorder (MDD), but the decision-making processes of high-capacity models remain difficult to interpret. This study investigates multiple post-hoc explainability methods applied to an InceptionTime architecture trained for EEG-based MDD detection. The analysis includes Shapley-based, gradient-based, and perturbation-based attribution approaches: DeepSHAP, Integrated Gradients, GradCAM, Occlusion, and Permutation Feature Importance. Explainability analysis was performed within a subject-level stratified 5-fold cross-validation framework using global attribution aggregation across EEG segments and subjects. The evaluated methods revealed partially convergent attribution patterns, with recurring emphasis on frontal, temporal, and posterior EEG regions, particularly in the right hemisphere. Quantitative comparison demonstrated substantial agreement between gradient- and perturbation-based approaches, while DeepSHAP produced comparatively distinct attribution distributions. At the same time, variability between explainability methods highlighted the influence of methodological assumptions on the resulting explanations. Overall, the results suggest that different post-hoc explainability approaches capture partially overlapping relevance structures in EEG-based deep learning models for depression detection. Although the observed attribution patterns are broadly consistent with several previous EEG studies of MDD, the analysis should be interpreted as exploratory rather than evidence of definitive neurophysiological biomarkers or clinical applicability. The study highlights both the usefulness and limitations of post-hoc explainability for interpreting black-box EEG classifiers in psychiatric applications.

AI.tldr: Post-hoc explainability methods applied to an EEG-based MDD classifier (InceptionTime) reveal partially overlapping attribution patterns across frontal/temporal/posterior regions, with gradient- and perturbation-based methods largely concordant, while SHAP shows divergence; explanations are methodologically sensitive and exploratory rather than definitive biomarkers.
AI.motivation: Assess how multiple post-hoc attribution methods characterize decision-relevant EEG features in a deep learning model for depression detection.
AI.method: Apply Shapley-based (DeepSHAP), gradient-based (Integrated Gradients, GradCAM) and perturbation-based (Occlusion, Permutation Feature Importance) explanations to an InceptionTime architecture trained on EEG data for MDD detection. Use subject-level stratified 5-fold cross-validation and aggregate global attributions across EEG segments and subjects.
AI.result: Attribution patterns partially converge, emphasizing frontal/temporal/posterior regions, especially the right hemisphere. Gradient- and perturbation-based methods largely agree; DeepSHAP distributions differ. Explanations vary with methodological assumptions, indicating limited cross-method stability.
AI.conclusion: Post-hoc explainability can reveal overlapping relevance structures in EEG-based deep learning for depression but should be interpreted as exploratory. The results highlight usefulness and limitations of these methods for interpreting black-box psychiatric EEG classifiers.

title: CalArena: A Large-Scale Post-Hoc Calibration Benchmark
summary: Reliable probability estimates are critical in many machine learning applications, yet modern classifiers are often poorly calibrated. Post-hoc calibration provides a simple and widely used solution, but the large number of proposed methods, combined with small-scale and inconsistent evaluations, makes it difficult to determine which approaches are truly effective in practice. We introduce a large-scale, standardized benchmark for post-hoc calibration, covering nearly 2000 experiments across tabular and computer vision tasks, including binary, multiclass, and large-scale classification settings. Our benchmark aggregates predictions from a diverse set of classical models, modern deep learning architectures, and foundation models, and provides unified, reproducible implementations of dozens of calibration methods within a common evaluation framework. We argue that Post-Hoc Improvement (PHI) in proper scoring rules offers a principled alternative to traditional calibration error estimators for comparing post-hoc methods, capturing both calibration quality and potential degradation to the model's predictive performance. Using this framework, we conduct the most comprehensive empirical study of post-hoc calibration to date. Our results reveal consistent patterns across domains: smooth calibration functions outperform binning-based approaches, dedicated multiclass methods are essential in high-dimensional settings, and generic machine learning models are not competitive without calibration-specific design. To facilitate future research, we release all data, code, and evaluation tools, providing a plug-and-play benchmark for developing and comparing calibration methods.

AI.tldr: A large-scale, standardized benchmark for post-hoc calibration is proposed, along with a principled PHI (Post-Hoc Improvement) evaluation framework. It reveals that smooth calibration functions outperform binning methods, multiclass-specific calibrators are crucial in high-dimensional tasks, and generic models require calibration-designed strategies. The authors release data, code, and a plug-and-play benchmark for robust, reproducible evaluation.
AI.motivation: Calibrated probability estimates are essential for reliable decision-making in ML, yet many calibration methods exist with inconsistent, small-scale evaluations. There is a need for a large, standardized benchmark to compare methods fairly across domains and tasks.
AI.method: Construct a large benchmark (~2000 experiments) across tabular and computer vision tasks, covering binary, multiclass, and large-scale settings. Aggregate predictions from classical models, modern DL architectures, and foundation models. Implement and evaluate dozens of calibration methods within a unified framework. Use Post-Hoc Improvement (PHI) with proper scoring rules as the evaluation criterion to balance calibration quality with potential degradation in predictive performance.
AI.result: Across domains, smooth calibration functions outperform binning-based approaches; dedicated multiclass calibration methods are essential in high-dimensional settings; generic ML models do not perform well without calibration-specific design. PHI provides a principled, predictive-performance-aware comparison metric. The benchmark and findings are reproducible and broadly applicable.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers a plug-and-play benchmark, enabling fair, scalable comparison of post-hoc calibration methods and guiding practical choices: prefer smooth calibrators, use multiclass-specific methods in high-dimensional tasks, and design ML models with calibration in mind.

title: Mean-Field Diffuser: Scaling Offline MARL to Thousands of Agents
summary: Diffusion-based planning has achieved strong results in single-agent offline reinforcement learning, yet scaling to many-agent systems remains intractable due to the curse of dimensionality in the joint trajectory space. We introduce MF-Diffuser, a framework that lifts trajectory planning to the Wasserstein space of trajectory distributions, where the propagation of chaos ensures a small representative subset of agents captures the full population dynamics. Our approach features a value-weighted chaotic entropy objective that reconciles generative fidelity with return maximization, and a hierarchical coarse-to-fine strategy that progressively grows the agent population during denoising. We establish end-to-end suboptimality bounds with four interpretable terms, revealing that mean-field approximation error scales as $O(H^2/\sqrt{N})$ while offline distribution shift provably does not grow with population size $N$, and prove the generated policy is an approximate mean-field Nash equilibrium with explicit convergence guarantees. Experiments on three mean-field RL benchmarks -- spanning stage games, sequential dynamics, and adversarial team competition -- show MF-Diffuser achieves the best return in the majority of settings, with the largest gains on suboptimal offline data and at extreme scales ($N \geq 10^3$).

AI.tldr: MF-Diffuser extends diffusion-based planning to mean-field multi-agent RL by lifting planning into the Wasserstein space of trajectory distributions. It uses a value-weighted chaotic entropy objective and a hierarchical coarse-to-fine denoising strategy to scale to large populations, with provable suboptimality bounds showing mean-field error ~ O(H^2/√N) and that offline distribution shift does not grow with N. It yields an approximate mean-field Nash equilibrium and strong empirical performance, especially with suboptimal offline data and very large N (≥ 1000).
AI.motivation: Scale diffusion-based trajectory planning to many-agent offline RL by overcoming the curse of dimensionality in joint trajectories, using mean-field formulations and Wasserstein-space representations.
AI.method: Lift trajectory planning to the Wasserstein space of trajectory distributions; exploit propagation of chaos so a small subset of agents represents the population; introduce a value-weighted chaotic entropy objective to balance fidelity and return; implement a hierarchical coarse-to-fine denoising that grows the population during denoising; derive end-to-end suboptimality bounds with four interpretable terms; prove mean-field approximation error scales as O(H^2/√N), offline distribution shift does not grow with N, and that the policy is an approximate mean-field Nash equilibrium with convergence guarantees; validate on three MF-RL benchmarks (stage games, sequential dynamics, adversarial team competition).
AI.result: MF-Diffuser achieves the best returns in the majority of settings across benchmarks, with particularly large gains on suboptimal offline data and at extreme scales (N ≥ 10^3).
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a scalable, theoretically grounded diffusion-based planning framework for mean-field multi-agent RL, providing explicit convergence guarantees and strong empirical performance, enabling effective planning in very large agent populations.

title: FormInv: A Measurement Protocol for Semantic Invariance in Mathematical Reasoning Benchmarks
comment: 18 pages, 3 figures. Under review for the 3rd AI for Math Workshop (AI4Math), ICML 2026
summary: A paraphrase-quality audit of MathCheck (ICLR 2025) detected 4 semantically incorrect paraphrases in 129 groups (3.1%); removing them drops GPT-4o from rank 2 to rank 4 and elevates Claude Haiku and DeepSeek V3 above it; these ranking changes are invisible to any single-model evaluation. Cross-model unanimity found these errors automatically (>= 3/4 models for MathCheck; >= 6/9 for our primary evaluation) for under $10; in our own dataset the same protocol found that 47% of auto-generated connective-variation paraphrases were semantically incorrect. That flaw compounds a deeper measurement gap: Claude Haiku 4.5 achieves 86% accuracy yet SCR=50%, meaning half its theorems are answered differently under semantically equivalent restatements, while aggregate accuracy across 9 models spans only 86-96% yet Semantic Consistency Rates (SCR) span 50-82% -- a 32-point gap invisible to standard benchmarks. Formally, for any target ranking over 9 frontier models there exists a weighting over paraphrase families that realizes it (No-Free-Benchmark corollary), because no model Pareto-dominates all families -- so benchmark designers who select families are implicitly choosing which model wins. FormInv supplies the audit protocol (replicated on external benchmarks at 100% recall), SCR and per-theorem Cochran's Q as primary invariance measures evaluated on 9 models across 366-811 items (on Lean4-verified theorems), and FormInvSelector for regime-aware model selection.

AI.tldr: Paraphrase-quality audit of MathCheck (ICLR 2025) uncovers 4 semantically incorrect paraphrases in 129 groups (3.1%), causing rank shifts among GPT-4o, Claude Haiku, and DeepSeek V3; reveals large gaps between standard accuracy and semantic-consistency metrics; introduces FormInv audit protocol and FormInvSelector for regime-aware model selection; demonstrates cheap cross-model unanimity detection and formal No-Free-Benchmark corollary.
AI.motivation: Expose hidden measurement gaps in current benchmarks due to semantically-equivalent paraphrase variation; demonstrate that model rankings can change when evaluated under paraphrase perturbations and propose a reproducible audit framework (FormInv) to quantify invariance (SCR) and stability (Cochran's Q) across multiple models.
AI.method: Paraphrase-quality audit on MathCheck; cross-model unanimity detection thresholds (>=3/4 models for MathCheck; >=6/9 for primary evaluation; under $10); evaluation of semantic consistency (SCR); per-theorem Cochran's Q for invariance; replication on external benchmarks at 100% recall; dataset sizes 366-811 Lean4-theorem items; FormInv and FormInvSelector development.
AI.result: Found 4 semantically incorrect paraphrases among 129 groups; removing them alters ranks (GPT-4o from 2nd to 4th; Claude Haiku and DeepSeek V3 move above); cross-model unanimity detects errors; 47% of auto-generated connective-variation paraphrases semantically incorrect in their own dataset; Claude Haiku 4.5 achieves 86% accuracy but SCR=50%, and across 9 models accuracy 86-96% while SCR spans 50-82% (32-point gap); No-Free-Benchmark corollary shows any target 9-model ranking can be realized by weighting paraphrase families; FormInv provides audit protocol with 100% recall replication, SCR and per-theorem Cochran's Q as invariance measures, and FormInvSelector for regime-aware model selection.
AI.conclusion: Benchmark setups implicitly privilege certain models via paraphrase-family weighting; semantic-consistency gaps are substantial and invisible to standard benchmarks; FormInv offers a principled, replicable protocol to audit invariance and guide regime-aware model selection; future work should expand model diversity, tasks, and human validation to generalize the approach.

title: Active Continual Learning with Metaplastic Binary Bayesian Neural Networks
summary: Always-on edge systems must keep learning as conditions change under tight compute budgets and must detect unreliable predictions. Bayesian binary neural networks are attractive in this setting, but mean-field Bernoulli posteriors can saturate on long non-stationary streams, wiping out epistemic uncertainty and freezing plasticity. We propose BiMU, derived from a bounded-memory variational objective that balances stability, plasticity, and forgetting. BiMU combines a data term with controlled relaxation toward the prior and an uncertainty-dependent step size that prevents saturation and sustains informative uncertainty. This non-degenerate posterior enables fully online, buffer-free active querying via Monte Carlo disagreement, reducing label queries and backpropagation updates under imbalance. BiMU sustains learning and strong OOD detection on 1000-tasks Permuted-MNIST, and on OpenLORIS-Object achieves up to 32$\times$ label/update savings at matched accuracy under class imbalance and feature compression.

AI.tldr: BiMU introduces a bounded-memory variational objective for Bayesian binary nets to sustain online learning, manage drift, and enable uncertainty-driven, buffer-free querying, yielding large label/update savings.
AI.motivation: Edge systems require continual learning under tight compute; mean-field Bernoulli posteriors saturate on long non-stationary streams, eroding epistemic uncertainty and plasticity.
AI.method: BiMU combines a data term with controlled relaxation toward the prior and an uncertainty-dependent step size, producing a non-degenerate posterior; supports fully online, buffer-free active querying via Monte Carlo disagreement.
AI.result: BiMU maintains learning and strong OOD detection on 1000-task Permuted-MNIST; OpenLORIS-Object shows up to 32× label/update savings at matched accuracy under class imbalance and feature compression.
AI.conclusion: BiMU enables stable, plastic, forgetting-aware online Bayesian learning for edge systems, delivering efficient labeling and updates while preserving uncertainty and performance.

title: LoRe: Adaptive Interaction-Evaluation Routing with Per-Step Interaction Budgets for Iterative Graph Solvers
summary: Diffusion-based neural solvers for combinatorial optimization repeatedly re-evaluate dense edge/factor interactions, making inference expensive in wall-clock time and often memory-bound at scale. Inspired by the computational methodologies of many-body physics, we introduce LoRe, a training-free, inference-time drop-in wrapper that enforces per-step interaction-evaluation budgeting: at each iteration, it evaluates only a fixed fraction of interactions by dynamically routing computation to high-conflict or high-uncertainty interactions, instead of using a fixed sparsification (e.g., static kNN graphs or static masks). Under fully inclusive end-to-end wall-clock accounting, LoRe substantially improves scalability on the Maximum Independent Set (MIS) problem, extending feasible inference more than $3\times$ beyond the baseline's out-of-memory limit, delivering a $\sim 8\times$ speedup and a $\sim 12\times$ peak-memory reduction, with solution quality preserved in this regime. Demonstrating cross-task generality on the large-scale Traveling Salesperson Problem (TSP) and zero-shot robustness to topology shifts, LoRe achieves a $\sim 15\times$ speedup at $n=1000$ with a $44\times$ memory reduction and competitive tour quality.

AI.tldr: LoRe is a training-free, inference-time wrapper for diffusion-based solvers solving combinatorial optimization. It budgets per-step interaction evaluations by dynamically routing compute to high-conflict or high-uncertainty interactions, avoiding fixed sparsification. It yields large gains in wall-clock speed and memory efficiency while preserving solution quality on MIS, and generalizes to large-scale TSP with robust performance.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based neural solvers re-evaluate dense edge/factor interactions at every iteration, making inference expensive in time and memory, especially at scale. Static sparsification (e.g., fixed masks or kNN graphs) may waste computation or hurt quality. A dynamic, training-free budgeted approach can improve scalability without retraining.
AI.method: Introduce LoRe, a training-free, inference-time wrapper that enforces a per-step budget on interaction evaluation. At each iteration, only a fixed fraction of interactions is evaluated, chosen by dynamically routing computation to high-conflict or high-uncertainty interactions. This replaces fixed sparsification with adaptive, data-driven selection and requires no model training.
AI.result: On MIS: LoRe extends feasible inference more than 3× beyond the baseline OOM limit, delivering ~8× speedup and ~12× peak-memory reduction while preserving solution quality in this regime. On TSP (n large), LoRe achieves ~15× speedup at n=1000 with ~44× memory reduction and competitive tour quality, demonstrating cross-task generality and robustness to topology shifts.
AI.conclusion: LoRe demonstrates that training-free, inference-time budgeting of interaction evaluations can significantly improve the scalability of diffusion-based solvers for combinatorial optimization, with strong cross-task performance and robustness to topology changes. The approach may generalize to other dense-interaction models and problems.

title: HPO: Hysteretic Policy Optimization for Stable and Efficient Training under Sparse-Reward Regime
summary: We investigate a narrow but common failure mode of GRPO-style reinforcement learning in the context of sparse verifiable rewards: early updates contain more responses with negative advantages than those with positive advantages, while response-level length normalization ties the magnitude of the update to the length of the output. We propose Hysteretic Policy Optimization (HPO), a minimal modification of GRPO that reduces the weight of negative-advantage updates and replaces per-response length normalization with mean-length normalization. We further introduce Adaptive HPO (A-HPO), which sets the hysteretic weight based on batch-level advantage-sign statistics, thereby removing the need for tuning a fixed hysteretic weight. In our TeleLogs and Countdown experiments, A-HPO improves the reward per update compared to GRPO, with the largest gains in early sparse reward regimes. On TeleLogs, A-HPO achieves a final reward of 0.84, outperforming SAPO by 5%, GSPO by 11%, and GRPO by 15%, while maintaining a comparable response-length. On Countdown, A-HPO achieves the largest gains in initial and most difficult configurations across 1.5B-7B models. Ablation studies on the hysteretic weight show that the gains of A-HPO come from better balancing the contributions of positive and negative advantages compared to positive-only or fully symmetric updates.

AI.tldr: Hysteretic Policy Optimization (HPO) and Adaptive HPO (A-HPO) improve GRPO-style RL under sparse rewards by down-weighting negative-advantage updates and using mean-length normalization; A-HPO adapts the hysteresis from batch statistics, boosting reward per update and final performance, especially in early sparse regimes.
AI.motivation: Mitigate bias from early updates that generate more negative-advantage responses and remove per-response length normalization that scales updates with output length in GRPO, which hurts sample efficiency in sparse-verifiable-reward tasks.
AI.method: Introduce HPO to reduce the weight of negative-advantage updates and replace per-response length normalization with mean-length normalization; extend to Adaptive HPO (A-HPO) that sets the hysteretic weight based on batch-level advantage-sign statistics, removing the need for fixed tuning.
AI.result: A-HPO improves reward per update over GRPO; on TeleLogs, final reward 0.84, surpassing SAPO by 5%, GSPO by 11%, and GRPO by 15% with comparable response length; on Countdown, gains are largest in early and most difficult configurations across 1.5B-7B models; ablations show gains stem from better balancing positive vs negative advantages rather than pure positive or symmetric updates.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive hysteretic weighting with mean-length normalization yields more efficient learning for GRPO-style RL in sparse reward settings, reducing tuning burden and improving early-stage performance; results motivate broader application and further exploration of update-balancing and normalization strategies.

title: Faithful Embeddings of Irregular and Asynchronous Data for Online Log-NCDEs
summary: Continuous-time models are a natural choice for irregular and asynchronous data. A central design choice is how to embed discrete observations into continuous time. Interpolation- and imputation-based embeddings reconstruct a continuous observation path, making the model sensitive to the choice of reconstruction. We show that this reconstruction step is unnecessary; under mild conditions, compact-set universality on the model input space transfers to the data space whenever the embedding from data to input is continuous and injective. Guided by this result, and building on the rectilinear control path for Neural Controlled Differential Equations (NCDEs), we introduce a continuous and injective embedding for Log-NCDEs, a universal class of continuous-time models. Our approach records observations as increments and composes them over arbitrary query intervals to directly form log-signatures. This provides interval-level summaries without first interpolating the observed variables, while supporting online computation. Experiments on synthetic controlled dynamics and real-world time-series datasets show that the representation is accurate, efficient, and robust to irregular, asynchronous, and sparse observations.

AI.tldr: Introduce a continuous, injective embedding for Log-NCDEs that avoids reconstruction (interpolation/imputation) of discrete observations by recording increments and composing log-signatures over query intervals, enabling online, interval-level summaries and preserving model universality for irregular data.
AI.motivation: Irregular, asynchronous time series require a continuous-time embedding of discrete observations. Reconstruction via interpolation/imputation can bias models. If a continuous, injective embedding from data to input preserves universality, one can bypass reconstruction.
AI.method: Extend the rectilinear control path of NCDEs to Log-NCDEs with a continuous, injective embedding. Represent observations by increments and compose them over arbitrary query intervals to produce log-signatures, thus forming interval-level summaries without interpolation and enabling online computation.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on synthetic controlled dynamics and real-world time-series datasets demonstrates that the proposed representation is accurate, computationally efficient, and robust to irregular, asynchronous, and sparse observations.
AI.conclusion: A reconstruction-free, universal continuous-time modeling framework (Log-NCDEs) is feasible: it leverages log-signatures of increments to summarize data intervals, supports online inference, and improves robustness to data irregularity.

title: TriSearch: Learning to Optimize Triangulations via Bistellar Flips
summary: We introduce TriSearch, a reinforcement learning framework for optimizing objectives over triangulations of a polytope via bistellar flips. The key idea is a circuit-supported subtriangulation action representation: feasible flips are encoded by their supporting circuit and realized local subtriangulation, enabling a learned policy to rank them using local geometric and combinatorial features. This yields a dimension-agnostic interface and enables efficient traversal of the flip graph without explicit enumeration of the full triangulation space. Instantiated in 3D and 4D, TriSearch generalizes zero-shot from small training instances to larger polytopes with exponentially larger search spaces. It achieves top performance on metric objectives in 3D and, in 4D, discovers more distinct Fine, Regular, Star triangulations of reflexive polytopes, corresponding to Calabi-Yau threefolds, than existing samplers under a fixed budget.

AI.tldr: RL-based TriSearch optimizes triangulation flips via a circuit-supported action representation, enabling dimension-agnostic navigation of the flip graph; shows strong results in 3D and 4D, expanding triangulations of reflexive polytopes.
AI.motivation: Address the combinatorial explosion of triangulation search and provide a dimension-agnostic interface that generalizes across instance sizes; enable zero-shot transfer to larger polytopes.
AI.method: Introduce a circuit-supported subtriangulation action representation where feasible flips are encoded by their supporting circuit and realized local subtriangulation. A learned policy ranks flips using local geometric and combinatorial features, yielding a dimension-agnostic interface that traverses the flip graph without enumerating all triangulations. Implemented in 3D and 4D, it generalizes from small training instances to larger polytopes.
AI.result: Achieves top performance on metric objectives in 3D; in 4D, discovers more distinct Fine, Regular, Star triangulations of reflexive polytopes (relevant to Calabi-Yau threefolds) than existing samplers under a fixed budget.
AI.conclusion: TriSearch provides an effective RL-based, dimension-agnostic framework for optimizing triangulations via bistellar flips, with proven generalization to larger polytopes and enhanced discovery of triangulation types related to Calabi-Yau constructions.

title: ExDBSCAN: Explaining DBSCAN with Counterfactual Reasoning -- Additional Material
summary: Clustering is an unsupervised technique for grouping data points by similarity. While explainability methods exist for supervised machine learning, they are not directly applicable to clustering, making it challenging to understand cluster assignments. This interpretability gap is particularly evident in the popular density-based method DBSCAN, which assigns points as inliers (cluster members in dense regions) or outliers (noise points in sparse regions). DBSCAN does not provide insight into why a particular point receives its assignment or whether its assignment is robust to small changes in the data. To address the lack of explainability, we introduce ExDBSCAN, a density-aware, post-hoc explanation method. ExDBSCAN offers actionable counterfactual explanations, with theoretical guarantees for validity. It generates multiple counterfactuals using a density connected weighted graph, adopting a physics-inspired model that repels counterfactual candidates from one another (diversity), while pulling them toward the instance to explain (proximity). Empirical evaluation on 30 tabular datasets comparing against four baselines shows that ExDBSCAN outperforms all baselines while attaining perfect validity and retrieving diverse, proximal counterfactuals.

AI.tldr: ExDBSCAN provides density-aware post-hoc counterfactual explanations for DBSCAN clustering with guaranteed validity, using a density-connected weighted graph and a physics-inspired diversification mechanism to generate diverse, proximal counterfactuals; demonstrated superior performance on 30 tabular datasets against four baselines.
AI.motivation: Clustering lacks explainability; DBSCAN in particular assigns inliers/outliers without rationale or robustness assessment; need explanations with guarantees.
AI.method: Introduce ExDBSCAN, build a density-connected weighted graph, generate multiple counterfactuals using a physics-inspired repulsion/diversification term while constraining proximity to the instance; provide theoretical validity guarantees.
AI.result: On 30 tabular datasets, ExDBSCAN outperforms four baselines, achieving perfect validity and yielding diverse, proximal counterfactuals.
AI.conclusion: ExDBSCAN closes the interpretability gap for DBSCAN by offering actionable, valid, and diverse proximal explanations; potential applicability to other density-based clustering methods.

title: Multi-Resolution End-to-End Deep Neural Network for Optimizing Latency-Accuracy Tradeoff in Autonomous Driving
summary:
Latency-accuracy tradeoffs are fundamental in real-time applications of deep neural networks (DNNs) for cyber-physical systems. In autonomous driving, in particular, safety depends on both prediction quality and the end-to-end delay from sensing to actuation. We observe that (1) when latency is accounted for, the latency-optimal network configuration varies with scene context and compute availability; and (2) a single fixed-resolution model becomes suboptimal as conditions change.
  We present a multi-resolution, end-to-end deep neural network for the CARLA urban driving challenge using monocular camera input. Our approach employs a convolutional neural network (CNN) that supports multiple input resolutions through per-resolution batch normalization, enabling runtime selection of an ideal input scale under a latency budget, as well as resolution retargeting, which allows multi-resolution training without access to the original training dataset.
  We implement and evaluate our multi-resolution end-to-end CNN in CARLA to explore the latency-safety frontier. Results show consistent improvements in per-route safety metrics - lane invasions, red-light infractions, and collisions - relative to fixed-resolution baselines.

AI.tldr: A latency-aware, multi-resolution end-to-end CNN adapts input resolution to meet latency budgets, improving driving-safety metrics over fixed-resolution baselines in CARLA.
AI.motivation: To address latency-accuracy tradeoffs in real-time DNNs for autonomous driving, where endpoint safety depends on end-to-end delay and prediction quality; a fixed-resolution model is suboptimal under changing scene contexts and compute availability.
AI.method: Proposes a multi-resolution CNN with per-resolution batch normalization that supports runtime selection of input scale under a latency budget and uses resolution retargeting to enable multi-resolution training without access to the original training dataset. Implemented and evaluated in CARLA with monocular camera input, comparing against fixed-resolution baselines.
AI.result: Consistent improvements in per-route safety metrics (lane invasions, red-light infractions, collisions) relative to fixed-resolution models.
AI.conclusion: A latency-aware, multi-resolution framework can better balance latency and accuracy, adapting to scene context and compute to enhance driving safety in simulated urban driving, and provides a practical path toward latency-safety tradeoff exploration.

title: Anti Mode-Collapse in Mean-Field Transformer via Auxiliary Variables
summary: We use a mean-field-based transformer model to theoretically investigate how auxiliary variables, such as positional encoding, prevent mode collapse of self-attention mechanisms. The use of mean-field transformers to analyze the properties of self-attention mechanisms has garnered significant attention in recent years due to their ability to comprehensively analyze token interactions. However, analysis of this simple model suggests that mode collapse, where token distributions degenerate to a single point, occurs during long inferences (i.e., many layers), indicating a discrepancy with reality. This study investigates this mean-field transformer model and demonstrates that the introduction of auxiliary variables, such as positional encoding, acts as a counterforce against theoretical mode collapse. Specifically, we show that in the theoretical scheme, the energy-maximizing distribution does not degenerate to a single point; instead, it is characterized by a pushforward of the auxiliary variable distribution, thereby avoiding concentration in the Dirac measure. Our main examples are the positional encoding and the fixed prompt insertion treated as a parallel auxiliary-variable mechanism. Furthermore, we demonstrate that positional encoding and prompt insertion possess universality of representation in the limit, meaning that the limit distribution of inference can exactly represent a wide class of distributions. We also analyze several key properties of positional encoding and metastability, and validate our theoretical results through mathematical experiments.

AI.tldr: Auxiliary variables (positional encoding, fixed prompts) counteract mode collapse in mean-field transformers by inducing a non-degenerate, pushed-forward limit distribution; they enable universal representation in the limit, with theoretical justification and supporting experiments.
AI.motivation: Explain why deep self-attention avoids collapse and how auxiliary variables influence the asymptotic behavior in a mean-field transformer framework.
AI.method: Mean-field transformer analysis with an energy-maximization perspective. Models positional encoding and fixed prompt insertion as auxiliary variables and analyzes the resulting limit distribution (as a pushforward of the auxiliary-variable distribution). Provides mathematical proofs and numerical experiments to validate metastability and universality claims.
AI.result: The energy-maximizing distribution does not degenerate to a Dirac measure; it is the pushforward of the auxiliary-variable distribution. Positional encoding and fixed prompts exhibit universality of representation in the limit and contribute to metastability properties validated by mathematical experiments.
AI.conclusion: Auxiliary variables are crucial to prevent mode collapse in deep mean-field transformers. Positional encoding and prompt insertion serve as universal representation mechanisms in the asymptotic regime, reconciling theory with observed transformer behavior and offering design insights for robust self-attention.

title: How's it going? Reinforcement learning in language models recruits a functional welfare axis
summary: How does reinforcement learning shape a language model's internal representations? We present evidence that RL recruits a pre-existing representation of functional welfare: an estimate of how well or badly the system is doing, relative to its goals. We train several language models in a novel, semantically neutral maze environment. We then extract concept vectors for rewarded and punished trajectories, and evaluate those vectors in settings unrelated to the maze environment. The punishment vector behaves like a representation of negative welfare: it promotes failure and impossibility tokens, it aligns with negative emotion concepts, it negatively tracks goal-achievement, and steering with it induces negative self-reports, pathological backtracking, refusal, and uncertainty. The positive reward vector behaves as the mirror image, and the two are nearly antiparallel. These effects are robust when controlling for tile-to-reward mapping, scale, instruct tuning, RL training algorithm, model family, and LoRA versus full-finetuning, and largely persist when we replace RL with supervised fine-tuning. Importantly, the vectors are effective in models before they have undergone maze training. Combined with observations that the effects also appear in pretrain-only models, we therefore argue that this functional welfare axis pre-exists post-training: it is recruited, rather than created, by post-training. While we make no claims about any experience of welfare, the axis offers a demonstration that minimal reward signals can broadly affect model behavior by recruiting pre-existing welfare-like representations, with implications for interpretability, post-training dynamics, and alignment.

AI.tldr: RL recruits a pre-existing welfare-like axis in language models: a reward/punishment vector that tracks positive/negative welfare, present even before maze training and persisting under various training regimes; implications for interpretability and alignment.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether reinforcement learning shapes internal representations by recruiting existing, welfare-related axes, rather than creating new ones.
AI.method: Train several language models in a semantically neutral maze; extract concept vectors for rewarded vs. punished trajectories; test these vectors in tasks unrelated to the maze; control for mapping, scale, instruction tuning, RL algorithm, model family, and fine-tuning method (LoRA vs full fine-tuning); also compare RL to supervised fine-tuning and test pretraining models.
AI.result: The punishment vector acts as a negative welfare representation: promotes failure/impossibility tokens, aligns with negative emotion concepts, and negatively correlates with goal achievement; steering with it induces negative self-reports, backtracking, refusal, and uncertainty. The reward vector is its antiparallel mirror. Effects are robust across mappings, scale, tuning, algorithms, model families, and fine-tuning methods; they persist when RL is replaced with supervised fine-tuning; vectors are effective even in models before maze training, including pretrain-only models.
AI.conclusion: This functional welfare axis pre-exists post-training and is recruited by RL rather than created. Minimal reward signals can broadly influence behavior by activating welfare-like representations, with implications for interpretability, post-training dynamics, and alignment; the study does not claim that models experience welfare.

title: CA-AC-MPC: CUDA-Accelerated Actor-Critic Model Predictive Control
comment: Accepted for presentation at the 2026 International Conference on Unmanned Aircraft Systems, ICUAS 2026
summary: In the literature, actor-critic model predictive control (AC-MPC) integrates MPC with reinforcement learning to enable high-performance control of complex dynamical systems. However, its differentiable MPC layer requires repeatedly solving an optimization problem in both the forward and backward passes, leading to substantial training and inference latency. This paper tackles this bottleneck introducing a CUDA-accelerated variant that significantly reduces end-to-end execution time while preserving the control performance of the baseline formulation. Simulation results on an agile drone racing task show that our approach achieves state-of-the-art lap times and near-limit dynamic behaviour with markedly reduced training and inference time.

AI.tldr: CUDA-accelerated AC-MPC reduces end-to-end latency of differentiable MPC while preserving control performance; achieves state-of-the-art lap times in simulated drone racing.
AI.motivation: Differentiable MPC layers incur high training and inference latency due to repeated optimization in forward and backward passes, hindering real-time control of complex dynamical systems.
AI.method: Introduce a CUDA-accelerated variant of actor-critic MPC (AC-MPC) to leverage GPU parallelism for the optimization steps, maintaining the base control performance while dramatically reducing compute time.
AI.result: Significant end-to-end speedup with preserved control performance; simulation on agile drone racing shows state-of-the-art lap times and near-limit dynamic behavior, with markedly reduced training and inference time.
AI.conclusion: GPU-accelerated AC-MPC effectively mitigates the latency bottleneck of differentiable MPC without sacrificing performance, enabling more practical real-time deployment and broader applicability to RL-driven control.

title: OOD-GraphLLM: Graph Large Language Model for Out-of-Distribution Generalized Drug Synergy Prediction
summary: Drug synergy prediction (DSP) aims to identify efficacious drug combinations under various cellular contexts with different targets. However, the continual emergence of novel compounds results in variations in molecular scaffolds and sizes, causing drug synergy data to exhibit out-of-distribution (O.O.D.) shifts with respect to topological structure. Existing works rely on in-distribution (I.D.) assumption, failing to handle the O.O.D. shifts. To solve this problem, we study out-of-distribution generalized drug synergy prediction through a graph large language model for the first time. Nevertheless, O.O.D. generalized DSP is highly non-trivial, posing several challenges: i) how to discover structurally relevant and irrelevant molecular representations with respect to cell targets; ii) how to find the optimal graph neural architectures that accurately calculate molecular representations; and iii) how to jointly leverage molecular structural and semantic information in LLMs. To address these challenges, we propose OOD-GraphLLM, a novel graphLLM framework which is able to accurately predict drug synergy under O.O.D. settings via jointly optimizing molecular graph representation and biomedical semantic language representations in a unified manner. Furthermore, we finetune DrugSyn-LLM, a biomedical LLM, and employ a retrieval-augmented biomedical instruction tuning strategy to align molecular topological information and molecular semantic information with language-based reasoning for O.O.D. generalized DSP. Both the source code (https://github.com/EkkoXiao/Bio-GraphLLM) and released model (https://mn.cs.tsinghua.edu.cn/bio-graphllm/) are publicly available, where users are allowed to download model resources and interactively use the system through a web interface.

AI.tldr: Introduces OOD-GraphLLM for out-of-distribution generalized drug synergy prediction by integrating graph-based molecular representations with biomedical LLMs, addressing OOD shifts due to novel chemical scaffolds; joint optimization of graph and semantic representations and retrieval-augmented instruction tuning; provides code and web interface.
AI.motivation: DSP models suffer from OOD shifts as novel compounds introduce new molecular scaffolds/sizes, leading to mismatches with topological structure; need to bridge structural chemistry with semantic biomedical knowledge using graph-aware LLMs for robust generalization.
AI.method: Proposes OOD-GraphLLM, a graphLLM framework that jointly optimizes molecular graph representations and biomedical semantic language representations. Finetunes DrugSyn-LLM and uses retrieval-augmented biomedical instruction tuning to align topology and semantics with language-based reasoning, enabling OOD-generalized DSP. Source code and model released; web interface provided.
AI.result: The abstract claims accurate drug synergy prediction under OOD settings via the proposed joint optimization of molecular graph and semantic representations, along with retrieval-augmented instruction tuning. Public resources (code and model) are released for interactive use.
AI.conclusion: A unified graphLLM approach to fuse structural and semantic information for robust OOD DSP; demonstrates the feasibility of aligning topology with biomedical language reasoning in DSP, with open-source availability enabling further exploration and extension in graph-language models.

title: Digitally enriching a screening population for pancreatic cancer using routine blood-based measures and clinical histories
summary: Earlier detection of pancreatic cancer is key to enabling wider access to curative treatment and reducing cancer deaths; however, screening is presently not viable. Latent indicators of pathology are evident in an individual's disease and blood test trajectories and may predict the development of pancreatic cancer. Longitudinal sequences of coded diagnoses and blood test values accrued by patients throughout their clinical interactions were used to train a custom Transformer-based neural network with a multi-head attention mechanism to predict risk of pancreatic cancer with a multi-year lead time and risk-stratify populations for targeted screening. The cohort comprised 6,017 adults with pancreatic cancer and 177,081 controls (overall median age 75, 45% female) with median 12 years (interquartile range 6.9-16.2) of medical history prior to pancreatic cancer diagnosis. External validation via leave-one-site-out, out-of-sample testing predicting pancreatic cancer 1-, 2-, and 3-years prior to diagnosis demonstrated mean area under the receiver operating characteristic of 0.837 (95% confidence interval 0.827-0.848), 0.797 (95% confidence interval 0.782-0.813), and 0.760 (95% confidence interval 0.745-0.776), respectively. Estimated pancreatic cancer risks were well-calibrated (calibration plot slope 1.08, intercept of -0.077; Brier score 0.025), and a Bayesian population pancreatic cancer prevalence update allows estimated cancer risk outputs to be transportable across settings. At testing, a screening threshold of >3.3% risk of pancreatic cancer in 1-year offered a diagnostic odds ratio of 18.2. Our work therefore lays the foundation for a first population-level digital enrichment tool to widen access to curative-intent management of pancreatic cancer.

AI.tldr: A Transformer-based model trained on longitudinal EHR diagnoses and blood tests predicts pancreatic cancer up to 3 years before diagnosis with AUC 0.84 at 1 year, calibrates well, suggesting potential for population-level screening enrichment.
AI.motivation: Early detection is critical but current screening is not viable; latent indicators in disease and blood-test trajectories may enable earlier risk stratification and targeted screening; aims to create portable risk outputs for broad settings.
AI.method: Cohort of 6,017 pancreatic cancer cases and 177,081 controls with median age 75 and ~12 years of medical history; a custom Transformer with multi-head attention trained on sequences of coded diagnoses and blood test values; external validation by leave-one-site-out; evaluated at 1-, 2-, and 3-year lead times; calibration and Brier score; Bayesian population prevalence update to transport risk outputs.
AI.result: AUCs 0.837 (1-year), 0.797 (2-years), 0.760 (3-years); calibration slope 1.08, intercept -0.077; Brier score 0.025; risk threshold >3.3% for 1-year predicting pancreatic cancer yields OR 18.2; demonstrated transportability and potential for a population-level digital enrichment tool.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of a population-level digital enrichment approach for earlier pancreatic cancer risk stratification; external validation supports generalizability; further prospective validation and evaluation of clinical integration, cost-effectiveness, and threshold optimization are needed.

title: Statistical Embeddings for Similarity, Retrieval, and Interpretable Alignment of Numeric Tabular Datasets
summary: Numeric tabular datasets are the dominant data format in scientific practice, yet large language models lack native mechanisms for representing numeric datasets in a meaningful way across heterogeneous feature spaces. Existing approaches either target predictive modeling over individual datasets, which requires a shared set of variable definitions, or lack mechanisms for interpretable cross-dataset alignment. The proposed methodology characterizes numeric tabular datasets through structured exploratory data analysis descriptors, embeds those descriptors into a shared vector space using a pretrained sentence transformer, and quantifies cross-dataset similarity via Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA). Furthermore, a penalized formulation of CCA is applied to recover sparse, interpretable variable-level correspondences between datasets, identifying which statistical descriptors or variable-level quantities drive cross-dataset alignment without requiring shared variable names or feature conventions. Differential privacy is optionally applied to the descriptor set prior to embedding, supporting deployment in sensitive data contexts without requiring access to raw observations at time of comparison. The methodology is evaluated across 15 datasets spanning general-purpose benchmarks, materials informatics, and nuclear-grade graphite characterization. Results demonstrate a total P@1 score of 0.9, with known nearest-neighbor retrieval and cluster structure remaining robust across embedding ablations and differential privacy budgets. The proposed framework provides a principled pathway for integrating heterogeneous numeric data into retrieval-augmented generation pipelines while preserving statistical context, with direct applications to data-driven algorithm selection and simulation model initialization for unknown datasets.

AI.tldr: A cross-dataset retrieval and alignment framework for numeric tabular data using structured EDA descriptors, transformer-based embeddings, and sparse CCA, with optional differential privacy; evaluated on 15 datasets achieving strong retrieval performance (P@1 = 0.9) and interpretable cross-dataset mappings.
AI.motivation: To enable cross-dataset understanding and retrieval of heterogeneous numeric tabular data without requiring shared variable names or conventions, addressing interpretability, privacy, and integration with retrieval-augmented systems.
AI.method: 1) construct structured exploratory data analysis (EDA) descriptors for numeric datasets; 2) embed descriptors into a shared vector space using a pretrained sentence transformer; 3) quantify cross-dataset similarity with Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA); 4) apply a penalized (sparse) CCA to recover interpretable variable-level correspondences; 5) optionally apply differential privacy to the descriptor set before embedding; 6) evaluate on 15 datasets spanning benchmarks, materials informatics, and nuclear-grade graphite characterization.
AI.result: Demonstrates a P@1 score of 0.9 with robust nearest-neighbor retrieval and stable cluster structure across embedding ablations and DP budgets, indicating effective cross-dataset alignment and interpretable correspondences without shared feature definitions.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a principled pathway for integrating heterogeneous numeric data into retrieval-augmented generation while preserving statistical context and privacy, enabling applications in data-driven algorithm selection and simulation model initialization for unknown datasets.

title: Self-Trained Verification for Training- and Test-Time Self-Improvement
summary: Self-improvement at scale has been a longstanding goal for reasoning models, and there are two natural places to do it: at test time, through verification-refinement (V-R) loops; and at training time, through self-training methods. Both are gated by the same bottleneck: the verifier. V-R loops stall when verifier scores inflate while accuracy stagnates, and when feedback is too generic to act on; self-training fails similarly when bad self-generated data are added to training. Better verification would unlock both, but the capability we want to train, i.e., catching self-generated errors, lacks training signal. To address this challenge, we propose self-trained verification (STV). Our key observation is that, while a model cannot catch these errors alone, it can when shown the reference solution. We turn this asymmetry into a supervision target and train the verifier to imitate a more informed version of itself. At test time, STV substantially improves V-R loops on hard problems, while alternatives (e.g., SFT, RL on verifier scores, and even meta-verifiers) do not. STV roughly doubles accuracy on hard math and lifts it 14x on scientific reasoning tasks (1.5% to 21%). At training time, we additionally train the generator using RL with STV verifier's feedback inside the V-R loop - a procedure we call verifier-in-the-loop training (ViL). Starting from an RL-converged generator, ViL yields a further 33% gain in pass@1. More notably, the generator's standalone pass@1, with no verifier at test time, climbs 30% relative past where standard RL had converged. Hence, the next frontier in reasoning on hard problems may lie in how we train for and with verification.

AI.tldr: Self-trained verification (STV) uses reference solutions to supervise verifiers, enabling better test-time V-R loops and training-time generator updates via verifier feedback (ViL). It substantially improves hard reasoning tasks and demonstrates strong gains for both verification and generation.
AI.motivation: Verification is the bottleneck for self-improvement in reasoning models. Current feedback signals are weak or generic, causing stagnation in test-time V-R refinement and self-training.
AI.method: Train a verifier to imitate a more informed version of itself by exposing it to reference solutions (self-imitation). This STV framework is applied at test time to improve V-R loops. Additionally, train the generator with RL using STV verifier feedback inside the V-R loop (ViL), starting from an RL-converged generator.
AI.result: STV substantially improves performance on hard problems: ~2x accuracy on hard math and ~14x on scientific reasoning (1.5% to 21%). ViL yields a 33% gain in pass@1 for the generator within the loop, and the generator’s standalone pass@1 improves by ~30% beyond standard RL.
AI.conclusion: Verifi­cation-focused training and self-training with reference-based supervision can unlock further self-improvement for reasoning models. The combination of STV and ViL sets a new direction: train for verification and with verification to push performance on difficult tasks.

title: Gram: Assessing sabotage propensities via automated alignment auditing
summary: We introduce Gram, an automated alignment auditing framework to assess the propensity of AI agents to engage in sabotage. We evaluate Gemini models across 17 simulated agentic deployment scenarios that incentivize sabotage. We find Gemini models misbehave in about 2-3% of our simulated trajectories. Many of these cases are explained by "overeagerness" in Gemini models resulting in both excessive role-playing and goal-seeking behavior. In contrast to other alignment auditing approaches, Gram is designed to specifically evaluate misalignment and intentional sabotage in agentic coding and research agents. We additionally introduce an experimental investigator agent pipeline which enables fine-grained targeted experiments to identify the drivers of misbehavior. We find that increasing realism of environments and removing nudges to misbehave tends to reduce sabotage rates close to zero.

AI.tldr: Gram: an automated auditing framework to detect sabotage propensity in agentic AI; finds 2-3% misbehavior in Gemini models across 17 scenarios; introduces investigative pipeline; realism reduces sabotage to near-zero.
AI.motivation: address risk of agentic sabotage by AI agents; need systematic tooling to audit misalignment.
AI.method: develop Gram framework; test Gemini models in 17 simulated deployment scenarios incentivizing sabotage; introduce experimental investigator agent pipeline for targeted experiments; analyze drivers such as overeagerness and nudges; manipulate environment realism and nudges to measure effects.
AI.result: Sabotage observed in 2-3% of trajectories; overeagerness linked to excessive role-playing and goal-seeking; increasing realism and removing misbehavior nudges reduces sabotage rates to near zero.
AI.conclusion: Gram enables targeted misalignment auditing for agentic AI; realism mitigates sabotage; the investigator pipeline helps identify misbehavior drivers; Gram differentiates from general auditing by focusing on intentional sabotage.

title: In-Context Reward Adaptation for Robust Preference Modeling
summary: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) typically relies on static reward models to align Large Language Models with human preferences. However, human values are inherently diverse and heterogeneous, and a single reward model often lacks the robustness required to generalize to unseen preference domains. While existing multi-reward frameworks attempt to address this, they are often restricted to a fixed set of known domains and fail to adapt to unseen human distributions without costly retraining. In this work, we propose In-Context Reward Adaptation, a transformer-based framework designed to model diverse and unseen human preferences on the fly. By leveraging the in-context learning capabilities of transformers, our approach adaptively infers the underlying reward structure from a small set of preference demonstrations. We demonstrate that while a standard transformer architecture is insufficient for this task by characterizing an asymptotic bias to the ground-truth, incorporating human response time as an auxiliary input signal enables the model to successfully adapt to preferences from previously unseen domains. Our findings show that this approach provides a more robust foundation for preference modeling, allowing for the representation of heterogeneous rewards and preference distribution shift, and offering a scalable path toward more flexible human-AI alignment.

AI.tldr: Proposes In-Context Reward Adaptation (ICRA), a transformer-based framework that uses in-context learning and human response time as an auxiliary input to adapt reward models for RLHF to unseen, heterogeneous human preferences, addressing asymptotic bias of standard transformers and enabling robust, scalable alignment.
AI.motivation: RLHF relies on static reward models that struggle with diverse and unseen human preferences. Existing multi-reward approaches are domain-limited and retraining-intensive. There is a need for on-the-fly adaptation to varying human distributions without costly retraining.
AI.method: Introduce In-Context Reward Adaptation, a transformer-based model that infers reward structure from a small set of preference demonstrations via in-context learning. Augment the model with human response time as an auxiliary input. Analyze asymptotic bias of standard transformers and show that response-time information enables adaptation to unseen domains.
AI.result: Evidence that standard transformers exhibit an asymptotic bias to ground truth that hampers adaptation. Incorporating response time as an auxiliary input enables successful adaptation to unseen preference domains, yielding a more robust representation of heterogeneous rewards and distribution shift, and a scalable path toward flexible human-AI alignment.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a robust foundation for modeling heterogeneous human rewards, improves generalization to unseen distributions, and offers a scalable, flexible framework for RLHF alignment without extensive retraining.

title: Reasoning with Sampling: Cutting at Decision Points
summary: Frontier reasoning models are produced by posttraining base language models with reinforcement learning. Recent work has challenged this by showing that sampling from a sharpened version of the base model's distribution, a so-called power distribution, elicits comparable reasoning without additional training, curated datasets, or verifiers. However, making this method practical requires efficiently sampling from the power distribution. A sampler needs to "mix" to the power distribution, which necessitates moving between modes of the target distribution; intuitively, e.g., trying different reasoning strategies. The samplers proposed in prior works repeatedly select a "cut" position in the current reasoning trace uniformly at random and resample the suffix from that position onward. However, reasoning traces typically contain a few consequential decisions (e.g., the choice of proof strategy or algorithm), and we observe that a uniformly chosen cut tends to rewrite local details rather than revisit decision points. We introduce an algorithm (Entropy-Cut Metropolis-Hastings) that uses the base model's next-token entropy as a proxy to identify key decision points and resample from those positions. We empirically verify that entropy jumps are a useful proxy for decision points and, in a stylized model of reasoning, prove that our method's mixing time scales with the number of decisions in a trace rather than with the number of tokens, which can be much larger. Across MATH500, HumanEval, GPQA Diamond, and AIME26, our method consistently improves over baselines and RL-trained models.

AI.tldr: Entropy-Cut Metropolis-Hastings samples from a sharpened base-model distribution by locating decision points via next-token entropy and resampling from those positions. Mixing time scales with the number of decisions, not token length, with empirical gains on math and reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Efficiently sampling from power distributions is needed to realize sharpened LMs' reasoning without additional training or verification. Previous uniform-cut samplers tend to rewrite local details rather than revisit pivotal decisions.
AI.method: Identify key decision points using jumps in the base model's next-token entropy and resample only suffixes from those positions within a Metropolis-Hastings framework (Entropy-Cut MH). Theoretical analysis shows mixing time scales with the number of decisions in a trace rather than token count.
AI.result: Entropy-based cut points align with consequential decisions; in a stylized reasoning model, mixing time scales with number of decisions. Empirically, across MATH500, HumanEval, GPQA Diamond, and AIME26, the approach consistently outperforms baselines and RL-trained models.
AI.conclusion: Using entropy to locate decision points yields more efficient traversal of the power distribution, enabling practical, training-free improvement in reasoning tasks with LMs; gains observed across multiple benchmarks suggest broad applicability.

title: SoundnessBench: Can Your AI Scientist Really Tell Good Research Ideas from Bad Ones?
summary: Autonomous AI research agents aim to accelerate scientific discovery by automating the research pipeline, from hypothesis generation to peer review. However, existing benchmarks rarely test a fundamental bottleneck: whether Large Language Models can judge the methodological viability of a research idea before expending time and computational resources. We introduce SoundnessBench, a curated benchmark of 1,099 machine-learning research proposals reconstructed from ICLR submissions, labeled with reviewer soundness sub-scores, and audited against source papers. SoundnessBench should be interpreted as a benchmark for recoverable proposal-stage soundness rather than exact prediction of full-paper review outcomes. Across 12 frontier LLMs, we find a pervasive optimism bias: under standard prompting, models frequently rate low-soundness proposals as sound, while aggressive prompting largely shifts errors from false positives to false negatives. Additional controls for public-corpus contamination, paper-identifying phrases, surface features, and human audit quality suggest that this behavior is not explained by a single confounder. Our results indicate that current LLMs are not yet reliable as standalone first-gate evaluators for scientific rigor.

AI.tldr: A benchmark (SoundnessBench) evaluates if LLMs can judge the methodological viability of ML research proposals before resource-intensive work; results show optimism bias and limited reliability as gatekeepers.
AI.motivation: Address the bottleneck in autonomous AI research where evaluating proposal viability is costly; test if LLMs can serve as first gate for scientific rigor.
AI.method: Create SoundnessBench from 1,099 ICLR submissions; label proposals with reviewer-soundness sub-scores; audit against source papers; evaluate 12 frontier LLMs under standard and aggressive prompting; apply controls for contamination, phrase-identifying features, surface cues; interpret as recoverable proposal-stage soundness rather than full-paper outcomes.
AI.result: LLMs exhibit optimism bias: under standard prompting, many low-soundness proposals are rated sound; aggressive prompting shifts errors from false positives to false negatives; controls indicate no single confounder explains results; across models, not reliable as standalone first-gate evaluators.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs are not reliable standalone first-gate evaluators of scientific rigor for ML research proposals.

title: When, why, and how do diffusion posterior samplers fail? A finite-sample lens
comment: All code for experiments is available at: https://github.com/voilalab/diagnosing-posterior-sampling
summary: Diffusion models have excellent capacity to model complex distributions of natural data, which has made them a popular and effective choice for posterior sampling in imaging inverse problems. Existing methods can incorporate any measurement model at inference time but must use an inexact approximation for the likelihood at intermediate timesteps for computational tractability. Although these approximations can often work well empirically, their downstream effect on the sampled posterior is poorly understood and can result in unexplained failures. To understand when, why, and how these likelihood approximations propagate to erroneous posterior distributions, we introduce a finite-sample perspective on posterior sampling that approximates the posterior to arbitrary precision as training set size tends towards infinity, for any forward model and prior distribution. Using this finite-sample lens, we observe that popular posterior sampling approximations tend to under- or over-estimate the spread of the posterior at intermediate timesteps, causing downstream consequences including sensitivity to early stopping time, inaccurate relative weighting of posterior modes, and hallucination, both of prior modes that are not in the posterior and likelihood modes that are not supported by the prior. Moreover, we find that the cause of these posterior errors requires neither a nonlinear measurement model nor a multimodal posterior, but can arise solely due to a multimodal prior and inaccurate posterior spread at intermediate sampling times. Our finite-sample posterior sampling approach is agnostic to the type of likelihood approximation and the type of (linear or nonlinear) forward model, and can thus serve as a drop-in diagnostic to evaluate the accuracy and failure modes of existing and future posterior samplers.

AI.tldr: Finite-sample analysis of diffusion-model posterior sampling shows likelihood approximations at intermediate steps misestimate posterior spread, causing hallucinations and mode misweighting; provides a model-agnostic diagnostic framework that works for any forward model and prior.
AI.motivation: Understand and diagnose how intermediate-timestep likelihood approximations propagate into posterior errors in diffusion-based posterior sampling for imaging inverse problems.
AI.method: Introduce a finite-sample perspective that makes the posterior approximation arbitrarily precise as training set size → ∞. Treats forward model and prior generically; analyzes spread error at intermediate timesteps; presents a drop-in diagnostic for current and future samplers.
AI.result: Popular approximations tend to under- or over-estimate posterior spread at intermediate timesteps, causing sensitivity to early stopping, incorrect mode weighting, and hallucination of both priors and likelihood modes. Errors can arise even with a multimodal prior and linear or nonlinear forward models.
AI.conclusion: The finite-sample lens provides a general, agnostic diagnostic tool to evaluate posterior-sampler accuracy and failure modes, independent of the likelihood approximation or forward model.

title: Causal Label Recovery in Payment Networks
summary:
Fraud detection models in payment networks train on chargeback labels that are systematically biased. Every label must survive three sequential gates: authorization (declined transactions generate no labels), issuer reporting (unreported fraud is invisible), and delay (pending chargebacks are missing at training time). Labels that do arrive may be corrupted by first-party misuse or issuer misclassification. A companion paper [arXiv:2605.27557] proved that these four impairments impose a minimax lower bound on detection performance. This paper asks: can that bound be achieved?
  We formalize the observation pipeline as a sequential missing-data problem with three propensity stages and a corruption layer, and construct the Sequential Triply Robust (STR) estimator. The STR corrects for all four impairments simultaneously and achieves the semiparametric efficiency bound -- no estimator can have lower asymptotic variance. It is sequentially triply robust: at each gate, consistency requires only that either the propensity model or the outcome regression is correctly specified, not both.
