We provide corruption correction via noise-rate-adjusted pseudo-labels, empirical Bayes shrinkage to stabilize inverse-propensity weights for small issuers, a plug-in variance estimator yielding valid confidence intervals, and a Bernstein concentration inequality for finite-sample guarantees.
  On the operational side, we derive the optimal training delay -- the maturity window that minimizes the sum of label-quality loss and model staleness -- and prove that the STR permits training on data that is days old rather than months old, decoupling model freshness from the chargeback maturity cycle. The STR provably dominates naive chargeback-based training in mean squared error for any sample size.

AI.tldr: Sequential Triply Robust (STR) estimator for missing/corrupted chargeback labels in fraud detection. It corrects three gating missingness and label corruption, achieves semiparametric efficiency, and enables earlier, more data-fresh training with finite-sample guarantees, outperforming naive chargeback-based training.
AI.motivation: Chargeback labels are systematically biased due to three gates (authorization, issuer reporting, delay) and potential label corruption. This degrades fraud-detection performance; a method that corrects all impairments and attains the efficiency bound is needed, along with practical guidance on training data freshness.
AI.method: Model the observation pipeline as a sequential missing-data problem with three propensity stages plus a corruption layer. Develop STR estimator that is sequentially triply robust (consistency if either the propensity model or outcome regression is correct at each gate). Corrections include noise-rate-adjusted pseudo-labels for corruption, empirical Bayes shrinkage to stabilize inverse-propensity weights for small issuers, plug-in variance estimator for CIs, and Bernstein-type concentration for finite-sample guarantees. Also derive optimal training delay (maturity window) to balance label quality and model staleness.
AI.result: STR attains the semiparametric efficiency bound (minimum asymptotic variance). It dominates naive chargeback-based training in mean squared error for any sample size. It provides finite-sample guarantees via the Bernstein inequality and valid CIs via the plug-in variance estimator.
AI.conclusion: The STR estimator provides a practically deployable solution to learn from biased and partially observed chargeback data, enabling earlier training without sacrificing statistical efficiency and outperforming naive training approaches.

title: Fairness-Aware Federated Learning with Trajectory Shapley Value
comment: Accepted for publication at the 24th European Control Conference (ECC 2026)
summary: Federated learning is an emerging distributed paradigm that addresses the challenges posed by heterogeneous, privacy-sensitive data. It enables multiple clients to train a model collaboratively by aggregating their local updates at a server. However, conventional aggregation schemes typically use fixed weights that fail to reflect unequal and time-varying client contributions, leading to biased and unstable learning. To improve fairness and stability, we propose the Trajectory Shapley Value (TSV), a contribution metric that evaluates how each client influences the optimization trajectory of the global model using a validation-based, temporally consistent utility. Building on TSV, we design FedTSV, an adaptive aggregation method that converts per-round evaluations into dynamic client weights, allowing the server to respond to heterogeneous and adversarial participation in real time. Experiments on benchmark datasets show that FedTSV accelerates convergence, improves robustness, and yields more equitable contribution assessments, thereby providing a principled foundation for fairness-aware federated optimization.

AI.tldr: Introduces Trajectory Shapley Value (TSV) to quantify each client’s impact on the global optimization trajectory in federated learning, and FedTSV, an adaptive aggregation method using per-round TSV-derived weights to improve convergence, robustness, and fairness.
AI.motivation: Fixed, time-invariant aggregation weights inadequately reflect heterogeneous and dynamic client contributions, causing biased, unstable learning; a principled, validation-based metric is needed to allocate influence fairly.
AI.method: Define TSV as a validation-based, temporally consistent utility that measures each client’s influence on the optimization trajectory; derive dynamic per-round weights from TSV evaluations; implement FedTSV as an adaptive server-side aggregation rule that weights client updates according to TSV.
AI.result: Empirical results on benchmark datasets show faster convergence, improved robustness to heterogeneous/adversarial participation, and more equitable contribution assessments compared to fixed-weight baselines.
AI.conclusion: TSV provides a principled fairness-aware framework for federated optimization; FedTSV leverages TSV to deliver adaptive, stable, and fair aggregation, with potential applicability to real-time federated settings.

title: LoopFM: Learning frOm HistOrical RePresentations of Foundation Model for Recommendation
comment: Shali Jiang, Hua Zheng, Boyang Liu contributed equally to this work
summary: Knowledge distillation (KD) transfers a single scalar prediction from a large foundation model (FM) to compact vertical models (VMs), suffering from diminishing transfer ratio -- the fraction of FM improvement captured by the VM -- as a single scalar cannot convey the rich intermediate knowledge that larger FMs learn. To address this bottleneck, we propose LoopFM (Learning frOm HistOrical ReP*resentations of FM), a framework that opens a high-bandwidth transfer channel by structuring FM intermediate embeddings as input features (e.g., user history sequence) for downstream VMs, without requiring real-time FM inference at serving and architectural coupling between FM and VM. We provide a theoretical framework for LoopFM with a gain decomposition and transfer-ratio analysis. On three public benchmarks, LoopFM demonstrates strong AUC improvements (e.g., 6\%+ on TaobaoAd) and complementary knowledge transfer capability with KD. On industrial-scale systems (billions of examples, trillion-parameter FMs), LoopFM approximately doubles the knowledge transfer ratio on top of KD, delivering a +0.5\% conversion improvement in Y1H1, and a +1.03\% and +1.22\% conversion improvement from two individual launches respectively in Y1H2.

AI.tldr: LoopFM opens a high-bandwidth transfer channel for knowledge distillation by feeding large foundation-model intermediate embeddings (e.g., user history) as input features for downstream vanilla models, avoiding real-time FM inference and architectural coupling. It provides a gain-decomposition framework and transfer-ratio analysis, and yields substantial improvements on both public benchmarks and production-scale systems.
AI.motivation: KD often suffers from diminishing transfer ratio when transferring a single scalar improvement from a large FM to compact VMs. Larger FMs learn rich intermediate representations that are not captured by a single scalar, motivating a high-bandwidth transfer mechanism.
AI.method: LoopFM structures FM intermediate embeddings as downstream VM input features (e.g., user history sequences), enabling high-bandwidth transfer without real-time FM inference or tight FM–VM coupling. The authors provide a theoretical framework with gain decomposition and transfer-ratio analysis, and validate on public benchmarks and large industrial-scale deployments.
AI.result: Public benchmarks show strong AUC improvements (e.g., 6%+ on TaobaoAd) and complementary transfer to KD. On industrial-scale systems (billions of examples, trillion-parameter FMs), LoopFM roughly doubles the knowledge transfer ratio on top of KD, with conversion gains of +0.5% in Y1H1 and +1.03% and +1.22% in Y1H2 across two launches.
AI.conclusion: LoopFM effectively enlarges the knowledge transfer bandwidth by leveraging FM intermediate representations as inputs for VMs, achieving notable performance gains and complementing traditional KD without requiring real-time FM inference.

title: Efficient Test-Time Finetuning of LLMs via Convex Reconstruction and Gradient Caching
summary: Test-time finetuning (TTFT) is a rapidly evolving paradigm that adapts a language model to each prompt by retrieving related sequences, updating the model on them, and then evaluating the prompt. However, TTFT is only practical if it is fast: selection and finetuning both happen per query, making each a direct bottleneck. Existing methods trade speed for quality: fast retrieval is often redundant, while stronger diversity-aware selection adds prohibitive per-query cost. We introduce HullFT, a geometric approach to TTFT that addresses both bottlenecks. Given a query, HullFT first represents the query embedding as a sparse convex combination of few training sequences, using efficient projection-free Frank-Wolfe optimization. This yields a support set that is inherently relevant and diverse. We then convert the fractional convex weights into an exact integer multiset for finetuning through a geometric integerization procedure. The resulting multiplicities naturally create repeated examples, which we exploit with Gradient Reuse to amortize forward-backward computation across repeated finetuning steps. Our experiments show that HullFT improves the quality-efficiency tradeoff over current state-of-the-art TTFT methods, achieving lower bits-per-byte at substantially lower total runtime.

AI.tldr: HullFT uses a projection-free Frank-Wolfe method to represent a query as a sparse convex combination of a few training sequences, then converts the fractional weights to an integer multiset for finetuning. It further amortizes compute with Gradient Reuse, achieving a better quality-efficiency tradeoff for test-time finetuning (TTFT) than prior methods.
AI.motivation: TTFT aims to adapt language models to individual prompts by retrieving related sequences and finetuning on them, but per-query retrieval and finetuning create two bottlenecks. The need is for a method that is both fast and yields high-quality prompt adaptation without excessive per-query cost.
AI.method: Represent the query embedding as a sparse convex combination of a small number of training sequences using projection-free Frank-Wolfe optimization. Convert the fractional convex weights into an exact integer multiset (geometric integerization) to obtain repeated finetuning examples. Apply Gradient Reuse to reuse forward-backward computations across repeated finetuning steps, amortizing cost. Evaluate TTFT performance with this pipeline and compare against state-of-the-art TTFT baselines.
AI.result: HullFT improves the quality-efficiency tradeoff compared with leading TTFT methods, achieving lower bits-per-byte at substantially lower total runtime, indicating faster yet effective prompt adaptation.
AI.conclusion: A geometric TTFT framework that jointly addresses selection diversity and finetuning cost, enabling faster, more effective test-time adaptation through sparse convex selection, integerization, and gradient reuse.

title: Do Physics Foundation Models Learn Generalizable Physics? A Bias-Aware Benchmark Across Physical Regimes and Distribution Shifts
summary: Recent physics foundation models claim general spatiotemporal forecasting ability, yet their evaluations often collapse performance into a single average score under a fixed training distribution. This makes it difficult to determine whether a model has learned generalizable physical dynamics or only performs well under particular settings. We construct a benchmark with 8 physical dynamics, 3 training-data mixtures, and 25 test regimes induced by dynamic-scale and initial-condition complexity shifts, covering in-distribution, distribution-shift, and out-of-distribution settings. We evaluate five physics foundation model architectures and four model variants per architecture (scratch and three pretrained sizes), resulting in 60,000 measurements. Our results show that current physics foundation models behave as conditional rather than universal generalists: their generality depends on the physical regime, temporal scale, initial-condition setting, pretraining, model size, and architecture. Improving the training data distribution only partially mitigates this limitation. Pretraining and scaling are also unable to reliably remove their ability biases. We argue that improving physics foundation models requires moving beyond scaling models or expanding data, toward learning mechanisms that better capture transferable physical knowledge across regimes, temporal scales, and distribution shifts.

AI.tldr: Physics foundation models exhibit conditional rather than universal generalization; scaling and data alone do not ensure cross-regime transfer, and future progress requires new mechanisms for transferable physical knowledge.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations compress performance into an average over a fixed training distribution, masking regime-specific generalization and hindering assessment of true physical transfer capabilities.
AI.method: Constructed a benchmark with 8 physical dynamics, 3 training-data mixtures, and 25 test regimes created by dynamic-scale and initial-condition complexity shifts; evaluated 5 physics foundation model architectures and 4 variants per architecture (scratch and three pretrained sizes), yielding 60,000 measurements.
AI.result: Models act as conditional generalists: their generality depends on regime, temporal scale, initial conditions, pretraining, model size, and architecture. Enhancing the training data distribution partially mitigates limitations; pretraining and scaling do not reliably remove biases. Across regimes, transfer remains uneven.
AI.conclusion: Advancing physics foundation models requires learning mechanisms that capture transferable physical knowledge across regimes and distribution shifts, beyond mere scaling or data expansion.

title: Differentiable Belief-based Opponent Shaping
summary: Human coordination often relies on the ability to influence the beliefs of others through strategic action. In multi-agent reinforcement learning, opponent shaping attempts to replicate this influence, though existing methods typically operate within an opponent's parameter, policy, or value space. Meanwhile, belief-manipulation techniques in hidden-role games often rely on hard-coded objectives, such as deception or belief saturation. We propose Differentiable Belief-based Opponent Shaping (D-BOS), a first-order method that treats each observer's belief as the shaped opponent state and differentiates through $k$-step softmax-Bayes belief dynamics. Rather than explicitly rewarding deceptive or cooperative behavior, our method treats the belief state as the target for shaping. This allows the optimal strategy to emerge naturally from the environment's reward structure. This belief-space formulation provides an opponent-shaping signal by differentiating through opponent belief updates, and naturally extends to multiple observers by aggregating gradients over their individual inferred belief trajectories. Empirically, D-BOS outperforms PPO and BBM in hidden-role games, with the largest gains in mixed-motive settings.

AI.tldr: Introduces Differentiable Belief-based Opponent Shaping (D-BOS), a differentiable, belief-space shaping approach for opponent modeling and strategy in multi-agent RL.
AI.motivation: To move beyond hard-coded deception objectives and explicit parameter/policy shaping by treating observers' beliefs as controllable states and differentiating through belief updates to influence strategy via the environment reward.
AI.method: First-order method; treat each observer's belief as shaped opponent state; differentiate through k-step softmax-Bayes belief dynamics; aggregate gradients across multiple observers for multi-agent settings.
AI.result: Empirically outperforms PPO and BBM in hidden-role games, with largest gains in mixed-motive settings.
AI.conclusion: Belief-space formulation yields an implicit opponent-shaping signal via differentiating belief updates; extends naturally to multiple observers; avoids explicit deception objectives by aligning cognition dynamics with reward-driven policies.

title: Pocket-Dentist: On-Device Dental Image Understanding via Efficient Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Evaluations of dental vision-language models remain fragmented across datasets, task definitions and metrics, and often ignore their computational cost. This limits their widespread deployment for dental screening outside specialist centres, where timely inference, limited hardware, and local handling of patient images are vital for practical, privacy-preserving clinical prescreening. Here we present Pocket-Dentist, an efficiency-aware benchmark for dental multimodal question answering that brings together three datasets spanning approximately 1,159 patients, five task types and seven metrics. Across typical 14 VLMs, our results reveals an interesting observation: compact VLMs (e.g., 2B-parameter models) outperform larger VLMs in accuracy while requiring substantially lower computational costs in dental image understanding. Deployed locally on an iPhone 17 Pro, our finetuned compact VLM Pocket-Dentist-2B processed each sample in 4.31 s, reducing latency by 4.9-fold and memory use by 2.3-fold compared with a 7B baseline.

AI.tldr: Pocket-Dentist shows compact VLMs (2B) outperform larger models in accuracy while using less compute for dental multimodal QA; on-device iPhone 17 Pro deployment yields 4.31 s per sample, with 4.9x latency reduction and 2.3x memory reduction vs a 7B baseline across 3 datasets, 5 tasks, 7 metrics, 14 VLMs.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations of dental vision-language models are fragmented across datasets, task definitions, and metrics and often ignore computational cost, hindering practical, privacy-preserving clinical prescreening on limited hardware.
AI.method: Construct a Pocket-Dentist benchmark by aggregating three dental datasets (≈1,159 patients) spanning five task types and seven metrics. Evaluate 14 vision-language models on this benchmark. Finetune a compact 2B-parameter VLM (Pocket-Dentist-2B) for dental QA and deploy it on-device (iPhone 17 Pro) to compare latency and memory against a larger 7B baseline across samples.
AI.result: Across typical dental VLMs, compact 2B models outperform larger models in accuracy while requiring substantially lower computational costs. Pocket-Dentist-2B, finetuned for on-device use, processed each sample in 4.31 seconds, achieving a 4.9× reduction in latency and a 2.3× reduction in memory compared with a 7B baseline.
AI.conclusion: Efficiency-aware, on-device dental multimodal QA is feasible: compact VLMs can match or exceed larger models in accuracy while dramatically reducing compute and memory needs, enabling private, real-time prescreening without specialist infrastructure.

title: Robust and Efficient Guardrails with Latent Reasoning
summary: Maintaining the safety of large language models (LLMs) is crucial as they are increasingly deployed in real-world applications. Existing safety guardrails typically rely on single-pass classification or, more recently, distilled reasoning. Reasoning-based guardrails significantly outperform classification-only baselines, but they incur substantial query latency and token overhead that make them impractical for highthroughput deployment. To address this challenge, we propose COLAGUARD, a guardrail model that transfers multi-step safety reasoning into a continuous latent space through a stage-wise training curriculum, enabling direct hidden-state propagation at inference. Evaluated on ten prompt- and response-moderation settings spanning eight safety benchmarks, COLAGUARD improves macro-F1 by 8.24 points over Llama Guard 3 and matches our explicit reasoning baseline, GuardReasoner, in macroF1 while delivering a 12.9X speedup and 22.4X reduction in token usage. Our results suggest that latent reasoning offers a practical alternative to explicit rationale generation for deployable guardrails, jointly improving safety robustness and inference efficiency rather than treating them as competing objectives.

AI.tldr: COLAGUARD encodes multi-step safety reasoning into a latent space to enable direct hidden-state inference, delivering strong performance with high throughput.
AI.motivation: To overcome the latency and token overhead of reasoning-based guardrails while preserving robustness.
AI.method: Stage-wise training curriculum to transfer multi-step safety reasoning into a continuous latent space, enabling direct hidden-state propagation at inference, evaluated on ten prompt- and response-moderation settings across eight benchmarks.
AI.result: Macro-F1 improved by 8.24 points over Llama Guard 3; matches GuardReasoner on macro-F1; 12.9x faster and 22.4x fewer tokens; outperforms single-pass classification baselines.
AI.conclusion: Latent reasoning is a practical alternative to explicit rationale generation for deployable guardrails, achieving joint improvements in safety robustness and inference efficiency.

title: Rethinking FID Through the Geometry of the Reference Dataset
comment: 9 pages, 2 figures. Accepted to ICML 2026 Workshop: Combining Theory and Benchmarks
summary: Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) is widely used to evaluate image generators, yet lower FID does not always correspond to better sample quality. We show that this mismatch depends in part on the geometry of the reference dataset. In a controlled study across six datasets, distributional density and effective rank significantly explain how FID changes as sample quality improves. Concentrated datasets tend to yield more favorable FID trends, whereas more dispersed datasets can make FID worsen despite better samples. Attribution to precision and recall and ablations with alternative feature spaces and distances support the same conclusion. These results suggest that distributional metrics should be interpreted together with the geometry of the reference dataset for more reliable benchmarking.

AI.tldr: FID's reliability as a proxy for sample quality depends on the geometry of the reference dataset; distributional density and effective rank explain FID changes; concentrated datasets show more favorable FID trends, while dispersed datasets can yield higher FID despite better samples; interpret FID with dataset geometry, alongside precision/recall and other metrics.
AI.motivation: FID is a standard metric for evaluating image generators, but its relationship to actual sample quality is not universal; understanding how reference dataset geometry affects FID is crucial for reliable benchmarking across models and datasets.
AI.method: A controlled study across six datasets assessing how FID responds to improved sample quality. The analysis examines distributional density and effective rank as key geometric factors, and uses attribution to precision and recall along with ablations using alternative feature spaces and distance metrics to validate the findings.
AI.result: Distributional properties of the reference data explain how FID changes with sample quality. Concentrated datasets yield more favorable FID trends; dispersed datasets can cause FID to worsen despite higher sample quality. Attribution analyses to precision and recall and ablations with different feature spaces/distances corroborate the same conclusion.
AI.conclusion: To benchmark generative models reliably, distributional metrics like FID should be interpreted in the context of the reference dataset's geometry (density and rank).

title: Lightweight Complementary-Cue Fusion for Robust Video Face Forgery Detection
summary: Current face video forgery detectors use wide or dual-stream backbones. We show that a single, lightweight fusion of two handcrafted cues can achieve higher accuracy with a much smaller model. Based on the Xception baseline model (21.9 million parameters), we build two detectors: LFWS, which adds a 1x1 convolution to combine a low-frequency Wavelet-Denoised Feature (WDF) with a phase-spectrum channel derived from Spatial-Phase Shallow Learning (SPSL), and LFWL, which merges WDF with Local Binary Patterns (LBP) in the same way. This extra module adds only 292 parameters, keeping the total at 21.9 million, smaller than F3Net (22.5 million) and less than half the size of SRM (55.3 million). Even with this minimal overhead, the fused models increase the average area under the curve (AUC) from 74.8% to 78.6% on FaceForensics++ and from 70.5% to 74.9% on DFDC-Preview, gains of 3.8% and 4.4% over the Xception baseline. They also consistently outperform F3Net, SRM, and SPSL in eight public benchmarks, without extra data or test-time augmentation. These results show that carefully paired, handcrafted features, combined through the lightweight fusion block, can provide competitive robustness at a significantly lower cost than comparable frequency-based detectors. Our findings suggest a need to reevaluate scale-driven design choices in face video forgery detection.

AI.tldr: Lightweight fusion of two handcrafted cues improves face video forgery detection with minimal parameter overhead.
AI.motivation: Questioning the necessity of large, multi-stream backbones; explore whether combining handcrafted cues via a tiny fusion block can match or exceed frequency-based detectors at lower cost.
AI.method: Extend Xception baseline (21.9M params) with two detectors: LFWS fuses Wavelet-Denoised Features (WDF) with SPSL-derived phase spectrum; LFWL fuses WDF with Local Binary Patterns (LBP) using an added 1x1 fusion conv (only 292 extra params). Compare against F3Net, SRM; evaluate on FaceForensics++ and DFDC-Preview, plus eight benchmarks.
AI.result: AUC improved from 74.8% to 78.6% on FaceForensics++ and 70.5% to 74.9% on DFDC-Preview (gains of 3.8% and 4.4% over Xception baseline). Total parameters remain 21.9M, still smaller than F3Net (22.5M) and far smaller than SRM (55.3M). Outperforms F3Net, SRM, and SPSL across eight public benchmarks without extra data or test-time augmentation.
AI.conclusion: Carefully paired handcrafted cues with a lightweight fusion block can achieve competitive robustness at a lower cost than scale-heavy frequency-based detectors; suggests reevaluating scale-driven design choices in face video forgery detection.
